Heard, useful, and unmeant — which is precisely the status this long movement was constructed to establish.

Verse 3.1.71.translation: In another injunctive sentence there is no number of the animal that conflicts; therefore that very one, together with its quality, is understood as existing with oneness.
Verse 3.1.71.commentary: The case of 3.1.55 resolved by the absence of a competitor. Vidhi-vakyantare samkhya pasoh na asti virodhini: in *another* injunctive sentence there is *no* number of the animal that *conflicts* — nothing elsewhere prescribes a different count, so nothing contests what this rule's singular presents. Tasmat sa-guna eva asau sad-ekatvena gamyate: therefore *that very one*, sa-guna, *together with its quality*, is understood as *existing with oneness*. The reasoning is negative and it is characteristic of the exegetical method: number stands here not because it was argued into place but because nothing dislodges it — the badha-machinery (2.66's annulment, 2.73's hierarchy of evidences) having found no conflict to adjudicate. Where nothing competes, what is heard holds. This is the settlement of the long movement on the sacrificial animal: one beast, and the oneness is neither enjoined (3.1.65-66) nor annulled — it simply survives, unopposed.

Verse 3.1.72.translation: A quality that is taught in the action, the substance-connection being already known — by that, its need for the substance is understood as though for an instrument.
Verse 3.1.72.commentary: The relation of quality to substance in an injunction, and it inverts the expected direction. Nirjnata-dravya-sambandhe — the *substance-connection* being *already known* (the rite's substance settled by an earlier rule) — yah karmani upadisyate gunah: a *quality* that is taught in the action: 'with a red cow', where the cow is given and the redness is added. Tena arthita tasya dravyena iva pratiyate: by *that* (quality), its *need* for the substance is understood *as though* for an instrument. The quality needs the substance the way an action needs its means — the redness cannot present itself except in a red thing (1.62's paratantra guna: the subordinate has no standing alone). So the taught quality does not qualify a substance already independently there; it *requires* one, and the requirement is what the injunction registers. The iva keeps the ontology honest: the quality does not literally use the substance as a tool, but its dependence has the same structure.

Verse 3.1.73-74.translation: (73) Just as some quality in a substance is characterized by capacity, so too a substratum for the quality, obtained, is characterized by capacity. (74) But when the two are needed separately, the connection that is understood — in that there is no injury, nor is anything unheard to be constructed.
Verse 3.1.73-74.commentary: The dependence of 3.1.72 shown to be mutual. (73): kascid eva guno dravye yatha samarthya-laksanah — as *some quality* in a substance is characterized by *capacity* (3.1.3's jatih sakty-upalaksanam: the property flagged because it flags a capacity) — adharo 'pi gunasya evam praptah samarthya-laksanah: so too the *substratum* for the quality, obtained, is characterized by capacity. The redness needs a cow, and the cow is wanted for what it can do. Neither is prior; each is there for the capacity the other completes. (74): tayoh tu prthag-arthitve — but when the two are *needed separately*, each on its own account — sambandho yah pratiyate, the *connection* that is understood: na tasmin upaghato 'sti — in that there is *no injury*: nothing is strained, no rule violated; kalpyam anyat na ca asrutam: nor is *anything unheard* to be *constructed* — no supplement must be imported. The maxim is the exegete's economy (2.84's guru/laghu: prefer the lighter assumption): a construal that needs no additions and breaks nothing is the one to take.

Verse 3.1.75.translation: The connection with the action is what is brought by the explicit text for those two; but the restriction of substratum and what rests on it stands from the sentence.
Verse 3.1.75.commentary: The two channels distinguished, and the distinction is the hierarchy of 2.73 at work. Kriyaya yo 'bhisambandhah sa sruti-prapitah tayoh: the connection *with the action* is *brought by the explicit text* for both quality and substance — sruti, what is actually said, delivers that they figure in the rite. But: asraya-asrayinoh vakyat niyamah tu avatisthate — the *restriction* of substratum and what rests on it stands *from the sentence*: which is the bearer and which the borne is settled not by any word but by the construal, vakya — the third and weakest of the evidences (sruti > linga > vakya). So the rite's participation is *said*; their internal architecture is *construed*. The stratification matters for what follows: a connection resting on vakya can be overridden by one resting on sruti, and 3.1.76 immediately draws the consequence.

Verse 3.1.76.translation: There, in the absence of substance and quality, there would be an option for each severally; for a connection obtained by the explicit text is stronger than one characterized by construal.
Verse 3.1.76.commentary: The consequence, and it is a rule of practical adjudication. Tatra dravya-guna-abhave pratyekam syad vikalpanam: in the *absence* of substance and quality — where the prescribed cow or the prescribed redness is unavailable — there would be *option* for each severally: one may take the substance without the quality, or the quality's bearer without this substance; the rite does not simply fail. And the ground: sruti-prapto hi sambandho balavan vakya-laksanat — a connection obtained by the *explicit text* is *stronger* than one characterized by *construal*. Since the text said that quality and substance figure (3.1.75's sruti-channel) but only construal linked them to each other (the vakya-channel), the link is the weak member and gives way first. So when the red cow cannot be had, one does not abandon the rite; one relaxes the construed bond and keeps what was said. The verse is fine exegetical engineering, and its principle — stronger evidence survives, weaker yields — is the same badha-machinery that 2.73 catalogued.

Verse 3.1.77.translation: But when a universal, or a capacity, is taught with respect to the action — then substance and quality are brought near, from capacity.
Verse 3.1.77.commentary: The reverse configuration. Where the injunction names not a substance and a quality but a jati or a sakti — 'sacrifice with cattle', 'use something that holds' — then dravya-gunau samarthyat sannidhiyete: substance and quality are *brought near*, from *capacity*: they are not said, and they arrive, because a universal cannot be sacrificed and a capacity cannot be handled — something must bear them (3.1.72's arthita, the need running the other way). Sannidhi is the term of 2.47 (fixity shining forth through mere proximity) and 2.51 (particulars manifested in co-presence): the unsaid participants are not inferred but *drawn in*, by what the said ones cannot do without. So the injunction's silence about the beast is not a gap: naming cattle-hood summons cattle, because capacity does the summoning. The pair 3.1.76-77 thus completes the picture: what is said may be relaxed under pressure, and what is unsaid arrives under necessity — and neither transaction runs through the words.

Verse 3.1.78.translation: Though universals and qualities are equally auxiliary with respect to the action, qualities are substituted; the universals of goats and the like are not.
Verse 3.1.78.commentary: An asymmetry that the parity-doctrine did not predict, and it is decisive. Jatinam ca gunanam ca tulye 'ngatve kriyam prati: universals and qualities are *equally* auxiliary — both contribute to the rite, both were called sadhana (3.1.27, 3.1.55). And yet: gunah pratinidhiyante chagadinam na jatayah — *qualities* are *substituted*; the *universals* of goats and the like are *not*. When the prescribed red goat cannot be had, one may use a black one — the quality yields. When no goat can be had, one may not use a sheep — the universal does not yield. The substitution-rule (3.1.3's pratinidhi, the samuddesa's opening evidence) discriminates sharply between two things it had treated as equals. So angatva, auxiliary-status, is not one status: within it, some auxiliaries are essential and some are negotiable, and ritual practice knows the difference infallibly while no doctrine so far advanced predicts it. The next verse explains the asymmetry.

Verse 3.1.79.translation: Universals are very near to the capacity of the individual; qualities are not so. The substance is directly conjoined with the action; therefore the quality is optional.
Verse 3.1.79.commentary: The asymmetry of 3.1.78 explained, and the explanation is a metaphysics of proximity. Vyakti-sakteh samasanna jatayah — *universals* are *very near* to the *capacity of the individual*: goathood is what makes this goat able to do what goats do in a rite; remove it and the capacity goes. Na tatha gunah: *not so* the qualities — the redness is not what the goat's ritual capacity rests on; it can go and the capacity stays. And: saksat dravyam kriya-yogi — the *substance* is *directly* conjoined with the action: it is what is handled, bound, offered. Gunas tasmat vikalpate: *therefore* the quality is *optional*. The account is exact and it rescues the substitution-rule from arbitrariness: what may be substituted is what stands at a remove from the capacity the action needs, and what may not is what the capacity is. This is 3.1.3-5's doctrine (the universal as indicator of capacity) doing real work at last — and it explains why the tradition never let a sheep stand for a goat while cheerfully accepting a discoloured one.

Verse 3.1.80.translation: By some, option is accepted where, both being equal, one or the other is absent; therefore the goat would be one lacking that quality, or the sheep would be one possessing it.
Verse 3.1.80.commentary: The hard case, where the asymmetry of 3.1.78-79 gives out. Samyena anyatara-abhave vikalpah kaiscid isyate: by *some*, *option* is accepted where — the two being *equal* — one or the other is *absent*. And the dilemma spelled out: atad-guno 'tas chagah syat — *therefore* the goat would be one *lacking* that quality (a goat, wrong colour); mesah va tad-guno bhavet — *or* the sheep would be one *possessing* it (right colour, wrong beast). Which? The verse reports that some allow a free option — take either. Bhartrhari does not adjudicate, and the case is genuinely hard: 3.1.79's rule says keep the universal and drop the quality, but that rule was derived from cases where the quality was clearly remote from the capacity, and here the colour may be doing ritual work of its own. The exegetical tradition's willingness to accept vikalpa — free option — as an outcome is itself notable: 2.85 recorded that option is the science's last resort, and here is a case that reaches it.

Verse 3.1.81-82.translation: (81) The operation of a universal whose number is dependent is observed; somewhere that same one operates, having relinquished any particular number. (82) A general that has become auxiliary to another is joined with the substance's number; but when it operates for its own sake, it does not lean upon number.
Verse 3.1.81-82.commentary: Number's dependence on the universal's role, and the rule is elegant. (82) states it: para-anga-bhutam samanyam yujyate dravya-samkhyaya — a general that has become *auxiliary to another* is *joined with the substance's number*: where cowhood is serving a cow ('bring the two cows'), it takes the number of what it serves. Sva-artham pravartamanam tu na samkhyam avalambate: but when it operates *for its own sake* — where cowhood is itself the topic ('cowhood is a universal') — it *does not lean upon* number: the abstract noun takes a singular that means nothing (3.1.52's unintended ekatva). So number attaches to universals only parasitically, through the substances they qualify, and drops away the moment the universal is spoken of in its own right. (81) records the observation the rule explains: sometimes the universal operates samkhya-visesam utsrjya, *having relinquished* any particular number. The verse-pair gives the grammar's number-apparatus its exact semantic scope: it reaches things, and reaches types only through things.

Verse 3.1.83.translation: In 'let him sacrifice with an animal' — the animal being heard for the sake of the sacrificing — the action is the principal, one animal having served the purpose.
Verse 3.1.83.commentary: The test-case's final settlement. Yajeta pasuna iti atra — in 'let him sacrifice with an animal' — yajy-arthayam pasu-srutau: the *animal* being *heard for the sake of the sacrificing*: the noun is there in service of the verb (2.48's karaka-architecture: nouns orbit the action). Krtartha ekena pasuna pradhanam bhavati kriya: *one animal* having *served the purpose*, the *action* is the *principal*. Every element of the long movement is gathered here. The action is principal (3.1.69's rule: what the text must enjoin is its business); the animal is its means, heard for its sake; one suffices because purpose is what governs (3.1.63-64's economy); and the oneness is neither enjoined nor annulled but simply what serving the purpose required (3.1.65's discrimination). Krtartha — 'having served the purpose' — is the operative word and it is the whole doctrine: the rite wanted a sacrificing, the animal was for that, and when the purpose is met the question of number does not arise.

Verse 3.1.84-85.translation: (84) If one should perform the slaying of as many as are possible for him — then by those qualities the principal would be occasioned. (85) But in the graha, where the ritual apparatus is being cleansed and there is a hearing of the action — we do not at all insist there on the grasping of a particular number.
Verse 3.1.84-85.commentary: The two poles of the movement set side by side. (84) states the maximalist temptation: yavatam sambhavo yasya sa kuryat tavatam yadi alambhanam — *if* one should slay *as many as are possible* for him — gunaih tena pradhanam syat prayojitam: then by *those qualities* the *principal* would be *occasioned*: more offerings, more merit, the principal served by abundance. The verse states it as a conditional and 3.1.63-64 has already refused it: beyond satisfaction there is no more to be had, and surplus is anarthaka. (85) gives the settled contrary: sammrjyamana-tantre grahe yatra kriya-srutih — in the *graha*, where the apparatus is being *cleansed* and the action is *heard* — samkhya-visesa-grahanam na eva tatra adriyamahe: we do *not at all insist* there on the grasping of a *particular number*. The first-person plural is worth noticing — adriyamahe, 'we do not insist': the author speaking as a member of the tradition, in its voice, settling a case. Between 84's refused maximalism and 85's relaxed cleansing, the movement's whole range is on display: number matters exactly where purpose makes it matter, and nowhere else.

Verse 3.1.86.translation: In a sentence whose purport concerns what is to be taught, the mention of 'one' that has been made — in the remainder, even where the number is distinct, that mark's exhibition is evident.
Verse 3.1.86.commentary: A refinement on how the number-word functions in a teaching-sentence. Sisyamana-pare vakye — in a sentence whose *purport* concerns *what is to be taught* (the sentence's business is instruction, not specification) — yad eka-grahanam krtam: the mention of *'one'* that has been made — sese visista-samkhye 'pi vyaktam tal-linga-darsanam: in the *remainder*, even where the number is *distinct*, that *mark's exhibition* is *evident*. Where the sentence teaches, its 'one' is a specimen: the number is exhibited as a mark, an illustration of the pattern, and applies to the remainder whatever their actual counts. This is the pedagogical function of forms that the whole work has insisted on (2.13's divisions for the unlearned; 2.176's instruction by the general for brevity's sake) — and it identifies a third status for number, beside intended and unintended: *exhibited*, offered as a sample rather than a stipulation.

Verse 3.1.87.translation: As the explicit text has fallen, in the rule for compounds and affixes — of qualities that are dependent, it is established by the principle itself.
Verse 3.1.87.commentary: The doctrine confirmed from the grammar's side. Samasa-pratyaya-vidhau yatha nipatita srutih: as the *explicit text* has *fallen* — nipatita, dropped into place, the wording as it happens to stand — in the rule for *compounds and affixes*. Gunanam paratantranam nyayena eva upapadyate: *of qualities that are dependent*, it is established *by the principle itself*. Panini's rules for compounds and affixes mention qualities, and the mentions carry numbers and genders that nobody supposes are prescribed: the wording fell that way, and the *principle* — that dependents take their determinations from what they depend on (3.1.82's rule) — supplies what the wording does not mean. So the grammar's own text exhibits exactly the phenomenon the ritual science has been sorting: forms present, heard, and semantically inert, their apparent content supplied by structure rather than by the words. The verse's nipatita is dry and exact: the text fell there; do not read fate into where it landed.

Verse 3.1.88.translation: Even in the quality, number is not admitted as auxiliary for the accomplishing of another principal; the agent, likewise, is understood in the object as undifferentiated.
Verse 3.1.88.commentary: The number-doctrine stated in its final restriction. Gune 'pi na angikriyate pradhanantara-siddhaye samkhya: *even in the quality*, number is *not admitted as auxiliary* for the accomplishing of *another* principal — a quality's number does not enter the service of a rite whose business is elsewhere; it does not get promoted into a factor merely because it is present (3.1.67's restriction: covariance establishes contribution, not signification). And the parallel: karta tatha karmani avisistah pratiyate — the *agent*, likewise, is understood in the *object* as *undifferentiated*: where the sentence's business is the object, the agent comes along unspecified, its determinations unmeant. Two categories, one rule: what the sentence is not about arrives without its features meant. This is 1.136's canon of sato 'vivaksa — the non-intention of what is actually there — applied across the karaka-system, and it prepares the Sadhana-samuddesa, the seventh, where the karakas get their full treatment.
Verse 3.1.88.contested: the second line's karta tatha karmani avisistah is compressed and our construal (the agent unspecified where the object is at issue) follows the parallel with the first line; some read it as a separate observation about agent-object identity in certain constructions

Verse 3.1.89.translation: That other thing which, being applicable, has a further explicit text for the sake of restriction — since it has served its purpose in the exclusion, number there is unintended.
Verse 3.1.89.commentary: A last case, and it turns on what a restricting text has already done. Yasya anyasya prasaktasya niyama-artha punah srutih: that other thing which, being *applicable* — prasakta, already in play, obtainable — has a *further explicit text* for the sake of *restriction* (the sruti here doing niyama-work: narrowing what was antecedently possible, as 'rice' narrows 'substance' at 2.66). Nivrttau caritarthatvat samkhya tatra avivaksita: since it has *served its purpose in the exclusion*, number there is *unintended*. The restricting text's whole office was to exclude the alternatives; having done that, it is caritartha — spent, its errand discharged (2.54's caritartha: the words that vanish into their meaning; 2.270's carita-asti-kriya). Whatever else it carries — its number among them — is residue. The verse completes the movement's inventory of ways a number can fail to be meant: forced (3.1.51), merely referential (3.1.59), exhibited as a mark (3.1.86), fallen with the wording (3.1.87), riding a dependent (3.1.88), and now spent along with its sentence's discharged errand.
Verse 3.1.89.contested: the source numbers this verse 88 in its final line while labelling the page 3.1.89; we follow the page-label, and note the numbering fault

Verse 3.1.90.translation: But the case-ending which is prescribed after an aggregate of like-formed things — there, one alone, being meaningful, is established as the expressor of the aggregate.
Verse 3.1.90.commentary: The movement closes by returning to ekasesa, and the return is pointed. Sarupa-samudayat tu vibhaktir ya vidhiyate: the *case-ending* prescribed after an *aggregate of like-formed* things — the single-remainder construction (2.108-110: 'ramau' for two Ramas, one form serving many). Ekas tatra arthavan siddhah samudayasya vacakah: *one alone*, being *meaningful*, is *established* as the *expressor of the aggregate*. One word, meaningful, expressing many — and the number that does the expressing is carried by an ending on a form that stands for all of them. So the samuddesa's long interrogation of number ends where the Vakyakanda's boundary-machinery ended: with the device in which a single form bears plural office (2.109: uttering them severally would be *incorrect*), and in which, therefore, number is neither distributed among the words nor absent from the meaning. It is a capacity of the one form, carved by the construction — which is what the samuddesa has been arguing about number all along, and what 3.1.6 said about every word's meaning in its sixth verse.

Verse 3.1.91.translation: In the rule for an affix, or for a principal, or even for a compound — when number is intended, assistance in the quality is established in every way.
Verse 3.1.91.commentary: A closing rule for the number-movement, gathering its cases. Pratyayasya pradhanasya samasasya api va vidhau — in the rule for an *affix*, for a *principal*, or even for a *compound*: the three sites where the grammar's number-apparatus operates. Siddhah samkhya-vivaksayam sarvatha anugraho gune: when number is *intended*, *assistance in the quality* is established *in every way*. Where number is genuinely meant (3.1.60's yatnat: what costs effort to say), it does its work wherever it stands — affix, principal, or compound — and the quality it attaches to is thereby served. The rule's interest is its scope: having spent forty verses establishing how rarely number is intended, the samuddesa closes the topic by noting that where it *is* intended, nothing blocks it. Sarvatha, 'in every way': the exceptions were all about vivaksa, never about capacity.

Verse 3.1.92.translation: The form of non-difference, similarity, and the powers that are one's very self — the expressing of these too, as synonyms of the universal, is described.
Verse 3.1.92.commentary: A widening of the universal's vocabulary, and the widening is philosophically loaded. Three things are named as jati-paryaya-vacitva — expressed as *synonyms of the universal*: abheda-rupam, the *form of non-difference*; sadrsya, *similarity*; and atma-bhutah saktayah, the *powers that are one's very self* (3.1.22-24's doctrine: the categories as powers of the one). So 'universal' names indifferently: what is not-different, what resembles, and what a thing can do. The three are not obviously the same, and their identification is the samuddesa's compressed argument: non-difference is what recurrence amounts to (3.1.14's anuvrtti), similarity is how recurrence is met in experience (2.90-94's respect-wise resemblance without shared parts), and capacity is what any of it is *for* (3.1.11's padarthas characterized by operation). Three descriptions, one phenomenon — and the verse's paryaya, 'synonym', is the term 2.150 used to deny that corrupt and correct forms are interchangeable. Here the interchange is granted.

Verse 3.1.93-94.translation: (93) Although one obtains a staff through the desire to take up a staff — not from that capacity alone is he understood as 'staff-bearer'. (94) The cognition 'he has desire' does not proceed from the occasioning ground of desire; therefore, even where the capacity exists, the cognition rests on another object.
Verse 3.1.93-94.commentary: A sharp argument that capacity does not by itself deliver a description. (93): a man's iccha, his *desire to take up a staff*, is what gets him the staff — the capacity is real and it operates. And yet: na tasmad eva samarthyat sa dandi iti pratiyate — *not from that capacity alone* is he understood as *'staff-bearer'*: he is called dandi because he *holds* one, not because he wanted one. Capacity does not name. (94) generalizes: na iccha-nimittad iccha-van iti jnanam pravartate — the cognition 'he *has* desire' does not proceed from the *occasioning ground* of desire: one does not know a man is desirous by inspecting his desire's cause. Tasmat sati api samarthye buddhih arthantarasraya: therefore, *even where the capacity exists*, the cognition *rests on another object*. The verse pair is doing quiet damage to 3.1.92's identification of the universal with capacity: if capacity does not deliver descriptions, then the universal — which does — is not simply capacity. The samuddesa is auditing its own equations.

Verse 3.1.95.translation: Either an indescribable own-nature, or capacity, stands at the end of everything — wherefore, from that, practice is not constructed.
Verse 3.1.95.commentary: The terminus of analysis, and its consequence. Svabhavo 'vyapadesyo va samarthyam va avatisthate sarvasya ante: either an *indescribable own-nature*, or *capacity*, stands *at the end of everything* — push any analysis far enough and one reaches either a nature that cannot be told (3.1.20-21's uncommingled thing, to which no category applies; 2.416's what-has-own-form-cannot-be-described) or a bare capacity. Yatah tasmat vyavaharo na kalpate: *wherefore*, *from that*, *practice is not constructed*. Neither terminus supports transaction: the indescribable cannot be said, and capacity does not name (3.1.93-94). So whatever is at the bottom, vyavahara does not rest on it — practice runs on the constructed middle (2.297's sabdah loka-nibandhanah: words are grounded in the world, the shared audited domain, not in the ultimate). The verse is the samuddesa's most economical statement of the work's persistent finding: the real is not what we speak from, and speech's ground is not the ground.

Verse 3.1.96.translation: When, having abandoned the differences, it is grasped by the intellect as though one — then it is the individual's very self, and a single cognition operates there.
Verse 3.1.96.commentary: The first of two accounts of how a plurality comes to be taken as one. Yada bhedan parityajya buddhya eka iva grhyate: when, *having abandoned the differences*, it is grasped by the *intellect* as though *one* — the mind drops the distinctions and takes what remains. Tada vyakty-atma eva tatra buddhih eka pravartate: then it is the *individual's very self*, and a *single cognition* operates there. Note what this account says: what is grasped is not a universal over the individuals but the individual's own self, the differences merely set aside. So oneness here is subtractive — an achievement of ignoring, not a perception of a type (against 3.1.29's face-in-water, where the universal itself was manifested). The iva marks it: grasped *as though* one. The verse offers the deflationary account of unity, and 3.1.97 will offer its companion.

Verse 3.1.97.translation: When one thinks of what is interwoven with the forms of difference as though it were one — then a cognition grasping the collection arises from the many.
Verse 3.1.97.commentary: The second account, and it inverts the first. Bheda-rupaih anusyutam — what is *interwoven with the forms of difference* (anusyuta: the weaver's word, 1.123's anuviddham iva sabdena — cognition shot through with word) — yada ekam iva manyate: when one thinks of it as though *one*. Then: samuha-avagraha buddhih bahubhyah jayate tada — a cognition *grasping the collection* arises *from the many*. Here the unity is not achieved by abandoning differences (3.1.96) but by taking the differentiated manifold *as* a collection: the many are retained and gathered. So there are two routes to oneness — subtractive (ignore the differences) and collective (gather them) — and they produce different cognitions: one of the individual's self, one of the samuha, the group. The pair is the samuddesa's contribution to a question the whole work has circled: what is it to take many as one? And its answer is that there is no single such operation.

Verse 3.1.98.translation: In a single operation, oneness; in repetition, the state of being of like self — the individuals, whose selves are distinct, attain this from the exclusion of difference.
Verse 3.1.98.commentary: The two routes of 3.1.96-97 given their conditions, and the last word is the loaded one. Sakrt-pravrttau ekatvam: in a *single* operation — one glance, one act — *oneness*; avrttau sadrsatmatam — in *repetition*, the state of being *of like self*: met again, the thing shows as similar rather than as identical. So oneness is what a single act delivers and similarity is what repeated acts deliver — a fine observation about how types are built out of encounters. And the mechanism: bhinnatmikanam vyaktinam bheda-apohat prapadyate — the individuals, *whose selves are distinct*, attain this *from the exclusion of difference*. Apoha — *exclusion*: the word that the Buddhist logicians would make the foundation of their entire semantics, and which 3.1.14 already canvassed as one of the two available definitions of the universal (vyavrtti). Here it names the operation by which distinct individuals come to present as one or as alike: not by sharing something but by having their differences excluded. The samuddesa on universals has arrived at the doctrine that would be used to abolish universals, and states it as its own mechanism.
Verse 3.1.98.contested: bheda-apohat is the crux: whether Bhartrhari uses apoha here as a settled technical term (and if so, whose) or in its plain sense of 'removal' cannot be determined from the karika. The relation between this verse and the developed apoha-doctrine of Dignaga is a genuine and unresolved question of intellectual history

Verse 3.1.99.translation: As a cognition, by way of recurrence, is understood as undivided — so the object too, though its form is exclusion, is grasped as the reality.
Verse 3.1.99.commentary: The parallel that closes the argument of 3.1.96-98. Anupravrtta iti yatha abhinna buddhih pratiyate: as a *cognition*, by way of *recurrence* (anupravrtti, 3.1.19's mark of the universal), is understood as *undivided* — the repeated cognition presents as one continuing thing, not as a series. Artho vyavrtta-rupo 'pi tatha tattvena grhyate: so the *object* too, *though its form is exclusion* (vyavrtta: 3.1.14's second definition, 3.1.98's apoha), is grasped *as the reality*. Both sides of the transaction are the same structure: what is really a recurrence presents as a unity; what is really an exclusion presents as a positive thing. Tattvena — 'as the reality': we take the excluded-from-others *to be* something. The verse states the illusion-mechanism with complete evenhandedness between the two definitions: whichever the universal is, it is not what it presents as, and the presenting is what makes speech possible (2.238's asatya vartman: standing on the untrue path to seek the true).

Verse 3.1.100-101.translation: (100) And of all like-formed things there exist no expressing words that fall upon their difference; nor is the difference ascertained. (101) The particulars that stand as the objects of cognition, word and meaning — because those are hard to ascertain, there is the seeing of oneness in cognition and the rest.
Verse 3.1.100-101.commentary: Why we do not see the differences we do not name. (100): sarupanam ca sarvesam na bheda-upanipatinah vidyante vacakah sabdah — of all *like-formed* things there exist no *expressing words* that *fall upon their difference*: language has no vocabulary for the distinctions among things it groups; na api bhedo 'vadharyate — *nor is the difference ascertained*: and consequently we do not make them out. The order of that sentence is the doctrine: no words, therefore no ascertainment (3.1.47's unnamed scent-difference, real and unexpressed). (101) applies it reflexively: the visesah — particulars — that are the objects of jnana, sabda and artha — of *cognition*, *word*, and *meaning* — are duravadharatvat, *hard to ascertain*; hence jnanady-ekatva-darsanam: the *seeing of oneness* in cognition and the rest. We take our cognitions to be one kind of thing, our words to be one kind, because their particulars escape us. So the very categories in which this treatise is written are unities-by-default, produced by the same failure that produces every other universal.

Verse 3.1.102.translation: As among objects, so among all cognitions too there are universals; in the seeing of commingling they exist, and they are what accomplish the meaning.
Verse 3.1.102.commentary: The universals extended to the cognitive side. Jnanesu api yatha arthesu tatha sarvesu jatayah: as among *objects*, so among *all cognitions* too there are universals — cognition-types, kinds of knowing, as real (or as constructed) as kinds of thing. And their conditions: samsarga-darsane santi — in the *seeing of commingling* they *exist* (3.1.20's prapanca arising only among things commingled: difference and identity appear where relation has been made); tah ca arthasya prasadhikah — and they are what *accomplish the meaning*. So the cognition-universals are what make meaning possible: it is because knowings fall into kinds that a word can wake the same knowing twice (2.152's transmitted pratibha; 1.71's recurrence-datum on the hearer's side). The verse quietly completes 3.1.6's doctrine — the word's own universal superimposed on the object's — by supplying the third term: the cognition's universal, which is what the superimposition happens *in*.

Verse 3.1.103.translation: The general residing in the knowable alone is what assists the cognitions; never is cognition, like the knowable, given form by an alien form.
Verse 3.1.103.commentary: A restriction on 3.1.102, and it is the samuddesa's turn to epistemology proper. Jneya-stham eva samanyam jnananam upakarakam: the general residing *in the knowable alone* is what *assists* the cognitions — cognition-types are conditioned by object-types; that this knowing is a cow-knowing is the cow's doing. But the converse is denied: na jatu jneyavat jnanam para-rupena rupyate — *never* is cognition, *like the knowable*, given form by an *alien* form. The knowable takes its form from another (the pot's form is the pot's); cognition does not — its form is its own, self-luminous, not conferred (1.50's cognition displaying its own form and the object's; the svaprakasa-doctrine the Prabhakaras would defend). So the assistance runs one way only: objects shape knowings, and knowings are not shaped from outside as objects are. The asymmetry is the ground of the next verse's simile and of the samuddesa's closing argument.

Verse 3.1.104.translation: As a light is not illuminated by another illumination — so the form of cognition is not grasped by another cognition.
Verse 3.1.104.commentary: The simile that has served the work at every load-bearing joint (1.46, 1.55, 2.298, 2.397, 2.472), here in its epistemological office. Yatha jyotih prakasena na anyena abhiprakasyate: as a *light* is not illuminated *by another* illumination — one does not need a second lamp to see the first; light shows itself in showing. Jnana-akaras tatha anyena na jnanena upagrhyate: so the *form of cognition* is not *grasped by another* cognition. The doctrine is self-luminosity, svaprakasatva: awareness is not known by a further awareness (on pain of regress: what would know *that* one?), but in knowing its object it displays itself. This is 1.50's thesis (in a cognition, its own form and the object's are both seen) now defended by the regress-argument, and it is the anti-Nyaya position — for the Naiyayika, a cognition is known by a subsequent introspective cognition (anuvyavasaya), and the regress is stopped by fiat. Bhartrhari's lamp stops it by structure.

Verse 3.1.105.translation: The cognition 'pot-cognition' is distinct from the pot-cognition; and the cognition that is 'pot' — that one falls upon its object.
Verse 3.1.105.commentary: The self-luminosity doctrine given its decisive analysis. Ghata-jnanam iti jnanam ghata-jnana-vilaksanam: the cognition *'pot-cognition'* — the reflective act by which I think about my seeing — is *distinct from* the pot-cognition itself. They are two, and the second is not the first inspecting itself; it is a new act with a new object (namely, the first). And: ghata iti api yaj jnanam visaya-upanipati tat — the cognition that is *'pot'* — that one *falls upon its object*: the original seeing is directed outward, at the pot, not inward at itself. So introspection does not deliver the original cognition; it replaces it with another about it. The analysis is exact and it is why 3.1.104's regress bites: if knowing a cognition requires a further cognition, and that one is distinct again, no cognition is ever known — unless each shows itself in showing its object, which is the lamp. The verse dismantles the introspective model with a distinction anyone can verify: thinking about your seeing is not seeing.

Verse 3.1.106.translation: Since the form of cognition is not grasped in the manner of an object — and its own form, separated from the object's form, is not ascertained.
Verse 3.1.106.commentary: The Jati-samuddeśa's last verse, and it ends on a limit rather than a thesis. Two negations. Yato visaya-rupena jnana-rupam na grhyate: since the *form of cognition* is *not grasped in the manner of an object* — cognition never stands before us as things do (3.1.105's analysis: try to catch it and you have another cognition). And: artha-rupa-viviktam ca svarupam na avadharyate — and its *own form*, *separated from* the object's form, is *not ascertained*: neither can we strip the object away and inspect awareness bare. So cognition is inaccessible from both directions: not as an object, and not in isolation from objects. What we have is always knowing-of-something, self-luminous and never separately given (1.124's transcendental argument: strip the word-form from awareness and the light would not shine). The samuddesa that began by asking what universals are ends by finding that the very cognition in which universals appear cannot be brought into view — which is 3.1.95's finding again (an indescribable nature at the end of everything, from which practice is not constructed), now reached on the subject's side. The Jati-samuddeśa closes as it opened: with an honest account of what its own instrument cannot reach.
Verse 3.1.106.contested: the verse is grammatically incomplete as transmitted (two subordinate clauses with no main clause), and readers supply the conclusion variously — 'therefore cognition is self-luminous', or 'therefore its universal is not ascertained'. We translate the fragment as it stands and leave the completion open

Verse 3.2.1.translation: Self, thing, own-nature, body, reality, and 'ipsita' as well — these are synonyms of 'substance'; and that is remembered as eternal.
Verse 3.2.1.commentary: The samuddesa opens with a list of synonyms, and the list is its whole argument in miniature. Dravya — *substance*, the second candidate for what words mean (3.1.2's jatir va dravyam eva va) — is named by: atma, *self*; vastu, *thing*; svabhava, *own-nature*; sarira, *body*; tattva, *reality*; ipsita, *what is desired* (the grammarians' term for the karman, the object aimed at: Panini 1.4.49's kartur ipsitatamam karma). The list runs from the most metaphysical (atman, tattva) to the most grammatical (ipsita), and its span is the point: what the Vedantin calls the Self and what the grammarian calls the object of a verb are, on this account, one category. And: tac ca nityam iti smrtam — and that is *remembered as eternal*: dravya, whatever else it is, does not come and go. The reader who expects the Vaisesika's substance — one of six categories, some eternal and some not, the pot as real as the atom — is being warned in the first verse that this is not that. Bhartrhari's dravya is the abiding reality itself, and 3.2.11 will identify it outright with sabda-tattva.

Verse 3.2.2.translation: The real thing is ascertained by those unreal configurations; by words with unreal adjuncts, it is the real alone that is expressed.
Verse 3.2.2.commentary: The samuddesa's central mechanism, and it is 2.127's doctrine given its home. Satyam vastu — the *real* thing — tad-akaraih asatyaih avadharyate: is *ascertained* by *unreal configurations*: the shapes, the forms, the determinations under which anything is met are not themselves real, and they are how the real gets ascertained. Then the semantic consequence: asatya-upadhibhih sabdaih satyam eva abhidhiyate — by words with *unreal adjuncts*, it is *the real alone* that is expressed. The word 'pot' carries an unreal adjunct (pot-shape) and what it expresses is the real (clay, ultimately Being). This is exactly 2.127's entry in the catalogue — asatyopadhi yat satyam tad va sabda-nibandhanam, 'the real with unreal adjunct is the word's fastening' — there reported as one option among many, here asserted as doctrine. And note what it rescues: words are not falsified by their unreal adjuncts. They reach the real *through* them, which is why speech works while being everywhere constructed (2.238's asatya vartman).

Verse 3.2.3.translation: As 'Devadatta's house', grasped by the word 'house' through an unstable occasioning ground, is nonetheless expressed as pure —
Verse 3.2.3.commentary: The first of two similes for 3.2.2. Adhruvena nimittena — by an *unstable occasioning ground*: Devadatta owns the house today and will not tomorrow; the ownership is a passing relation, and it is what the phrase 'Devadatta's house' hangs on. Grhitam grha-sabdena suddham eva abhidhiyate: grasped by the word 'house', the *pure* thing alone is *expressed*. The house itself — not the ownership, not Devadatta — is what the phrase delivers; the unstable relation was the route, not the cargo. So a word may reach its object through a ground that will not last and still deliver the object cleanly. This is 2.172's landmark-simile (the road named by the tree, the ant-hill, the mountain — the landmark picking out the road without being it) transposed into ontology: the adjunct locates, the real is what is had.

Verse 3.2.4.translation: — and as gold, though differentiated by its own perishing configurations, comes to be what is expressed as pure, from the namings 'bracelet' and the like.
Verse 3.2.4.commentary: The second simile, and it is the tradition's great one. Suvarnadi — *gold* and the like — bhinnam svaih akaraih apayibhih: *differentiated* by its *own* configurations, which are *perishing*: the bracelet, the ring, the coin, each a shape gold takes and loses. Rucakady-abhidhananam suddham eva eti vacyatam: from the *namings* 'bracelet' and the rest, the *pure* thing alone comes to be what is *expressed*. Say 'bracelet' and what you have named is gold. The simile is chosen because everyone already thinks this way about gold — the goldsmith weighs the bracelet and quotes you for gold; nobody imagines the bracelet is a substance in its own right — and because it is the Upanisads' own figure for the one and its modifications. What 3.2.2 asserted abstractly (words with unreal adjuncts express the real) is here made obvious: the shapes are real enough to be worn and unreal enough that no one confuses them with what they are shapes *of*.

Verse 3.2.5.translation: And by exclusion through the configurations, its all-objecthood is restricted — just as the capacity of the eye and the rest is by tubes and the like.
Verse 3.2.5.commentary: How the adjuncts work, and the simile is a fine piece of observation. Akaraih ca vyavacchedat sarvarthyam avarudhyate: by *exclusion* through the *configurations*, its *all-objecthood* is *restricted*. Sarvarthya — the plenitude, omni-significance: the same word 2.250 used for what a word means before context carves it. So dravya has all-objecthood, and the configurations *restrict* it: the gold is potentially every shape and this bracelet is one. And the simile: yatha eva caksuradinam samarthyam nadikadibhih — just as the *capacity of the eye* and the rest is (restricted) by *tubes* and the like: look through a tube and you see less, not because your eye has weakened but because the tube excludes. The configuration is a tube on Being. This is 2.250-251's semantics — one plenitude, carved by nimittas — now stated as the metaphysics that underwrites it: words carve because reality is carvable, and both carvings are exclusions of a totality rather than additions to a blank.

Verse 3.2.6.translation: The word that operates upon those configurations, when they are of that sort — by that too, since they have reality for their self, the eternal alone is expressed.
Verse 3.2.6.commentary: The consequence for words that name the shapes themselves. One might object: granted 'bracelet' delivers gold — but what of 'bracelet-shape', a word that names the configuration and not the metal? The reply: tesu akaresu yah sabdah tathabhutesu vartate — the word that operates upon *those configurations*, when they are of that sort — tattvatmakatvat tena api nityam eva abhidhiyate: *by that too*, since they have *reality for their self*, the *eternal alone* is expressed. The configurations are not a second stuff standing outside the real; they have tattva for their atman (3.1.33's satta carved by relata: the carvings are Being too). So a word naming a shape still names the real, because there was never anything else for it to name. The escape-route closes: there is no word anywhere in the language that succeeds in meaning something other than Being.

Verse 3.2.7.translation: 'There is no difference between reality and non-reality' — such is the tradition from the elders; what they consider non-reality is reality itself, unexamined.
Verse 3.2.7.commentary: The samuddesa's most disarming verse, and it is offered as inherited teaching. Na tattva-atattvayoh bhedah iti vrddhebhya agamah: 'there is *no difference* between reality and non-reality' — such is the *tradition from the elders* (vrddha: the old ones, whom 2.485 said knowledge does not grace those who have not waited upon). And the gloss: atattvam iti manyante tattvam eva avicaritam — what they consider *non-reality* is *reality itself*, *unexamined*. The unreal is not a second kind of thing; it is the real, not looked into. Avicarita — unexamined, un-scrutinized: the bracelet is 'unreal' exactly in the sense that one has not asked what it is made of, and asking dissolves it into gold. So the asatya-akaras of 3.2.2 are not falsehoods but unexamined truths, and the whole apparatus of unreal adjuncts through which words reach the real (3.2.2-6) is an apparatus of not-yet-asking. This is the most economical account of the two truths anyone has given: they differ by a question.

Verse 3.2.8.translation: Reality itself, unconstructed, takes on the form of construction; and here there is no difference of time — yet a difference of time is grasped.
Verse 3.2.8.commentary: The doctrine of 3.2.7 stated as a process, and the process is vivarta. Vikalpa-rupam bhajate tattvam eva avikalpitam: *reality itself*, *unconstructed*, *takes on* the form of *construction* — bhajate, takes on, wears: not becomes (parinama) but appears-as (1.1's vivartate). And the temporal paradox: na ca atra kala-bhedo 'sti — and *here there is no difference of time* (the real is undivided, sequence being no part of it: 3.1.35's sequence-withdrawn Being) — kala-bhedas ca grhyate: *yet a difference of time is grasped*. Both halves stand, and the iva is not even needed: the seeing supplies what the seen lacks (2.25's sequencing power; 3.1.37's time-seeing standing as though divided). The verse states the work's central structure in fourteen syllables: no time in it, time in our grasp of it, and the construction that mediates is reality itself unexamined.

Verse 3.2.9.translation: As there is an utter impossibility of the object's properties in cognition — and yet it is established as though having that for its self, while being utterly not of that self —
Verse 3.2.9.commentary: The first half of a precise analogy, and it revisits 3.1.103-106. Yatha visaya-dharmanam jnane atyantam asambhavah: as there is an *utter impossibility* of the *object's properties* in *cognition* — the pot's clay is not in my pot-cognition; awareness has no properties of what it knows (a cognition of fire is not hot). And yet: tad-atma iva ca tat siddham — it is established *as though having that for its self*: the cognition presents as though it were the pot, is of it, wears it; atyantam atad-atmakam — *while being utterly not of that self*. Three clauses holding a genuine paradox: the object's properties cannot be in cognition, cognition presents as the object, cognition is utterly not the object. This is the intentionality-structure stated without a theory to soften it, and it is offered as the *known* case — the thing everyone grants — from which 3.2.10 will argue to the contested one.

Verse 3.2.10.translation: — so there is an utter impossibility of the forms of modification in reality; and yet reality is established as though having that for its self, while being utterly not of that self.
Verse 3.2.10.commentary: The apodosis, and the analogy's force is exact. Tatha vikara-rupanam tattve atyantam asambhavah: so there is an *utter impossibility* of the *forms of modification* in *reality* — the bracelet-shape cannot be *in* the gold-as-such, as the pot cannot be in the cognition. Tad-atma iva ca tat tattvam atyantam atad-atmakam: and yet reality is established *as though having that for its self*, while being *utterly not of that self*. The three clauses repeat 3.2.9's exactly, and that is the argument: the relation of the real to its modifications has the same structure as the relation of cognition to its objects — a structure everyone already accepts without being able to explain. So if one is not scandalized that awareness presents as its object while being nothing like it, one should not be scandalized that Being presents as the world while being nothing like it. The vivarta-doctrine is not a special metaphysical pleading; it is the intentionality-structure, generalized. This is the most powerful defence of vivarta in the work, and it works by pointing at the reader's own mind.

Verse 3.2.11.translation: The real, which stands at the end when the configurations are withdrawn — that is eternal, that is what words express, that is the word-essence: it is not different.
Verse 3.2.11.commentary: The identification, and the samuddesa has been building to it since its first verse. Satyam akrti-samhare yad ante vyavatisthate: the *real* which stands *at the end* when the *configurations are withdrawn* — strip the bracelet, strip the ring, strip every shape, and what remains standing. Three predicates, and the third is the one: tan nityam — that is *eternal* (3.2.1's promise); sabda-vacyam tat — that is what *words express* (3.2.2-6's demonstration: every word, through every adjunct, reaches only this); sabda-tattvam tat na bhidyate — that is the *word-essence*: *it is not different*. Dravya, at the end of analysis, is sabda-tattva — 1.1's anadi-nidhanam brahma sabda-tattvam yad aksaram, the Brahman without beginning or end whose essence is the Word. So the second candidate for word-meaning (3.1.2's dravya) turns out to be what the first turned out to be (3.1.33's satta, in which all words stand established), and both are the Brahmakanda's opening word. The Padakanda's two great alternatives have collapsed into each other and into the work's first verse.

Verse 3.2.12.translation: It is not, nor is it not; it is not one, nor is it separate; it is not commingled nor divided; it is not modified, nor is it otherwise.
Verse 3.2.12.commentary: The negative tetralemma, and it is among the most striking verses in Sanskrit philosophical literature. Eight negations in four pairs: na tad asti na tan nasti — it *is not*, nor *is it not*; na tad ekam na tat prthak — not *one*, not *separate*; na samsrstam vibhaktam va — not *commingled* nor *divided*; vikrtam na ca na anyatha — not *modified*, nor *otherwise*. The form is the catuskoti's — the four-cornered negation the Madhyamikas made their instrument — and the first pair is its classical opening. But the target is Bhartrhari's own: what is being denied every predicate is *dravya*, just identified with sabda-tattva (3.2.11). And the denials are earned rather than borrowed: 3.1.20-21 already established that oneness, manifoldness, existence and non-existence are found only among things *commingled*, and that uncommingled reality bears none of them. So the tetralemma here is not a sceptical device but the strict consequence of the samuddesa's own doctrine: the real, taken apart from the adjuncts through which alone anything is ascertained (3.2.2), has nothing left that could be said of it. What is remarkable is what comes next.
Verse 3.2.12.contested: the relation of this verse to Madhyamaka is a genuine and unresolved question of intellectual history; the four-cornered form is unmistakable and the direction of influence — if any — cannot be established from the karika. We flag the resonance and decline to date it

Verse 3.2.13.translation: It is not, and it does exist; it is one, and it is separate, severally; it is both commingled and divided; it is modified, and it is otherwise.
Verse 3.2.13.commentary: The affirmative tetralemma, and its placement immediately after 3.2.12 is the samuddesa's masterstroke. Every predicate just denied is now asserted, in the same order, with the same vocabulary: tan nasti vidyate ca tat — it *is not*, and it *does exist*; tad ekam tat prthak prthak — it is *one*, it is *separate, severally*; samsrstam ca vibhaktam ca — *commingled* and *divided*; vikrtam tat tad anyatha — *modified*, and *otherwise*. The two verses together do not contradict; they distribute. 3.2.12 speaks of the real as it is — uncommingled, where no category applies (3.1.21). 3.2.13 speaks of the real as ascertained — through the unreal configurations (3.2.2), where every category applies, because the configurations are what categories are. Neither verse is the truth about dravya; the pair is. And the pair is why the work can say that the sciences describe only avidya (2.233) without concluding that they describe nothing: what they describe is 3.2.13, which is 3.2.12 unexamined (3.2.7). Two tetralemmas, and between them the whole architecture of the two truths, stated without either being denied.

Verse 3.2.14.translation: Of that one, the form of the word-meaning connection is seen; that is the seen, the seeing, the seer — and the purpose in the seeing.
Verse 3.2.14.commentary: The one differentiating itself into the whole structure of knowledge. Tasya sabdartha-sambandha-rupam ekasya drsyate: of *that one*, the form of the *word-meaning connection* is *seen* — the very relation on which the Brahmakanda founded scripture's authority (1.23's nitya sambandha) is *that one's* form, not a bond between two independent things (2.31: word and meaning as two aspects of one self). And then the fourfold self-differentiation: tad drsyam darsanam drasta — *that* is the *seen*, the *seeing*, the *seer* — the tripuiti of 1.4's bhoktr-bhoktavya-bhoga, subject, object, act — darsane ca prayojanam: and the *purpose* in the seeing, the fourth term, which is what 2.113 made sentence-meaning itself. So the one becomes: what is known, the knowing, the knower, and the point of it. Every term in the epistemology of both preceding kandas is here derived from a single source, and derived as its *forms* rather than as its products (3.2.8's bhajate: it takes them on). The verse is the samuddesa's brief cosmology, and its economy is startling.

Verse 3.2.15.translation: As gold is the real when the modification is gone, in the case of an earring — so, when the modification is gone, they call the supreme prakrti the real.
Verse 3.2.15.commentary: The gold-simile (3.2.4) returned at the samuddesa's close, now to name the terminus. Vikara-apagame satyam suvarnam kundale yatha: as, *when the modification is gone*, *gold* is the real in the case of an *earring* — melt the earring and gold is what you have; the earring was never a second thing. Tatha ahuh prakrtim param: so they call the *supreme prakrti* the real, vikara-apagame, when the modification is gone. Prakrti — the primal matrix, the Samkhya's own word (3.1.43's pralaya, differences dissolving into prakrti) — but here param, *supreme*, and identified with what 3.2.11 called sabda-tattva. The verse performs a careful appropriation: the Samkhya's prakrti-vikara relation is accepted as a *simile* and its metaphysics declined, since for Samkhya the transformation is real (parinama) and here it is not (3.2.8's bhajate, 3.2.10's atyantam atad-atmakam). Gold is the right figure precisely because the goldsmith's transformations are reversible and the gold is never other.

Verse 3.2.16.translation: She is what all words express, and words are not separate from her; and in the non-separateness, there is a connection of those two, as though they were of distinct selves.
Verse 3.2.16.commentary: The samuddesa's doctrinal conclusion, and it holds two things that ought to conflict. Vacya sa sarva-sabdanam: *she* — the supreme prakrti of 3.2.15 — is what *all words* express (3.2.6: no word reaches anything else). Sabdas ca na prthak tatah: and *words are not separate* from her — the expressers are not outside what they express; language is not a system standing over against reality and referring to it (2.31's one self with two aspects). And then the clause that saves the whole enterprise: aprthaktve ca sambandhah tayoh nana-atmanoh iva — and in the *non-separateness*, there is a *connection* of those two, *as though* they were of *distinct selves*. The eternal word-meaning bond of 1.23 — on which Vedic authority rests, and which 2.16's objector feared the holism would forfeit — is here preserved *inside* the identity: word and meaning are one, and their oneness presents as a connection between two. The iva does the work it has done since 1.2. Nothing is lost and nothing is really two.

Verse 3.2.17-18.translation: (17) As the contradictory forms — self and other, dear and hateful, speaker, what is spoken, and purpose — belong in a dream to one and the same mind, (18) so, in the reality that is unborn, eternal, devoid of before-and-after, the being of birth and the rest is found, though contradictory.
Verse 3.2.17-18.commentary: The samuddesa closes with the dream, and the choice is deliberate: it is the analogy 1.1's Vrtti reached for at the work's very opening. (17): in a *dream*, one mind — ekasya cetasah — produces forms that are viruddhani, *contradictory*: atma parah — *self* and *other*; priyo dvesyah — the *dear* and the *hateful*; vakta vacyam prayojanam — the *speaker*, *what is spoken*, and the *purpose* (3.2.14's fourfold, now dreamt). The dreamer is both parties to his dream's quarrel, speaks and hears his dream's speech, wants what his dream's other withholds — and there is only one mind. (18) applies it: ajanmani tatha nitye paurvaparya-vivarjite tattve — so, in the *reality* that is *unborn*, *eternal*, *devoid of before-and-after* — janmadi-rupatvam viruddham upalabhyate: the being of *birth and the rest* (3.1.36's six modifications) is *found*, *though contradictory*. The dream licenses the contradiction without dissolving it: we do not doubt that one mind holds a dream's warring parties, and we should not doubt that one unborn reality holds a world of births. And the analogy answers the tetralemmas: 3.2.12 and 3.2.13 are both true of dravya as both are true of a dream — nothing in it is real, everything in it happens, and the dreamer is one.
Verse 3.2.17-18.contested: the dream-analogy's force depends on how strongly one reads svapne: as a mere illustration of one-holding-many (weak), or as a claim about the world's ontological status (strong). 1.1's Vrtti uses it in the strong sense, and the parallel supports that here — but the karika itself only asserts the structure, not the unreality

Verse 3.3.1.translation: The speaker's cognition, the external object, and the own-form are understood; by words when uttered, the connection of these stands established.
Verse 3.3.1.commentary: The samuddesa opens by naming its three relata. What a word delivers is: jnanam prayoktuh — the *speaker's cognition* (what he had in mind); bahyo 'rthah — the *external object* (what it was about); svarupam ca — and the *own-form* (the word itself, 1.50's self-presentation, 3.1.6's sva jati expressed first of all). Three things, and: sabdaih uccaritaih tesam sambandhah samavasthitah — by words *when uttered*, the *connection* of these stands established. Note uccaritaih — *when uttered*: the connection is established in the uttering, not before (2.399's door: the eternal bond is a standing possibility and utterance is the walking through; 2.401's relation constituted within the act). So the samuddesa's subject is announced as a three-term relation that comes into being when someone speaks. Everything that follows will ask what such a relation could be — and the answer, prepared since 2.46, is that it is not a thing at all.

Verse 3.3.2.translation: Comprehension occurs with respect to the object; in the cognition there is sometimes doubt; but when the own-forms are apprehended, there is no deviation.
Verse 3.3.2.commentary: The three relata of 3.3.1 sorted by reliability, and the sorting is exact. Pratipattir bhavaty arthe: *comprehension* occurs with respect to the *object* — hearing a word, one gets its meaning. Jnane va samsayah kvacit: in the *cognition* there is *sometimes doubt* — what the speaker actually had in mind is often uncertain (he may have misspoken, or meant otherwise, or not known his own mind). But: svarupesu upalabdhesu vyabhicaro na vidyate — when the *own-forms* are *apprehended*, there is *no deviation*: about the word itself there is never error; hearing 'gauh', one cannot be wrong that 'gauh' was said. So the three relata have three epistemic grades: the word-form is indefeasible, the object is reliably reached, the speaker's mind is doubtful. Vyabhicara — deviation — is the logician's term for a mark that strays from what it marks (3.1.32's satya/asatya sorting), and its absence in the word's own case is why 3.1.6 could make the word's self-expression primary: it is the one thing in the transaction that cannot fail.

Verse 3.3.3.translation: 'This is the expressor of that, that is the expressible' — so it is understood through the sixth case; the yoking of word and meaning is therefore designated as their reality.
Verse 3.3.3.commentary: How the connection presents itself in language. Asya ayam vacakah vacyah iti sasthya pratiyate: 'this is *the expressor of that*, that is *the expressible*' — it is understood through the *sixth case*, the genitive: we speak of the word *of* a meaning, the meaning *of* a word, and the genitive is the case of relation as such (2.197-198's doctrine: the genitive expresses relations whose generating action has withdrawn). Yogah sabdarthayoh tattvam ity ato vyapadisyate: the *yoking* of word and meaning is *therefore designated as their reality*. Mark the 'therefore': it is *because* we say it in the genitive that we call the yoking their reality. The designation follows the grammar. So the samuddesa's opening move is to notice that our whole conviction that there *is* a word-meaning relation rests on a case-ending — which, given everything the work has said about case-endings being postings (2.198's samasasya nivrttaye: the genitive prescribed to block compounding), is not a reassuring foundation. The interrogation begins here.

Verse 3.3.4.translation: There is no naming that expresses the connection by its own property; because it is utterly dependent, no form of it is designated.
Verse 3.3.4.commentary: The samuddesa's central thesis, and it is 2.46's finding stated as doctrine. Na abhidhanam sva-dharmena sambandhasya asti vacakam: there is *no naming* that expresses the connection *by its own property* — no word reaches it as words reach things, by delivering what it is in itself. And the reason: atyanta-paratantratvat rupam na asya apadisyate — because it is *utterly dependent*, no *form* of it is *designated*. Paratantra — dependent, other-tenanted: the term 1.62 used for the subordinate member that takes no operations directly, and 2.196 for the particles that cannot stand alone. Here it is atyanta, *utterly*: the relation is not merely dependent in some respect; dependence is all it is. A thing with no independent standing has no rupa, no form, to be named — and so the relation, which every semantics needs, is the one item language cannot reach. This is why 2.46 said its form is never seen and it is utterly beyond thinghood: not a defect in our access, but the absence of anything to access.

Verse 3.3.5.translation: Where it exists from assistance, there the property is followed along; it is the capacity even of capacities, the quality even of qualities.
Verse 3.3.5.commentary: How the unnameable relation is nonetheless tracked. Upakarat sa yatra asti dharmah tatra anugamyate: where it exists *from assistance* — where one thing assists another, and the assisting is the relation's whole presence — there the *property* is *followed along*: we detect the relation by its work, never by its face (2.46's karya-anumeyah: inferable from its effect). And then two formulations of remarkable compression: saktinam api sa saktih — it is the *capacity even of capacities*; gunanam api asau gunah — the *quality even of qualities*. The relation is what enables enabling, what qualifies qualifying. It stands one level up from every category and inside each: nothing has its power except by relation, and relation has no power of its own. This is the samuddesa's answer to how the formless can be omnipresent — it is not a further item alongside capacities and qualities, it is their functioning, and functioning has no face.

Verse 3.3.6.translation: But the use of that word for conjunction and inherence, which have that for their property — for those two also, the adjuncts of that are fixed, for the sake of assistance.
Verse 3.3.6.commentary: The Vaisesika's two relations placed. Tad-dharmanoh tu tacchabdyam samyoga-samavayayoh: the *use of that word* — 'relation' — for *conjunction* and *inherence*, which have *that* (relation-ness) for their property. So samyoga (contact between substances) and samavaya (the intimate inherence binding quality to substance, universal to particular) are called relations by courtesy: they have relation-hood as a property, they are not relation itself. And: tayoh api upakara-artha niyatah tad-upadhayah — for *those two also*, the *adjuncts* of that are *fixed*, *for the sake of assistance*. Even the named relations are, on inspection, adjuncts fixed for assistance's sake — determinations laid on so that the assisting can be described (3.2.2's asatya-upadhi: unreal adjuncts through which the real is ascertained). The Vaisesika thinks he has two relation-*categories*; Bhartrhari grants him two upadhis and no category. 2.435 already drew this distinction — conjunction nameable because it is a property of its relata, relation-as-such reachable only obliquely.

Verse 3.3.7.translation: For there is some state, indicated by the production of an effect, of one thing with another, wherein conjunction arises.
Verse 3.3.7.commentary: Conjunction analysed away. Kacid eva hi sa avastha — there is *some state*, and the vagueness is deliberate: kacid eva, 'some or other', the shrug marking that nothing determinate can be said. Karya-prasava-sucita: *indicated by the production of an effect* — we know the state only because something results (3.3.5's upakara; 2.288's asadharanam karyam, the non-common effect by which the real is discriminated). Kasyacit kenacid yasyam samyoga upajayate: of *one thing with another*, wherein *conjunction arises*. So samyoga is not a relation observed between two things; it is a state — undescribable except by its effects — in which we say conjunction has arisen. The Vaisesika's clearest, most tangible relation (two things touching: what could be plainer?) has dissolved into an effect-indicated somewhat. And if samyoga goes, samavaya — which nobody ever claimed to perceive — goes with it.

Verse 3.3.8-11.translation: (8) That restriction which occurs somewhere in the arising of things that have no selves, and the non-separation effected by that very thing where there is no difference in attainment; (9) that by which the self of one self is ascertained as though it were the self of another; and from which the manifoldness of one thing is not determined as its reality; (10) that capacity called inherence, which assists the capacities, which transcends difference and non-difference and stands in quite another way — (11) the connection favours that property of all word-meanings, itself transcending all characterization: such is the tradition from the earlier ones.
Verse 3.3.8-11.commentary: A single four-verse sentence, the samuddesa's most sustained period, defining the relation by everything it does and nothing it is. The subject is deferred to 11 and the accusatives pile up. It is (8) the *restriction* — niyamah — that shows up in the arising of nir-atmakanam, things *that have no selves* (things whose being is wholly relational); and the *non-separation* where attainment does not differ. It is (9) that by which atma-antarasya atma tad-atma iva avadharyate — the self of one thing is ascertained *as though* it were another's (the intentionality-structure of 3.2.9-10: cognition presenting as its object while being utterly not it); and that from which ekasya nanatvam — the *manifoldness of one thing* — is *not determined as its reality*. It is (10) tam saktim samavaya-akhyam — the *capacity called inherence* — saktinam upakarinim, *assisting the capacities* (3.3.5), bheda-abhedau atikrantam — *transcending difference and non-difference*: it is neither identical with its relata nor other than them, anyatha eva vyavasthitam, standing *in quite another way*. And (11) the main clause at last: sambandhah anugrhnati dharmam sarva-padarthanam, atitah sarva-laksanah — the *connection favours* the property of all word-meanings, *itself transcending all characterization*. Iti purvebhya agamah: *such is the tradition from the earlier ones* — reported, not invented. The passage defines by exclusion (transcending difference and non-difference, transcending all characterization) because 3.3.4 established that no positive naming is available. What it names is the enabling of everything, which is nothing.
Verse 3.3.8-11.contested: the syntax across the four verses is genuinely hard and our reconstruction — accusatives in 8-10 governed by anugrhnati in 11 — is one of several; the transcription's reading at several points is insecure. What is not in doubt is the doctrine: relation as bheda-abheda-atikranta, transcending both difference and identity

Verse 3.3.12.translation: By others, it is admitted everywhere only as having been made into a word-meaning; therefore the meaning of the word 'connection' cannot be analysed out.
Verse 3.3.12.commentary: The paradox of talking about relation at all. Padarthikrta eva anyaih sarvatra abhyupagamyate sambandhah: by others, the connection is admitted, everywhere, *only as having been made into a word-meaning* — padarthikrta: *made* a padartha, converted into an item by the very act of discussing it. The moment one says 'the relation', one has treated as a thing what 3.3.4 showed has no form. Tena sabdarthah pravibhaktum na sakyate: *therefore* the meaning of the word ('connection') *cannot be analysed out* — one cannot extract, by apoddhara (2.10's method), a word-meaning for 'sambandha', because what the word purports to name is not the sort of thing that survives extraction. The verse is exactly self-aware: this samuddesa is seventy-six verses about relation, and its twelfth verse says that its subject exists only as an artifact of being talked about. Which is consistent — the whole work has held that padarthas are creatures of laksana (2.436) — but here the point turns on the treatise itself.

Verse 3.3.13.translation: From inherence, one's own substratum and one's own universal are understood; but from inherence in a single object, a quality is one whose substratum is its own.
Verse 3.3.13.commentary: The Vaisesika's machinery set out, and the setting-out is scrupulous. Samavayat sva adharah sva ca jatih pratiyate: from *inherence*, one's *own substratum* and one's *own universal* are understood — samavaya is what ties a quality to the substance it is in, and a universal to the particular it characterizes; both relations are 'inherence' on that system. Eka-artha-samavayat tu gunah sva-adhara eva ye: but from *inherence in a single object*, a quality is one whose substratum is its own. Eka-artha-samavaya — co-inherence in one object — is the Vaisesika's device for relating two qualities to each other (the colour and the taste of one mango are related by both inhering in the mango, not directly). Bhartrhari reports the system accurately before dismantling it: the reader should see that he knows exactly what he is refusing. And the refusal is already prepared: whatever samavaya does, it does as an upadhi fixed for assistance's sake (3.3.6), and it transcends difference and non-difference (3.3.10) — which is not what the Vaisesika thinks samavaya is at all.

Verse 3.3.14.translation: Substance-hood, existence, conjunctions, the divisions of their locations, and the qualities such as duality — these have their basis in their own substratum and in another's.
Verse 3.3.14.commentary: The catalogue continued, and its selection is pointed. Dravyatva-satta-samyogah — *substance-hood*, *existence*, *conjunctions*; tat-pradesa-vibhagah ca — and the *divisions of their locations*; guna dvitvadayah ca ye — and the *qualities such as duality* (number, which the Jati-samuddesa spent forty verses on). All of these are sva-anya-adhara-upabandhanah: having their *basis* in their *own* substratum *and in another's*. That is: none of them is self-standing; each is anchored partly in itself and partly elsewhere — which is to say, each is relational through and through. And the list is the Vaisesika's own inventory of what is real: substance-hood, existence (satta, their highest universal), conjunction, number. Bhartrhari is showing that the categories the realist takes as bedrock all have the two-footed structure that makes them dependent — and 3.3.4 established that what is atyanta-paratantra has no form of its own to be named. The realist's furniture is being catalogued for demolition.

Verse 3.3.15.translation: Some are conjoined with their own substratum, some inhere in it; others likewise inhere in what inheres in what is conjoined.
Verse 3.3.15.commentary: The relational hierarchy at its most baroque, and the baroqueness is the argument. Kecit sva-asraya-samyuktah: *some* are *conjoined* with their own substratum; kecit tat-samavayinah: *some* *inhere* in it; samyukta-samavetesu samavetah tatha pare: *others* likewise *inhere in what inheres in what is conjoined*. That last is a three-storey relation — inherence in an inherent in a conjunct — and the Vaisesikas needed such ladders to explain how, say, a sound in the ether relates to a distant hearer, or how a quality of a part relates to the whole. Bhartrhari lists the storeys without comment. The comment is the list: a system that must climb three relations to connect two things has made relation do so much work that the question 3.3.4 asked — what *is* it, that it can do all this and be named by nothing? — becomes unavoidable. The samuddesa's method here is the same as 2.77-86's exegetical catalogue: recite the opponent's machinery at length, fairly, and let its weight be felt.

Verse 3.3.16.translation: But the all-pervading is understood as conjoined with things conjoined with their own substratum; no further connection of inherence is seen there.
Verse 3.3.16.commentary: The regress hunted to its lair in the Vaisesika's own system. Sva-asrayena tu samyuktaih samyuktam vibhu gamyate: the *all-pervading* (vibhu: ether, time, the self — the omnipresent substances) is understood as *conjoined with* things which are themselves conjoined with their substrata (3.3.15's three-storey ladder). And then the observation that matters: samavayasya sambandho na aparah tatra drsyate — *no further connection of inherence* is seen *there*. Ask the Vaisesika what connects samavaya to its relata, and he cannot answer with another samavaya (that would start the regress his opponents have always pressed). He must say: samavaya connects by itself, needing no further connector. Bhartrhari records the answer without objecting — because it is *his* answer too, and he will generalize it at 3.3.28 (an operation has no further operation). The realist stops the regress by fiat at exactly one place; Bhartrhari will stop it by principle everywhere.

Verse 3.3.17.translation: And because the connection is undifferentiated, no restriction would obtain here; therefore no connection of word and meaning is constructed at all.
Verse 3.3.17.commentary: The consequence for the samuddesa's own subject, and it is devastating. Sambandhasya avisistatvat: because the connection is *undifferentiated* — samavaya, on the Vaisesika's account, is *one*: a single eternal relation, the same everywhere, in which everything inheres in everything it inheres in. Na ca atra niyamo bhavet: therefore *no restriction* would obtain here — an undifferentiated relation cannot explain why *this* quality is in *this* substance and not another; it relates indiscriminately or not at all. Tasmat sabdarthayoh na eva sambandhah parikalpyate: *therefore* no connection of *word and meaning* is constructed at all — if relation cannot restrict, it cannot pair a word with its meaning either, and the semantic bond the whole work rests on (1.23's nitya sambandha) has no relational account. The verse is the samuddesa's crisis, deliberately staged: the realist's relation cannot do the work, and Bhartrhari's has no form to be named (3.3.4). What is left is 3.3.29's yogyata, and everything between here and there clears the ground for it.

Verse 3.3.18.translation: As, by the obtaining of an unseen operation, there is somewhere a conjunction of the self, called the owner-owned relation — in non-difference, that pattern holds elsewhere too.
Verse 3.3.18.commentary: A relation that is manifestly not a physical connection, offered as the model. Sva-svami-yoga — the *owner-owned* relation: what connects a man to his field? No contact, no inherence; the field is not touching him and not in him.
