Compare 3.2.1's list, where dravya's synonyms ran from atman and tattva to the grammarians' ipsita: here the metaphysical end of that list has dropped away entirely, and what remains is the pronoun's target. The two samuddesas on substance thus approach it from opposite ends — 3.2 from the eternal reality, 3.4 from the grammar's slot — and the Padakanda's method is to let both stand (2.179's licensed double description).
Verse 3.4.3.contested: the relation between this samuddesa and the Dravya-samuddesa (3.2) is a standing question: whether they treat two senses of dravya (the metaphysical and the grammatical) or one sense from two angles. The tradition's own titling — this one Dravyalaksana, 'the definition of substance' — suggests the second, and we read it so

Verse 3.5.1.translation: Whatever is understood as commingling, as differentiating, and as possessed of operation — its quality-hood, from its being dependent, has been exemplified in the science.
Verse 3.5.1.commentary: The definition of quality, and it is built from three marks. Samsargi — *commingling*: a guna is never met alone; it is always with something (3.4.1's samsarga-rupat sambhutah, and 1.62's paratantra guna). Bhedakam — *differentiating*: its office is to distinguish — the red cow from the black (3.4.3's bhedyatvena vivaksitah, substance being what is *to be* differentiated: the two definitions interlock exactly). Sa-vyaparam — *possessed of operation*: it does something, which will be the samuddesa's whole argument. And the ground of the classification: gunatvam paratantratvat tasya sastra udahrtam — its *quality-hood*, from its being *dependent*, has been *exemplified in the science*. Paratantra: other-tenanted — the term 3.3.4 used of the relation (atyanta-paratantra, whence no form to name). Quality is dependent but not *utterly* so: it has an operation of its own, and that is what distinguishes a guna from a relation. The samuddesa will spend nine verses establishing that the operation is real.

Verse 3.5.2.translation: The quality which is taught, as differentiator, of a substance that is not designated — for the sake of its designation, that thing's excellence is what is expressed.
Verse 3.5.2.commentary: Quality's office in designation. Dravyasya avyapadesasya — of a substance *that is not designated*, that cannot be picked out as it stands (one cow among many) — yah upadiyate gunah bhedakah: the *quality* which is taught as *differentiator* — vyapadesaya, *for the sake of its designation*. And what such a quality expresses: tat-prakarsah abhidhiyate — that thing's *excellence* is what is expressed. Prakarsa: eminence, degree, the more-ness that 1.64 analysed (the quality as prakarsa-hetu, the ground of the comparative). So a differentiating quality does not merely mark; it *grades* — 'the red cow' picks her out by her being more red, standing out. The verse introduces the samuddesa's technical problem: prakarsa is what qualities deliver, and the next verses ask what prakarsa itself rests on.

Verse 3.5.3.translation: Of every principal whatever, there is no excellence apart from a cause of differentiation; nor does it come to be expressible by a word.
Verse 3.5.3.commentary: The dependence of excellence, stated twice over. Sarvasya eva pradhanasya — of *every principal whatever* (any substance, anything that gets qualified) — na vina bheda-hetuna prakarso vidyate: there is *no excellence* apart from a *cause of differentiation*. Nothing is eminent in itself; eminence is always eminence-by-something (the cow is not simply outstanding, she is outstanding *in redness*). And: na api sabdasya upaiti vacyatam — nor does it *come to be expressible* by a word: bare excellence, unattached to a differentiating ground, cannot even be said. So prakarsa is doubly dependent — ontologically on a cause of difference, semantically on the same. This is 1.64's doctrine (the quality that is the ground of pre-eminence is indicated as though independent, and exists only asrita, resting on its substrate) now made the axis of the samuddesa: excellence is what qualities give, and it is nothing on its own.

Verse 3.5.4.translation: Not all the causes of differentiation that are present in the principals are stated by particular-words, which express excluded meanings.
Verse 3.5.4.commentary: A limit on what language marks. Vidyamanah pradhanesu na sarve bheda-hetavah: *not all* the causes of differentiation that are *present* in the principals — a thing has indefinitely many features that could distinguish it — visesa-sabdaih ucyante: are *stated* by *particular-words*. And those words are characterized: vyavrtta-artha-abhidhayibhih — *expressing excluded meanings*: a particular-word means by excluding (3.1.14's vyavrtti, 3.1.98's apoha). So language selects: of a thing's countless differentiating features, a few get words, and those words work by exclusion. This is 3.1.47's finding (the real difference in the scent of jasmine, unnamed because too transient) generalized: what has a word is not what is distinguishable but what usage has troubled to exclude for (3.1.51's yatnat: what costs effort to say is what is meant).

Verse 3.5.5.translation: If the particular were there engaged in the indicating of the thing — then, from the absence of restriction, the excellence would have an unknown cause.
Verse 3.5.5.commentary: The first horn of a dilemma about what the quality-word does. Suppose the visesa — the particular, the differentiating quality — is vyaprtah vastu-upalaksane: *engaged in* the *indicating of the thing*, doing the pointing-out (3.4.3's vastu-upalaksana: the pronoun's office). Then: niyama-abhavat prakarsah syad avijnata-hetukah — from the *absence of restriction*, the *excellence* would have an *unknown cause*: if the quality is busy indicating, nothing is left to ground the grading, and the eminence 3.5.2 said it expresses would come from nowhere. The dilemma's structure is the samuddesa's own: a quality cannot both point out the substance and grade it, if it has only one operation. Which is why 3.5.8 will insist that the quality has its own operation, distinct from its indicating work.

Verse 3.5.6.translation: And everything is necessarily and by rule made excellent from every side — by a commingling cause, whether inferior or superior.
Verse 3.5.6.commentary: The universality of grading. Sarvam ca sarvatah avasyam niyamena prakrsyate: *everything* is *necessarily* and *by rule* made excellent *from every side* — there is nothing that is not graded relative to something; every thing stands in comparisons in every direction. Samsargina nimittena nikrstena adhikena va: by a *commingling cause*, whether *inferior* or *superior*. The grading-cause is always a samsargin — something the thing is with (3.5.1's first mark) — and it may be less or more: a thing is eminent by exceeding and eminent by being exceeded (the small stone is eminently small among boulders). So prakarsa is not a property a thing has but a relational fact holding in all directions at once, generated by whatever it is commingled with. This is 3.1.15-16's structure (places belonging to what is not a place, conferred by relata) applied to degree.

Verse 3.5.7.translation: And if what is engaged in the excellence does not depend on an occasioning ground — then the taking-up of the substance would be purposeless with respect to the excellence.
Verse 3.5.7.commentary: The second horn. Suppose the reverse of 3.5.5: the quality is engaged in the *excellence* and does *not* depend on an occasioning ground — the grading is intrinsic to the quality, needing nothing else. Then: dravyasya syad upadanam prakarsam praty anarthakam — the *taking-up of the substance* would be *purposeless* with respect to the excellence: why mention the cow at all, if the redness grades by itself? The substance becomes idle. Anarthaka — purposeless: the word 2.34 used for what results when a word-meaning is added or subtracted against the sentence's one meaning, and 3.1.63's superfluous second animal. So both horns fail: engage the quality in indicating and the grading is groundless (3.5.5); engage it in grading and the substance is idle (3.5.7). The samuddesa's dilemma is complete, and 3.5.8 resolves it.

Verse 3.5.8.translation: Therefore the quality is possessed of operation, grounded in its own excellence; it differentiates the substance's very self, installing it in its own excellence.
Verse 3.5.8.commentary: The resolution, and it grants the quality genuine agency. Tasmat sa-vyaparo gunah: *therefore* the quality is *possessed of operation* — 3.5.1's third mark, now established by the dilemma's exhaustion: neither horn works unless the quality does something of its own. Sva-prakarsa-nibandhanah: *grounded in its own excellence* — the quality's operation rests on its own grading, not on a borrowed one. And the operation itself: dravya-atmanam bhinatti eva sva-prakarse nivesayan — it *differentiates the substance's very self*, *installing it* in its *own excellence*. The redness does not merely accompany the cow and get noticed; it *installs* her in redness, and the installing is what differentiates her. Nivesayan — installing — is 2.45's verb for the relation delimiting the hovering general and settling it in the particular. So the quality is the operator that takes an undesignated substance (3.5.2's dravyasya avyapadesasya) and puts it somewhere on a scale, and its being-there is its being-differentiated. Quality is dependent (3.5.1's paratantra) and active, which is exactly what distinguished it from relation at the samuddesa's opening.

Verse 3.5.9.translation: As a formless substance is designated by way of another's form — so a quality that is without excellence is entered into by way of excellence.
Verse 3.5.9.commentary: The samuddesa's closing parallel, and it folds quality back into the kanda's master-doctrine. Arupam para-rupena dravyam akhyayate yatha: as a *formless* substance is *designated by way of another's form* — the substance, having no own-form available to designation (3.2.2's satyam vastu ascertained by unreal configurations; 3.3.54's word stating the object by way of another form), is reached through what is not it. Aprakarsam prakarsena gunasya avisyate tatha: *so* a quality that is *without excellence* is *entered into* by way of *excellence*. The quality, in itself ungraded, is reached through grading. So both categories — the kanda's two great ones, substance and quality — are accessible only through what they are not: substance through alien form, quality through degree. Neither is ever met as itself. This is 3.3.80's footless station arriving in the humblest possible setting: a nine-verse technical discussion of adjectives, ending with the finding that adjectives reach their objects the way everything reaches everything — obliquely, and by what is not there.

Verse 3.6.1.translation: Direction, means, action, time — these, though expressing things, are utterly unsettled in the capacity-form of word-meanings.
Verse 3.6.1.commentary: A manifesto for the four samuddesas it opens. Dik sadhanam kriya kalah — *direction*, *means*, *action*, *time*: the subjects of samuddesas 6, 7, 8 and 9, named together because they share a status. Vastv-abhidhayinah — they *express things*: the words for them behave like thing-words, take cases, get qualified, function as nouns. And yet: sakti-rupe padarthanam atyantam anavasthitah — in the *capacity-form* of word-meanings, they are *utterly unsettled*: taken as saktis — powers, which is what the Padakanda holds all categories to be (3.1.22-23: dravyadayah sarvah saktayah) — they will not stay put. The four are the hardest cases for the whole scheme: each is indispensable to the grammar, each is treated as a thing by the language, and none of them will submit to being one. Anavasthita — unsettled — is a word Bhartrhari has used for the unsettledness of doctrines (2.138) and of the divergence among schools (2.107); here it names the categories' own instability. The next hundred pages will not stabilize them. They will show why they cannot be stabilized.

Verse 3.6.2-3.translation: That which is the cause of separation of the limit and the thing to be conveyed — since, from it, a cognition proceeds as simply 'straight', without another.
Verse 3.6.2-3.commentary: Direction defined by its office, and the source transmits the passage defectively (the page carries verse 2 only, verse 3 having dropped). Vyatirekasya yo hetuh avadhi-pratipadyayoh: that which is the *cause of separation* of the *limit* and the *thing to be conveyed* — dik is what holds apart the reference-point and what is located from it: 'east of the village' requires that village and thing be separated, and direction is the separating. And the criterion: rju iti eva yato 'nyena vina buddhih pravartate — since, *from it*, a *cognition* proceeds as simply *'straight'*, *without another*: direction delivers straightness immediately, needing no further term. So dik is a primitive: what makes two things stand apart in a way that a single cognition registers as a line. The samuddesa will spend twenty-seven pages showing that this primitive is a power and not a thing.
Verse 3.6.2-3.contested: the wisdomlib page for 3.6.2-3 carries only one verse; verse 3 appears to have dropped from the source. We translate what is transmitted and note the lacuna

Verse 3.6.4.translation: Priority and posteriority of corporeal things are grounded in difference of place; from that very source they are constructed — but in the form of sequence, they are from time.
Verse 3.6.4.commentary: The division of labour between direction and time. Para-aparatve murtanam desa-bheda-nibandhane: *priority and posteriority* of *corporeal* things are grounded in *difference of place* — 'this is before that' said of bodies in space is a fact about where they are, and dik supplies it. Tata eva prakalpyete: from *that very source* they are *constructed*. But: krama-rupe tu kalatah — in the *form of sequence*, they are *from time*: the same before-and-after, said of events, comes from kala. So para/apara is ambiguous between a spatial and a temporal reading, and the two samuddesas divide it. The verse is doing careful preliminary work — priority is not one thing with two applications but two constructions sharing a word — and it prepares 3.6.13's argument that direction, like time, does its work by conferring divisions on what has none.

Verse 3.6.5.translation: By a location of ether, and by other portions severally — that (direction) serves as the limiting adjunct of conjunctions and disjunctions.
Verse 3.6.5.commentary: Direction's mechanism, and it operates on ether. Akasasya pradesena bhagaih ca anyaih prthak prthak: by a *location of ether*, and by *other portions* *severally* — akasa is undifferentiated (3.1.15), and 'portions' of it are constructed by the things conjoined with it (3.1.15-16's doctrine: places belonging to what is not a place). Sa samyoga-vibhaganam upadhitvaya kalpate: *that* — direction — serves as the *limiting adjunct* of *conjunctions and disjunctions*. Upadhi: the limiting condition (3.2.2's asatya-upadhi; 1.75's grahana-upadhi-bheda). So dik is what makes contacts and separations *directional* — what turns a bare conjunction into a coming-from-the-east. It is an adjunct on an adjunct: ether's portions are already constructed, and direction conditions the constructions. Nothing in the chain is a thing.

Verse 3.6.6.translation: There is a settled arrangement of places by direction; there is no settled arrangement of direction. For the capacities of things are brought about by assistance.
Verse 3.6.6.commentary: The asymmetry that gives the samuddesa its thesis. Diso vyavastha desanam: there is a *settled arrangement of places* *by direction* — dik fixes where things are; it is the principle of spatial order. Dig-vyavastha na vidyate: there is *no settled arrangement of direction* — nothing fixes dik itself; ask where east is and no answer is forthcoming that does not presuppose a standpoint. And the ground: saktayah khalu bhavanam upakara-prabhavitah — for the *capacities of things* are *brought about by assistance*: powers exist in the assisting they do (3.3.5's upakara, by which the unnameable relation is tracked; 3.3.37's connection just being cause-of-cognition). Direction is a power, powers are their assisting, and an assisting has no station of its own. So dik orders everything and is itself unordered — which is 3.6.1's anavasthita made precise.

Verse 3.6.7.translation: Direction, whose form is dissolved away in things, is called 'east'; that from which the cognition 'east' arises, that is direction — otherwise it is mere designation.
Verse 3.6.7.commentary: The criterion for when 'direction' names a power and when it names nothing. Praty-asta-rupa bhavesu dik purva iti abhidhiyate: direction, *whose form is dissolved away* in things — pratyasta-rupa: its own form set aside, effaced, having no standing apart from what it orders (3.6.6's no-arrangement-of-direction) — is called *'east'*. And the test: purva-buddhir yato dik sa — that *from which* the cognition *'east'* arises, *that* is direction; samakhya-matram anyatha: *otherwise* it is *mere designation*. Where 'east' wakes an actual orientation-cognition, direction is operating; where it is merely a label (the eastern province, so named on a map nobody orients by), it is samakhya-matra — mere naming, an aupacariki-satta posting (3.3.39). The verse gives the samuddesa a working criterion: direction is present exactly where it does cognitive work, and its name outruns its presence.

Verse 3.6.8.translation: That arrangement which is in the world from one's own body — in it the directions are not fixed; what is behind for one facing west is in front for one turned about.
Verse 3.6.8.commentary: The relativity of direction, demonstrated by turning around. Sva-angad vyavastha ya loke: the arrangement which is in the world *from one's own body* — the primary way anyone orients: front, back, left, right. Na tasyam niyata disah: in it the *directions are not fixed*. And the demonstration: pratyan-mukhasya yat pascat tat purastad viparyaye — what is *behind* for one *facing west* is *in front* *when turned about*. Turn, and behind becomes before. Nothing moved but the body. So the body-centred directions are wholly standpoint-relative, and they are the ones we actually live by — which means the primary directional facts are not facts about the world at all. This is 3.6.6's thesis made undeniable by an experiment anyone can run standing still.

Verse 3.6.9.translation: The restriction of the arrangement of places does not stand fixed among the directions; even what is established as posterior is spoken of as 'prior'.
Verse 3.6.9.commentary: The relativity extended past the body. Desa-vyavastha-niyamah disu na vyavatisthate: the *restriction* of the arrangement of *places* does *not stand fixed* among the *directions* — even the fixed cardinal directions do not fix things, because which is which depends on where one stands. Rudham api aparatvena purvam iti abhidhiyate: even what is *established as posterior* — rudha, conventionally settled as the further one (2.128's rudhi: hardened currency) — is *spoken of as 'prior'*. The village east of me is west of my neighbour, and both of us are right. So even the conventionally-hardened directional assignments invert with standpoint. The verse pairs with 3.6.8: bodily directions invert by turning, cardinal directions invert by moving, and neither survives as a property of anything.

Verse 3.6.10.translation: Therefore, from its having a spoken masculine, the masculine-substitution is not established; in this meaning, no engendering is anywhere stated by a word.
Verse 3.6.10.commentary: A grammatical consequence, and it is technical. Panini's rules provide for pumvad-bhava — *masculine-substitution*: in certain compounds, a feminine form is replaced by the corresponding masculine, and the rule applies where the stem is bhasita-pumska, 'having a spoken masculine' (a feminine whose masculine counterpart exists in usage with the same meaning). The direction-words are feminine (dik, prac) and the question is whether they qualify. Atah bhasita-pumskatvat pumvad-bhavo na sidhyati: *therefore*, from its having a spoken masculine, the masculine-substitution is *not established*. And the reason: asmin arthe na sabdena prasavah kvacid ucyate — in *this meaning*, *no engendering* is anywhere stated by a word: direction has no sex, and the grammatical gender of 'dik' answers to nothing in the thing. Prasava — engendering, generation: the ground on which grammatical gender is sometimes said to rest (the Linga-samuddesa, the thirteenth, will take this up). Direction fails the test because there is nothing there to be engendered.

Verse 3.6.11.translation: But when the capacity of direction is being expressed, the seeing of 'east' and the rest is fixed in the direction — as sixty is fixed in the determining of a lifetime.
Verse 3.6.11.commentary: The complement to 3.6.7: where direction *is* operating, it operates determinately. Dik-sakteh abhidhane tu: but when the *capacity of direction* is being *expressed* — where 'east' does its orienting work rather than merely labelling — niyatam disi darsanam purvadinam: the *seeing* of 'east' and the rest is *fixed* in the direction. And the simile: yatha sasteh jivitasya avadharane — as *sixty* in the *determining of a lifetime*: the traditional span, by which one says a man is in his sixties — a number that fixes nothing absolutely and fixes everything once the scale is granted. So direction's determinacy is scale-relative, exactly as the lifespan's is: within the frame it is exact, and the frame is not given by the thing. This is the samuddesa's balance-point — relativity (3.6.8-9) without arbitrariness (here): once a standpoint is taken, east is east, and no one is confused.

Verse 3.6.12.translation: By shade and light, a difference of portions is constructed for mountains and the like; among things whose own nature is not of that property, a difference of portions is not constructed.
Verse 3.6.12.commentary: How directional portions get attached to things. Chaya-abhabhyam nagadinam bhaga-bhedah prakalpate: by *shade and light*, a *difference of portions* is *constructed* for mountains and the like — the mountain's sunny side and shady side, and thence its east face and west face: the divisions come from the sun's relation to it, not from the mountain. Atad-dharma-svabhavesu bhaga-bhedo na kalpate: among things whose *own nature* is *not of that property* — things that cast no shade, take no light — a difference of portions is *not constructed*. So directional divisions attach only where something extrinsic can differentiate, and where nothing can, the thing has no sides. This is 3.1.15's ether-argument in the mountains: portions are conferred by relata (samyogi-dharma-bhedena), and where no relatum differentiates, there are no portions to have.

Verse 3.6.13.translation: For the partless atom, a part is prescribed by direction; that they call the first capacity — the capacity of constructing parts.
Verse 3.6.13.commentary: The samuddesa's most consequential verse. Paramanoh abhagasya disa bhago vidhiyate: for the *partless atom*, a *part* is *prescribed by direction* — the atom is by definition without parts, and direction gives it sides: an east face, a west face, whence it can touch its neighbours (the classic problem that broke Vaisesika atomism: if atoms are partless, contact must be total, and aggregates could not have magnitude). Direction supplies the parts the physics needs and the definition forbids. And the naming: bhaga-prakalpana-saktim prathamam tam pracaksate — that they call the *first capacity*: the *capacity of constructing parts*. Prathama — first: dik is the primordial power, prior to everything, because everything that has parts got them from it. And prakalpana — constructing: not revealing, constructing (2.236's apandita imagining the atom with parts, there a diagnosis of mental compulsion, here the compulsion's power named and honoured). Direction is what makes a divided world out of an undivided one, and it is not a thing.

Verse 3.6.14.translation: Things are without place, without parts, without motion, without support; but from the form of what is commingled, a difference of capacities is constructed.
Verse 3.6.14.commentary: The consequence of 3.6.13, stated at full generality. Adesah ca api abhagah ca niskrama nirupasrayah bhavah: things are *without place*, *without parts*, *without motion*, *without support* — four privations, and they strip a thing of everything spatial. Nothing has a location of its own, nothing has sides of its own, nothing moves of itself, nothing rests on anything. And: samsargi-rupat tu sakti-bhedah prakalpate — but from the *form of what is commingled*, a *difference of capacities* is *constructed*. Everything spatial is conferred by relation — by what a thing is *with* (3.1.16's places belonging to relata; 3.6.5's ether-portions; 3.6.12's shade and light). The verse is the Dik-samuddesa's answer to 3.6.1's promise: direction will not settle as a thing because there are no things for it to be a property of. There are commingling and the capacities commingling constructs.

Verse 3.6.15.translation: Partlessness is equal for the atom and for the pot; a part there is another capacity — and so is whatever magnitude belongs to those two.
Verse 3.6.15.commentary: The great leveling, and it is the samuddesa's boldest claim. Nirbhagatmakata tulya paramanoh ghatasya ca: *partlessness* is *equal* for the *atom* and for the *pot*. The pot is as partless as the atom. This is not a claim about size: it is 3.6.14's doctrine applied without exception — nothing has parts of its own, so the pot's evident parts are as conferred as the atom's disputed ones, and the two stand on exactly the same footing. Bhagah sakty-antaram tatra: a *part* there is *another capacity* — parthood is a sakti, constructed by direction (3.6.13), not a feature of the thing. Parimanam ca yat tayoh: and so is whatever *magnitude* belongs to those two — size likewise. So the Vaisesika's whole problem — how do partless atoms compose extended things? — is dissolved rather than solved: the extended thing is no more composed than the atom is partless, both being capacity-constructions on what has neither parts nor magnitude. The samuddesa on direction has arrived, in fifteen verses, at the dissolution of the atomic ontology.

Verse 3.6.16.translation: That from which difference is constructed — there too a difference is seen; therefore they know difference, whose ceasing is unseen, to be the more reasonable view.
Verse 3.6.16.commentary: The regress of differentiators, and its resolution by beginninglessness. Yatah prakalpate bhedah bhedah tatra api drsyate: that *from which* difference is *constructed* — direction, the first capacity (3.6.13) — *there too* a *difference is seen*: direction itself comes differentiated (east from west), and what differentiated *it*? The regress threatens exactly as it did for the atom's parts. And the resolution: adrsta-uparatim bhedam atah yuktataram viduh — therefore they know *difference whose ceasing is unseen* to be the *more reasonable* view: difference has no observed terminus, no point at which one reaches an undifferentiated differentiator. Adrsta-uparati: its cessation is not seen. This is not a proof that difference is beginningless but a methodological verdict — yuktatara, *more reasonable*: given that every candidate for a first differentiator turns out differentiated, the reasonable course is to stop looking for one. The same move stopped the samavaya-regress at 3.3.16 and the operation-regress at 3.3.28: refuse the demand rather than satisfy it.

Verse 3.6.17.translation: Because its effect is seen everywhere, it is held to be all-pervading; this very thing they call all-pervadingness — another is the rule for things that have effects.
Verse 3.6.17.commentary: What 'all-pervading' actually means, and it is deflationary. Sarvatra tasya karyasya darsanat vibhur isyate: because its *effect* is seen *everywhere*, it is held to be *all-pervading* — direction is called vibhu not because it has been found everywhere but because its working shows everywhere. Vibhutvam etad eva ahuh: *this very thing* they call *all-pervadingness*. So vibhutva is defined by universal efficacy, not by universal presence — there is no omnipresent stuff, only an operation nowhere absent (3.3.37's connection just being cause-of-cognition; 3.6.6's capacities brought about by assistance). And the contrast: anyah karyavatam vidhih — *another* is the rule for things that *have* effects: ordinary things, which have effects, are located where they are and their effects elsewhere. Direction is not such a thing. Its being everywhere *is* its effect being everywhere, and there is no further fact.

Verse 3.6.18.translation: The construction of direction and time stands in the world like consciousness itself; for who will establish otherwise that nature of living beings?
Verse 3.6.18.commentary: The samuddesa's most striking claim, and its simile is 1.41's. Caitanyavat sthita loke dik-kala-parikalpana: the *construction of direction and time* stands in the world *like consciousness itself*. The comparison is exact in the way 1.41's was (tradition persisting avicchedena, caitanyam iva — like consciousness): what is compared to consciousness is what one cannot get behind, because every attempt to get behind it uses it. Directional and temporal construing is not a theory anyone holds; it is the shape of any holding. And the rhetorical question seals it: prakrtim praninam tam hi ko 'nyatha sthapayisyati — for *who will establish otherwise* that *nature of living beings*? Prakrti here is nature-as-constitution: orienting and timing is what living beings *are*, not what they do. So the parikalpana that 3.6.13 called the first capacity is here called our nature — and the two descriptions are one: what constructs the world's parts is what we cannot help being.

Verse 3.6.19.translation: There would be a confusion of practices in the reversal of that nature; therefore one who abandons these entities takes hold of them again.
Verse 3.6.19.commentary: The practical necessity, and the verse's last clause is wry. Sankaro vyavaharanam prakrteh syad viparyaye: there would be a *confusion of practices* in the *reversal* of that nature — abolish directional and temporal construing and no transaction survives; nothing could be located, sequenced, or done (3.3.71's vyavaharo nivartate: practice withdraws when reality and otherness are both abandoned). Tasmat tyajann iman bhavan punar eva avalambate: *therefore* one who *abandons* these entities *takes hold of them again*. The philosopher who has argued direction into a capacity and the atom into a construction still says 'to the east' when giving directions to his house. Avalambate — takes hold, leans on: he leans on what he has just refuted, and must. This is 3.3.82's doctrine (all worldly practice is carried on by a being raised up by construction alone, as though by a primary word-meaning) with a smile at the theorist's expense — including, unmistakably, the author's own.

Verse 3.6.20.translation: But of that capacity, the difference of 'east' and the rest rests upon another entity; direction, differentiated by that difference, serves for differentiation alone.
Verse 3.6.20.commentary: Direction's own differentiation, located outside it. Tasyah tu sakteh purvadi-bhedah bhava-antara-asrayah: of *that capacity*, the difference of *'east' and the rest* rests upon *another entity* — east is east relative to something else (the sun, the body, the reference-point of 3.6.2's avadhi); the differences within direction are conferred from outside, as direction confers differences on what it orders. Bhinna dik tena bhedena bhedaya eva upakalpate: *direction*, *differentiated by that difference*, serves *for differentiation alone*. So the sequence runs: something else differentiates direction; direction, so differentiated, differentiates everything else; and it does nothing but that. The verse answers 3.6.16's regress by accepting it and locating direction within it: dik is a differentiated differentiator, not a first term, and 3.6.16 already ruled that looking for a first term is unreasonable.

Verse 3.6.21.translation: And when there is a dependence by way of being a limit, the rule characterized by direction applies; the sixth case alone is seen, as in 'this is prior to that', where another property is the substrate.
Verse 3.6.21.commentary: The grammar of directional expressions. Avadhitvena ca apeksa-yoge: when there is a *dependence by way of being a limit* — where one thing serves as the reference-point from which another is located — dig-laksano vidhih: the *rule characterized by direction* applies (Panini's provisions for directional words: purva, apara, daksina and their case-government). And the observation: purvam asya iti sasthy eva drsta dharma-antara-asraye — *'prior to this'*: the *sixth case alone* is seen, where *another property* is the *substrate*. The genitive, not the ablative: one says 'this village's east' rather than 'east from this village', and the genitive is the case of relation whose generating action has withdrawn (2.197-198). So direction's grammar registers exactly its metaphysics: a relation, standing without any live action, expressed by the case that expresses standing relations.

Verse 3.6.22.translation: The inversion of 'east' and the rest is not seen when the limits are unconfused; and this mark — 'this is straight from that' — is not intermixed.
Verse 3.6.22.commentary: The determinacy that survives the relativity. 3.6.8-9 showed directions inverting with standpoint; here the limit of that. Purvadinam viparyasah adrstah avadhy-asankare: the *inversion* of 'east' and the rest is *not seen* when the *limits are unconfused* — hold the reference-point fixed and east does not become west; the inversions of 3.6.8-9 all came from *changing* the avadhi. Rju etad asya iti etat ca lingam na vyatikiryate: and this *mark* — 'this is *straight* from that' (3.6.2's rju: the straightness direction delivers) — is *not intermixed*: the straight-line relation between two fixed points is determinate and does not blur. So directional relativity is standpoint-relativity and nothing more: relative to a limit, everything is exact. This is 3.6.11's balance (within the frame, exact; the frame not given by the thing) stated as a rule, and it is what keeps the samuddesa's constructivism from collapsing into arbitrariness.

Verse 3.6.23.translation: Or it is a property of the inner organ that shines forth thus, outwardly; but in this procedure, there is no being-within or being-without.
Verse 3.6.23.commentary: The inner/outer question raised and dismissed. Antahkarana-dharmo va bahir evam prakasate: *or* it is a property of the *inner organ* that *shines forth* thus, *outwardly* — direction as a form of the mind, projected: the reading a Kantian would recognize, and it is offered as an alternative (va). But then the dismissal: asyam tu antar-bahir-bhavah prakriyayam na vidyate — but in *this procedure*, there is *no being-within or being-without*. The inner/outer distinction is itself a directional construction (3.6.13's bhaga-prakalpana: the constructing of parts), so it cannot be used to place direction. To ask whether direction is in the mind or in the world is to ask a directional question about direction, and 3.3.77's rule forbids it: thing-distinctions do not deserve definitions among non-things. The verse is a small masterpiece of self-restraint: it names the idealist option, sees that stating it presupposes what it explains, and declines.

Verse 3.6.24.translation: Knowing that the construction of oneness or of manifoldness for these capacities has fallen among non-things — one should not reflect upon it as real.
Verse 3.6.24.commentary: A methodological injunction, and it is the samuddesa's discipline. Ekatvam asam saktinam nanatvam va iti kalpane: the *construction of oneness* or of *manifoldness* for these *capacities* — is direction one power or many (east, west, north as distinct saktis)? Avastu-patite jnatva: *knowing* that it has *fallen among non-things* — the question is about capacities, and capacities are not things, and thing-questions do not apply (3.3.77 again). Satyato na paramrset: one should *not reflect upon it as real*. The injunction is precise: not 'do not ask' but 'do not take the answer as real' — one may construct oneness or manifoldness as the description requires (2.179's licensed double description) and must not mistake either for a finding. This is the samuddesa policing itself: having just declined the inner/outer question (3.6.23), it now declines the one/many question, on the same ground.

Verse 3.6.25.translation: Among entities whose reality is beyond construction, the practices resting on convention — the world is followed along in those.
Verse 3.6.25.commentary: Where the injunction of 3.6.24 leaves us. Vikalpa-atita-tattvesu bhavesu: among *entities* whose *reality is beyond construction* — everything, on this samuddesa's showing (3.6.14: things are without place, parts, motion, support). Sanketa-upanibandhanah vyavaharah ye: the *practices* resting on *convention* — sanketa: agreed usage, the working namings of 2.142. Lokah tatra anugamyate: *the world is followed along* in those. Not: the world is *corrected*. Followed. The wise man does not construct alternatives against the world's namings (2.142's tat prajnah na vikalpayet), and here that counsel is applied to the most fundamental case: the world says east and west and before and after, its entities are beyond all such construction, and one follows the world. Anugamyate is the samuddesa's last practical word, and it is the same verb 3.6.19 used for the theorist taking hold again of what he abandoned.

Verse 3.6.26.translation: There is no oneness without non-manifoldness, and not the other without oneness; in the highest sense, this difference between the two does not exist at all.
Verse 3.6.26.commentary: The one/many question of 3.6.24 answered by dissolution. Na ekatvam asti ananatvam: there is *no oneness* without *non-manifoldness* — to be one is to be not-many; the concept is negative through and through. Vina ekatvena na itarat: and *not the other* without *oneness* — to be many is to be not-one. Each is the other's negation and neither has independent content. Paramarthe tayoh esa bhedo 'tyantam na vidyate: in the *highest sense*, this *difference* between the two does *not exist at all*. So the question 'is direction one or many?' has no answer because its two options are not two: each is the denial of the other, and where neither can be affirmed independently, the disjunction is empty. This is 3.3.60-61's structure exactly (being and non-being mutually understood, hence constructions of one self), now applied to number — and it explains 3.6.24's injunction: one does not reflect on it as real because there is no *it*.

Verse 3.6.27.translation: There is no difference of the capacities such as belongs to the standing of things that have capacities; nor is there a worldly oneness among their selves.
Verse 3.6.27.commentary: The capacities placed outside the number-categories altogether. Na saktinam tatha bhedo yatha sakti-matam sthitih: there is *no difference of the capacities* such as belongs to the *standing of things that have* capacities — powers are not distinct in the way their bearers are distinct: two cows are two, and the powers in them are not two in that way (1.2's aprthaktve 'pi saktibhyah: not separate from its powers, yet functioning as though separate). Na ca laukikam ekatvam tasam atmasu vidyate: nor is there a *worldly oneness* among their *selves* — neither are they one in the way a thing is one. So capacities are neither many as things are many nor one as a thing is one: they fall outside the number-scheme, which is exactly 3.6.24's avastu-patite (fallen among non-things). The verse is the samuddesa's most precise statement of what a sakti is, and it says what it is not.

Verse 3.6.28.translation: Oneness would not stand fixed if one did not construct manifoldness; and manifoldness would fall away if one did not construct oneness.
Verse 3.6.28.commentary: The mutual dependence of 3.6.26 stated as a constructive fact. Na ekatvam vyavatistheta nanatvam cen na kalpayet: *oneness* would not *stand fixed* if one did not *construct manifoldness* — the one is available only against a many that construction supplies. Nanatvam ca avahiyeta yadi ekatvam na kalpayet: and *manifoldness* would *fall away* if one did not construct *oneness* — the many is available only against a one. So both members of the pair are held in place by the other's construction, and neither is found. This is the Dik-samuddesa's fitting close: a samuddesa about the power that constructs all parts (3.6.13's bhaga-prakalpana-sakti) ends by showing that the categories in which we count the parts are themselves mutually-propping constructions. Direction makes the many; the many and the one make each other; and nothing in the sequence rests on anything. The next samuddesa — Sadhana, on the karakas, the largest in the kanda — will find the same structure in the grammar's account of who does what to whom.

Verse 3.7.1.translation: The capacity, in the completing of actions inherent in their own substratum — and likewise in another substratum — they know as the means.
Verse 3.7.1.commentary: The definition of sadhana, and it is functional through and through. Sva-asraye samavetanam — of actions *inherent in their own substratum* (the cooking that is in the cook); tadvad eva asrayantare — *and likewise in another substratum* (the cooking that reaches the rice). Kriyanam abhinispattau samarthyam sadhanam viduh: the *capacity*, in the *completing* of those actions, they know as the *means*. Not: a means is a thing used. A means is a *capacity*, individuated by its contribution to an action's completion — samarthyam, the word this work has used for what settles every disputed case (2.371's samarthya-laksanam deciding distribution; 3.1.79's capacity explaining why universals cannot be substituted). So the karakas — agent, object, instrument, and the rest, the whole architecture of Sanskrit syntax — are from the outset capacities and not entities. What makes something an agent is not what it is but what it does toward a completion, and the samuddesa's 157 pages will not add anything to that definition; they will only show how far it reaches.

Verse 3.7.2.translation: Since the universe — an aggregate of mere capacities, of manifold properties — exists always and in every way, somewhere something is intended.
Verse 3.7.2.commentary: The metaphysics behind 3.7.1, and it is 2.250's semantics turned outward. Sakti-matra-samuhasya visvasya aneka-dharmanah: the *universe* is an *aggregate of mere capacities*, of *manifold properties* — sakti-matra: capacities *only*, nothing underneath them bearing them (3.1.22-23's dravyadayah sarvah saktayah; 3.6.14's things without place, parts, motion, support). Sarvada sarvatha bhavat: since it exists *always* and in *every way* — everything is everything, potentially, at all times. Kvacit kimcid vivaksyate: *somewhere*, *something* is *intended*. Out of a totality where every capacity is everywhere available, speech selects — and the selecting is vivaksa (1.155, 2.400's directed eye). This is the exact structural twin of the word's sarvarthya (2.250: one word, all meanings, carved by nimittas): the world is omni-capable as the word is omni-significant, and in both cases intention does the carving. The karaka-system is where the two carvings meet.

Verse 3.7.3.translation: And the practice of means is grounded in a state of the intellect; a difference among the forms of objects, whether existent or non-existent, is constructed by the intellect.
Verse 3.7.3.commentary: The samuddesa's thesis, stated in its third verse. Sadhana-vyavaharah ca buddhy-avastha-nibandhanah: the *practice of means* is *grounded in a state of the intellect* — which thing counts as agent, which as instrument, is a fact about how the situation is being held in mind. Nibandhana: the grounding-word (1.13's words as the basis of practice; 2.297's sabdah loka-nibandhanah). And the reach of the claim: san asan va artha-rupesu bhedo buddhya prakalpyate — a *difference* among the *forms of objects*, whether *existent or non-existent*, is *constructed by the intellect*. Not merely the karaka-assignments: the object-differences themselves, and *whether they exist or not* (3.3.42's figurative existence, granted by mention). So the whole furniture on which the karaka-system operates is buddhi-constructed, and the system's assignments are constructions on constructions. This is 2.428's doctrine — the speaker alone intends the sadhya-sadhana structure — given its ground: he can, because there is nothing there to resist him.

Verse 3.7.4.translation: When a speaker, having brought the Pancalas to oneness by the intellect along with the Kurus, divides them again — then a separation is understood.
Verse 3.7.4.commentary: The thesis of 3.7.3 in a worked example. Buddhya samahita-ekatvan pancalan kurubhih yada: when, *by the intellect*, the *Pancalas* have been *brought to oneness* *with the Kurus* — two peoples held in one thought, as one group. Punah vibhajate vakta: *the speaker divides them again* — and says something like 'the Pancalas came away from the Kurus'. Tada apayah pratiyate: *then* a *separation* is understood — and with it the ablative case, apadana, the karaka of departure. Note the sequence: the *unity* was made by buddhi first, and only against that made unity can the division register as a separation. Nothing in Pancalas and Kurus made them one; the speaker did; and the ablative he then uses reports a departure from a unity he manufactured a moment earlier. The karaka is the residue of the speaker's own prior construing — which is what 3.7.3 said, now visible in a single sentence.

Verse 3.7.5.translation: And things such as Kamsa, whose forms are conditioned by words and which have come to be objects of the intellect, one considers as means — as though they were perceptible.
Verse 3.7.5.commentary: The thesis extended to what does not exist. Sabda-upahita-rupan buddheh visayatam gatan: things *whose forms are conditioned by words* and which have *come to be objects of the intellect* — beings that have no existence except through being spoken of and thought (3.3.39's aupacariki satta; 3.3.53's cognition born of designation). Kamsadin: *Kamsa* and the like — the tyrant of the Krsna-legend, long dead and perhaps never living, and the grammarians' own standard example (Panini has a rule for kamsa-vadha, the slaying of Kamsa). Pratyaksam iva sadhanatvena manyate: one considers them *as means* — *as though they were perceptible*. One says 'Krsna killed Kamsa', and Kamsa is the karman, the object, doing full grammatical work. So the karaka-system operates identically on the real and the fabulous, which is decisive for 3.7.3: if karakas were features of events, the non-existent could not bear them. They are buddhi-postings, and buddhi posts them on Kamsa as readily as on the pot.

Verse 3.7.6.translation: And by the expressers, having superimposed the form of the intellect's operation upon the objects, a construction of differences of capacity is made.
Verse 3.7.6.commentary: The mechanism named, and the verb is 3.1.6's. Buddhi-pravrtti-rupam ca samaropya: having *superimposed* the *form of the intellect's operation* — samaropa, the superimposition by which the word's own universal is laid over the object's (3.1.6's adhyaropa-kalpana). Abhidhatrbhih arthesu: by the *expressers*, upon the *objects*. Saktibheda nam kriyate parikalpana: a *construction of differences of capacity* is *made*. So the karakas are the shape of the mind's own operating, projected onto what it operates about: the intellect proceeds from agent through instrument to object, and it lays that procession on the world and calls it the structure of the event. The verse is the Padakanda's most explicit statement of where its categories come from — not from things, and not arbitrarily either, but from the form of thinking itself, externalized (3.6.23's antahkarana-dharmo va bahir evam prakasate: a property of the inner organ shining forth outwardly, there declined as unstatable, here stated of the karakas).

Verse 3.7.7.translation: When the individual is the word-meaning, the means-hood of an object being produced from a word and the like is likewise established, constructed by the form of the intellect.
Verse 3.7.7.commentary: The doctrine applied to the hardest karaka: the nirvartya-karman, the object *produced by* the action. Vyaktau padarthe: when the *individual* is the word-meaning. Sabdadeh janyamanasya karmanah sadhanatvam: the *means-hood* of an *object being produced* — the mat that the weaving makes, the sound the striking makes: the thing that does not exist until the action completes, and is nonetheless the action's object throughout. This is the case that most embarrasses any realist account: what is the action reaching toward, while it reaches? Tatha siddham buddhi-rupa-prakalpitam: it is *likewise established*, *constructed by the form of the intellect*. The produced object is a buddhi-posting held in place across the production — exactly like Kamsa (3.7.5), and by the same mechanism. So the karaka-system's most paradoxical member is not an anomaly needing special treatment; it is the ordinary case with its constructedness visible, since here the thing manifestly is not there to be reached.

Verse 3.7.8.translation: Independence and dependence, and the form of sequence, have been shown; even among things that are without striving, they rest upon construction.
Verse 3.7.8.commentary: The two poles of the karaka-system placed. Svatantra-paratantratve: *independence* and *dependence* — the agent is defined by Panini as svatantra, independent (1.4.54: svatantrah karta), and every other karaka is dependent on it. Krama-rupam ca darsitam: and the *form of sequence* has been shown (3.6.4's sequence from time; 3.1.35's Being with sequence). Nirihesu api bhavesu kalpana-upanibandhanam: *even among things that are without striving* — nir-iha: without effort, without intention, without any doing of their own — these *rest upon construction*. The axe does not strive to cut, the fire does not strive to burn, and yet we make the axe an instrument and the fire an agent ('the fire cooks'). Independence and dependence are conferred on the striving-less by construction, and the whole hierarchy of the karakas — that celebrated architecture in which everything orbits an independent agent — is laid on a world in which nothing is doing anything.

Verse 3.7.9.translation: For the proponents of commingling, all entities are capacities and possessors of capacities; among those, which are not expressed by their own words, means-hood is determined.
Verse 3.7.9.commentary: The position placed in its school. Saktayah sakti-mantah ca sarve samsarga-vadinam bhavah: for the *proponents of commingling* — the samsarga-vadins, those for whom things exist only in relation (3.4.1's samsarga-rupat sambhutah: word-meanings arisen from the form of commingling) — *all* entities are *capacities* and *possessors of capacities*, both at once, nothing being purely one or the other. Tesu asva-sabdesu sadhanatvam nirupyate: among *those*, which are *not expressed by their own words*, *means-hood* is *determined*. Asva-sabda: not having its own word — the capacities are not named directly by any word; they are determined as means only through the case-endings and the construction (3.7.13 will say this outright). So the karakas are capacities in a world of capacities, reached by no word of their own, fixed by the grammar's oblique apparatus. The verse is the samuddesa's placement of itself: this is what the karaka-system looks like from inside a relational ontology.

Verse 3.7.10.translation: In the pot's being the object of seeing, largeness and the rest are the means; in colour's being the object of seeing, colour-hood and the rest are the means.
Verse 3.7.10.commentary: Two worked cases, and their pairing is the argument. Ghatasya drsi-karmatve mahattvadini sadhanam: in the *pot's* being the *object of seeing*, *largeness* and the rest are the *means* — what makes the pot visible is not the pot but its magnitude (the Vaisesika's own doctrine: mahattva is a condition of visibility; atoms are invisible for want of it). Rupasya drsi-karmatve rupatvadini sadhanam: in *colour's* being the object of seeing, *colour-hood* and the rest are the means. So in each case, what does the enabling is a property, and the thing we call the object of seeing is not what enables its own being seen. The karman — the object — is thus doubly derivative: it is a buddhi-posting (3.7.3), and even its availability to the action runs through properties that are themselves capacities. Nothing in the chain is the solid thing the grammar's accusative seems to name.

Verse 3.7.11.translation: Tastes and the rest are possessors of capacity by their own generals and particulars; in the world, those capacities are of fixed apprehension, likewise by their substrata.
Verse 3.7.11.commentary: The fixity within the constructedness. Svaih samanya-visesaih ca sakti-mantah rasadayah: *tastes* and the rest are *possessors of capacity* by their *own* generals and particulars — sweetness has its capacity by being sweet, and by the kind of sweet it is. Niyata-grahanah loke: in the *world*, they are of *fixed apprehension* — the tongue takes taste and not colour, invariably; no confusion has ever occurred. Saktayah tah tatha asrayaih: those *capacities* likewise, by their *substrata*. This is 3.3.29's yogyata (the senses' beginningless fitness for their objects) reappearing in the karaka-system: the capacities are constructed (3.7.6) and they are not arbitrary — each is fixed to its apprehension as the eye is fixed to colour. So the samuddesa's constructivism has the same shape as the Dik-samuddesa's: relative to the frame, exact (3.6.22); and the frame is not the thing's.

Verse 3.7.12.translation: The connection of sense-faculty, object, mind and agent is somewhere the means; whatever, at whatever time, is what assists — that, at that time, is the means there.
Verse 3.7.12.commentary: The general rule, and it is as loose as the samuddesa's thesis requires. Indriya-artha-manah-kartr-sambandhah sadhanam kvacit: the *connection* of *sense-faculty*, *object*, *mind* and *agent* is *somewhere* the means — the whole four-term complex of perception can itself be the instrument, on occasion. And then the formula: yad yada yad-anugrahi tat tada tatra sadhanam — *whatever*, *at whatever time*, is *what assists* — *that*, *at that time*, is the *means* *there*. Three indexicals in seven words: whatever, whenever, wherever. Nothing is an instrument; anything is an instrument when it is assisting. Anugrahi — assisting: the upakara by which the unnameable relation is tracked (3.3.5), by which capacities are brought about (3.6.6), and now by which karakas are assigned. The rule is the samuddesa's centre: means-hood is occasion-relative assistance, full stop.

Verse 3.7.13.translation: But when expression is by their own words, that property is not expressed; that assistance is understood by the case-endings alone.
Verse 3.7.13.commentary: How the karakas actually get into a sentence, and it is not by being named. Sva-sabdaih abhidhane tu sa dharmo na abhidhiyate: when expression is by *their own words* — when one says 'axe', 'Devadatta', 'rice' — *that property* (means-hood, agency, objecthood) is *not expressed*: the noun names the thing and says nothing about its role. Vibhakty-adibhih eva asau upakarah pratiyate: that *assistance* is understood by the *case-endings alone*. The role rides on the ending, not the stem: 'devadattena' says instrument, and 'devadatta' names a man. So the karaka-system is carried entirely by the inflectional apparatus, and the words that name the participants are silent about what they are doing. This is 3.7.9's asva-sabdesu made precise — the capacities have no words of their own — and it explains why the samuddesa is possible at all: the roles are a grammatical layer, assigned by endings, and one can therefore ask what the assignment tracks. The answer, since 3.7.3, is buddhi.

Verse 3.7.14.translation: The state of being an occasioning ground for things, resorted to for the sake of assistance — 'bending', 'turning toward' — thus established, it is held to be the means.
Verse 3.7.14.commentary: The definition of 3.7.1 restated through etymology. Nimitta-bhavah bhavanam upakara-artham asritah: the state of being an *occasioning ground* for things, resorted to *for the sake of assistance* (3.7.12's anugrahi: whatever assists is the means). And then two glosses of nimitta by its supposed roots: natih, *bending*; avarjana, *turning toward*. The etymologists derive nimitta from a bending-toward, an inclination of one thing toward another's completion — and Bhartrhari, who spent 2.170-175 showing that derivations are landmarks rather than foundations, uses this one for what it is worth: it pictures the means as a leaning, not a thing. Iti evam siddhah sadhanam isyate: *thus established*, it is held to be the means. What a sadhana *is*, on this account, is an inclination toward an action's completion — which is a relational posture, not an object.

Verse 3.7.15.translation: That is either distinct from them, or is their very self; having resorted to separation, it is constructed as means-hood.
Verse 3.7.15.commentary: The ontology of the means, and the two options are offered without decision. Sa tebhyo vyatirikto va: *that* (the nimitta-bhava, the being-a-ground) is either *distinct from* them — a further item over and above the things it relates; tesam atma eva va tatha: or is *their very self* — nothing but the things themselves under a description. The dispute is the tradition's standing one about relation (3.3.6's samyoga and samavaya: are they properties of the relata, or items?) and Bhartrhari declines it as he declined it there. But he says what happens in either case: vyatirekam upasritya sadhanatvena kalpyate — *having resorted to separation*, it is *constructed as* means-hood. Whatever the metaphysics, the *construction* requires taking the means as separate — the instrument must be held apart from the action to count as its instrument. So means-hood is a construction on a separation that may or may not answer to anything, and the grammar proceeds identically either way (2.179's licensed double description).

Verse 3.7.16-17.translation: (16) Perceiving, in the case of seeking; and next, in the case of resolving; and resolve, likewise, in undertaking — serve as means. (17) The very action that was in the prior case is held to be the means in the subsequent; but in perceiving, they know a distinguished consciousness to be the means.
Verse 3.7.16-17.commentary: The chain of practical reason, analysed as a series of karaka-assignments. Sandarsanam prarthanayam: *perceiving* is the means in *seeking* — one wants what one has seen; vyavasaye tv anantara: and *next* (perceiving-and-seeking) in *resolving*; vyavasayah tatha arambhe: and *resolve* likewise in *undertaking*. Seeing, wanting, deciding, doing: each stage is the instrument of the next. And the principle: purvasmin ya kriya sa eva parasmin sadhanam mata — *the very action* that was in the *prior* case is held to be the *means* in the *subsequent*. So the same item is action here and instrument there, purely by where in the chain one stands (2.429's cooking that is object, instrument and sadhya at once, assigned by the sentence's construction). And the chain's first term: sandarsane tu caitanyam visistam sadhanam viduh — in *perceiving*, they know a *distinguished consciousness* to be the means. The regress stops at consciousness, which is not itself a stage.

Verse 3.7.18.translation: In the mere completing, agency exists in every karaka whatever; when a difference of operation is depended upon, the possibility of being instrument and the rest arises.
Verse 3.7.18.commentary: The samuddesa's most consequential structural doctrine. Nispatti-matre kartrtvam sarvatra eva asti karake: in the *mere completing*, *agency* exists in *every karaka whatever*. Every participant is an agent: the axe cuts, the wood splits, the fire cooks — each does its own thing, and each is doing it. Agency is not the privilege of the grammatical subject; it is what every contributor exercises. And then: vyapara-bheda-apeksayam karanaditva-sambhavah — when a *difference of operation* is *depended upon*, the possibility of being *instrument and the rest* arises. The differentiation into instrument, object, locus and the rest appears only when one attends to *how* each contributes — which operation each performs. So the karaka-system is a hierarchy imposed on a democracy: all are agents, and the assignment of subordinate roles is a matter of which operation one is attending to. The verses following will make this the samuddesa's organizing insight.

Verse 3.7.19.translation: As, in the birth of a son, agency is spoken of for both parents — 'he in her', 'she from him' — the difference is by intention.
Verse 3.7.19.commentary: The doctrine of 3.7.18 in the plainest possible case. Putrasya janmani yatha pitroh kartrtvam ucyate: as, in the *birth of a son*, *agency* is spoken of for *both parents* — both are agents of the begetting; neither is the instrument of the other. And yet the grammar must assign roles: ayam asyam — *he in her* (locative: she the locus, he the agent); iyam tu asmat — *she from him* (ablative: he the source, she the agent). Two sentences, the same event, opposite assignments. Iti bhedo vivaksaya: the *difference is by intention*. Nothing in the begetting makes the mother a locus rather than an agent; the speaker's construing does (2.428: the speaker alone intends the sadhya-sadhana structure). The example is chosen for its symmetry — no one can claim that biology settles which parent is the karta — and it demonstrates 3.7.18 exactly: both are agents in the mere completing, and the role-difference is imposed by attention.

Verse 3.7.20-21.translation: (20) The agents of qualities and actions, their capacities subordinated by the agent — even when subordinated, they are furnished with their own complete operations. (21) Known by instrument-hood and the rest, which follow upon the differences of action, having obtained a subsequent independence, they attain agency with respect to the principal.
Verse 3.7.20-21.commentary: The mechanism of subordination, and it is precisely described. (20): guna-kriyanam kartarah — the *agents* of qualities and actions (every participant, each an agent by 3.7.18) — kartra nyakkrta-saktayah: their *capacities subordinated by the agent* — nyakkrta: pressed down, demoted, made to bow. The principal agent's presence demotes the rest. And the crucial concession: nyaktayam api sampurnaih svaih vyaparaih samanvitah — *even when subordinated*, they are furnished with their *own complete operations*: the axe's cutting is not diminished by the woodsman's being the karta; it is complete, and it is the axe's. Demotion is a matter of grammatical rank, not of diminished doing. (21): karanatvadibhih jnatah kriya-bheda-anupatibhih — *known* by *instrument-hood* and the rest, which *follow upon* the differences of *action*; svatantryam uttaram labdhva pradhane yanti kartrtam: having obtained a *subsequent independence*, they *attain agency* with respect to the *principal*. The subordinate karakas regain agency at the next level up: within its own operation, the axe is svatantra. So the system is a nested hierarchy of agents, each independent in its own sphere and demoted in the sphere above.

Verse 3.7.22-23.translation: (22) As, among those appointed by the king, the state of being fighters stands in the fighters; but when they act, it is the king who obtains victory and defeat. (23) So, among all those appointed by the agent, doing one work — agency, subsequent, is not in conflict with instrument-hood and the rest.
Verse 3.7.22-23.commentary: The political simile, and it is the samuddesa's finest. (22): rajna niyuktesu — among those *appointed by the king* — yoddhrtvam yoddhrsu sthitam: the *state of being fighters* stands *in the fighters*: the soldiers do the fighting, entirely and without remainder; the king is not fighting. Tesu vrttau tu labhate raja jaya-parajayau: but *when they act*, it is *the king* who obtains *victory and defeat*. The doing is theirs; the outcome is predicated of him. (23) applies it: tatha kartra niyuktesu sarvesu eka-artha-karisu — so among *all those appointed by the agent*, *doing one work* — kartrtvam karanaditvaih uttaram na virudhyate: *agency*, *subsequent*, is *not in conflict* with instrument-hood and the rest. The axe is an agent (it cuts) and an instrument (in the woodsman's sentence), as the soldier is a fighter and the king's instrument, and no contradiction arises because the two predications belong to different levels. The simile explains what 3.7.20-21 asserted, and it explains why we say 'the king conquered' without lying and 'the soldiers fought' without contradicting ourselves.

Verse 3.7.24.translation: But when the operation is not resorted to, the occasioning ground is held to be the hetu; when the state of being a limit is resorted to, they know the mark to be in the case of the mark.
Verse 3.7.24.commentary: Two karakas distinguished by what they do not do. Anasrite tu vyapare nimittam hetur isyate: when the *operation is not resorted to* — where the ground contributes without any operation being attributed to it — the occasioning ground is held to be *hetu*, the cause (Panini's karana in its causal sense, and the ablative of cause: 'he suffers *from* illness'). Asrita-avadhi-bhavam tu laksane laksanam viduh: when the state of being a *limit* is resorted to, they know the *mark* to be in the case of the *mark*. So hetu and laksana are the two karakas that operate without operating: one contributes causally without a vyapara of its own, the other locates by being a boundary (3.6.2's avadhi: the limit from which direction separates). The verse is fine taxonomy: having established that every karaka is an agent with its own complete operation (3.7.20), it identifies the two that are exceptions — and they are the two that do not act.

Verse 3.7.25.translation: The hetu has substance and the rest for its domain; the karaka has a fixed action. An agent depending on another agent is held to be the hetu in the action.
Verse 3.7.25.commentary: The hetu/karaka distinction sharpened. Dravyadi-visayo hetuh: the *hetu* has *substance and the rest* for its *domain* — it can be anything: a substance, a quality, an action, so long as it grounds. Karakam niyata-kriyam: the *karaka* has a *fixed action* — a karaka is defined by its determinate contribution to a determinate act (3.7.1's samarthyam in the completing). And the causative: karta kartr-antara-apeksah kriyayam hetur isyate — an *agent depending on another agent* is held to be the *hetu* in the action. This is the grammar of causation: when Devadatta makes Yajnadatta cook, Yajnadatta is an agent who depends on another agent, and Devadatta is the hetu. So the causative construction is analysed as an agent-over-agent relation — which is exactly 3.7.22's king and soldiers, now given its morphological home in the nic-affix.

Verse 3.7.26.translation: For an action, a substitute for the instrument is seen; but the action is for the sake of the hetu — therefore that one is not substituted.
Verse 3.7.26.commentary: A test that discriminates the two. Kriyayai karanantasya drstah pratinidhih tatha: for an *action*, a *substitute for the instrument* is *seen* — the axe failing, use a hatchet; instruments are replaceable (3.1.78's substitution-rule discriminating what may be substituted from what may not). Hetv-artha tu kriya: but the *action* is *for the sake of* the *hetu* — the hetu is not a means the action uses; the action exists for it. Tasmat na sa pratinidhiyate: *therefore* that one is *not substituted*. One does not substitute a different reason for acting and call it the same act. The test is the same one 3.1.78-79 ran on universals and qualities (what stands near the capacity cannot be replaced; what stands at a remove can), and it places hetu and karana precisely: the instrument serves the action and is fungible; the hetu is what the action serves and is not.

Verse 3.7.27.translation: The hetu is the accomplisher of the meaning by way of the contrary and by way of the favourable; and purposiveness, by way of the favourable, is what follows upon hetu-hood.
Verse 3.7.27.commentary: The hetu's two modes. Pratilomya-anulomyabhyam hetur arthasya sadhakah: the *hetu* is the *accomplisher of the meaning* by way of the *contrary* and by way of the *favourable* — one acts *because of* a thing one wants (favourable) and *because of* a thing one flees (contrary): both are reasons, and the ablative of cause covers both ('from desire', 'from fear'). Tadarthyam anulomyena hetutva-anugatam tu tat: and *purposiveness* — tadarthya, being-for-that, the dative's sense ('for the sake of') — *by way of the favourable*, is what *follows upon hetu-hood*. So the dative of purpose is a special case of the hetu: the favourable reason, taken as goal. The verse quietly unifies two of Panini's cases (the ablative of cause and the dative of purpose) under one karaka-analysis, distinguished only by direction — and the unification follows from 3.7.26's finding that the hetu is what the action is *for*.

Verse 3.7.28.translation: Everywhere the innate capacity stands as long as the substance does; but at the time of the action, from its manifestation, it becomes assisting through its substratum.
Verse 3.7.28.commentary: The temporality of capacity, and it settles a real puzzle. Sarvatra sahaja saktih yavad-dravyam avasthita: *everywhere*, the *innate* capacity stands *as long as the substance* does — the axe's cutting-power is in it while it is an axe, whether or not anyone is cutting; capacities do not come and go with their exercise. Kriya-kale tu abhivyakteh asrayad upakarini: but *at the time of the action*, from its *manifestation*, it becomes *assisting* through its *substratum*. So the capacity is permanent and its *assisting* is occasional — it is always there and it helps only when manifested (1.44's manifestation-doctrine: the sphota permanent, the sounds occasioning). This resolves how a karaka can be both a standing property of a thing (the axe is an instrument even in the shed) and an occasion-relative posting (3.7.12's whatever-whenever-wherever): the capacity stands, the assisting happens.

Verse 3.7.29.translation: The wall's capacity is in obstructing, that of a sword and the like in cutting; that property, being always existent, is determined at the time of the action.
Verse 3.7.29.commentary: The doctrine of 3.7.28 in two homely cases. Kudyasya avarane saktih: the *wall's* capacity is in *obstructing* — a wall is for stopping, and it is for stopping when nothing is being stopped. Asyadinam vidarane: that of a *sword* and the like in *cutting*. Sarvada sa tu san dharmah: that property, being *always existent* — the wall does not acquire obstruction-power when someone walks into it. Kriya-kale nirupyate: it is *determined* at the *time of the action* — nirupyate: ascertained, fixed, brought into determinacy (3.3.54's sva-rupena anirupitam: the object's own form left undetermined by the word). So the capacity is eternal and its determination is occasional, and the karaka-assignment records the determination. The examples are chosen for their obviousness: nobody thinks the wall becomes obstructive on impact, and nobody can say what the wall's obstruction *is* except by reference to a walking-into that has not yet happened.

Verse 3.7.30.translation: As the Varuna-nooses of the demons, conjoined with their own limbs, are made manifest — so are the capacities of substances made manifest for those who seek to conquer.
Verse 3.7.30.commentary: The samuddesa's most vivid image for manifestation. The varuna-pasa — Varuna's nooses, the god's binding weapon of the Vedic hymns, which the demons carry sva-anga-samyoginah, *conjoined with their own limbs*: the noose is in them, part of them, unseen until it binds. Vyajyante: they are *made manifest* — the noose does not arrive; it appears out of what was already there. Dravyanam saktayah tatha vijigisunam: so the *capacities of substances*, for those who *seek to conquer*. The image completes 3.7.28-29 with a picture: capacities are latent bindings, carried in the thing, showing when the occasion draws them out. And vijigisunam — for those who seek to conquer — quietly returns the samuddesa to its own subject: the capacities manifest *for* someone with a purpose, and a purpose is what makes an event into a structure of agent and instrument and object. The karaka-system is the world's latent nooses, drawn out by an intention (3.7.2's kvacit kimcid vivaksyate: somewhere, something is intended).

Verse 3.7.31.translation: When the sword, by its own sharpness, weight, hardness and shape, is engaged toward what is to be cut — then it is apprehended as capacity-possessing.
Verse 3.7.31.commentary: The manifestation of 3.7.30 given its physics. Taiksnya-gaurava-kathinya-samsthanaih svaih: by its *own* *sharpness*, *weight*, *hardness* and *shape* — four properties, each permanently the sword's (3.7.28's sahaja sakti yavad-dravyam avasthita: the innate capacity standing as long as the substance). Asir yada chedyam prati vyapriyate: when the *sword* is *engaged toward* what *is to be cut*. Saktiman grhyate tada: *then* it is *apprehended* as *capacity-possessing*. The verse locates the apprehension precisely: not when the sword exists (it always has the four properties), and not after the cutting (that is too late), but when it is *engaged toward* — vyapriyate, in the middle of its being brought to bear. Capacity is apprehended in the engaging. And the four properties are named because they are what the engaging draws on: the karana-hood is not a fifth property alongside them but their being brought to bear on something.

Verse 3.7.32.translation: Some hold the means of the action to be produced beforehand from another occasioning ground; some hold it innate; others hold the action to be prior.
Verse 3.7.32.commentary: Three positions on the means' origin, catalogued. Prak nimittantara-udbhutam kriyayah kaiscid isyate: some hold the means *produced beforehand from another occasioning ground* — the sword's cutting-power was made by the smith; capacities have causal histories. Sahajam kaiscit: some hold it *innate* — the capacity is the thing's own, unmade (3.7.28's sahaja). Kriya anyaih purvam isyate: *others* hold the *action* to be *prior* — the capacity is constituted by the action rather than preceding it, so that the sword becomes an instrument by cutting. Three genuine options, and Bhartrhari does not choose. He does not need to: 3.7.28-31 have established that whatever the capacity's origin, it is *apprehended in the engaging*, and that is all the karaka-system requires. The origin-dispute is 3.1.107's substrate-dispute again — canvassed, tolerated, and shown not to touch the doctrine that depends on it.

Verse 3.7.33.translation: The operation itself is first, somewhere not resorting to anything at all; the capacity, in a single substratum, draws downward, like a current.
Verse 3.7.33.commentary: The third option of 3.7.32 developed, with an image. Pravrttir eva prathamam kvacid api anapasrita: the *operation itself* is *first*, somewhere *not resorting to* anything at all — the doing is primary, leaning on no prior capacity: sometimes there simply *is* a going, and the goer is read off it. Saktir eka-adhikarane sroto-vad apakarsati: the *capacity*, in a *single substratum*, *draws downward*, *like a current*. The image is exact: a river's current is not a thing in the water that makes it flow; it is the flowing, and it carries what is in it. So capacity is the operation's own momentum, drawing its bearer along — which inverts the ordinary picture (thing has power, power produces act) into: act runs, and what runs in it is called its power. The verse is the samuddesa's most process-metaphysical moment, and it is offered as one option among three.

Verse 3.7.34.translation: Some, in some fashion, call that entity of such a character the unprecedented, or the capacity of time, or action, or time itself.
Verse 3.7.34.commentary: The current of 3.7.33 named, and the names do not agree. Apurvam: the *unprecedented* — the Mimamsa's technical term for the unseen ritual potency that bridges rite and remote fruit (2.119's test-word). Kala-saktim va: or the *capacity of time* (1.3's kala-sakti, on which the six modifications rest). Kriyam va: or *action*. Kalam eva va: or *time itself*. Tam evam-laksanam bhavam kecid ahuh kathamcana: some, *in some fashion*, call *that entity of such a character* by these names. Kathamcana — 'in some fashion': the shrug is 2.171's (kaiscit kathamcid akhyatah, of the etymologists' derivations). Four schools, four names, one phenomenon: the drawing-onward that makes a sequence of events into an action. Bhartrhari records the disagreement and adds nothing, because 3.6.16 already ruled that where every candidate for a first term turns out to be another differentiated item, the reasonable course is to stop looking.

Verse 3.7.35.translation: For others, six capacities are eternal, endowed with difference and non-difference; for the accomplishing of action they stand established among objects, like universals.
Verse 3.7.35.commentary: The doctrine that will organize the rest of the samuddesa. Nityah sat saktayah anyesam: for *others*, *six* capacities are *eternal* — the six karakas (agent, object, instrument, recipient, source, locus) taken as permanent powers, not as occasional postings. Bheda-abheda-samanvitah: *endowed with difference and non-difference* — they are distinct from each other and not distinct from what has them (1.2's saktis, apthak and yet functioning prthaktvena iva). Kriya-samsiddhaye arthesu jativat samavasthitah: *for the accomplishing of action* they stand established *among objects*, *like universals*. The comparison to universals is the point: as cowhood stands in cows eternally and manifests in each (3.1.25-26's jati as self-manifesting power), so agenthood stands in things and manifests when they act. The six become a fixed ontological furniture — and the samuddesa will spend its remaining verses both using this scheme and undermining it.

Verse 3.7.36.translation: And by difference of substance, configuration and the rest, they are seen as though unlimited; but in the settling of the reality, one does not transgress the six capacities.
Verse 3.7.36.commentary: The six defended against their apparent multiplicity.
