Dravya-akaradi-bhedena tah ca aparimita iva drsyante: by *difference of substance*, *configuration* and the rest, they are seen *as though unlimited* — every instrument is a different instrument, every agent a different agent; the world's roles look innumerable. Iva: as though. Tattva-sayyam tu sat saktih na ativartate: but in the *settling of the reality*, one does *not transgress* the *six* capacities. The unlimited variety reduces to six modes. This is the ordinary structure of the work's reductions (2.250's one word carved by nimittas into unlimited meanings; 3.1.33's one satta carved by relata into all the universals) — and its application here yields the karaka-system's central claim: however many participants an event has, they occupy six slots, and the slots are exhaustive.

Verse 3.7.37.translation: From difference of occasioning ground, one and the same capacity is understood as differentiated; they call agency alone six-fold — it is the ground of that operation.
Verse 3.7.37.commentary: The reduction pressed one step further, and this is the samuddesa's deepest structural claim. Nimitta-bhedad eka eva bhinna saktih pratiyate: from *difference of occasioning ground*, *one and the same* capacity is understood as *differentiated* — the six are not six powers but one, carved by nimittas (2.250-251's mechanism exactly). And the identification: sodha kartrtvam eva ahuh: they call *agency alone* *six-fold*. Not: there are six capacities of which agency is one. Rather: there is *agency*, and it appears sixfold. Tat-pravrtteh nibandhanam: it is the *ground of that operation*. This completes 3.7.18 (in the mere completing, agency exists in every karaka whatever): if every karaka is an agent, then the six karakas are six modes of *one* thing, agency, differentiated by the occasions on which it is attended to. Instrument-hood is agency-under-a-nimitta. The whole architecture of Sanskrit syntax reduces to one power, sixfold-appearing.

Verse 3.7.38.translation: Whether in reality or in separation, that is spoken of as separate; the world has the word for its authority, and it is followed along by the science.
Verse 3.7.38.commentary: The methodological placement of 3.7.37's reduction. Tattve va vyatireke va vyatiriktam tad ucyate: *whether* in *reality* or in *separation* — whether the six are really distinct or only construed as distinct (3.7.15's undecided ontology) — that is *spoken of as separate*: language treats them as six regardless. And the ground of the treatment: sabda-pramanako lokah — the *world* has the *word* for its *authority*: people's ontology is their grammar (2.328's principle: what a word means is what is understood, and there is no other criterion). Sa sastrena anugamyate: *it* is *followed along by the science* — and the grammar follows the world, rather than correcting it (2.142's tat prajnah na vikalpayet; 3.6.25's lokah tatra anugamyate). So the six karakas are in the sastra because they are in the loka, and they are in the loka because that is how words work. The reduction to one agency (3.7.37) is true and changes nothing, because the science's business is the world's usage.

Verse 3.7.39.translation: But in the highest sense there is no oneness whose character is distinct from separateness; reality itself shines forth in the form of separateness and oneness.
Verse 3.7.39.commentary: The reduction of 3.7.37 given its metaphysical honesty. Paramarthe tu na ekatvam prthaktvad bhinna-laksanam: in the *highest sense* there is *no oneness* whose character is *distinct from separateness* — the 'one agency' of 3.7.37 is not a unity standing over against the six; oneness and separateness are not two conditions (3.6.26's finding: neither exists without the other, and in the highest sense the difference does not exist at all). Prthaktva-ekatva-rupena tattvam eva prakasate: *reality itself* *shines forth* in the *form of separateness and oneness*. So one must not read 3.7.37 as saying that the truth is one agency and the six are illusion. The truth shines as both — six, and one — and neither is the correction of the other. This is 3.2.12-13's paired tetralemmas applied to the karaka-system: everything true of it is true both ways, and the pair is the doctrine.

Verse 3.7.40-41.translation: (40) That separateness which is beyond doubt is not different from oneness; that oneness which is beyond doubt is not different from separateness. (41) Sky, earth, wind, sun, oceans, rivers, directions — these are portions of the inner-organ reality, standing outside.
Verse 3.7.40-41.commentary: (40) states the mutual non-difference with a symmetry that is itself the argument: yat prthaktvam asandigdham tad ekatvan na bhidyate — that *separateness* which is *beyond doubt* is *not different from* oneness; and the mirror. Whatever is indubitably many is not other than one, and whatever is indubitably one is not other than many. Then (41) delivers, without warning, one of the most remarkable sentences in the work: dyauh ksama vayur adityah sagarah saritah disah — *sky*, *earth*, *wind*, *sun*, *oceans*, *rivers*, *directions* — antah-karana-tattvasya bhagah bahir avasthitah: are *portions of the inner-organ reality*, *standing outside*. The cosmos is the inner organ's parts, externalized. This is 3.7.6's doctrine (the intellect's operation superimposed on objects) at maximum scale, and 3.6.23's declined option (a property of the inner organ shining forth outwardly) here affirmed without the hedge. The list is deliberately elemental — the things anyone would call most obviously *out there* — and they are named as the mind's own portions standing outside.
Verse 3.7.40-41.contested: 41's antah-karana-tattvasya bhagah admits a weaker reading ('portions of the reality that is [known by] the inner organ') and a stronger ('portions of the inner-organ-reality itself'). The stronger is the natural construal and coheres with 3.7.6 and 3.7.3; we adopt it while noting that the verse stands without argument here

Verse 3.7.42.translation: That very one stands, single, in the form of the division of time; for that portion, being without prior and posterior, is marked by another's form.
Verse 3.7.42.commentary: The cosmos of 3.7.41 given its temporal aspect. Kala-viccheda-rupena tad eva ekam avasthitam: *that very one* stands, *single*, in the *form of the division of time* — the same inner-organ reality that stands outside as sky and sun stands also as time's divisions. Sa hi apurvaparo bhagah: for that *portion* is *without prior and posterior* — the portion itself has no before and after (3.1.37's sequence being Being's own self, undivided; 3.2.8's no difference of time in the real). Para-rupena laksyate: it is *marked by another's form* — its temporal divisions come from elsewhere, as the mountain's sides come from the sun (3.6.12). So time's articulation is conferred on what has none, exactly as space's was (3.6.13's bhaga-prakalpana-sakti), and both articulations are of one thing which is the inner organ's own reality. The Kala-samuddesa, two samuddesas ahead, will take this up at length.

Verse 3.7.43.translation: For separation is seen even where there is no separation, when there is no syntactic connection; therefore the meaning of the case-ending is held to be other than the meanings of 'tree' and the rest.
Verse 3.7.43.commentary: The argument that case-meanings are distinct from stem-meanings. Drsto hi avyatireke 'pi vyatireko 'nvaye 'sati: *separation* is seen even where there is *no separation*, when there is *no syntactic connection* — one can distinguish things that are not in fact distinct, wherever the construction does not link them. Vrksadyarthan layas tasmad vibhakty-artho 'nya isyate: therefore the *meaning of the case-ending* is held to be *other than* the meanings of 'tree' and the rest. The reasoning: the stem 'vrksa' gives a tree; the ending gives a role; and since the role can vary while the tree stays fixed ('the tree', 'to the tree', 'from the tree'), the two meanings are separable — which the distributional method (2.12's anvaya-vyatireka) certifies. This is the grammatical basis for the whole samuddesa: karaka-meanings are borne by the endings (3.7.13), and here that is established by method rather than asserted.
Verse 3.7.43.contested: the transcription's vrksadyarthan layah is corrupt; we translate the evident sense (the meanings of 'tree' etc. being distinguished from the case-meaning) and flag the fault

Verse 3.7.44.translation: The karaka is the general; the seventh and the rest are the wombs of its differences: six, by the difference of what is called karman and so on — but the seventh is the difference of the remainder.
Verse 3.7.44.commentary: The system's formal architecture, and it is Panini's. Samanyam karakam: the *karaka* is the *general* — karaka is the genus, and the individual roles are its species (Panini 1.4.23: karake, the heading under which all the role-definitions fall). Tasya saptadya bheda-yonayah: the *seventh* and the rest are the *wombs of its differences* — the case-endings are what generate the distinctions. Sat karma-akhyadi-bhedena: *six*, by the difference of what is called *karman* and so on — the six karakas proper. Sesa-bhedas tu saptami: but the *seventh* is the *difference of the remainder*. This last is precise and it is Panini's own architecture: the genitive (sasthi) is not a karaka at all; it is the sesa, the *remainder* case (2.3.50: sasthi sese), used where no karaka-relation holds. So the system has six roles plus a residue-case for everything else — and the residue is where relation as such lives (3.3.3: the word-meaning connection understood through the sixth case; 2.197-198's genitive for relations whose action has withdrawn).

Verse 3.7.45.translation: The object is held to be three-fold: what is to be produced, what is to be modified, and what is to be reached. Among these, the karman is what is most desired; but another is constructed four-fold.
Verse 3.7.45.commentary: The object's classical taxonomy. Nirvartyam ca vikaryam ca prapyam ca iti tridha matam: the *object* is held to be *three-fold* — nirvartya, *what is to be produced* (the mat that the weaving makes: 3.7.7's janyamana karman, the object that does not exist until the act completes); vikarya, *what is to be modified* (the gold that the smith works: it exists and is changed); prapya, *what is to be reached* (the village one goes to: it exists and is unchanged). The three exhaust the ways an action can bear on an object. Tatra ipsitatamam karma: among these, the *karman* is *what is most desired* — Panini's own definition (1.4.49: kartur ipsitatamam karma, the object is what the agent most desires), and note that it is defined by the *agent's desire*, not by any feature of the thing (3.4.3's dravya defined by pronominal referability; 3.7.3's karakas grounded in buddhi). Caturdha anyat tu kalpitam: but *another* is constructed *four-fold* — a rival taxonomy, unnamed. The object's classification is doctrine-relative, as everything in this kanda has been.

Verse 3.7.46.translation: What is reached by way of indifference; what is not desired by the agent; what is not named by other designations; and whatever has another as its antecedent —
Verse 3.7.46.commentary: The object's residual categories, and they qualify 3.7.45's definition sharply. Panini defined the karman as ipsitatamam — *what is most desired* by the agent. But four classes of object are manifestly not desired. Audasinyena yat prapyam: what is reached by way of *indifference* — the grass one treads on while walking to the village (Panini's own example at 1.4.51's akathitam ca provisions); yat ca kartuh anipsitam: what is *not desired* by the agent — the poison one swallows unknowingly, the thief one meets on the road. Samjnantaraih anakhyatam: what is *not named by other designations* — the residue that no other karaka-term covers (the akathita, 'unstated', of Panini's rule). Yat ca api anya-purvakam: and whatever has *another as its antecedent*. So the definition by desire fails for most objects, and the grammar has had to build a residue-category to catch them. The verse is the samuddesa's honest report that its central karaka is defined by a criterion its own instances violate — and 3.7.3's answer stands ready: the karakas are buddhi-postings, and the postings need not answer to the definition.

Verse 3.7.47.translation: That for which a transforming material cause — whether existent or non-existent — is not resorted to: of that they declare the state of being what-is-to-be-produced.
Verse 3.7.47.commentary: The nirvartya defined by an absence. Sati va avidyamana va prakrtih parinamini: a *transforming material cause* — whether *existent or non-existent* (the clause is careful: it does not matter whether one holds the material really transforms) — yasya na asriyate: *for which* it is *not resorted to*. Tasya nirvartyatvam pracaksate: of *that* they declare the state of being *what-is-to-be-produced*. So the nirvartya-karman is the object in whose production no material is being attended to: 'he makes a mat' does not present the reeds; the mat simply comes to be. And the contrast is with vikarya (3.7.48), where the material *is* attended to. The distinction is thus not between two kinds of event but between two ways of construing one — whether the stuff is in view — which is 3.7.3's thesis (the karaka-practice is grounded in a state of the intellect) applied to the object's own taxonomy.

Verse 3.7.48.translation: But when the material cause is intended, it is what-is-to-be-modified; by some, otherwise. Both what-is-to-be-produced and what-is-to-be-modified have been exhibited as the object in the science.
Verse 3.7.48.commentary: The complement of 3.7.47, and the criterion is named. Prakrteh tu vivaksayam vikaryam: when the *material cause* is *intended*, it is *what-is-to-be-modified*. Vivaksa — intention: the samuddesa's constant (3.7.2's kvacit kimcit vivaksyate; 3.7.19's difference by intention). Whether an object is nirvartya or vikarya depends on whether the speaker means to bring its material into view: 'he makes an ornament' (nirvartya, the gold unattended) versus 'he works the gold into an ornament' (vikarya, the gold in view). Same goldsmith, same afternoon. Kaiscid anyatha: *by some*, *otherwise* — the criterion is disputed. Nirvartyam ca vikaryam ca karma sastre pradarsitam: both have been *exhibited as the object* in the *science* — Panini's system recognizes both, and it does not tell you which is which, because the assignment is the speaker's.

Verse 3.7.49.translation: What, being non-existent, comes to be; or what, being existent, is made manifest by the birth — that is the object established two-fold, as what-is-to-be-produced and what-is-to-be-modified.
Verse 3.7.49.commentary: The two-fold object given its metaphysical alternatives, and they are 3.3.43's dilemma. Yad asat jayate: what, *being non-existent*, *comes to be* — the asatkarya-vada option, the Vaisesika's: the mat was not, and now is. Sad va janmana yat prakasyate: or what, *being existent*, is *made manifest by the birth* — the satkarya-vada option, the Samkhya's: the mat was in the reeds and the weaving revealed it. Tan nirvartyam vikaryam ca karma dvedha vyavasthitam: *that* is the object established *two-fold*. The verse maps the grammar's two objects onto philosophy's two theories of causation — and note what this implies: the choice between satkarya and asatkarya, over which the schools fought for centuries, is here a choice of *construal*, available to any speaker, and Sanskrit has a case-assignment for each. The metaphysical dispute is a difference in vivaksa (3.7.48), and both parties speak grammatically.

Verse 3.7.50.translation: Something arises from the destruction of the material — like ash from wood; something by the arising of another quality — like the modification of gold and the rest.
Verse 3.7.50.commentary: The vikarya's two modes. Prakrty-uccheda-sambhutam kimcit: *something* arises from the *destruction of the material* — kastha-adi-bhasmavat, *like ash from wood*: the wood is gone, and the ash is what became of it; the material is consumed in the modification. Kimcid gunantara-utpattya: *something* by the *arising of another quality* — suvarnadi-vikaravat, *like the modification of gold*: the gold persists entire and takes a new shape (3.2.4's suvarnadi bhinnam svaih akaraih apayibhih: gold differentiated by its own perishing configurations, and the gold never other). So the vikarya splits: destructive modification, where the material does not survive, and qualitative modification, where it does. And the two examples are the tradition's standing pair — ash and gold — precisely because they mark the limit: at one end the material is annihilated, at the other it is untouched, and the grammar's single category vikarya covers both. What unites them is only that the material is in view (3.7.48), which is a fact about the speaker.

Verse 3.7.51.translation: Where an accomplishment of the particulars belonging to the action is not understood, either from seeing or from inference — that is called what-is-to-be-reached.
Verse 3.7.51.commentary: The third object, defined by a double absence. Kriya-gata-visesanam siddhih yatra na gamyate: where an *accomplishment* of the *particulars belonging to the action* is *not understood* — nothing about the object shows that the action happened to it. Darsanad anumanad va: either from *seeing* or from *inference* — neither observation nor reasoning finds a mark. Tat prapyam iti kathyate: *that* is called *what-is-to-be-reached*. The village one goes to is unchanged by the going; look at it and infer as you like, and no trace of the arrival is in it. So the prapya-karman is the object that the action leaves as it found it — which makes it, of the three, the one whose objecthood is *purely* a construal: the nirvartya at least comes to be, the vikarya at least changes, and the prapya does nothing at all except get reached. Its karman-hood is entirely the speaker's posting (3.7.3).

Verse 3.7.52.translation: Everywhere a gaining of particulars exists, through seeing and the rest; for some, the establishing of that manifestation is in the case of seeing, poison and so on.
Verse 3.7.52.commentary: An objection to 3.7.51, and it is a good one. Visesa-labhah sarvatra vidyate darsanadibhih: *everywhere* a *gaining of particulars* exists, through *seeing* and the rest — even the prapya *does* acquire something: being-seen, being-reached, being-arrived-at. Look hard enough and every object is marked by the action that took it. Kesamcit tad-abhivyakti-siddhih drsti-visadisu: for *some*, the *establishing of that manifestation* is in the case of *seeing*, *poison* and so on — where the object visibly registers the act (the poisoned man shows the poison; the seen thing is, on some accounts, altered by being seen). So the third category may collapse: if every object gains a particular, none is merely reached. The objection presses 3.7.51's double absence, and the next verse answers by naming what the prapya does gain.

Verse 3.7.53.translation: Coming-to-appearance, manifestation, and being-endured: these particulars of the object that is being reached stand established in the accomplishing of the action.
Verse 3.7.53.commentary: The answer to 3.7.52: the prapya's gains are named, and they are all relational. Abhasa-upagamah — *coming-to-appearance*: the object comes into view; vyaktih — *manifestation*: it becomes manifest; sodhatvam — *being-endured*, being-put-up-with, being-undergone. Iti karmanah visesah prapyamanasya kriya-siddhau vyavasthitah: these *particulars* of the object *that is being reached* stand established *in the accomplishing of the action*. Every one of the three is a fact about the object's standing-in-relation to the act, not about the object: coming-to-appearance is appearing to someone, manifestation is manifestation to a cognition, being-endured is enduring an action. So 3.7.52 is granted its datum and denied its conclusion: the prapya does gain particulars, and they are relational particulars, conferred in the act and belonging to no thing (3.6.14's things without place, parts, motion, support; 3.7.14's nimitta-bhava as a leaning).

Verse 3.7.54.translation: Among what-is-to-be-produced and the rest, having first experienced independence, the object comes to be, thereafter, in the operation of other agents.
Verse 3.7.54.commentary: The object's own agency, and this is 3.7.18's doctrine reaching the karman. Nirvartyadisu tat purvam anubhuya svatantratam: among the nirvartya and the rest, *having first experienced independence* — the object is *svatantra*, independent, before it is an object: the wood is doing its own thing, the village stands on its own. Svatantrya is Panini's own mark of the agent (1.4.54: svatantrah karta), and it is here predicated of the object in its prior condition. Kartrantaranam vyapare karma sampadyate tatah: in the *operation of other agents*, the object *comes to be*, *thereafter*. Objecthood is a demotion suffered when another agent's operation arrives (3.7.20's nyakkrta-saktayah: capacities subordinated by the agent). So the karman is an agent that has been overtaken. Every object in every sentence was independent a moment before the verb reached it, and its objecthood is the grammar's record of a subordination.

Verse 3.7.55.translation: Even in the separation from that operation, the object, standing in its own operation, obtains in some cases the rules taught in the science that rest upon it.
Verse 3.7.55.commentary: The object's retained agency, and it has grammatical consequences. Tad-vyapara-viveke 'pi: even in the *separation from that* operation — even when the other agent's action is withdrawn or not in view — sva-vyapare vyavasthitam: the object stands *in its own operation*: the wood goes on being wood, doing what wood does (3.7.20's sampurnaih svaih vyaparaih samanvitah: furnished with their own complete operations even when subordinated). Karma apadistan labhate kvacit sastrasrayan vidhin: the *object* obtains, in some cases, the *rules taught* in the science that *rest upon it*. Because the object retains its own agency, the grammar can address rules to it — and this is exactly what the karma-kartari construction does (the next verses' subject): 'the rice cooks itself', where the object's own operation surfaces and it takes the agent's endings. The doctrine that every karaka is an agent (3.7.18) is not a metaphysical flourish; it is what makes a whole class of Sanskrit constructions statable.

Verse 3.7.56.translation: The object, its being-directed having ceased, stands in a portion of its own action; the objecthood withdrawing, it stands in its own agency.
Verse 3.7.56.commentary: The karma-kartari construction analysed, and the analysis is exact. Nivrtta-presanam karma: the *object*, its *being-directed having ceased* — presana: the sending, the directing by another agent; when that withdraws — sva-kriya-avayave sthitam: it *stands in a portion of its own action*. Nivartamane karmatve sve kartrtve 'vatisthate: the *objecthood withdrawing*, it stands *in its own agency*. So 'pacyate odanah' — 'the rice cooks' — is not a passive with a suppressed agent; it is the object having reverted to the agency it always had (3.7.54's svatantrata, experienced first) once the cook is out of view. The construction's morphology (the passive endings with an active sense) records exactly this: objecthood withdrawn, agency resumed. And the doctrine that makes it intelligible has been in place since 3.7.18: agency was never the subject's privilege; it was every karaka's, differently attended to.

Verse 3.7.57.translation: Those they know as simply other roots — like 'pac' and 'sidhyati'; though there is difference, from likeness of form there is a construction of oneness.
Verse 3.7.57.commentary: A deflationary account of the karma-kartari's morphology. Tani dhatv-antarani eva viduh: those they know as *simply other roots* — the 'cooks' of 'the cook cooks' and the 'cooks' of 'the rice cooks' are two verbs, not one verb in two constructions. Paci-sidhyati-vat: *like* 'pac' (to cook, transitive) and 'sidhyati' (to be accomplished, intransitive) — two roots that mean nearly the same and are undeniably two. Bhede 'pi tulya-rupatvat ekatva-parikalpana: though there is *difference*, from *likeness of form* there is a *construction of oneness*. We take them for one verb because they sound alike (2.169's kupa-supa-yupa: phonetic overlap is semantically inert; 2.177's rule: different meaning-behaviour, different base). So the elegant analysis of 3.7.56 is offered a rival: there is no reversion of agency, just two verbs that rhyme. Bhartrhari reports it without deciding — and the report is itself instructive, since the two accounts are 2.57's two darsanas (whole-first or parts-first) meeting in a single conjugation.

Verse 3.7.58.translation: In a single portion and in an aggregate of operations, 'pac' and the rest proceed by their own nature, endowed with like form.
Verse 3.7.58.commentary: The rival account of 3.7.57 developed. Eka-dese samuhe ca vyaparanam pacadayah: in a *single portion* and in an *aggregate* of operations — whether the verb names one act or a complex of them — svabhavatah pravartante: they proceed *by their own nature*: the roots simply do what they do, without a scheme of role-assignments organizing them. Tulya-rupa-samanvitah: *endowed with like form*. So on this view the verb-family's unity is entirely superficial, and its members are separate items that happen to share a shape and a rough sense. The account is the atomist's (2.52's nominalism: word and sentence as mere designations upon order), and its virtue is economy: no reverting agency, no subordination-hierarchy, just roots. Its cost is that it explains nothing about why the forms cluster as they do — which is why 3.7.59-60 return to the structural analysis.

Verse 3.7.59-60.translation: (59) Subordinating and being-subordinated are understood in the pure root; subordinating and being-subordinated are found in the causative as well. (60) They call it the fifth state; in the causative, in the object-as-agent — when the directing has ceased, the causative affix is stated of the root in its natural meaning.
Verse 3.7.59-60.commentary: The structure of 3.7.20-21 found at every morphological level. (59): nyag-bhavana nyag-bhavanam rudhau suddhe pratiyate — *subordinating* and *being-subordinated* are understood in the *pure root*: even the simple verb contains the hierarchy — something subordinates, something is subordinated (nyak: down, the same root as 3.7.20's nyakkrta). And: nyanta 'pi pratipadyate — they are found in the *causative* as well: the nic-affix adds a level and the same structure repeats. So subordination is not a feature the causative introduces; it is present in the bare root and iterated by the affix (3.7.25's karta kartr-antara-apeksah: an agent depending on another agent). (60): avastham pancamim ahuh nyante tam karma-kartari — they call it the *fifth state*, in the *causative*, in the *object-as-agent*; nivrtta-presanad dhatoh prakrte 'rthe nij ucyate: when the *directing has ceased*, the causative affix is stated of the root *in its natural meaning*. The verse tracks a construction where the causative morphology appears without causative sense, because the directing (3.7.56's presana) has withdrawn — the morphology outliving its semantics, which is 2.229-231's ledger-shuffling in the verb's own paradigm.
Verse 3.7.59-60.contested: 60's avastham pancamim ('the fifth state') is not further identified and the commentators differ over what series it numbers; we translate the phrase and leave the enumeration open

Verse 3.7.61.translation: 'Sidhyati' states the meaning of 'pacati' only with the causative affix; that causative-ending stands in the natural meaning of 'pacati'.
Verse 3.7.61.commentary: The two roots of 3.7.57 shown to be related after all, and by a morphological argument. Braviti pacater artham sidhyatir na vina nijam: 'sidhyati' (is accomplished) states the meaning of 'pacati' (cooks) *only with the causative affix* — sadhayati, the causative of sidh, means what pacati means: one has to add nic to get from the intransitive to the transitive sense. Sa nyantah pacater artho prakrte vyavatisthate: *that causative-ending* stands in the *natural* meaning of 'pacati' — what the one root needs a causative to say, the other says in its bare form. So pac contains, in its natural meaning, what sidh requires an affix to reach: transitivity is built into pac and added to sidh. This is 3.7.59's doctrine (subordinating and being-subordinated present in the pure root) given its evidence — the hierarchy is inside pac already, which is why it needs no nic, and its being inside is visible precisely by comparison with a root that lacks it.

Verse 3.7.62.translation: For some, that operation of Devadatta and the rest which is in a transitive verb — that same is intended, without Devadatta and the rest, in mats and the like.
Verse 3.7.62.commentary: The karma-kartari's semantics restated. Kesamcid devadattader vyaparo yah sakarmake: for some, that *operation* of Devadatta and the rest which is in a *transitive* verb — the weaving that Devadatta does. Sa vina devadattadeh katadisu vivaksyate: *that same* is *intended*, *without* Devadatta and the rest, in *mats* and the like. 'The mat weaves itself' intends the very operation that 'Devadatta weaves the mat' intends — the same weaving, with the weaver simply not meant (3.7.56's nivrtta-presana: the directing having ceased). Vivaksyate: *is intended* — the agent's absence is a fact about the speaker's aim, not about the event. So the construction subtracts nothing from the action; it withdraws attention from one of its participants, and the mat, which was an agent all along (3.7.54's svatantrata experienced first), surfaces.

Verse 3.7.63.translation: The object, its directing having ceased, is the occasioner of its own agent; when there is connection with another directing, in the causative it is expressed by the 'la'-affix.
Verse 3.7.63.commentary: The construction's morphology tracked through a further level. Nivrtta-presanam karma svasya kartuh prayojakam: the *object*, its directing having ceased, is the *occasioner of its own agent* — the mat, freed from the weaver, occasions its own agency (3.7.56 verbatim, and prayojaka is 3.1.25's word for the universal deploying its causes). Presanantara-sambandhe nyante lena abhidhiyate: when there is connection with *another* directing — a second agent arriving above the first — in the *causative* it is expressed by the *la*-affix: the personal endings (Panini's lakaras) carry it. So the sequence is: object directed (accusative), directing withdrawn (object becomes agent), new directing added (causative, and the la-affix marks the new arrangement). Each level of subordination is a morphological layer, and the layers stack exactly as 3.7.59's nyag-bhavana predicted. The grammar is doing hierarchy-bookkeeping, and the hierarchy is 3.7.22's king and soldiers.

Verse 3.7.64.translation: Where an object attains agency in 'sadrsa' and the like — there, in the bringing-about of a state, objecthood belongs to the counter-action.
Verse 3.7.64.commentary: A further case, and it is technical. Sadrsadisu yat karma kartrtvam pratipadyate: where an *object* attains *agency* in 'sadrsa' (resembling) and the like — verbs of resembling, of becoming-like, where what is compared behaves now as object, now as agent. Apatty-apadane tatra visayatvam pratikriye: there, in the *bringing-about of a state* (apatti: coming-to-be-such), *objecthood* belongs to the *counter-action*. Where the construction expresses a becoming-like, the objecthood shifts to the reciprocal act — resembling being a relation each relatum bears to the other (3.7.19's parents, both agents, roles assigned by intention). The verse extends the karma-kartari analysis to a family of verbs where the agent/object assignment is symmetrical in the world and asymmetrical in every sentence — which is the samuddesa's constant finding since 3.7.19.

Verse 3.7.65.translation: And when a word is fetched from somewhere and so construed, there would be object-located-state-hood for the expressers of seeing and the rest.
Verse 3.7.65.commentary: A construal-dependent classification. Kutascid ahrtya padam evam ca parikalpane: when a *word* is *fetched from somewhere* and *so construed* — supplied from context, imported into the analysis (2.327's unuttered word supplied by the cognition). Karma-stha-bhavakatvam syad darsanady-abhidhayinam: there would be *object-located-state-hood* for the expressers of *seeing* and the rest. Karma-stha-bhavaka is the grammarians' term for verbs whose state resides in the object rather than the agent: 'he sees the pot' — where does the seeing's result lodge? In the pot (which becomes seen) or in the seer? The classification decides how the verb's affixes behave. And the verse's point is that the classification follows the *construal*: fetch a word from context, construe accordingly, and the verb changes class. Nothing in seeing settles where seeing lodges (3.7.3's buddhi-grounding, at the level of verb-taxonomy).

Verse 3.7.66.translation: Where the seeing of a particular is, there the action stands settled; but for others, the settling of the action is made manifest by words alone.
Verse 3.7.66.commentary: Two criteria for locating an action. Visesa-darsanam yatra kriya tatra vyavasthita: where the *seeing of a particular* is, *there* the action stands settled — locate the action where its result shows: the cooking is in the rice, because the rice softens; the difference is visible, so the action is there (3.7.51's criterion for the prapya, run in reverse). Kriya-vyavastha tu anyesam sabdaih eva prakasyate: but for *others*, the settling of the action is made manifest *by words alone* — the action is where the grammar says it is, and no observation is relevant. Two schools: one locates actions by their visible effects, the other by the sastra's assignments. And the samuddesa has been arguing throughout that the second is right (3.7.13: the assistance is understood by the case-endings alone) — not because words are authoritative over the world but because the location of an action is not a fact the world contains (3.7.8's nirihesu api bhavesu kalpana-upanibandhanam: even among the striving-less, it rests on construction).

Verse 3.7.67.translation: For time, state, road and place, in connection with all intransitive verbs having other actions included within them, objecthood arises.
Verse 3.7.67.commentary: How intransitive verbs acquire objects. Kala-bhava-adhva-desanam: for *time*, *state*, *road* and *place* — the four that Panini's rule (1.4.51's kala-adhvanor atyanta-samyoge and its kin) makes accusative even with intransitives: 'he sleeps a month', 'he goes a league'. Antarbhuta-kriyantaraih sarvaih akarmakaih yoge: in connection with *all intransitive* verbs having *other actions included within them* — antarbhuta: absorbed, included (3.3.47's antarbhava, the self-bearing of 'asti'). Karmatvam upajayate: *objecthood arises*. So 'he sleeps a month' has an object because the sleeping has an occupying-of-time included in it, and the month is that included action's object. The intransitive verb is not really intransitive: it contains a second action, and the accusative belongs to that one. The analysis is elegant and it is the samuddesa's method throughout — where the grammar assigns a role that seems unearned, find the included operation that earns it.

Verse 3.7.68.translation: Having attained, as it were, the state of being a locus, those — time and the rest — again, in the case of substance-objects, attain a subsequent objecthood of a different level.
Verse 3.7.68.commentary: The levels of 3.7.67 sorted. Adharatvam iva praptah te: having attained, *as it were*, the state of being a *locus* — time and place naturally present as loci ('he sleeps *in* the night', locative), and the iva marks that this is their default construal. Punah dravya-karmasu: *again*, in the case of *substance-objects* — where the verb already has a substance for its object ('he studies the book a month'). Kaladayah bhinna-kaksyam yanti karmatvam uttaram: time and the rest attain a *subsequent objecthood* of a *different level* — bhinna-kaksya: of another compartment, another tier. So a sentence can carry two objects at different levels: the book at one, the month at another, and they do not compete because they are objects of different included actions (3.7.67's antarbhuta-kriya). Kaksya — level, compartment — is the samuddesa's term for the hierarchy 3.7.20-23 built, and here it does taxonomic work: objecthood is stratified, and the strata do not collide.

Verse 3.7.69.translation: Therefore a root joined with those objects, which are not substances, is intransitive; it serves as the occasioning ground for the 'la'-affix in the object and in the state.
Verse 3.7.69.commentary: The consequence for morphology. Atah taih karmabhih dhatur yuktah adravyaih akarmakah: *therefore* a root joined with *those objects* — which are *not substances* (time, place, road, state: 3.7.67's four) — is *intransitive*. The verb has an accusative and is still akarmaka, because its accusative is not a substance-object. Lasya karmani bhave ca nimittatvaya kalpate: it serves as the *occasioning ground* for the *la*-affix *in the object and in the state* — that is, it takes the passive/impersonal endings, which is what intransitives do. So the grammar's classification tracks *substance*-objecthood, not accusatives: a verb with a temporal accusative counts as intransitive for affixation. The verse is fine technical bookkeeping and its interest is that the category 'transitive' turns out not to mean 'takes an accusative' — which is exactly what one expects if the karakas are postings assigned per operation (3.7.12) rather than features read off surface case.

Verse 3.7.70.translation: Or every unstated object is understood as of a different level; by difference in the indication of the root's meaning, that is not what is most desired.
Verse 3.7.70.commentary: The akathita placed. Sarvam va akathitam karma bhinna-kaksyam pratiyate: *every unstated object* is understood as of a *different level* — akathita: 'not stated', Panini's own term (1.4.51: akathitam ca — 'and what is unstated' is called karman), the residue-category that catches every object no other rule assigns. Bhinna-kaksyam: they belong to another tier (3.7.68's stratification). Dhatv-artha-uddesa-bhedena tan na ipsitatamam kila: by difference in the *indication of the root's meaning*, *that* is *not what is most desired* — kila: 'so they say', the reportive particle carrying a shade of irony. So the akathita objects fail Panini's own definition of karman (3.7.45's ipsitatamam, what is most desired), and the grammar catches them in a residue-rule anyway. The verse's kila registers the awkwardness: the definition is by desire, most objects are not desired, and the system works by a catch-all. 3.7.46 already listed the undesired classes; here their status is named.

Verse 3.7.71.translation: The principal object is what is stated, which is the occasioner of the action; what is joined with the action for the accomplishing of that is remembered as unstated.
Verse 3.7.71.commentary: The two objects distinguished by office. Pradhana-karma kathitam: the *principal object* is what is *stated* — yat kriyayah prayojakam: which is the *occasioner of the action*: the object the action is *for*, whose desirability drives the whole undertaking (3.7.45's ipsitatama, now confined to this one). Tat-siddhaye kriya-yuktam anyat tu akathitam smrtam: what is joined with the action *for the accomplishing of that* is remembered as *unstated*. So in 'he milks the cow of milk' (the classic double-accusative), the milk is the principal — it is what he wants — and the cow is akathita: joined to the action in service of getting the milk. The distinction is teleological through and through: which object is principal depends on which is wanted, and the other is machinery. This is 2.113's prayojana-doctrine (sentence-meaning is purpose) reaching down into the accusative case.

Verse 3.7.72.translation: In 'nayati' and the like, as in 'duhi' and the rest, objecthood rests on what is not stated; but when the finite verb is not employed, the remainder is held to obtain, by rule.
Verse 3.7.72.commentary: The double-object verbs, and their two families. Duhy-adi-vat nayaty-adau karmatvam akatha-srayam: in *'nayati'* (leads) and the like, as in *'duhi'* (milks) and the rest, *objecthood* rests on *what is not stated* — both families take two accusatives, and in both the second is akathita (3.7.70-71). Akhyata-anupayoge tu niyamat sesa isyate: but when the *finite verb is not employed* — in nominal constructions, participles, compounds, where no finite verb governs — *the remainder* is held to obtain, *by rule*: the genitive, Panini's sesa-case (3.7.44: sasthi sese), takes over. So 'he milks the cow of milk' has two accusatives, and 'the milking of the cow' has a genitive: withdraw the finite verb and the karaka-relations lapse into the residue-case. This is 2.197-198's doctrine exactly — the genitive expresses relations whose generating action has withdrawn — now shown operating in the double-object constructions.

Verse 3.7.73.translation: For 'duhi' and the rest, whose causative-meaning is included within, objecthood is established by the prior rule as though they ended in the causative; and the restriction is likewise as for causative-ending forms.
Verse 3.7.73.commentary: The double-object verbs explained by a hidden causative. Antarbhuta-nij-arthanam duhy-adinam: of *'duhi'* and the rest, whose *causative-meaning is included within* — antarbhuta again (3.7.67's included actions): 'milks' contains a 'causes-to-flow'; the causative is in the root's own meaning without any affix. Nijantavat siddham purvena karmatvam: *objecthood* is *established by the prior rule* *as though they ended in the causative* — the grammar treats them like causatives because they are causatives semantically, and causatives take two objects (the one caused, and the thing done). Nijantam niyamas tatha: and the *restriction* is likewise as for causative-ending forms. So the double accusative of 'duhi' is not an idiosyncrasy: it is the ordinary causative pattern, with the causative buried in the root. This completes 3.7.61's finding (pac contains what sidh needs a nic to say) — the roots' meanings carry morphology invisibly, and the case-assignments reveal it.

Verse 3.7.74.translation: As for the instrument, in its own compartment, there is no resort to excellence — so for the object too, in its own compartment, there would be no surpassing.
Verse 3.7.74.commentary: An objection to the ipsitatama-definition, from the grammar's own logic. Karanasya sva-kaksyayam na prakarsa-asrayo yatha: as for the *instrument*, in its *own compartment*, there is no resort to *excellence* — one instrument is not more an instrument than another; the karaka-roles do not come in degrees. Karmano 'pi sva-kaksyayam na syad atisayas tatha: so for the *object* too, in its own compartment, there would be *no surpassing*. But Panini's definition is ipsitatamam — the *most* desired: a superlative, which requires degrees. So the definition demands a gradation the category cannot support: if objecthood does not admit of more and less, 'most desired' picks out nothing. The objection is sharp, and it targets the definition that 3.7.45, 3.7.70 and 3.7.71 have all leaned on. The answer follows.

Verse 3.7.75.translation: But since, for the object, when the state of being wished-to-be-attained is resorted to, a surpassing is present — therefore it is resorted to; hence there is an utter difference from the prior object.
Verse 3.7.75.commentary: The answer to 3.7.74, and it relocates the gradation. Karmanas tu aptum istatve asrite atisayo yatah: for the *object*, when the state of *being wished-to-be-attained* is *resorted to*, a *surpassing* is *present* — the degrees are not in objecthood but in *desiredness*: one may want the milk more than the cow, and that is a real gradation. Asriyate tatah: *therefore* it is resorted to — the definition invokes desire precisely because desire admits of degrees where objecthood does not. Atyantam bhedah purvena karmana: hence there is an *utter difference* from the *prior* object — the ipsitatama (the milk) differs in kind from the akathita (the cow), because the first is wanted most and the second is machinery (3.7.71). So 3.7.74's objection is granted and disarmed: objecthood has no degrees, desire does, and the superlative in Panini's definition qualifies the desire. The definition survives by being read exactly.

Verse 3.7.76.translation: And as, in a causative-ending form, an agent possessing action is employed — so in 'nayati' and the like, an agent devoid of action is also employed.
Verse 3.7.76.commentary: The parallel completed, and its second half is the odd one. Nijante ca yatha karta sakriyah san prayujyate: in a *causative-ending* form, an agent *possessing action* is employed — in 'he makes Devadatta cook', Devadatta is an agent and he is cooking: the caused agent acts. Nayaty-adau tatha karta niskriyo 'pi prayujyate: in *'nayati'* (leads) and the like, an agent *devoid of action* is *also* employed — 'he leads the goat to the village': the goat is grammatically an agent of a sort (it is the one led, and the leading is its going), and it does nothing; it is dragged. Niskriya: without action (3.7.8's niriha, without striving). So the pattern that 3.7.73 established — the double-object verbs behaving as causatives — extends to cases where the 'caused agent' has no agency whatever. The grammar constructs an agent out of the inert, exactly as 3.7.8 said it does: independence and dependence rest upon construction, even among things that are without striving.

Verse 3.7.77.translation: But the analytic sentence which is used for a causative-ending form, and that which is used for the base of 'ni' and 'duh' — from its being another word, there is no contact of it with the root.
Verse 3.7.77.commentary: The paraphrase disowned. Bheda-vakyam tu yat nyante: the *analytic sentence* used for a *causative-ending* form — the gloss 'he causes X to cook' by which the grammar explains 'pacayati'; ni-duhita-prakrtau ca yat: and that used for the *base* of 'ni' and 'duh' (the double-object verbs of 3.7.72-73). Sabdantaratvat na eva asti samsparsah tasya dhatuna: *from its being another word*, there is *no contact* of it with the *root*. The paraphrase is a different word entirely and touches the root not at all. This is 2.35cd's doctrine (the vrtti-analysis employing a verb-like element for what stands otherwise, the paraphrase faithful to the meaning and false to the form) and 2.37's (the resemblance constructed for the sake of reaching the meaning). So the causative analyses of 3.7.61-76, however illuminating, are glosses: they explain by substituting, and what they substitute has no contact with what it explains. The samuddesa audits its own method at the point where it has leaned hardest on it.

Verse 3.7.78.translation: Just as one and the same ablative is exhibited in the science by way of difference — so one and the same object too is expounded by way of difference.
Verse 3.7.78.commentary: The taxonomies placed. Yatha eva ekam apadanam sastre bhedena darsitam: as *one and the same* ablative is exhibited in the *science* by way of *difference* — Panini divides apadana into kinds (the moving source, the fixed source, the source of fear, the source of learning) and it is one karaka throughout. Tatha ekam eva karma api bhedena pratipaditam: so *one and the same* object too is *expounded* by way of *difference* — the nirvartya, vikarya and prapya of 3.7.45 are one karman, divided for exposition. So the object's three-fold taxonomy is a teaching-device, not a discovery of three objecthoods (2.176's instruction by the general for brevity's sake; 3.7.36's unlimited-seeming capacities reducing to six). The verse tidies the samuddesa's own apparatus: everything it has distinguished about the karman is distinction *in the science*, and the karman is one.

Verse 3.7.79.translation: Whether what-is-to-be-produced, or what-is-to-be-modified, or what-is-to-be-reached — the substratum of the means, since actions alone are what is to be accomplished, is expressed as having an accomplished form.
Verse 3.7.79.commentary: The object's grammatical form explained. Nirvartyo va vikaryo va prapyo va sadhana-asrayah: whatever kind it is, the object is the *substratum of the means*. Kriyanam eva sadhyatvat: since *actions alone* are *what is to be accomplished* — only actions are sadhya; nothing else is ever brought about (3.7.1's definition: the means is capacity in the completing of *actions*). Siddha-rupo 'bhidhiyate: it is expressed as having an *accomplished* form. So the object, however unaccomplished it may be in fact (the mat not yet woven, 3.7.7's janyamana karman), is grammatically *siddha* — presented as a standing thing, a noun, complete. The sadhya/siddha distinction is the grammar's deepest: verbs present the to-be-accomplished, nouns the accomplished, and every participant in an action is nominally presented as though finished. This is why 3.7.7 needed buddhi to hold the unmade mat in place: the grammar has no way to present an object as unfinished.

Verse 3.7.80.translation: As, in the case of things not conducive, a desire of the agent arises from greed — so, in the case of poison and the like, it proceeds from fear and the rest.
Verse 3.7.80.commentary: The ipsitatama-definition rescued at its hardest case. 3.7.46 listed the undesired objects, and poison is the standing example: 'he eats poison' has an object that nobody most-desires. The answer: ahitesu yatha laulyat kartur iccha upajayate — in the case of things *not conducive*, a *desire* of the agent arises *from greed*: the glutton eats what harms him, and there *is* a desire — a bad one. Visadisu bhayadibhyah tatha eva asau pravartate: in the case of *poison* and the like, *that* (desire) proceeds *from fear* and the rest. The suicide's desire for poison comes from fear; the desire is real, its source is dread. So the undesired objects are desired after all, under desires the agent would disown — and the definition holds, at the cost of making 'most desired' cover what one takes in terror. It is an honest rescue: it saves the rule and shows what the rule then means.

Verse 3.7.81-82.translation: (81) Where the capacity of a substance, in the principal and the other, is separately in the two actions — there the capacity resting on the subordinate follows the principal. (82) The capacity whose domain is the principal is expressed by the affix; when it is in the subordinate, then, though unstated, it likewise shines forth.
Verse 3.7.81-82.commentary: How karaka-roles propagate between clauses. (81): where a substance has a capacity in *two* actions — one principal, one subordinate — guna-asraya saktih pradhanam anurudhyate: the capacity resting on the *subordinate* *follows the principal*: the role assigned in the main clause governs the role in the subordinate one. (82) gives the morphology: pradhana-visaya saktih pratyayena abhidhiyate — the capacity whose domain is the *principal* is *expressed by the affix* (the finite verb's endings carry it); yada gune tada tadvat anukta api prakasate — when it is in the *subordinate*, then, *though unstated*, it *likewise shines forth*: the subordinate clause's role is not expressed and is understood, carried over from the principal. This is why 'having cooked, he eats' needs no agent for the cooking: the eater's agency propagates backward. The mechanism is 2.305's anupatta (what is not taken up by the word is nonetheless understood) operating across clause-boundaries.

Verse 3.7.83.translation: The object not stated for 'paci', in a gerund expressing the state — for 'bhuji', though another capacity is stated, that property of it shines forth.
Verse 3.7.83.commentary: The propagation of 3.7.81-82 in a worked case. Pacau anuktam yat karma: the *object not stated* for 'paci' (cooks) — in 'pakva bhunkte', 'having cooked, he eats', the cooking's object is not said. Ktvante bhava-abhidhayini: in a *gerund* expressing the *state* — the ktva-affix, which forms the gerund and expresses the action without an agent of its own. Bhujau sakty-antare 'pi ukte: for 'bhuji' (eats), *though another capacity is stated* — the eating has its own object, said. Tat tad-dharmah prakasate: *that property* of it *shines forth* — the unstated cooking-object is understood as the same as the stated eating-object. One cooks what one eats, and no word says so. The verse shows the propagation working across a difference of role (the rice is the cooking's nirvartya and the eating's prapya) — and it works because the roles ride on the buddhi's holding of the situation (3.7.3), not on the words.

Verse 3.7.84.translation: And the 'la'-affix which is prescribed for 'isi', from its contact with 'gami', in 'the village' — there, by 'usi' alone, a separation of the going-object is effected.
Verse 3.7.84.commentary: A worked case of case-assignment under verb-contact. The construction is 'gramam icchati' — 'he wants the village', meaning he wants to *go* to the village: iseh gami-samsparsat, from *'isi'* (wanting) having *contact with* *'gami'* (going) — the wanting has a going absorbed in it (3.7.67's antarbhuta-kriyantara), and the village is the *going's* object, taking the accusative that 'icchati' alone could not license. Tatra usina eva nirbhogah kriyate gati-karmanah: there, by *'usi'* alone, a *separation* of the *going-object* is effected. The technical detail is obscure in transmission; the principle is not. When two actions fuse in one verb, the case-assignments come from the buried one — which is 3.7.73's finding about 'duhi' (the double accusative coming from an included causative) applied to a different fusion.
Verse 3.7.84.contested: the verse's technical apparatus (the la-affix, usi, nirbhoga) is compressed to the edge of recoverability and the transcription may be at fault; we translate the evident structure and note that the specific Paninian derivation intended is not confidently reconstructible

Verse 3.7.85.translation: In 'having cooked, it is eaten' — for some, 'pacati' does not require the rice; that one is understood by inference.
Verse 3.7.85.commentary: The propagation of 3.7.83 disputed. Paktva bhujyate iti atra: in 'having cooked, it is eaten'. Kesamcid na vyapeksate odanam pacatih: for *some*, 'pacati' does *not require* the rice — the cooking-verb has no unstated object waiting to be supplied; it is simply a cooking. So 'apeksa', the expectancy that 3.7.83 relied on, is denied. Sa asau anumanat pratiyate: *that one* is understood *by inference* — the rice arrives, but by inference from the situation rather than by any grammatical expectancy. The difference matters: on 3.7.83's account the object is *carried over* by the construction; on this account it is *inferred* by the hearer, and the construction is silent. This is 2.149's mechanism (the corrupt form conveying by inference, through a recovery so practiced it vanishes) offered as a rival to grammatical propagation — and the two are hard to distinguish, since both deliver the rice and neither shows its working.

Verse 3.7.86.translation: There, for 'abhi-nivis', the object which is expressed in the finite verb — in the gerund, even where there is locus-hood, they do not desire the locative there.
Verse 3.7.86.commentary: A case where the propagation is grammatically visible. The verb abhi-nivis (to enter into, to settle upon) takes its object in the accusative by a special rule of Panini's (1.4.47: abhinivisas ca — the locus of abhinivis is called karman, not adhikarana). Tatra abhinivisau karma yat tinante 'bhidhiyate: the *object* which is expressed in the *finite verb*. Ktvante adhikaranatve 'pi na tatra icchanti saptamim: in the *gerund*, *even where there is locus-hood*, they do *not desire the locative* there. So the special accusative propagates into the gerund: even though the thing entered is by nature a locus, and gerunds might be expected to revert to the natural case, the rule's assignment carries across. The verse is evidence for 3.7.81-82's propagation-doctrine against 3.7.85's inference-account: here the carried-over role is *marked*, by the refusal of a case the situation would otherwise take.

Verse 3.7.87.translation: That object whose substratum is accomplished, but which is not yet augmented by the attaining — being differentiated by the coming-about of the state of being edible and the like — that is what is desired.
Verse 3.7.87.commentary: The ipsitatama located with precision. Yan nirvrtta-asrayam karma: that object whose *substratum is accomplished* — the thing is there; the rice exists. Prapteh apracitam punah: but which is *not yet augmented by the attaining* — it has not yet been got; the eating has not happened. Bhaksyadi-visaya-apattya bhidyamanam: being *differentiated* by the *coming-about* of the state of being *edible* and the like — apatti: coming-to-be-such (3.7.64's term). Tad ipsitam: *that* is what is *desired*. So the desired object is the existing thing not yet attained, taken under the aspect it will have when attained: the rice, not yet eaten, wanted *as edible*. Every clause is doing work: existing (else there is nothing to want), unattained (else wanting is idle: 3.3.63-64's already-pregnant woman), and aspectually differentiated (one wants it *as* something). The definition by desire, which 3.7.74 attacked and 3.7.75 rescued, is here given its exact content.

Verse 3.7.88.translation: From the root's operating in another meaning; from the object's being included within by the root's meaning; from established usage; from non-intention — the action becomes objectless.
Verse 3.7.88.commentary: The four ways a transitive verb goes intransitive, and they are a complete taxonomy. Dhatoh arthantare vrtteh: from the *root's operating in another meaning* — the verb used in a sense that has no object ('he sees' meaning he understands). Dhatv-arthena upasangrahat karmanah: from the *object's being included within* by the root's meaning — the object absorbed into the verb, so that nothing is left to state (3.3.47's antarbhava: the self-bearing of 'asti', whence it takes no accusative). Prasiddheh: from *established usage* — the verb simply is not used with an object, whatever its sense allows (2.253's prasiddhi: currency deciding). Avivaksatah: from *non-intention* — the speaker does not mean to bring an object into view, and there is none (3.7.62's vina devadattadeh: the agent unmeant). Karmano akarmika kriya: the action becomes *objectless*. Four routes — semantic, absorptive, conventional, intentional — and only the first two are about the verb. The other two are about usage and the speaker, which is the samuddesa's constant finding.

Verse 3.7.89.translation: These four differences that have been exhibited in general are again differentiated manifoldly, by difference of occasioning ground and the rest.
Verse 3.7.89.commentary: The taxonomy's status. Bhedah ya ete catvarah samanyena pradarsitah: these *four differences* that have been exhibited *in general* — 3.7.88's four routes to intransitivity. Te nimittadi-bhedena bhidyante bahudha punah: they are *again* *differentiated manifoldly*, by *difference of occasioning ground* and the rest. The four are not a bottom; each subdivides by nimitta, and the subdivision has no evident end (3.7.36's aparimita iva: seen as though unlimited, before the reduction to six). So the samuddesa reports its own taxonomies as provisional — general exhibitions that further analysis will multiply — which is 3.4.1's charter (kecit padarthah vaksyante samksepena yatha-agamam: certain word-meanings, in brief, according to tradition) honoured in the working.

Verse 3.7.90.translation: When the complete accomplishment of the action is intended as immediately following upon whose operation — then instrument-hood is remembered there.
Verse 3.7.90.commentary: The instrument defined, and the definition is temporal. Kriyayah parinispattih yad-vyaparad anantaram: the *complete accomplishment* of the action *immediately following* upon whose *operation* — the karana is the participant whose contribution is the *last* one, after which the act is done: the axe's stroke, and the tree is felled. Vivaksyate yada: when it is *intended* so. Tatra karanatvam tada smrtam: *then* instrument-hood is remembered *there*. This is Panini's own definition (1.4.42: sadhakatamam karanam, the most effective means) recast: what makes something 'most effective' is that the accomplishment follows immediately on it. And vivaksyate is the samuddesa's constant: the immediacy is a matter of what the speaker construes as immediately-prior, not of any causal fact. The verse sets up 3.7.91, which says this outright.

Verse 3.7.91.translation: In reality it is not to be specified; for no thing stands settled — since this intention is seen: 'it is cooked with a pot'.
Verse 3.7.91.commentary: The samuddesa's most direct statement of its thesis, and it is delivered as a shrug. Vastutah tad anirdesyam: *in reality* it is *not to be specified* — there is no fact about which participant is the instrument. Na hi vastu vyavasthitam: for *no thing stands settled* — nothing in the world is an instrument (3.6.14's things without place, parts, motion, support; 3.7.8's nirihesu api bhavesu kalpana-upanibandhanam). And the evidence, which is a single ordinary sentence: sthalya pacyate iti esa vivaksa drsyate yatah — since *this intention* is *seen*: '*it is cooked with a pot*'. The pot. Not the fire, not the heat, not the cook's stirring — the *pot*, which does nothing but sit there, is Sanskrit's perfectly ordinary instrument of cooking. If the pot can be the karana, then the karana is not the most effective cause, and 3.7.90's definition tracks nothing but what the speaker chose to put in the instrumental. Sthalya pacyate is the samuddesa's kupa-supa-yupa: one everyday phrase, and the realist account of the karakas is finished.

Verse 3.7.92.translation: But in the instruments, they undergo refinements again and again, and particular applications, for the accomplishing of the principal.
Verse 3.7.92.commentary: The instruments' ritual dimension. Karanesu tu samskaran labhante punah punah: in the *instruments*, they undergo *refinements* *again and again* — samskara: the preparation, purification, making-fit that the rite performs on its implements (1.146's therapeutic triad; 3.3.35's samskara of the object by the word). Viniyoga-visesan ca: and *particular applications* — viniyoga, the deployment to a ritual station (2.258's mantras obtaining difference by their application). Pradhanasya prasiddhaye: for the *accomplishing of the principal*. So instruments are worked on, repeatedly, and assigned to stations, all in service of the rite's principal act. The verse returns the abstract karana-analysis to the sacrificial ground it came from: the ladle is consecrated, and the consecrating is what makes it the instrument — which is 3.7.91's thesis in ritual dress. Nothing is an instrument; things are *made* instruments, and in the rite the making is visible and liturgical.

Verse 3.7.93.translation: And among the instruments, in their own compartments, no excellence exists; but the state of possessing a surpassing, resorted to from outside, is the mark there.
Verse 3.7.93.commentary: The objection of 3.7.74 revisited for the instrument, and answered as the object's was. Sva-kaksyasu prakarsah ca karananam na vidyate: among the *instruments*, in their *own compartments*, no *excellence* exists — one instrument is not more an instrument than another; instrument-hood does not grade (3.7.74's premise, granted). Asrita-atisayatvam tu paratah tatra laksanam: but the state of *possessing a surpassing*, resorted to *from outside*, is the *mark* there. Panini's definition is sadhakatamam — the *most* effective — and the superlative must qualify something. It qualifies not instrument-hood but effectiveness, which is conferred paratah, from outside: by the action's needs, by the speaker's construal. So the karana's definition works exactly as the karman's did (3.7.75: objecthood has no degrees, desire does): the superlative attaches to a gradable property that is not the role itself.

Verse 3.7.94.translation: Even in their independence, the employers assist only from a distance; for the operation of all is separated by the instrument.
Verse 3.7.94.commentary: The agent's remoteness, and it explains the whole hierarchy. Svatantrye 'pi prayoktarah arad eva upakurvate: even in their *independence*, the *employers* — the agents, svatantra by Panini's definition (1.4.54) — assist *only from a distance*: arat, from afar (2.82's arad visesaka, the qualifier from afar). The agent does not touch the work. Karanena hi sarvesam vyaparo vyavadhiyate: for the *operation of all* is *separated by the instrument* — the instrument stands between every agent and every act. The woodsman does not fell the tree; the axe does, and he swings the axe. So agency is constitutively mediated: to be an agent is to act *through*, and what one acts through is the karana. This is 3.7.22's king and soldiers given its mechanism — the king obtains victory precisely because he does not fight — and it explains why 3.7.90 could define the instrument as what the accomplishment immediately follows: the instrument is where the action touches the world, and the agent is always one remove back.

Verse 3.7.95.translation: In the accomplishing of the action there is excellence; but in the agent there is only subordination. For when the accomplishment exists, the general means-hood is surpassed.
Verse 3.7.95.commentary: The hierarchy's paradox, stated exactly. Kriya-siddhau prakarso va: in the *accomplishing of the action* there is *excellence* — the instrument, being nearest the accomplishment (3.7.90), has the surpassing. Nyag-bhavas tv eva kartari: but in the *agent* there is *only subordination* — nyag-bhava: the being-pressed-down (3.7.59's nyag-bhavana). The agent is *subordinated*? The independent one, the svatantra? Yes — and the reason: siddhau satyam hi samanyam sadhakatvam prakrsyate — when the *accomplishment* exists, the *general means-hood* is *surpassed*. Measured against the accomplishment, the agent's contribution is the most distant and the least effective (3.7.94's arat), so in the ranking-by-effectiveness he comes last. The grammar's most independent karaka is efficacy's least. This is the samuddesa's finest structural irony, and it is not a paradox: independence and effectiveness are different scales, and the karta tops one by placing last on the other.

Verse 3.7.96.translation: But in the agency of a sword and the like, they know sharpness and the rest to be the instrument; and in the independence of sharpness and the rest, the self stands two-fold.
Verse 3.7.96.commentary: The regress of instruments, and it does not terminate. Asy-adinam tu kartrtve taiksnyadi karanam viduh: in the *agency* of a *sword* and the like — when we say 'the sword cuts', the sword is agent — *sharpness* and the rest are the *instrument*: the sword cuts *by* its sharpness (3.7.31's four properties). So the sword, promoted to agent, needs an instrument of its own, and its own property serves. Taiksnyadinam svatantratve dvaidha atma vyavatisthate: and in the *independence* of sharpness and the rest — promote the sharpness to agent ('the sharpness cuts') — the *self* stands *two-fold*: it must now be both agent and (through some further property) instrument. The regress is 3.7.20-21's nesting run downward without a floor: every agent needs an instrument, every instrument can be promoted to agent, and each promotion generates a new instrument. Dvaidha atma — the self standing two-fold — names the splitting that each step requires.

Verse 3.7.97.translation: Even when there is a difference of self, one and the same object stands thus; from its resting on that, agency, though there is difference, is therefore what annuls.
Verse 3.7.97.commentary: The regress of 3.7.96 stopped. Atma-bhede 'pi sati evam ekah arthah sa tatha sthitah: even when there is a *difference of self* — even granting the splitting — *one and the same* object stands thus: the sword and its sharpness are not two things; the splitting was analytical (3.7.15's vyatirekam upasritya: the construction resorting to separation, whatever the metaphysics). Tad-asrayatvad bhede 'pi kartrtvam badhakam tatah: from its *resting on that*, *agency*, though there is difference, is *therefore what annuls*. Agency wins: where sharpness and sword are one thing, the agency-assignment overrides the instrument-assignment, and the regress stops because there is nothing further to promote. Badhaka — annulling: the badha-machinery (2.66, 2.73's hierarchy of evidences) adjudicating between two karaka-assignments to one thing. So the regress is real as analysis and idle as ontology, and the grammar stops it by ranking.

Verse 3.7.98.translation: And as instrument-hood is understood from proximity — just so, even in the absence of proximity, it is understood from the accomplishing of the action.
Verse 3.7.98.commentary: The instrument's criterion loosened. Yatha ca sannidhanena karanatvam pratiyate: as instrument-hood is understood from *proximity* — the axe in hand, the pot on the fire (3.7.91's sthalya pacyate). Tatha eva asannidhane 'pi kriya-siddheh pratiyate: *just so*, even in the *absence of proximity*, it is understood from the *accomplishing* of the action. Distant things are instruments too: one conquers by an army not present, cooks by a fire one is not touching. So proximity was never the criterion; the accomplishment's dependence was (3.7.90's parinispattih yad-vyaparad anantaram — and 'immediately following' is a matter of construal, not of distance). The verse removes the last physical residue from the karana's definition: instrument-hood is a functional and construal-relative posting, and nothing about nearness survives in it.

Verse 3.7.99.translation: From the coming-about of what is little, or from its not ceasing — they declare the establishing of instrument-hood for 'little' and the like.
Verse 3.7.99.commentary: A hard case: adverbial instrumentals. Stokasya va abhinirvrtteh anivrtteh ca tasya va: from the *coming-about* of what is *little*, or from its *not ceasing*. The construction is 'stokena mukta' — 'released by a little', 'he escaped by a hair' — where 'little' takes the instrumental and is not an instrument in any recognizable sense: no thing is being used. Prasiddhim karanatvasya stokadina pracaksate: they declare the *establishing* of instrument-hood for *'little'* and the like. Two accounts are offered, and both are functional: the littleness *came about* (and its coming-about is what the escape followed on: 3.7.90's criterion), or it *did not cease* (and its persisting is what the escape depended on). Either way, the instrumental marks what the accomplishment hung on — which in this case is a quantity, not a thing. The verse shows the karana-slot accepting what is not remotely an instrument, because the slot tracks dependence and not instrumentality.

Verse 3.7.100.translation: It is distinguished by the difference and the non-difference of the properties from what possesses them — as it is differentiated by a difference in the delimitation of the action's limit.
Verse 3.7.100.commentary: The general principle behind 3.7.99. Dharmanam tadvata bhedad abhedac ca visisyate: it is *distinguished* by the *difference* and the *non-difference* of the *properties* from *what possesses them* — a property may be taken as one with its bearer or apart from it (3.7.96-97's sword and sharpness), and the karaka-assignment follows the taking. Kriya-avaccheda-visesad bhidyate yatha: *as* it is differentiated by a difference in the *delimitation of the action's limit* — where one draws the action's boundary decides what falls inside it as participant and what stands outside as its instrument. So two variables govern every karaka-assignment: how one takes the property's relation to its bearer, and where one cuts the action. Both are construals (3.7.3's buddhy-avastha-nibandhanah), and between them they generate the whole taxonomy the samuddesa has been cataloguing.

Verse 3.7.101-102.translation: (101) From the obtaining of capacity previously from another; from the bringing-about of subordination; from their operating in dependence on that; from the turning-back of those who have set out — (102) from the non-observation of a substitute; and from its being seen in separation — even where the assistance is from a distance, independence is said of the agent.
Verse 3.7.101-102.commentary: Six marks of agency, gathered into a definition. The agent is identified by: (i) prag anyatah sakti-labhat — the others *obtaining capacity previously from* him (he empowers them); (ii) nyag-bhava-apadanat — his *bringing about their subordination* (3.7.20's nyakkrta); (iii) tad-adhina-pravrttitvat — their *operating in dependence* on him; (iv) pravrttanam nivartanat — his *turning back those who have set out* (he can stop what he started: the mark of control); (v) adrstatvat pratinidheh — the *non-observation of a substitute* for him (agents are not substitutable: 3.1.78's test, which discriminates what stands near the capacity from what stands at a remove); (vi) prativeke ca darsanat — his *being seen in separation* (he is identifiable apart from the act). And the conclusion: arad api upakaritve svatantryam kartur ucyate — *even where the assistance is from a distance* (3.7.94's arat), *independence* is said of the *agent*. Six operational marks, no metaphysics: the karta is whoever exhibits these, and 3.7.103 will say what that means.

Verse 3.7.103.translation: By the properties that have arisen, the restriction is in the word, not in the thing; when the agent's property is intended, the agent is understood from the word.
Verse 3.7.103.commentary: The six marks of 3.7.101-102 placed, and the placing is the samuddesa's thesis. Dharmaih abhyuditaih sabde niyamah na tu vastuni: by the *properties* that have *arisen*, the *restriction* is *in the word*, *not in the thing*. The six marks do not identify a thing that is the agent; they govern when the *word* takes agent-morphology. Kartur dharma-vivaksayam sabdat karta pratiyate: when the *agent's property* is *intended*, the *agent* is *understood from the word*. So agency is a fact about what is intended and expressed, and the marks are the conditions under which the expression is licensed. Na tu vastuni — not in the thing: the phrase is 3.7.91's (vastutah tad anirdesyam, na hi vastu vyavasthitam) applied now to the karta as it was there to the karana. Neither end of the karaka-system rests on anything.

Verse 3.7.104.translation: And when a difference is constructed, by states of the intellect, for one and the same thing — objecthood, instrument-hood and agency arise.
Verse 3.7.104.commentary: The samuddesa's culminating statement, and it gathers a hundred verses. Ekasya buddhy-avasthabhih bhede ca parikalpite: when a *difference* is *constructed*, by *states of the intellect*, for *one and the same thing* — one thing, and the buddhi's states carve it. Karmatvam karanatvam ca kartrtvam ca upajayate: *objecthood*, *instrument-hood* and *agency* *arise*. All three, from one thing, by cognitive states. This is 3.7.3 (the practice of means is grounded in a state of the intellect) now demonstrated rather than asserted, and it explains everything the samuddesa has found: why the same cooking is object, instrument and sadhya at once (2.429); why both parents are agents and the roles are assigned by intention (3.7.19); why the pot is the instrument of cooking (3.7.91). Buddhy-avastha — states of the intellect: not arbitrary, not private, but *states*, which come and go and can be occupied. The karaka-system is a map of the positions a mind can take up toward an event.

Verse 3.7.105.translation: The prior non-existence before the arising is grounded in a state of the intellect; not distinguished from another existent thing, it becomes the agent of the birth.
Verse 3.7.105.commentary: The doctrine of 3.7.104 applied to its hardest case: origination. Utpatteh prag asad-bhavah buddhy-avastha-nibandhanah: the *prior non-existence* before the *arising* is *grounded in a state of the intellect* — the pot's not-yet-being is a way the mind holds the situation, not a condition the pot is in (3.3.79's finding: 'the prior state of a non-thing' is not a describable condition; neither the priority nor the state exists in a non-thing). Avisistah sata anyena karta bhavati janmanah: *not distinguished* from another *existent* thing, it *becomes the agent of the birth*. So 'the pot comes to be' has the pot as agent — and the pot, at that moment, is a buddhi-posting indistinguishable from any existing thing, because buddhi does not distinguish them (3.3.86's mind that will not halt in non-being). This is 3.3.43-48's dilemma of origination dissolved by the karaka-analysis: nothing is born, and the birth's agent is a state of the intellect, and the grammar conjugates it.

Verse 3.7.106.translation: Or, as a cause stands in the form of the effect — then, having obtained the word 'effect', it thereupon arises as the effect.
Verse 3.7.106.commentary: The alternative account of origination. Karanam karya-bhavena yatha va vyavatisthate: *or*, as a *cause* stands in the *form of the effect* — the clay standing as the pot, the satkarya-vadin's picture (3.7.49's sat va janmana yat prakasyate). Karya-sabdam tada labdhva karyatvena atha jayate: *then*, *having obtained the word 'effect'*, it *thereupon arises as the effect*. The order is the verse's shock: the thing obtains the *word* first, and *then* arises as the effect. Naming is prior to being. This is not a slip — it is 3.1.6's doctrine (every word expresses its own universal first, and superimposes it on the object) applied to origination: what makes the clay into a pot is its receiving the word 'pot', and the arising follows the naming. The Padakanda's constructivism reaches its limit here, and the limit is that things come to be by being called.

Verse 3.7.107.translation: As the snake's becoming-coiled, and the wholeness of things dispersed — just so, some declare, is the birth-form of existent things.
Verse 3.7.107.commentary: Two similes for a birth that is no birth. Yatha aheh kundali-bhavah: as the *snake's becoming-coiled* — the snake does not acquire a new thing by coiling; it arranges itself, and 'the coil' comes to be without anything coming to be. Vyagranam ca samagrata: and the *wholeness* of things *dispersed* — scattered things gathered, and the 'whole' arises without any new entity: the heap is not a further thing beside the grains. Tatha eva janma-rupatvam satam eke pracaksate: *just so*, some declare, is the *birth-form* of *existent* things. So birth is rearrangement and gathering — configuration, not creation. The similes are chosen for cases where everyone already sees that the 'new thing' is a way of taking the old: nobody thinks a coil was manufactured.
