This is 3.9.12's identification given its argument: time is the weighing, not the scale-pan.

Verse 3.9.29.translation: It abandons the actions that stand co-occurrent with it; as rice abandons water — thereby it obtains the name 'year'.
Verse 3.9.29.commentary: The samuddesa's most compressed image, and its transcription is difficult. Jahati saha-vrttah ca kriyah sa samavasthitah: it *abandons* the *actions* that stand *co-occurrent* with it — time lets go of what accompanies it (3.9.25's tirobhavanti; 3.9.22's srsta). Vrihir yatha udakam: as *rice* abandons *water* — the boiling rice, which takes up water and gives it off as steam; or the ripening grain, which draws water and dries. Tena hayana-akhyam prapadyate: *thereby* it obtains the name *'year'* — hayana, the year, whose etymology the tradition connects with ha, to abandon: the year is what lets go. So time gets its name from its abandoning, as at 3.9.14 it got its name from its reckoning — two etymologies, two operations, and both are 3.9.4's: obstruction and permission, holding and letting go. The year is a letting-go, and the rice giving up its water is the figure for it.
Verse 3.9.29.contested: the rice-and-water simile is compressed and the transcription uncertain; whether the picture is boiling (rice releasing steam) or ripening (grain drying) cannot be settled, and the hayana-etymology (from ha, to abandon) is the traditional one the verse evidently invokes

Verse 3.9.30.translation: That operation of it which consists of obstruction and permission is everlasting; being divided by that, it takes on the form of sequence.
Verse 3.9.30.commentary: Time's self-division, and it is reflexive. Pratibandha-abhyanujnabhyam vrttir ya tasya sasvati: that *operation* of it consisting of *obstruction and permission* (3.9.4's two) is *everlasting* — the holding-and-releasing never stops, and it is what time *is* (3.9.12's kala eva vyaparah). Taya vibhajyamano 'sau bhajate krama-rupatam: *being divided by that*, it *takes on* the *form of sequence*. So time is divided by its own operation: the holding-and-releasing is what carves time into before and after, and time is the holding-and-releasing. The circle is exact and it is not vicious — it is what a self-operating power looks like. Bhajate: takes on, wears (3.2.8's tattvam eva avikalpitam vikalpa-rupam bhajate: reality itself, unconstructed, takes on the form of construction). Sequence is time's own garment, worn by its own act.

Verse 3.9.31.translation: From difference of agents, having gone to excellence and diminution in respect of their purposes — that one thing attains sameness or unevenness.
Verse 3.9.31.commentary: Time's apparent variability. Kartr-bhedat tad-arthesu prakarsa-apacayau gatah: from *difference of agents*, having gone to *excellence and diminution* in respect of *their purposes* — the same hour is long to the waiting and short to the busy: time's felt extent varies with whose purposes are in play. Samatvam visamatvam va sa ekah pratipadyate: *that one* thing attains *sameness or unevenness*. So time is one and is met as even or uneven, according to the agents and their ends. The verse does not psychologize this away: the unevenness is real, and it belongs to time-as-met, since time is nothing but its operating on somebody's purposes (3.9.12). And prakarsa — excellence, the graded more-ness of 3.5.2-3 — is here temporal: an hour can be more.

Verse 3.9.32.translation: As, from difference of action, the designation 'carpenter' and the rest proceeds with respect to one thing — so, from difference of action, the designation 'season' and the rest arises with respect to one thing.
Verse 3.9.32.commentary: The seasons explained by an occupational analogy. Kriya-bhedad yatha ekasmin taksady-akhya pravartate: as, from *difference of action*, the designation *'carpenter'* and the rest proceeds with respect to *one* thing — one man is carpenter, cook, father: the names track what he does, and he is one. Kriya-bhedat tatha ekasmin rtv-ady-akhya upajayate: so, from difference of action, the designation *'season'* and the rest arises with respect to *one* thing. Spring and autumn are one time under different doings — as teacher and uncle are one man under different relations (3.8.63's closing simile). So the calendar is an occupational list: time named by what it is doing. And the analogy's force is that nobody thinks the carpenter and the cook are two men, or that carpentry is a substance the man is in.

Verse 3.9.33.translation: 'Commencement', 'action', and 'completion' — these are spoken of, from a difference of superimposition of other properties, upon what is of existent-and-non-existent self.
Verse 3.9.33.commentary: The three phases of any process, and their status. Arambhah ca kriya ca eva nistha ca iti abhidhiyate: *'commencement'*, *'action'*, and *'completion'* — the three moments every act is analysed into. Dharmantaranam adhyasa-bhedat: from a *difference of superimposition* of *other properties* — adhyasa: superimposition (3.1.6's central term; 3.8.11's asad-adhyasa, the superimposition of what is not there). Sad-asad-atmanah: upon what is of *existent-and-non-existent self* — the action, whose phases are existent and non-existent by sequence (3.8.6, 3.8.19's atma eka iva grhyate). So the three phases are superimposed on something that is neither wholly there nor wholly not, and they differ only in which properties are laid on. This is the Kriya-samuddesa's analysis (3.8.4's construed collection) restated as time's doing: the phases are time's superimpositions.

Verse 3.9.34.translation: And as much as it is for the dyad and the rest, so much is it also for a mountain; for the self of anything whatever cannot be split or accumulated.
Verse 3.9.34.commentary: Time's indifference to magnitude. Yavan ca dvy-anukadinam tavan bimavato 'py asau: as much as it is for the *dyad* and the rest — the smallest composite of the atomists — *so much* is it also for a *mountain*. Time is not more for big things. Na hi atma kasyacid bhettum pracetum vapi sakyate: for the *self* of anything whatever cannot be *split* or *accumulated*. Nothing's self can be divided or added to — which is 3.6.15's great leveling (partlessness is equal for the atom and for the pot) now applied to time: as nothing has parts of its own, nothing has more time than anything else. So the mountain's age and the dyad's are not different quantities of a stuff. Duration is not a possession.

Verse 3.9.35.translation: But by other entities, an accumulation is constructed for others; thereby it is understood: 'this slowly, this quickly'.
Verse 3.9.35.commentary: Where duration comes from, then. Anyaih tu bhavaih anyesam pracayah parikalpyate: by *other entities*, an *accumulation* is *constructed* for *others* — durations are conferred by comparison with something else: the mountain is old *relative to* the flower's seasons. Sanaih idam idam ksipram iti tena pratiyate: *thereby* it is understood: '*this slowly*, *this quickly*'. So fast and slow are relational constructions, borrowed from a comparison-term, and neither belongs to the process. This is 3.9.28's balance (weight measured by an instrument that contributes none) and 3.9.34's leveling (no thing has its own duration) combining: durations are conferred, comparisons confer them, and time is the conferring.

Verse 3.9.36.translation: And being conditioned by action, it attains past-ness, future-ness and present-ness, divided by eleven forms.
Verse 3.9.36.commentary: The tense-doctrine, announced. Kriya-upadhih ca san: *being conditioned by action* — upadhi: the limiting adjunct (3.2.2's asatya-upadhi; 1.75's grahana-upadhi-bheda). Time takes its tenses from the actions that condition it, not from itself. Bhuta-bhavisyad-vartamanatah: *past-ness*, *future-ness* and *present-ness* — the three tenses, and they are *-ta* abstractions: past-*ness*, conditions rather than times. Ekadasabhih akaraih vibhaktah pratipadyate: *divided by eleven forms* it attains them. Eleven: Panini's ten lakaras plus one, or the traditional enumeration the next verses give. So the three tenses are eleven forms, and all of them are time conditioned by action. Time has no tense of its own — which follows from 3.9.12: if time is the operating, its tenses are the operations'.

Verse 3.9.37.translation: And being conditioned by action, it attains past-ness, future-ness and present-ness, divided by eleven forms.
Verse 3.9.37.commentary: The source transmits this verse identically with 3.9.36 — the same two lines under two page-labels. Whether the wisdomlib edition has duplicated a single karika across two pages, or whether the text genuinely repeats it (a not-unknown scribal or recensional event), cannot be settled from the transcription alone. We record the duplication rather than silently collapsing it, and the substantive commentary is given at 3.9.36.
Verse 3.9.37.contested: the verse is transmitted identically with 3.9.36 in the source; this is very likely a pagination or transcription fault rather than a genuine repetition in the text, and readers consulting other editions should expect the numbering here to diverge

Verse 3.9.38.translation: The past there is five-fold, and the future four-fold; the present is declared two-fold — thus eleven constructions.
Verse 3.9.38.commentary: The eleven enumerated. Bhutah panca-vidhah tatra: the *past* is *five-fold* — Sanskrit's several past forms (the aorist, the imperfect, the perfect, and the periphrastics, distinguished by remoteness and witness). Bhavisyan ca catur-vidhah: the *future* *four-fold*. Vartamano dvidha akhyatah: the *present* is declared *two-fold*. Ity ekadasa kalpanah: thus *eleven* *constructions*. And the last word is the doctrine: kalpanah — *constructions*, not times. The tense-system is eleven ways of construing, not eleven regions of a substance. So Sanskrit's remarkably fine past-tense apparatus (five forms where a modern European language has two or three) is not a finer map of a real past; it is a richer set of postings (3.7.103's niyamah sabde na tu vastuni: the restriction is in the word, not in the thing).

Verse 3.9.39.translation: Having deposited their own form in time, what is grasped by the understanding — from that, entities withdraw, their capacities having passed over into it.
Verse 3.9.39.commentary: The past, and it is a deposit-account. Kale nidhaya svam rupam: *having deposited their own form in time* — things leave their shape with time as one leaves goods in trust. Prajnaya yan nigrhyate: what is *grasped by the understanding* — what memory holds is that deposit. Bhavas tato nivartante: *from that*, entities *withdraw* — they go, and the form stays. Tatra sankranta-saktayah: their *capacities having passed over into it* — sankranta: transferred, migrated. So the past is where things leave their forms and powers when they withdraw, and what we grasp of the past is the deposit, not the things. This is a genuinely strange and beautiful account: the past is a repository, the deposits are real, and the depositors are gone. And it explains how the past is knowable (its forms are there) without being present (its things are not).

Verse 3.9.40.translation: And the form of things to come — the reflection of that, too, is possible in time alone, as in a well-polished mirror.
Verse 3.9.40.commentary: The future, and the image is exact. Bhavinam ca eva yad rupam: the *form* of *things to come*. Tasya ca pratibimbakam: the *reflection* of that. Sunirmrsta iva adarse: as in a *well-polished mirror* — sunirmrsta: thoroughly wiped, burnished. Kala eva upapadyate: is possible *in time alone*. So the future is time reflecting what has not arrived — a mirror-image with no object yet before it. The image completes 3.9.39's pair: the past is a deposit, the future is a reflection, and time holds both. And the reflection-figure carries all its earlier freight (1.49's borrowed properties; 2.294's reflection failing the effect-test; 3.9.19's shadows in clear water): the future is *there* in time as a face is in a mirror — present to be seen, doing nothing, and not a second thing. Sunirmrsta — well-polished — is the condition: a clouded mirror shows no future, and time's clarity is what makes anticipation possible.

Verse 3.9.41.translation: As a current draws along grass, leaves, creepers and the like — so time too impels the measures of things possessing measure.
Verse 3.9.41.commentary: The current, and it is the samuddesa's third great image. Trna-parna-lata-adini yatha srotah anukarsati: as a *current* *draws along* *grass*, *leaves*, *creepers* and the like — the river carrying its debris: each thing moving at the water's speed, none of them swimming. Pravartayati kalo 'pi matra matravatam tatha: so *time* too *impels* the *measures* of things *possessing measure*. So things are carried, not travelling: their durations are the current's, as the leaf's speed is the river's. The image was already used at 3.7.33 (the capacity, in a single substratum, draws downward like a current) and here it is time's. And what a current is matters: not a thing in the water but the water's own moving — which is 3.9.12's identification exactly. Time is not what carries things along; it is the carrying.

Verse 3.9.42.translation: As the wind, having entered them as it were, connects up the motions of things possessing motion — so the self of time brings about the form of sequence.
Verse 3.9.42.commentary: The wind, and it is the fourth image and the most intimate. Avisya iva anusandhatte yatha gatimatam gatih vayuh: as the *wind*, having *entered* them *as it were*, *connects up* the *motions* of things possessing motion — the wind that carries the leaves does not push them from outside; it enters and moves them from within, and their separate motions become one motion. Anusandhatte: connects up, threads together, joins into a sequence (the same root as the sutradhara's thread, 3.9.4). Tatha eva kala-atma vidhatte krama-rupatam: so the *self of time* *brings about* the *form of sequence*. So time works as wind does: entering (avisya *iva*: as it were — the iva is careful) and threading the many motions into one order. This is the puppeteer (3.9.4), the string (3.9.15), the current (3.9.41) and now the wind — four images for one operation, and each more inward than the last.

Verse 3.9.43.translation: The division of the solstices, and the fixed motion of the luminaries; and the ceasings and originations of beings — these are grounded in that.
Verse 3.9.43.commentary: The observable order, grounded. Ayana-pravibhagah ca: the *division of the solstices* — the year's great turning-points, by which the calendar is fixed. Gatih ca jyotisam dhruva: and the *fixed motion* of the *luminaries* — the stars' unfailing courses, the most reliable regularity anyone knew. Nivrtti-prabhavah ca eva bhutanam: and the *ceasings and originations* of *beings* — birth and death. Tan-nibandhanah: these are *grounded in that* — in time. So the astronomical order and the biological one have one ground, and it is the holding-and-releasing (3.9.4). The verse is quietly ambitious: it claims that what makes the sun turn at the solstice and what makes a man die are the same operation, differently scheduled. And nibandhana is the grounding-word the whole work has used since 1.13.

Verse 3.9.44-45.translation: (44) Those measures of the moments which follow upon time's operation are severally called 'naksatra' among them; but the stars are a mere mark. (45) By the cries of beasts and birds, by the operations of stationary things, and by the transformations of shadow and the rest — the season's abode is determined.
Verse 3.9.44-45.commentary: How time is read, and the epistemology is careful. (44): matranam parimanah ye kala-vrtty-anuyayinah: the *measures* of the *moments* which *follow upon time's operation* — the divisions that track the operating. Naksatra-akhya prthak tesu: they are severally called *'naksatra'* — the lunar mansions, the sky's calendar. And the correction: cihna-matram tu tarakah — the *stars* are a *mere mark*. The stars do not constitute the naksatras; they mark them. What is real is the operation; the constellations are its dial. (45) then lists the other dials: rutaih mrga-sakuntanam — by the *cries* of beasts and birds; sthavaranam ca vrttibhih — by the *operations* of *stationary* things (the flowering, the leaf-fall); chaya-adi-parinamaih ca — and by the *transformations of shadow* and the rest (the gnomon's shadow, the day's lengthening). Rtu-dhama nirupyate: the *season's abode* is *determined*. So one reads time from birdsong, budding and shadow as much as from stars — and all of them equally are marks (cihna-matra), none of them time.

Verse 3.9.46.translation: This coming-to-appearance which is seen as though possessing sequence — of a universe that is sequenceless — that is time's activity.
Verse 3.9.46.commentary: The samuddesa's thesis, stated in one line. Nirbhasa-upagamo yo 'yam kramavan iva drsyate: this *coming-to-appearance* which is *seen* *as though possessing sequence* — the iva marks it, as it has marked every claim of this kind since 1.48. Akramasya api visvasya: *of a universe* that is *sequenceless* — the world does not run; nothing is in order. Tat kalasya vicestitam: *that* is *time's activity*. So the whole appearance of sequence, in a sequenceless universe, is what time does. Vicestita: activity, exertion, the writhing or working of something — a strong word, and it fits 3.9.12 (time is operation): time does not exist and then act; the acting is all there is, and its product is the look of order. Everything the samuddesa has built converges here: the puppeteer, the string, the current, the wind — four figures for one activity, and this is what the activity produces.

Verse 3.9.47.translation: As the settled arrangement of far and near has the road for its locus — so the settled arrangement of long and quick has time for its locus.
Verse 3.9.47.commentary: The parallel that places time among the constructions. Duranti ka-vyavasthanam adhva-adhikaranam yatha: as the *settled arrangement* of *far and near* has the *road* for its *locus* — distance is measured along a road, and the road is where far-and-near live. Cira-ksipra-vyavasthanam kala-adhikaranam tatha: so the settled arrangement of *long and quick* has *time* for its *locus*. The parallel is exact and it is deflationary: as a road is not distance but where distance is reckoned, time is not duration but where duration is reckoned. And a road, notoriously, is not a thing — it is a way, made by going. Adhikarana: the locus, the karaka that assists at two removes without touching anything (3.7.148). So time is the locus of durations as the road is of distances: indispensable, reckoned along, and not an item.

Verse 3.9.48.translation: Of that undivided time, in practice, three differences — as it were, made by action — are established, which the world does not transgress.
Verse 3.9.48.commentary: The three tenses, and every qualification matters. Tasya abhinnasya kalasya: of that *undivided* time — one, partless (3.9.7's neither one nor many). Vyavahare: *in practice* — the divisions belong to transaction, not to time (2.297's sabdah loka-nibandhanah). Kriya-krtah bheda iva trayah siddhah: *three* differences, *as it were*, *made by action*, are *established* — kriya-krta: action-made (3.9.36's kriya-upadhih: time conditioned by action), and iva: as though. Yan loko na ativartate: which the *world does not transgress* — the phrase 2.462 used of sequence and simultaneity (which the world does not transgress). So the three tenses are practical, action-made, apparent divisions of an undivided time — and nobody escapes them. Both halves stand: constructed, and inescapable (3.6.18's dik-kala-parikalpana standing like consciousness itself).

Verse 3.9.49.translation: Three capacities of the one time stand established, by connection with which there is the seeing and the non-seeing of entities that exist.
Verse 3.9.49.commentary: The tenses recast as saktis, and this is the samuddesa's central move. Ekasya saktayah tisrah kalasya samavasthitah: *three capacities* of the *one* time stand established — not three times but three powers of one (1.2's Brahman resting on its differentiated powers; 3.7.37's agency alone, sixfold). And their office: yat-sambandhena bhavanam darsana-adarsane satam — by *connection with which* there is the *seeing* and the *non-seeing* of entities *that exist*. Satam — *that exist*: the entities exist throughout; what the tenses govern is whether they are *seen*. So past and future things are not absent; they are unseen. The tense-system is an optics, not an inventory — and this single word satam is the hinge of the whole doctrine. Everything is; the three powers decide what shows.

Verse 3.9.50.translation: By two of its capacities it is, indeed, of the nature of a screen for entities; but the capacity called 'present' is the illuminator of the entities' form.
Verse 3.9.50.commentary: The three sorted, two against one. Dvabhyam sa kila saktibhyam bhavanam varanatmakah: by *two* of its capacities it is *of the nature of a screen* — varana: a covering, a curtain, a screening-off. Past and future are screens: they hide what exists (3.9.49's satam). Saktih tu vartamana-akhya bhava-rupa-prakasini: but the capacity called *'present'* is the *illuminator* of the entities' *form*. So of time's three powers, two conceal and one reveals; and 'the present' names not a stretch of time but a lighting. This is the samuddesa's most economical statement of its tense-doctrine, and it inverts the ordinary picture entirely: things do not come into the present and pass out; they stand, and time's screens draw back and close.

Verse 3.9.51.translation: The capacity called 'future' is not the obstructor of the capacity of birth; but that capacity called 'past' — by it, birth is opposed.
Verse 3.9.51.commentary: The two screens distinguished by their relation to birth. Anagata janma-sakteh saktir apratibandhika: the capacity called *'future'* is *not the obstructor* of the *capacity of birth* — the future screens without preventing: what is future is unborn and *may* be born; the screen does not bar the door. Atita-akhya tu ya saktih taya janma virudhyate: but that capacity called *'past'* — by *it*, *birth* is *opposed*. The past screens and bars: what is past cannot be born again. So the two screens differ in modality, and the difference is exactly the asymmetry we feel between past and future — the future is open and the past is closed. And the samuddesa has derived that asymmetry from time's powers rather than assuming it: pratibandha (3.9.4's obstruction) is what the past does, and the future merely conceals.

Verse 3.9.52.translation: These three roads stand established, having the nature of darkness and light; they are sequenceless, and in them the sequence of entities is apprehended.
Verse 3.9.52.commentary: The image that gathers 3.9.50-51. Tamah-prakasavat tv ete trayo 'dhvano vyavasthitah: these *three roads* stand established, having the nature of *darkness and light* — adhvan: road, the word 3.9.47 used for the locus of far-and-near; and the three roads are two dark and one lit. Akramah tesu bhavanam kramah samupalabhyate: they are *sequenceless*, and *in them* the *sequence of entities* is *apprehended*. So the three tenses are themselves without sequence — the past is not before the future — and the sequence we find is the entities' as apprehended in them. The image is precise: a lit stretch of road between two dark ones, and things showing as they pass through the light. Nothing moves the road; the road does not run. And 3.9.46's finding stands: the sequence is the appearance, and the appearing is time's activity.

Verse 3.9.53.translation: But two of them there are of the form of darkness; of one, the standing is like light. And for some, even the past turns round again.
Verse 3.9.53.commentary: The image confirmed and then complicated. Dvau tu tatra tamo-rupau: *two* of them are of the *form of darkness* — past and future, the screens (3.9.50's varanatmakah). Ekasya alokavat sthitih: of *one*, the *standing* is *like light* — the present. Then: atitam api kesamcit punar viparivartate — and for *some*, even the *past* *turns round again*. Some hold that the past returns — the cyclical cosmologies, the recurring ages, the doctrine that what has been will be again. So 3.9.51's asymmetry (the past bars birth) is not universally granted: on a cyclical view the past's screen is not final. Bhartrhari reports it (kesamcit) without adopting it, and its presence here is honest doxography: the tense-doctrine he has built assumes an asymmetry his tradition does not unanimously hold.

Verse 3.9.54.translation: What has that property attains present-ness simultaneously; and from the present-ness of some, it likewise goes to past-ness.
Verse 3.9.54.commentary: The cyclical view of 3.9.53 developed. Yugapad vartamanatvam tad-dharma pratipadyate: what has *that* property attains *present-ness* *simultaneously* — on the recurrence-view, the returning past becomes present all at once. Kesamcid vartamanatvat ca eti tadvad atitatam: and from the *present-ness* of *some*, it *likewise goes to past-ness*. So things cycle through the three, and the cycling is what the recurrence-doctrine describes. Bhartrhari is reporting a view in which the three saktis are not fixed stations but phases each thing passes through and re-enters — and it is compatible with his own account (the three are powers of one time, not regions) while its cyclicality is an addition he does not endorse. The report is careful and the reader is left to weigh it.

Verse 3.9.55.translation: Drawn forth by the assistance of a cause, it has come to present-ness; the cause's assistance having subsided, it does not again come to seeing.
Verse 3.9.55.commentary: The linear account, against 3.9.53-54's cyclical one. Hetu-upakarad aksiptah vartamanatvam agatah: *drawn forth* by the *assistance of a cause*, it has come to *present-ness* — a thing enters the light because something assists it (3.9.23's favouring by commingling entities). Santa-hetupakarah san punar na upaiti darsanam: the cause's assistance having *subsided*, it does *not again* come to *seeing*. Santa: quieted, stilled. So on this account the present is where a thing stands while its causes assist, and when they subside it goes dark and does not return — 3.9.25's death (the supporters withdrawing) as the general law of pastness. This is the asymmetry of 3.9.51 given its mechanism, and it is set here against 3.9.53's recurrence without adjudication. Two accounts of whether the dark road behind is final.

Verse 3.9.56.translation: For some, there are only two capacity-paths of the all-pervading time, by which it effects the opening and closing of the eyes of entities.
Verse 3.9.56.commentary: A rival reduction, and its image is lovely. Dve eva kalasya vibhoh kesamcit sakti-vartmani: only *two* *capacity-paths* of the *all-pervading* time, for *some*. Karoti yabhyam bhavanam unmilana-nimilane: by which it effects the *opening and closing of the eyes* of entities — unmilana and nimilana: the eye's opening and shutting; also the blossoming and closing of a flower. So on this account there are not three powers but two — a showing and a hiding — and past and future collapse into one darkness. The reduction is economical: 3.9.50 already made two of the three screens, and this view asks why two screens are two powers. And the image is the samuddesa's gentlest: things blink open and shut, and time is the blinking.

Verse 3.9.57.translation: Some declare that they are divided, by their own nature, by times which have separate meanings, and are characterized by the intellect's gathering-together.
Verse 3.9.57.commentary: The idealist option. Kalabhih prthag-arthabhih pravibhakte svabhavatah: divided, *by their own nature*, by *times* which have *separate meanings* — the tenses as genuinely distinct times, each with its own content. Kecid buddhy-anusamhara-laksane te pracaksate: *some* declare them to be *characterized by the intellect's gathering-together*. Buddhy-anusamhara: the mind's drawing-together — 2.1's sixth view of the sentence (buddhy-anusamhrtih, the gathering-together in cognition, which the Vakyakanda's whole argument favoured). So the tenses are the mind's gatherings: past is what the intellect has drawn together, future what it projects. The view makes tense wholly cognitive — and it is very close to Bhartrhari's own (3.7.104's buddhy-avastha carving the karakas), differing only in making the gathering constitutive of time rather than of its divisions.

Verse 3.9.58.translation: Whether a capacity that follows upon cognition, or something external standing in truth — without resorting to a self of time, it is not possible to transact.
Verse 3.9.58.commentary: The dispute of 3.9.57 declined, and the declining is the samuddesa's method. Jnana-anugata-saktim va: whether a *capacity that follows upon cognition* — the idealist option (3.9.57); bahyam va satyatah sthitam: or something *external* *standing in truth* — the realist option (3.9.1's substance). Kala-atmanam anasritya vyavhartum na sakyate: *without resorting to a self of time*, it is *not possible to transact*. So: whichever it is, one cannot do without it. The office is undeniable and the ontology is undecided — which is 1.28's move (eternal or produced, no beginning either way), 2.433's (all-natured or self-less, the word is sovereign either way), 3.1.107's (whatever the substrate, the doctrine stands). Vyavahartum: to transact — and 3.6.19 already said the theorist who abandons these entities takes hold of them again.

Verse 3.9.59.translation: For some, there are three being-capacities of each and every entity; by those own capacities, everything always both is and is not.
Verse 3.9.59.commentary: The three saktis relocated from time to things. Tisro bhavasya bhavasya kesamcid bhava-saktayah: *three* *being-capacities* of *each and every* entity — every thing carries its own past, present and future as powers, rather than passing through time's three. Tabhih sva-saktibhih sarvam sada eva asti ca na asti ca: by those *own capacities*, *everything* *always* both *is* and *is not*. Sada eva: always — and both. So on this view nothing ever changes tense; each thing is permanently existent-and-non-existent by its own three powers, and the appearance of passage is the shifting of which power shows. This is 3.3.87's finding (the eternal one, whose self is both being and non-being, shines forth as multiform) applied to each thing severally — and it dissolves time into the things, as 3.9.12 dissolved it into operation.

Verse 3.9.60.translation: Being not different from existence, those three also stand established; and from their non-difference from that, sequence and existence-and-non-existence are not differentiated.
Verse 3.9.60.commentary: The reduction completed, and it lands where the Padakanda always lands. Sattvad avyatirekena tah tisro 'pi vyavasthitah: *being not different from existence*, those *three* also stand established — the three capacities are not other than satta (3.1.33's sole universal, in which all words stand established). Kramah tah tad-abhedac ca sad-asattvam na bhidyate: and from their *non-difference* from that, *sequence* and *existence-and-non-existence* are *not differentiated*. So: the three tenses are satta's powers, satta is one, and therefore sequence and being/non-being are not two things but one Existence variously showing. This is 3.3.84 exactly (in reality there is no difference whatever of simultaneity from sequence, just as no non-being is determined as other than being) — and its arrival here completes the Kala-samuddesa's absorption into the Padakanda's single doctrine. Time, action, being: one satta, carved.

Verse 3.9.61.translation: By seeing and non-seeing it is one; but that very thing is seen and unseen. There is no oneness of the roads, and nothing whatever withdraws.
Verse 3.9.61.commentary: The three roads (3.9.52) audited, and the audit is severe. Darsana-adarsanena ekam: by *seeing* and *non-seeing* it is *one* — the thing is one, whether shown or screened (3.9.49's satam: the entities exist throughout). Drstadrstam tad eva tu: but *that very thing* is *seen and unseen* — one thing, both. Adhvanam ekata na asti: there is *no oneness of the roads* — the three are not one road; the metaphor of a single track through darkness and light is refused. Na ca kimcin nivartate: and *nothing whatever* *withdraws* — nothing goes away, ever. So the picture of things passing along a road out of the light is dismantled: nothing travels, nothing leaves, and the three roads are not a road. What is left is 3.9.50's screens — powers that show and hide what stands unmoving.

Verse 3.9.62.translation: The seeing of time is differentiated by the positions of capacity, of self, and of deity; that is first in nescience — which does not exist in knowledge.
Verse 3.9.62.commentary: The three schools of time named, and then placed. Sakty-atma-devata-paksaih bhinnam kalasya darsanam: the *seeing of time* is differentiated by the positions of *capacity* (time as sakti — Bhartrhari's own, 3.9.49), of *self* (time as atman, a substance: 3.9.1's Vaisesika), and of *deity* (time as a god — Kala the destroyer, the Mahabharata's dark deity). Three doctrines, three darsanas. And then the placing: prathamam tad avidyayam yad vidyayam na vidyate — *that* is *first in nescience*, *which does not exist in knowledge*. All three belong to avidya — the domain of constructed difference (2.233's sciences describing only avidya) — and none survives into vidya. So the samuddesa's own doctrine, the sakti-paksa, is named as one of three and consigned with the others. This is the most unsparing thing in the samuddesa: it classifies its own position as nescience.

Verse 3.9.63-64.translation: (63) If, time being undivided, an appearance of difference is seen in short, long, prolated and the rest — that is like the cognition of slow and quick. (64) By the repetition of short, long and prolated, in water-clocks and the like — how could there be a connection with accumulation, caused by mere construction?
Verse 3.9.63-64.commentary: An objection about measured time. (63): if time is *undivided* (3.9.48's abhinna), whence the *appearance of difference* in the vowel-quantities — hrasva, dirgha, pluta, the measured units by which recitation is timed? The answer offered: sa cira-ksipra-buddhi-vat — that is *like the cognition of slow and quick* (3.9.35's constructed durations; 2.23's cognitions of slow and quick, which do not themselves differ in time). But (64) presses: hrasva-dirgha-pluta-avrttya nalika-salilaadisu — by the *repetition* of short, long, prolated, in *water-clocks* and the like — katham pracaya-yogah syat kalpana-matra-hetukah: *how* could there be a *connection with accumulation*, caused by *mere construction*? Water accumulates in the clock; the accumulation is measured; and a mere construction does not fill a vessel. The objection is genuine and it is the strongest against the whole doctrine: constructed time does not explain the clepsydra. 3.9.70 will answer it.

Verse 3.9.65-67.translation: (65) It is accumulated by the accumulation of the occasioning ground of manifestation; but the word's reality, though undivided, is not of the nature of accumulation. (66) Thus a difference of a quarter-mora, or of ten such, by an option of measure, does not exist in the word's self. (67) Those words which stand, as it were, in the interval, by a kind of arising-in-consequence, are the means of comprehension for the comprehenders.
Verse 3.9.65-67.commentary: The answer for the word, before the answer for time. (65): abhivyakti-nimittasya pracayena praciyate: it is *accumulated* by the *accumulation of the occasioning ground of manifestation* — what accumulates is the manifesting sound, not the word; abhinnam api sabdasya tattvam apracaya-atmakam: the word's *reality*, though *undivided*, is *not of the nature of accumulation*. So the morae pile up in the utterance and not in the sphota (1.76-77's prakrta and vaikrta dhvani, the quantities lodged in the manifesting sound). (66): hence no *quarter-mora* difference, nor of ten, exists *in the word's self* — the measures are the medium's. (67): and the intermediate words — antarale iva sthitah, standing *as it were in the interval*, arising anunispadi (2.121's entailed) — are *the means of comprehension*: upayah pratipattaye, the ladder (2.38's discardable means). So the measured time of recitation is the manifesting stream's, and the word is untouched. The parallel is being laid for time itself.

Verse 3.9.68.translation: Taking up this or that particular limit, the one time of things possessing moments is constructed as partaking of the differences of instant, month and season.
Verse 3.9.68.commentary: How time's units are made. Visistam avadhim tam tam upadaya prakalpyate: taking up *this or that particular limit* — avadhi: the limit, the reference-point (3.6.2's avadhi from which direction separates; 3.7.137's dhruva). Kalah kalavatam ekah: the *one* time of things possessing moments. Ksana-masa-rtu-bheda-bhak: constructed as *partaking of* the differences of *instant*, *month*, *season*. So the units are made by taking up limits: pick a reference-point and a return, and you have a unit. The instant, the month, the season differ only in which avadhi is taken — the heartbeat, the moon, the solstice. And prakalpyate: constructed. Time is one; the units are chosen limits; and nothing in time favours one choice over another (3.9.47's road, along which distances are reckoned).

Verse 3.9.69.translation: And from a difference in the intellect's grasping, in the standing that is of the nature of practice — just so much is the instant, the time, or the aeons and the world-ages.
Verse 3.9.69.commentary: The units' relativity, stated without hedging. Buddhy-avagraha-bhedat ca: from a difference in the *intellect's grasping* — the units follow how the mind takes hold (3.7.104's buddhy-avastha carving the karakas; 3.7.3's sadhana-vyavahara grounded in a state of the intellect). Vyavahara-atmani sthiteh: in the *standing* that is *of the nature of practice*. Tavan eva ksanah kalo yugam anvantarani va: *just so much* is the *instant*, the *time*, or the *aeons* and the *world-ages*. Tavan eva: just so much — the instant and the aeon are made the same way, by a grasp, and differ only in the grasp's scale. So there is no smallest unit and no largest: the ksana is not time's atom, and the manvantara is not its total. Both are how long a mind held something.

Verse 3.9.70.translation: By obstruction and permission, in that which rests in the aperture of the tube — that trickling in the water: that is time's very activity.
Verse 3.9.70.commentary: The water-clock, and it answers 3.9.64's objection exactly. The nalika is the clepsydra: a pierced vessel set in water, filling through its hole and sinking at a fixed interval. Pratibandha-abhyanujnabhyam: by *obstruction* and *permission* — 3.9.4's two operations, and here they are visible: the tube's wall obstructs, the aperture permits. Nalaka-vivara-asrite yad ambhasi praksaranam: that *trickling* in the *water* which *rests in the aperture of the tube*. Tat kalasya eva cestitam: *that* is *time's very activity* — cestita: 3.9.46's word for what time does. So the clepsydra does not measure time; the trickling *is* time — obstruction and permission, made of brass and water. The objection of 3.9.64 asked how a mere construction fills a vessel, and the answer is that time was never a construction laid over the trickle: the trickle is the holding-and-releasing, which is what time is (3.9.12). The clock is not time's instrument; it is time's portrait.

Verse 3.9.71.translation: Whether the hole be small or large, in connection with that it does not differ; time's operation, and its self too, follows just that of it.
Verse 3.9.71.commentary: The clepsydra pressed, and the result is the samuddesa's. Alpe mahati va chidre tat-sambandhe na bhidyate: whether the *hole* be *small or large*, in connection with that it does *not differ* — time does not run faster through a wide aperture; the clock is calibrated to the hole, and time is not. Kalasya vrttir atma api tam eva asya anuvartate: time's *operation*, and *its self* too, *follows just that of it*. So time's operation follows the trickling and time's *self* follows it too — atma api: even its self. There is nothing behind the trickle that the trickle measures. This completes 3.9.70: the clock is not a portrait of time but time itself, and asking whether the clock is accurate is asking whether the trickling trickles. The verse is the samuddesa's flattest identification, and it is delivered in plumbing.

Verse 3.9.72.translation: That which is seen as it were a playground of time, of the many-formed one, by its own capacities — by that it is manifoldly differentiated among the entities.
Verse 3.9.72.commentary: Time's variety, and the image is a play-space. Akrida iva kalasya drsyate yah sva-saktibhih: that which is *seen* as it were a *playground* of *time* — akrida: a pleasure-garden, a place of sport; the word carries lila, the divine play. Bahu-rupasya bhavesu bahudha tena bhidyate: of the *many-formed* one, it is *manifoldly differentiated* among the *entities*. So the world is time's playground, and time's many forms are its games. The image softens the puppeteer (3.9.4's sutradhara: a stage-manager working the strings) into something less coercive — not a machine driven but a garden played in. And both are true on the doctrine: the holding-and-releasing is a discipline and a sport, and 3.9.15's tethered birds are exactly a game.

Verse 3.9.73.translation: Or bearing the growth of a bark-pithed tree, or of a grass-pithed one — to just that extent, by connection with their growth, time's reality is optioned.
Verse 3.9.73.commentary: Time read from growth-rates. Tvaci-sarasya va vrddhim trna-sarasya va dadhat: *bearing* the *growth* of a *bark-pithed* tree, or of a *grass-pithed* one — two kinds of plant, growing at their own rates: the hardwood slowly, the reed fast. Tavad tad-vrddhi-yogena kala-tattvam vikalpate: to *just that extent*, by *connection with their growth*, *time's reality* is *optioned*. Vikalpate: is optioned, constructed as an alternative (3.9.69's tavan eva ksanah: just so much is the instant). So time's reality is measured out by whatever is growing, and the hardwood's year and the reed's are different lengths of the same year. This is 3.9.35's relational duration (accumulation constructed for one thing by another) with living clocks — and it belongs with 3.9.44-45's birdsong and shadow: time is read from anything that changes, and every reading is a marking.

Verse 3.9.74.translation: Even in the transgression of the measures, there is no transgression of it; there is no difference whatever of the road by a difference of the goer's going.
Verse 3.9.74.commentary: Time's invariance under all its measurements. Vyatikrame 'pi matranam tasya na asti vyatikramah: even in the *transgression of the measures* — even when the units are exceeded, miscounted, or run over — there is *no transgression of it*: time is not violated by a bad clock. Na gantr-gati-bhedena marga-bhedo 'sti kascana: there is *no difference whatever* of the *road* by a *difference of the goer's going*. The road does not change when travellers walk at different speeds. So the measures vary (3.9.73's trees) and the measured does not — and the road-image (3.9.47's adhva-adhikaranam; 3.9.52's three roads) does the work: what is reckoned along is untouched by the reckoning. This is the invariance that 3.9.71's clepsydra seemed to threaten, restored: the trickle is time, and a different hole is a different trickle and the same time.

Verse 3.9.75.translation: By the repetition of the risings and settings of the luminaries, established in the world — though there is no lapsing of time, a likeness of property, as it were, is obtained.
Verse 3.9.75.commentary: The astronomical clock, and its status. Udaya-astamaya-avrttya jyotisam loka-siddhaya: by the *repetition* of the *risings and settings* of the *luminaries*, *established in the world* — the day, the tradition's most fundamental unit, given by the sun's return. Kalasya avyatipate 'pi: *though there is no lapsing of time* — time does not pass; nothing withdraws (3.9.61's na ca kimcin nivartate). Tad-dharmyam iva labhyate: a *likeness of property*, *as it were*, is *obtained*. So the sun's returning lends time an appearance of passing, and the appearance is a borrowed property (1.49's reflection following the water's movement; 3.9.30's krama-rupata worn). The day is not a piece of time; it is a resemblance conferred on the unlapsing by something that comes back. Iva, as always.

Verse 3.9.76.translation: But others — knowers of time — know time as the pulsation of sun, planets and constellations, differentiated by a difference of revolutions.
Verse 3.9.76.commentary: The astronomers' reduction, reported. Aditya-graha-naksatra-parispandam: the *pulsation* of *sun*, *planets* and *constellations* — parispanda: throbbing, vibration, the regular beat of the heavens. Atha apare bhinnam avrtti-bhedena kalam kala-vido viduh: *others* — *knowers of time*, the kala-vids, which is to say the astronomers — know *time* as *that*, *differentiated by a difference of revolutions*. So for the professionals, time simply *is* the celestial motion: no substance behind it, no sakti, just the turning. And the samuddesa reports this without objection, because it is very nearly its own view — 3.9.71 said time's self follows the trickle, and this says it follows the stars. The difference is only that the astronomer stops at the heavens and 3.9.44's correction stands: the stars are a mere mark (cihna-matram tu tarakah), and what they mark is the operating.

Verse 3.9.77.translation: That action which proceeds toward the delimitation of another action, and whose measure is well ascertained — that is expressed as 'time'.
Verse 3.9.77.commentary: The functional definition, and it makes time an action. Kriyantara-pariccheda-pravrtta ya kriyam prati: that *action* which *proceeds toward* the *delimitation* of *another action* — one process used to bound another: the sun's course delimiting the day's work, the clepsydra's trickle (3.9.70) delimiting the recitation. Nirjnata-parimana sa: and whose *measure is well ascertained* — the delimiting process must itself be known-in-measure, or it delimits nothing. Kala iti abhidhiyate: *that* is *expressed* as '*time*'. So time is any well-measured action used to bound another. Not a substance (3.9.1 refused), not a container: an *action*, doing a job. This is 3.9.12's identification (kala eva vyaparah) given as a definition — and it means that anything regular enough can *be* time, which is what 3.9.44's stars, 3.9.73's trees and 3.9.70's water-clock have all been showing.

Verse 3.9.78.translation: In cognition there is a passing-over of the form; by cognition alone there is the gathering-together. Therefore, in the absence of another action, that action is held to be time.
Verse 3.9.78.commentary: Time's limiting case: when there is nothing to measure by. Jnane rupasya sankrantih: in *cognition* there is a *passing-over* of the *form* — sankranti: migration, the word 3.9.39 used for capacities passing over into time. Jnanena eva anusamhrtih: by *cognition alone* there is the *gathering-together* — anusamhrti: 2.1's sixth view of the sentence, the mind's drawing-together; 3.9.57's buddhy-anusamhara. Atah kriyantara-abhave sa kriya kala isyate: *therefore*, in the *absence of another action*, *that* action is held to be *time*. So where no external process delimits, cognition's own gathering does — and cognition's gathering is then time. This completes 3.9.77: time is the delimiting action, and when nothing outside delimits, the mind's own act does, and the mind's act is time. Which is why 3.9.69 could say that the instant and the aeon are both *just so much* as the intellect grasped.

Verse 3.9.79.translation: And this 'the pot was' is the past-ness of Existence alone; 'Existence was' — thus the existence of Existence is called 'past'.
Verse 3.9.79.commentary: The tenses reduced to Existence's tenses. Bhuto ghata iti iyam sattayah eva bhutata: '*the pot was*' is the *past-ness* of *Existence alone* — not the pot's pastness: the pot is nothing over satta (3.1.33), so what was past was Being, potwise. Bhuta satta iti sattayah satta bhuta abhidhiyate: '*Existence was*' — thus the *existence of Existence* is called '*past*'. So every past-tense sentence says: Being was. And the reduction is complete — subject, tense and predicate all collapse into satta and its temporal powers (3.9.60's three saktis not different from existence). This is the Kala-samuddesa reaching what the Jati- and Kriya-samuddesas reached (3.1.33's sole universal; 3.8.62's every root meaning satta): there is one thing, and the tenses are its powers, and 'the pot was' is Being wearing pastness.

Verse 3.9.80.translation: Everything is differentiated by another; but the self is not optioned. Therefore the standing of a mountain and the rest is differentiated by another's form.
Verse 3.9.80.commentary: The principle behind 3.9.79. Paratah bhidyate sarvam: *everything* is *differentiated by another* — nothing differentiates itself (3.9.8's time divided by its commingled things; 3.1.15-16's places conferred by relata). Atma tu na vikalpate: but the *self* is *not optioned* — the thing's own self admits no alternatives; it is not one of several ways it might be. Parvatadi-sthitih tasmat para-rupena bhidyate: *therefore* the *standing* of a *mountain* and the rest is *differentiated by another's form*. The mountain's endurance is measured by what passes, and the passing is not the mountain's. So a mountain does not have a long duration; other things have short ones. This is 3.9.35's relational accumulation and 3.9.73's growth-clocks stated as a general law — and it is why 3.9.79 could reduce the pot's pastness to Being's: the pot never had a tense of its own to lose.

Verse 3.9.81.translation: Operations whose difference is well established, and whose part-actions are of different form, are differentiated by accompaniment from those whose part-actions are of like form.
Verse 3.9.81.commentary: How actions are told apart. Prasiddha-bheda vyaparah virupa-avayava-kriyah: *operations* whose *difference* is *well established*, and whose *part-actions* are of *different form* — cooking and walking: their sub-actions differ visibly, and nobody confuses them. Sahacaryena bhidyante sarupa-avayava-kriyah: those whose *part-actions are of like form* are differentiated *by accompaniment* — two walkings, two cookings: identical in their parts, told apart only by what accompanies them (where, when, whose). So actions of unlike parts are distinguished by their parts, and actions of like parts by their company. And the second class is where time does its work: two identical processes are two only because they are accompanied differently — which is to say, timed differently. The verse quietly explains why time is needed at all: without it, repetitions would be indistinguishable.

Verse 3.9.82-84.translation: (82) The collection of actions such as eating comes, as it were, to interruption; it is seen as though ceased, by reason of the operations in the interval. (83) And though its form is severed, it does not withdraw, from want of a stopping; for every action is apprehended as though intermixed with another. (84) Or every part-action seen in that interval, though a difference exists, is grasped as a limb of it, from likeness.
Verse 3.9.82-84.commentary: The problem of interrupted actions. (82): a meal is interrupted — one pauses, talks, resumes: the collection of eatings *comes as it were to interruption*, vyavadhanam iva upaiti, and is *seen as though ceased*, nivrtta iva drsyate, because of what happened in the interval. Two iva's: the interruption is apparent. (83): na ca vicchinna-rupo 'pi so 'viramat nivartate — though its *form is severed*, it does *not withdraw*, from *want of a stopping*: the eating did not stop, only its manifestation did (3.9.50's screens). And the general truth: sarva eva hi kriya anyena sankirna iva upalabhyate — *every* action is apprehended as though *intermixed* with another; there are no pure processes. (84) then explains the resumption: the interval's part-actions are *grasped as limbs of it*, from *likeness* — sadrsyat, and sati bhede, *though a difference exists*. So an interrupted meal is one meal because likeness lets the intervals be annexed. This is 3.8.4's construed unity, and the interruption shows the construing at work.

Verse 3.9.85-90.translation: (85) A thing must be either existent or non-existent; there is no third whatever. Therefore, apart from past and future, no middle exists. (86) The form of completion, for one thing, is not construed, from the absence of difference; how then could one thing, existent or non-existent, have the form of sequence? (87) And from the non-standing of many, only one is apprehended; and there, remembrance according to apprehension is also possible. (88) On the construction of oneness for everything, the one would be of existent-and-non-existent form; or the form of completion would be the general of completion. (89) Or, being capable in the arising of the effect by its own property — it would be grasped so in its self-reality, and that is present-ness in it. (90) That form of the continuum of action which is inwardly grasped, its reflection having passed over into one place — that they call present-ness.
Verse 3.9.85-90.commentary: The samuddesa's hardest problem: whether a present exists. (85) states it with brutal economy: sad asad vapi vastu syat trtiyam na asti kimcana — a thing must be *existent or non-existent*; there is *no third whatever*. Tena bhuta-bhavisyantau muktva madhyam na vidyate: *therefore*, apart from *past and future*, *no middle exists*. The present is squeezed out: what has happened is past, what has not is future, and there is nothing between. This is the classical argument that eliminates the present, and Bhartrhari states it at full strength. (86-87) press: no completion-form for one thing, no sequence for what is simply existent or non-existent; and from many things' *non-standing*, only *one* is apprehended, with remembrance following. (88-90) then rebuild. (88): construct oneness and the one is *of existent-and-non-existent form* (3.9.59's every entity always both is and is not). (89): or a thing *capable in the arising of the effect by its own property* is grasped so in its *self-reality* — and *that* is *present-ness*: the present is capability-in-act. (90) gives the final account, and its image is the mirror again: kriya-prabandha-rupam yad adhyatmam vinigrhyate — the *continuum of action* which is *inwardly grasped*, sankranta-bimbam ekatra, its *reflection having passed over into one place* — tam ahur vartamanatam: *that* they call *present-ness*. So the present is not a stretch of time but the inward grasping of a continuum, its image gathered into one — which is 3.9.78's cognition-gathering, and 3.9.40's mirror. The present is where the mind's mirror holds the process.
Verse 3.9.85-90.contested: the six-verse passage is the samuddesa's most compressed and its argumentative structure is genuinely hard to reconstruct; we read 85-87 as the objection (no present) and 88-90 as the answer (present-ness as capability, and as the inwardly-gathered reflection of a continuum). Readers who take 88-89 as further horns of the objection will find 90 the sole answer, which the text also permits

Verse 3.9.91.translation: The lapsing of an action has, as its mark, the utter non-arising of the action; and the past is not un-arisen — nor is the future of that kind.
Verse 3.9.91.commentary: A third category distinguished from both tenses. Kriya-atipattih atyantam kriya-anutpatti-laksana: the *lapsing of an action* has as its *mark* the *utter non-arising* of the action — the kriya-atipatti is the grammarian's category for the action that was to have happened and did not (Panini's conditional, the lin of unfulfilled condition: 'had he come, he would have seen'). Its mark is atyantam anutpatti: *utter* non-arising — not merely absent but never to arrive. Na ca bhutam anutpannam: and the *past* is *not un-arisen* — the past arose and is screened (3.9.50). Na bhavisyat tatha-vidham: *nor* is the *future* of that kind — the future has not arisen and may (3.9.51's future not obstructing birth). So the lapsed action is neither past nor future: it is what will never have been. And Sanskrit has a tense for it. The verse identifies a genuine fourth category that 3.9.85's dichotomy (existent or non-existent, no third) cannot accommodate.

Verse 3.9.92.translation: From the arising of a contrary action beforehand, or when a contrary operation has been completed — the domain there is differentiated by a difference of limit.
Verse 3.9.92.commentary: How the lapsed action of 3.9.91 comes about. Prag-viruddha-kriya-utpadat: from the *arising* of a *contrary action* *beforehand* — something incompatible happened first, and the action could not arise: the messenger died, so the message never came. Nirvrtte va virodhini vyapare: or when a *contrary operation* has been *completed* — the incompatible thing finished, and the window closed. Avadhi-bhedena visayas tatra bhidyate: the *domain* there is *differentiated* by a *difference of limit* — avadhi: the limit, the samuddesa's constant (3.9.68's units made by taking up limits). So a lapsing is fixed by where one sets the limit: relative to this moment the action is merely future; relative to that one, it has lapsed forever. The category of the never-to-be is thus limit-relative like every other temporal category — and 3.9.68's doctrine (take up a limit and the units follow) reaches even the tense of what will never have happened.

Verse 3.9.93-94.translation: (93) When the occasioning ground deviates, correctness is not constructed; by the sutra 'bhavya asit', it is taught elsewhere, at that time. (94) If it is correct only in its own time, how is there passage in a difference of time? From the sentence-meaning, distinctness is not established in respect of things not of that meaning.
Verse 3.9.93-94.commentary: A grammatical problem about tense-mismatch. (93): vyabhicare nimittasya sadhutvam na prakalpyate — when the *occasioning ground deviates*, *correctness* is *not constructed*: if a form's licensing condition fails, the form is incorrect (1.27's sadhutva established from the sistas). The sutra cited (bhavya asit — 'what was to be' plus a past 'was') licenses a future-participle with a past verb: a form whose two halves point opposite ways in time. (94) presses: sva-kale eva sadhus cet kala-bhede gatih katham — if it is *correct only in its own time*, *how is there passage* in a *difference of time*? A form correct only in its own tense should not survive transplantation into another. Vakya-arthad atad-arthesu visistatvam na sidhyati: from the *sentence-meaning*, *distinctness* is not established in respect of things *not of that meaning*. So the tense-mismatched construction is a genuine puzzle: the grammar licenses it, and no account of why is forthcoming. The next verses supply one.
Verse 3.9.93-94.contested: the sutra invoked at 93 is compressed to 'bhavya asit' and its identification is uncertain; the construction at issue — a future-sense stem under a past-tense finite verb — is clear enough from 94-101's discussion

Verse 3.9.95.translation: If the part is of that meaning, there would be no understanding of past-ness for what is future; utter past-ness alone would be possible there.
Verse 3.9.95.commentary: The first horn. Tad-arthas ced avayavah: if the *part* — the stem — is *of that meaning*, i.e. genuinely future in sense. Bhavino bhutata-gatih na syat: there would be *no understanding of past-ness* for what is *future* — the two senses would simply conflict and the form would say nothing. Atyanta-bhutatvam eva ekam tatra sambhavet: *utter past-ness alone* would be *possible* there — the finite verb's past would swallow the stem's future entirely, and the construction's whole point (that something *was going to* happen) would be lost. So taking the stem's futurity at face value destroys the form. The horn is real, and 3.9.96 offers the other.

Verse 3.9.96.translation: Even so, the state of having a particular time is prior; but in the qualifier, from its resting on it, and from its being internal — the correct form will be there.
Verse 3.9.96.commentary: The second horn, and it appeals to derivational order. Visista-kalata purvam: the state of having a *particular time* is *prior* — the stem's tense is settled first, before the finite verb arrives. Tatha api tu visesane: even so, *in the qualifier*. Asrayat sah antarangatvat: from its *resting on* it, and from its being *internal* — antaranga: the Paninian priority-notion (3.1.182's antarangatva, operations belonging to the inner layer, settled before the outer). Tatra sadhur bhavisyati: the *correct form* will be *there*. So the stem's futurity is settled antaranga, at the inner layer, and the finite verb's past is applied outside it — and the two do not conflict because they belong to different derivational strata. This is 3.7.182's argument (what is settled antaranga is settled before the outer operations touch it) transferred to tense, and it dissolves 3.9.95's horn: the stem is future *in its layer*, past-ness is predicated *of the whole*, and no contradiction arises.

Verse 3.9.97.translation: That word-meaning, being of such a kind, is commenced only as commingled; the state of being intermixed does not hold for what is isolated, in an eternal meaning.
Verse 3.9.97.commentary: The stem's tense placed. Amisra eva prakrantah sa padarthah tatha-vidhah: that *word-meaning*, being of such a kind, is *commenced only as commingled* — amisra: mixed; the stem never occurs unmixed, and its tense is always already in composition. Kevalasya vimisratvam nitye 'rthe na upapadyate: the state of *being intermixed* does *not hold* for what is *isolated*, in an *eternal* meaning. So the isolated stem, whose meaning is eternal (3.1.2's nityau eva; 3.8.62's every root meaning satta), has no tense at all — tense belongs to the mixture. This resolves 3.9.94's puzzle at the root: the stem does not have a time to be transplanted out of, because unmixed it has no time. Time enters at composition, and 3.9.96's antaranga ordering describes how.

Verse 3.9.98.translation: And what has been explained in the case of pure time does not obtain in the commingled; that correctness, contrary to the time, is taught by the sutra.
Verse 3.9.98.commentary: The two domains separated. Suddhe ca kale vyakhyatam amisre na prasidhyati: what has been *explained* in the case of *pure* time does *not obtain* in the *commingled* — the analysis of time-as-such does not govern composed forms. Sadhutvam ayatha-kalam tat sutrena upadisyate: that *correctness*, *contrary to the time*, is *taught by the sutra*. Ayatha-kalam: not-according-to-time — and it is *taught*, prescribed by rule. So the tense-mismatched form is correct by stipulation, and the stipulation is possible because the stem had no tense to be violated (3.9.97). The grammar simply posts a tense where its operations need one — 3.7.103's niyamah sabde na tu vastuni (the restriction is in the word, not in the thing), now at the finest morphological grain.

Verse 3.9.99.translation: In the meaning expressible by the finite-verb word, principality belongs from its being what-is-to-be-produced; the qualifier, from its being implied by that, stands settled at that time.
Verse 3.9.99.commentary: The construction's semantic architecture. Akhyata-pada-vacye 'rthe nirvartyatvat pradhanata: in the meaning expressible by the *finite verb*, *principality* belongs from its being *what-is-to-be-produced* — the finite verb carries the nirvartya (3.7.45's object to be produced), and hence the sentence's principal (3.8.40's operations expressed as principal by the finite verbs). Visesanam tad-aksepat tat-kale vyavatisthate: the *qualifier*, from its being *implied* by that, stands settled *at that time*. So the stem's tense is not its own; it is implied by the finite verb and settled at the verb's time. This is 3.7.81-82's propagation (the capacity resting on the subordinate follows the principal) applied to tense — and it completes the answer to 3.9.94: the stem has no time, the verb supplies one, and the supplying is aksepa, implication.

Verse 3.9.100.translation: Either an imitation of the comprehension, or else the word's very operation, is superimposed upon a contrary meaning — and it is not thereby contradicted.
Verse 3.9.100.commentary: The mechanism named, and it is superimposition. Sampratyaya-anukaro va: either an *imitation of the comprehension* — the form mimics how the thing is understood; sabda-vyapara eva va: or else the *word's very operation*. Adhyasyate viruddhe 'rthe: is *superimposed* upon a *contrary meaning* — adhyasa (3.1.6's central term; 3.8.11's asad-adhyasa; 3.9.33's phases superimposed). Na ca tena virudhyate: and it is *not thereby contradicted*. So the future-stem's sense is laid over a past-tense meaning, and the superimposition does not contradict because superimpositions never contradict what they are laid on (3.2.10's atyantam atad-atmakam: reality established as though having that for its self while being utterly not of that self). The tense-mismatch is a superimposition, and superimposition is what the whole Padakanda has said every category is.

Verse 3.9.101.translation: These two cognitions, 'past' and 'future', not abandoning present-ness, attain the form of resting on contraries.
Verse 3.9.101.commentary: The resolution, and it is elegant. Bhutam bhavisyad iti etau pratyayau: these two *cognitions*, '*past*' and '*future*' — and note pratyayau: they are cognitions, not times. Vartamanatam atyajantau: *not abandoning* *present-ness* — the cognitions of past and future are themselves present: to think of the past is to think now (3.9.90's present-ness as the inwardly-grasped continuum). Viruddha-asraya-rupatam prapadyete: they attain the *form of resting on contraries*. So past-cognition and future-cognition are both present cognitions resting on contrary contents — which is why a form can be future in its stem and past in its verb without contradiction: both tenses are present cognitions, and present cognitions of contraries coexist perfectly well in one mind. The verse resolves 3.9.93-95's puzzle by relocating tense into cognition, where contraries are ordinary.

Verse 3.9.102.translation: That remainder of the present road, and that which is its commencement — that proximity to the present is shown by difference in the science.
Verse 3.9.102.commentary: The near-past and near-future. Adhvano vartamanasya yah sesah ya upakramah: that *remainder* of the *present road*, and that which is its *commencement* — the road-image again (3.9.52's three roads): the lit stretch has a beginning and a remainder, and things just entering or just leaving are neither simply present nor simply not. Tad vartamana-samipyam sastre bhedena darsitam: that *proximity to the present* is *shown by difference* in the *science* — Panini has rules for the immediate past and the immediate future (the lat used of what is just about to be, the near-past forms). So the grammar recognizes a penumbra around the present and marks it, and the penumbra is the road's ends. The verse is fine observation: languages do mark 'just now' and 'about to', and the samuddesa's road-image predicts exactly such a zone.

Verse 3.9.103.translation: Expectation, though present, is designated in the Bhasya as 'of future time', for the sake of the operation, by reason of its object being future.
Verse 3.9.103.commentary: A case where the grammar names a tense by its object. Asamsa vartamana api: *expectation*, *though present* — hoping is happening now; the hope is a present state. Visayena bhavisyata: by reason of its *object* being *future* — what one hopes for is future. Bhasye bhavisyat-kala iti karyartham vyapadisyate: in the *Bhasya* it is *designated* as '*of future time*', *for the sake of the operation*. So Patanjali calls expectation future-tensed, though it is a present state, because its object lies ahead — and the naming is karyartham, for the sake of the rule's operation, not because anyone thinks hoping happens later. This is 3.9.98's ayatha-kalam sadhutva (correctness contrary to the time, taught by the sutra) with its authority named: the Bhasya does it, and it does it for the machinery.

Verse 3.9.104.translation: In 'cikirsati', desire conforms to its own time; in the base-meaning which is future, it does not conform to that time.
Verse 3.9.104.commentary: The desiderative analysed. Iccha cikirsati iti atra sva-kalam anurudhyate: in *'cikirsati'* ('he wishes to do'), *desire* *conforms to its own time* — the wishing is present, and the form's tense is the wishing's. Bhavisyati prakrty-arthe tat-kalam na anurudhyate: in the *base-meaning* which is *future* — the doing that is wished — it does *not conform to that time*: the doing's futurity does not govern the form. So the desiderative's tense is the desire's, not the desired's — and this is the exact reverse of 3.9.103's expectation, where the grammar took the object's tense. Two closely-related constructions, opposite treatments, and no principle decides: the sastra posts what its operations need (3.9.98). The pair is the samuddesa's evidence that tense-assignment is stipulation.

Verse 3.9.105.translation: From its being dependent on what is expected, in expectation there is the reverse; the speaker's property is attached to the word's meaning by words alone.
Verse 3.9.105.commentary: Why 3.9.103 and 3.9.104 differ. Asamsyamana-tantratvat asamsayam viparyayah: from its being *dependent on what is expected*, in *expectation* there is *the reverse* — expectation's whole structure points at its object, so the object's tense governs; desire's structure centres on the desiring, so the desirer's time governs. Prayoktr-dharmah sabdarthe sabdaih eva anusajyate: the *speaker's property* is *attached* to the *word's meaning* *by words alone* — and this last is the doctrine: the speaker's own temporal position enters the word-meaning through the words, and nowhere else. So tense is where the speaker's now gets into what is said, and it gets in by morphology. This is 3.7.103's niyamah sabde na tu vastuni given its temporal application: the speaker's present is a property of his words, not of his meaning.

Verse 3.9.106.translation: When 'nispad' operates on the conjunction of water and rice-seed, there — from its resting in the part — there is the negation of the future.
Verse 3.9.106.commentary: A worked case. Ap-sali-bija-samyoge vartate nispadir yada: when *'nispad'* (to be produced) *operates* on the *conjunction* of *water* and *rice-seed* — the sowing, where the crop's production is under way but not complete. Tatra avayava-vrttitvad bhavisyat-pratisedhanam: there — from its *resting in the part* — there is the *negation of the future*. The production is already occurring *in its part* (the germination), so it is not future: one says 'it is being produced', not 'it will be'. So the future is negated by the process's having begun anywhere in itself — and the whole-at-each doctrine (3.8.5's pratibhedam samapyate) makes the germination the whole production, present. This is why a sown field is not merely going to bear: the bearing has started, and the tense follows the part.

Verse 3.9.107.translation: But in 'nispad' whose form is the bringing-forth of fruit, there is past-ness; that form, having been superimposed on other properties, is constructed.
Verse 3.9.107.commentary: The complement of 3.9.106. Phala-prasava-rupe tu nispadau bhuta-kalata: in *'nispad'* whose form is the *bringing-forth of fruit* — the production taken as complete, at its fruit — there is *past-ness*. Dharmantaresu tad-rupam adhyasya parikalpyate: that *form*, having been *superimposed* on *other properties*, is *constructed*. So the same root is present when taken at its part (3.9.106) and past when taken at its fruit, and the pastness is a superimposition (3.9.100's adhyasa) laid on the other phases. One process, two tenses, and the difference is where one looks: at the germinating or at the grain. This is 3.9.92's avadhi-bheda (the domain differentiated by a difference of limit) inside a single verb's semantics.

Verse 3.9.108.translation: When the operation of the occasioning grounds has been employed for the accomplishing of the fruit — the form that is superimposed there is understood as of that time.
Verse 3.9.108.commentary: The general rule behind 3.9.106-107. Upayukte nimittanam vyapare phala-siddhaye: when the *operation* of the *occasioning grounds* has been *employed* for the *accomplishing of the fruit* — the causes have done their work. Tatra rupam yad adhyastam tat-kalam tat pratiyate: the *form* that is *superimposed* there is understood as *of that time*. So the tense follows the superimposed form, and the superimposition follows where the causes have got to. Tense is thus a report on causal progress — but a report by superimposition (adhyasta), which means it is a posting on the process rather than a reading of it. And 3.9.107 showed the posting is optional: the same causal state bears a present or a past according to where the limit is set.

Verse 3.9.109.translation: In the completion, some limit is intended, and some other; from dependence on the cause's birth, it is called 'the fruit's birth'.
Verse 3.9.109.commentary: The limits multiplied. Nispattau avadhih kascit kascit prati-vivaksitah: in the *completion*, *some* limit is *intended*, and *some other* — different speakers set the boundary differently, and each setting is a vivaksa (3.7.2's kvacit kimcit vivaksyate). Hetu-janma-vyapeksatah phala-janma iti ca ucyate: from *dependence on the cause's birth*, it is called '*the fruit's birth*'. So one may date the fruit's birth from the cause's beginning (the sowing is the harvest's birth) or from the fruit's own arrival — and the two datings are both correct, being different avadhis. This is why 3.9.106-107 could give one process two tenses: the limit is chosen, and the tense follows the limit (3.9.92's avadhi-bhedena visayo bhidyate).

Verse 3.9.110.translation: Where an accomplishment not dependent on external means is intended — that, from the absence of another means, is taught as 'accomplished'.
Verse 3.9.110.commentary: The limiting case of completion. Abahih-sadhana-adhina siddhir yatra vivaksita: where an *accomplishment not dependent on external means* is *intended* — nothing outside is still required; the process contains everything it needs. Tat sadhanantara-abhavat siddham iti upadisyate: *that*, from the *absence of another means*, is *taught as 'accomplished'*. So a thing counts as done when no further instrument is wanted — completion is defined by the exhaustion of requirements, not by the arrival of a result. This is 3.8.17's krtarthatva (the expectancy of the means withdrawing, its purpose served) given as the criterion for the past tense: something is past when nothing more is needed for it. And it explains the tense-system's deepest feature — that the past is where akanksa has closed.

Verse 3.9.111-114.translation: (111) Therefore, by a difference of limit, past-ness is established as primary indeed; future-ness is existence, when the cause's property is depended upon. (112) From the sense-connection of existent things, that same Existence is distinguished; for practice by way of difference is grounded in another thing. (113) The existence of the mere thing is grasped by the intellect; that difference which comes from approach is not intended there. (114) Or, from connection with femininity and masculinity, nothing whatever stands settled; in its own self there, something else is called past and future.
Verse 3.9.111-114.commentary: The samuddesa's close, and it gathers everything into satta. (111): tasmad avadhi-bhedena siddha mukhya eva bhutata — *therefore*, by a *difference of limit*, *past-ness* is established as *primary indeed*: the past is not a derived or figurative tense but the primary one, and what makes it past is where the limit falls (3.9.109). And: anagatatvam astitvam hetu-dharma-vyapeksane — *future-ness* is *existence*, when the *cause's property* is *depended upon*: the future *is* existence, viewed from the causes. So both tenses are existence under limits. (112): satam indriya-sambandhat sa eva satta visisyate — from the *sense-connection* of *existent* things, *that same Existence* is *distinguished*: the present is Existence sense-connected (3.9.49's darsana-adarsane: the seeing and non-seeing of entities that exist). Bhedena vyavaharo hi vastv-antara-nibandhanah: *practice* by way of *difference* is *grounded in another thing* (3.9.80's paratah bhidyate sarvam). (113): astitvam vastu-matrasya buddhya tu parigrhyate — the *existence of the mere thing* is *grasped by the intellect*; yah samasadanad bhedah sa tatra na vivaksitah: the *difference* that comes from *approach* is *not intended* there — bare existence, intellect-grasped, carries no tense. (114) closes with a grammatical parallel: yogad va stritva-pumstvabhyam na kimcid avatisthate — from *connection with femininity and masculinity*, *nothing whatever* stands settled: as gender settles nothing about a thing (3.6.10's direction-words, feminine, with nothing in direction to be engendered), so tense settles nothing. Svasmin atmani tatra anyad bhutam bhavi ca kathyate: *in its own self* there, *something else* is called *past and future*. The samuddesa that began by refusing time-as-substance (3.9.1) ends by refusing tense-as-property: in a thing's own self nothing is past or future, and the tenses are said of something else — which is Existence, limited (3.9.111).
Verse 3.9.111-114.contested: the four-verse close is compressed and its argumentative joints are hard; we read 111-112 as the positive doctrine (both tenses are satta under limits, the present being satta sense-connected) and 113-114 as the negative (bare existence is tenseless; tense is no more a property than gender). The gender-parallel of 114 is our construal of a genuinely obscure verse

Verse 3.10.1.translation: Inwardness, and also otherness, are the adjuncts of agent and object; the middle and the highest persons are the expressors of those two, by a particularity of the hearing.
Verse 3.10.1.commentary: The definition of grammatical person, and it is built from two adjuncts. Pratyakta: *inwardness* — the turning-inward, the being-here-as-oneself; para-bhavah ca api: and also *otherness*. Upadhi kartr-karmanoh: these two are the *adjuncts* of *agent and object* — upadhi: the limiting condition (3.2.2's asatya-upadhi; 3.9.36's kriya-upadhih, time conditioned by action). So person is not a property of the participants but a condition laid on them: the agent taken inwardly is 'I', taken as other is 'he'.
