Tayoh sruti-visesena vacakau madhyama-uttamau: the *middle* and the *highest* persons are the *expressors* of those two, by a *particularity of the hearing* — the verbal endings differ, and that difference is the whole marking. The Sanskrit order is worth noting: uttama is the first person (the 'highest'), madhyama the second. So the samuddesa opens by making person an adjunct-pair on the karakas — and the next verse will say what the adjuncts are adjuncts *of*.

Verse 3.10.2.translation: Consciousness, whether existent or non-existent, is understood by these two; but the first person does not operate in the portion that is consciousness.
Verse 3.10.2.commentary: The samuddesa's central and strangest claim. Sad asad vapi caitanyam etabhyam avagamyate: *consciousness*, *whether existent or non-existent*, is *understood* by these two — the first and second persons carry caitanyam: to say 'I' or 'you' is to bring consciousness into the sentence, and the sentence works whether or not the consciousness is there (one addresses the dead, the dreamt, the absent). Caitanya-bhage prathamah puruso na tu vartate: but the *first person* — prathama, which in Sanskrit's ordering is the *third* person, the 'he' form — does *not operate* in the *portion that is consciousness*. So 'he goes' says nothing about consciousness; 'I go' and 'you go' do. The third person is the tenseless, consciousness-free default, and the other two are marked for awareness. This is a genuine observation about what the person-system encodes, and it is not the obvious one: person is usually taken to mark participant-role, and here it marks *sentience*.

Verse 3.10.3.translation: With 'budh', 'jna', 'cit' — the first person being present, and their meanings being cognition — the employment of consciousness is not made manifest.
Verse 3.10.3.commentary: The claim of 3.10.2 tested on the consciousness-verbs themselves. Budhi-janati-citibhih: with *'budh'* (to awaken, know), *'jna'* (to know), *'cit'* (to be aware) — verbs whose very meaning is cognition. Prathame puruse sati: the *first person* — the third-person form — being present: 'he knows', 'he is aware'. Samjnanarthaih na caitanyasya upayogah prakasyate: their meanings being *cognition*, the *employment of consciousness* is *not made manifest*. So even 'he knows' does not bring consciousness into the sentence as 'I know' does: the verb's meaning is cognition and the *person* is unmarked for it. The test is decisive and well chosen — if any third-person form were going to encode awareness it would be these — and the result confirms 3.10.2: what the first and second persons carry is not supplied by lexical meaning. It is carried by the ending.

Verse 3.10.4.translation: By some, the meaning of address is accepted everywhere in the middle person; and likewise, in address, they know every first-case form of 'yusmad'.
Verse 3.10.4.commentary: The second person analysed as address. Sambodhana-arthah sarvatra madhyame kaiscid isyate: by *some*, the *meaning of address* is accepted *everywhere* in the *middle person* — every 'you' is a vocative in disguise: to use the second person is to call. Tatha sambodhane sarvam prathamam yusmado viduh: and *likewise*, in *address*, they know *every* first-case form of *'yusmad'* — every nominative 'tvam' is a calling. So the second person is the vocative's home, and the vocative (3.7.163's mere facing-toward, siddhasya abhimukhi-bhava-matram) is what the middle person means. This connects the person-system to the karaka-system's outlier: the vocative was not a karaka at all but the doorway into the sentence (3.7.163), and the second person is that doorway grammaticalized into the verb.

Verse 3.10.5.translation: In the world there is no address by means of a thing that is to be brought about — as 'Svaha! Grow, O slayer of Indra', and 'Be a king'.
Verse 3.10.5.commentary: A limit on 3.10.4's identification, and its example is famous. Sambodhanam na loke 'sti vidhatavyena vastuna: in the *world* there is *no address* by means of a *thing that is to be brought about* — one cannot call something by what it is not yet. And the examples: svaha indra-satrur vardhasva — '*Svaha!* *Grow*, O *slayer of Indra*': the mantra of Tvastr's sacrifice, whose mis-accentuation famously produced a son slain *by* Indra rather than Indra's slayer — the tradition's standing case of a rite destroyed by grammar. Yatha raja bhava iti ca: and '*Be a king*' — one addresses a man and tells him to become a king; one does not address him *as* king. So the address must fix on what is, and what is to be brought about cannot do the fixing (3.7.163's siddhasya: the vocative's target is what is *accomplished*). The Indra-satru example is chosen with some wit: it is the case where addressing something as what it was not yet went catastrophically wrong.

Verse 3.10.6-7.translation: (6) From the accomplished-ness of 'yusmad's meaning, and the fixed initial-acute accent — of 'yusmad' ending in the first case, if it is subsequent, it is not from a word. (7) The difference of subordinate and principal, the inversion of person and the rest, and a designation otherwise in the science — from eternality, there is no contradiction.
Verse 3.10.6-7.commentary: Two technical notes, and the second is the samuddesa's methodological one. (6): yusmad-arthasya siddhatvat — from the *accomplished-ness* of 'yusmad's meaning (3.10.5's siddhasya: the addressed is what stands); niyata ca ady-udattata — and the *fixed initial-acute accent*: 'yusmad' forms have their own accentual rule. Yusmadah prathamantasya paras cen na padad asau: of 'yusmad' ending in the *first case*, *if it is subsequent*, it is *not from a word* — a technical condition on the accent-rule's application. (7) then generalizes: guna-pradhanata-bhedah puruso-adi-viparyayah — the *difference of subordinate and principal*, the *inversion of person* and the rest; nirdesah ca anyatha sastre: and a *designation otherwise* in the *science*. Nityatvat na virudhyate: *from eternality*, there is *no contradiction*. So the grammar's inversions — where person-marking runs contrary to the situation — do not contradict, because the language is eternal and its postings are not answerable to the moment (3.7.103's niyamah sabde na tu vastuni).

Verse 3.10.8-9.translation: (8) The meanings would be as the designation has it, for those of whom the science is the ordainer; but where it stands, having resorted to some general, the conveying is otherwise. (9) As in 'he who is on a horse', 'he who is on a seat' — the horse and the seat being existent — there is the purpose of indication: so is the teaching with respect to meanings.
Verse 3.10.8-9.commentary: The samuddesa closes with a methodological simile of great reach. (8): yatha-nirdesam arthah syuh yesam sastram vidhayakam — the *meanings* would be *as the designation has it*, for those of whom the *science is the ordainer*: if the sastra ordained meanings, meanings would be whatever it said. Kimcit samanyam asritya sthite tu pratipadanam: but where it *stands*, having *resorted to some general*, the *conveying* is otherwise — the science rests on generals it did not make, and its conveying follows them. (9) gives the simile: yo 'sve yah pitha iti atra — as in '*he who is on a horse*', '*he who is on a seat*' — bhutayoh asva-pithayoh, the horse and the seat *being existent*: one identifies a man by what he sits on, and the horse is not thereby part of him. Yatha upalaksana-arthatvam tatha arthesu anusasanam: as there is the *purpose of indication*, so is the *teaching* with respect to *meanings*. Upalaksana: indication, marking-out without describing (3.3.51's saptaparna; 3.4.3's dravya defined by pronominal referability). So the grammar's terms *indicate* their meanings as 'the man on the horse' indicates a man — pointing, not constituting. The Purusa-samuddesa ends by saying what the whole Padakanda has said: the science marks, and does not make.

Verse 3.11.1.translation: Every meaning that is a substance-entity whatever is expressed as possessing number; for the division of difference and non-difference, in the world, is grounded in number.
Verse 3.11.1.commentary: The samuddesa's opening claim, and it is unrestricted. Samkhyavan sattva-bhuto 'rthah sarva eva abhidhiyate: *every* meaning that is a *substance-entity* whatever is expressed as *possessing number* — nothing nominal escapes number (3.1.51's na vina samkhyaya kascit sattva-bhuto 'rtha ucyate: nothing that is a substance-entity is spoken of at all without number). And the ground: bheda-abheda-vibhago hi loke samkhya-nibandhanah — the *division of difference and non-difference*, in the *world*, is *grounded in number*. So number is what makes the one/many distinction available at all: without it there is no telling whether we face one thing or several. Nibandhana — the grounding-word since 1.13. The Jati-samuddesa spent forty verses on when number is *meant* (3.1.51-90); this one asks what it *is*, and it begins by making it the ground of the most basic division there is.

Verse 3.11.2.translation: That property is either distinct from them, or is their very self; having resorted to its being the cause of difference, it is designated as 'number'.
Verse 3.11.2.commentary: The ontology canvassed and declined, in the samuddesa's second verse. Sa dharmo vyatirikto va: that *property* is either *distinct from* them — a real item over and above the things counted (the Vaisesika's samkhya, a quality inhering in substances). Tesam atma eva va tatha: or is *their very self* — nothing but the things themselves. Bheda-hetutvam asritya samkhya iti vyapadisyate: having resorted to its being the *cause of difference*, it is *designated* as 'number'. So whichever it is metaphysically, number is *named* by its office: causing difference. This is 3.7.15 verbatim in structure (the means either distinct from them or their very self; having resorted to separation, constructed as means-hood) — and the same move: the ontology is undecided, the function is definite, and the grammar proceeds identically either way (2.179's licensed double description).

Verse 3.11.3.translation: Inherent in what is to be delimited, it stands somewhere else; it constructs the difference of number for entities, and likewise for its own self.
Verse 3.11.3.commentary: Number's peculiar location. Samaveta paricchedye: *inherent* in what is *to be delimited* — number inheres in what it counts. Kvacid anyatra sa sthita: it stands *somewhere else* — and yet it is elsewhere: the twoness of two pots is not in either pot. Both, and the tension is the point. Prakalpayati bhavanam samkhya-bhedam tatha atmanah: it *constructs* the *difference of number* for *entities*, and *likewise for its own self* — number numbers itself: there are two twos in four, and the numbering of numbers is number's own doing. So number is inherent-and-elsewhere, and reflexive. This is 3.1.28's stratification (the universal of number resting on what rests on the individual) given its structural description — and 3.1.9's regress (universals of universals) reappearing as number's self-application.

Verse 3.11.4.translation: As in priority and posteriority the hearing is alike in respect of difference — so is the state of being expressible by number-words, in the quality that is the cause of difference.
Verse 3.11.4.commentary: A parallel with the directional pair. Paratve ca aparatve ca bhede tulya srutir yatha: as in *priority* and *posteriority* the *hearing* is *alike* in respect of *difference* — 'before' and 'after' sound alike as relational terms, and neither is a property of anything (3.6.4's para-aparatve grounded in difference of place; 3.6.8-9's inversions with standpoint). Samkhya-sabda-abhidheyatvam bheda-hetoh tatha gune: so is the *state of being expressible by number-words*, in the *quality* that is the *cause of difference*. So number-words behave as directional words do: they express a relational condition, not a thing's property. And the parallel is apt because 3.6 already established that direction orders everything and is itself unordered (3.6.6) — which is exactly what 3.11.3 said of number.

Verse 3.11.5.translation: As independence in what is not independent is another's property, in a quality — so also the state of being differentiable in what is not differentiable is a substance's property, in a quality.
Verse 3.11.5.commentary: Two properties a quality wears that are not its own. Asvatantre svatantratvam para-dharmo yatha gune: *independence* in what is *not independent* is *another's property*, in a *quality* — a guna is paratantra by nature (3.5.1's gunatvam paratantratvat) and is spoken of as though free-standing (1.64's guna indicated as though independent, svatantryena). Abhedye bhedya-bhavo 'pi dravya-dharmas tatha gune: the state of being *differentiable* in what is *not differentiable* is a *substance's* property, in a quality. So a quality is treated as independent (which is a substance's way) and as differentiable (which is also a substance's way), and neither belongs to it. Number, being a guna, wears both borrowed properties — which is why it can be counted (3.11.3's numbering of numbers) though it is not the sort of thing that comes in ones and twos.

Verse 3.11.6.translation: Having abstracted it by its own intellect, the world too, having resorted to tradition, explains it by another's property rather than its own — for the sake of comprehension.
Verse 3.11.6.commentary: Why the borrowed properties of 3.11.5 are not an error. Sva-buddhya tam apoddhrtya: having *abstracted* it by its *own intellect* — apoddhrtya: the kanda's constant verb (3.1.1, 3.4.1: word-meanings abstracted from the sentence). Loko 'pi agamam asritah: the *world* too, having resorted to *tradition*. Sva-dharmad anya-dharmena vyacaste pratipattaye: *explains* it by *another's property* rather than its *own* — *for the sake of comprehension*. So the world, not only the grammarians, describes qualities by substance-properties, and does so deliberately, for understanding's sake. This is 2.13's divisions for the avidvan and 3.7.147's separations constructed for instruction — now credited to the loka itself. The borrowing is a teaching-device that everyone uses and nobody invented.

Verse 3.11.7.translation: The word, which expresses with independence the realities of all word-meanings — whose nature is to assist another — is drawn away from their own property.
Verse 3.11.7.commentary: The general diagnosis. Para-upakara-tattvanam: of things *whose nature is to assist another* — every padartha is such, on this kanda's showing (3.7.124's all proceeding for their own becoming, and every one of them assisting; 3.3.5's upakara). Svatantryena abhidhayakah sabdah sarva-padarthanam: the *word*, which expresses *with independence* the realities of *all word-meanings*. Sva-dharmad viprakrsyate: is *drawn away* from their *own property*. So language necessarily misdescribes: it presents as independent what is nothing but assisting, because that is what nouns do (3.8.12's paritah paricchinnam: what is delimited on all sides is spoken of as an entity). The verse generalizes 3.11.5 from qualities to everything: nothing has the independence its name confers on it, and naming confers it regardless.

Verse 3.11.8.translation: Just as a cognition without an object shines forth as nothing whatever — so an entity, uncommingled, is not apprehended as anything whatever.
Verse 3.11.8.commentary: The parallel that justifies 3.11.7's necessity. Yatha eva avisayam jnanam na kimcid avabhasate: as a *cognition without an object* shines forth as *nothing whatever* — an awareness of nothing is not an awareness (3.1.106's cognition never grasped apart from its object's form; 1.124's light that would not shine). Tatha bhavo 'py asamsrsto na kascid upalabhyate: so an *entity*, *uncommingled*, is *not apprehended* as anything whatever. Asamsrsta: uncommingled — 3.1.21's word (neither oneness nor manifoldness nor existence nor non-existence is found in the self-realities of things uncommingled). So the uncommingled thing is as unavailable as the objectless cognition, and for the same reason: both are terms of a relation, taken out of it. This is why language must borrow (3.11.7): there is no unrelated thing whose own property could be expressed.

Verse 3.11.9.translation: But that designation by way of difference which the world too follows along — from tradition, that practice is described as resembling the science.
Verse 3.11.9.commentary: The world's practice given its status. Bhedena tu samakhyanam yal loko 'py anuvartate: that *designation by way of difference* which the *world too* *follows along* — the ordinary way of speaking, which divides everything (3.11.1's division grounded in number). Agamat sastra-sadrso vyavaharah sa varnyate: *from tradition*, that *practice* is described as *resembling the science*. So the world's dividing talk resembles the sastra's — both divide, both do it from tradition, and neither invented it. Sastra-sadrsa: resembling the science. The verse quietly levels the grammarian and the layman (3.3.55's bala and pandita alike on the worldly path): both are running the same inherited apparatus of division, and the scholar's is only more explicit.

Verse 3.11.10.translation: When they thus stand in the intellect, superimposition is not hard to come by; for here the existence or non-existence of another's property is not the occasioner.
Verse 3.11.10.commentary: Why the borrowing works. Buddhau sthitesu tesu evam adhyaropo na durlabhah: when they thus *stand in the intellect*, *superimposition* is *not hard to come by* — once things are held in mind, laying one's properties on another is effortless (3.1.6's adhyaropa; 3.9.100's superimposition on a contrary meaning). Para-dharmasya na hi atra sad-asattvam prayojakam: for here the *existence or non-existence* of another's property is *not the occasioner*. So the superimposition does not require that the borrowed property really belong to the lender: one may lay on what is not there. This is the mechanism that lets 3.11.5's quality wear independence — nothing needs to be independent for independence to be superimposed — and it is 3.3.42's doctrine (the negated thing granted figurative existence so the negation can operate).

Verse 3.11.11.translation: Thus a general is constructed even among generals, particularity among particulars, number among numbers, and gender among genders.
Verse 3.11.11.commentary: The reflexivity of every category, stated as a rule. Samanyesu api samanyam: a *general* even among *generals* — generality has its own generality (3.1.9's universals of universals). Visesesu visistata: *particularity* among *particulars*. Samkhyasu samkhya: *number* among *numbers* (3.11.3's number numbering itself). Lingesu lingam: and *gender* among *genders* — anticipating the thirteenth samuddesa. Evam prakalpate: *thus* it is *constructed*. So every category applies to itself, and the self-application is a construction — prakalpate, not a discovery. The regress is admitted and defused exactly as 3.3.28 defused all regresses (there is no operation of an operation; therefore do not resort to infinite regress everywhere): the self-applications are postings, and postings do not generate real hierarchies.

Verse 3.11.12.translation: Therefore the proponents of commingling call number a thing resting on substance, ordaining difference and non-difference among things that have transcended difference and non-difference.
Verse 3.11.12.commentary: The samuddesa's own position, and it is stated as a paradox. Atah dravya-asritam samkhyam ahuh samsarga-vadinah: the *proponents of commingling* (3.7.9's samsarga-vadins, for whom all entities are capacities and possessors of capacities) call *number* a thing *resting on substance*. And the paradox: bheda-abheda-vyatitesu bheda-abheda-vidhayinim — *ordaining* difference and non-difference among things *that have transcended* difference and non-difference. The things are beyond the one/many distinction (3.1.21's uncommingled realities, bearing neither); number ordains it anyway. So number confers a distinction on what is incapable of bearing it — which is precisely 3.6.13's direction giving parts to the partless atom, and 3.6.14's things without place receiving places. Vidhayini: ordaining, prescribing. Number does not find ones and manys; it legislates them.

Verse 3.11.13.translation: That by which the self of other selves is marked as though of that form — from commingling with what is not of that form: that is the likeness to the occasioning ground.
Verse 3.11.13.commentary: The mechanism of number's ordaining. Atmantaranam yena atma tad-rupa iva laksyate: that by which the *self of other selves* is *marked* *as though of that form* — one thing taking on another's look. Atad-rupena samsargat: from *commingling* with what is *not of that form* — and the lender does not have the form it lends (3.11.10's sad-asattvam na prayojakam: the borrowed property need not exist). Sa nimitta-sarupata: *that* is the *likeness to the occasioning ground*. So number works by conferring on things a likeness to what conferred it — and neither the conferrer nor the conferred has the property. This is 3.2.9-10's intentionality-structure exactly (cognition established as though having the object for its self, while being utterly not of that self), and its recurrence here shows how general Bhartrhari takes that structure to be: it is how *everything* wears what it did not have.

Verse 3.11.14.translation: As, even among the commingled elements, which are partless, effective action is understood — so it is understood among all the measures, in 'sattva' and the rest.
Verse 3.11.14.commentary: The doctrine confirmed on the elements. Samsrstesu api nirbhage bhutesu artha-kriya yatha: as, even among the *commingled elements*, which are *partless*, *effective action* is understood — arthakriya: the criterion of the real (2.288's asadharanam karyam; 2.33's own vocabulary). The elements are partless and commingled, and they *work*: fire burns, water wets, and neither has parts to do it with. Sattvadisu ca matrasu sarvasu evam pratiyate: so it is understood among *all the measures*, in *'sattva'* and the rest — the gunas of Samkhya, sattva rajas tamas: equally partless, equally commingled, equally effective. So partlessness does not prevent working, and commingling does not require parts. The verse defends 3.11.12's paradox by precedent: if partless elements can act, partless things can be numbered — the ordaining does not need joints to work on (3.6.15's partlessness equal for the atom and for the pot).

Verse 3.11.15.translation: Oneness is the womb of duality and the rest, since the differences are preceded by it; without it there is no possibility of the other numbers.
Verse 3.11.15.commentary: The foundation of the number-series. Dvitvadi-yonir ekatvam: *oneness* is the *womb* of *duality* and the rest — yoni: the birth-word (3.1.3's six modifications as the wombs of differentiation; 2.58's samhita as the womb of the words; 3.7.44's case-endings as the wombs of the karaka's differences). Bhedas tat-purvaka yatah: since the *differences* are *preceded by* it — every number is built of ones. Vina tena na samkhyanam anyasam asti sambhavah: *without it* there is *no possibility* of the *other numbers*. So the series rests on one, and one is not itself a member of the series in the way the others are — it is their womb. And this places the samuddesa's central problem: if oneness generates the numbers, what generates oneness? The verses following (3.11.16-17) canvass how duality arises from it, and none of the answers is comfortable.

Verse 3.11.16.translation: Oneness, accompanied by the intellect, is the occasioning ground in the birth of duality; or duality is understood as arisen from two onenesses in this way.
Verse 3.11.16.commentary: Two accounts of how two arises. Ekatve buddhi-sahite nimittam dvitva-janmani: *oneness*, accompanied by the *intellect*, is the *occasioning ground* in the *birth of duality* — one thing plus a mind makes two: the counting is the intellect's, and two is one-as-counted-twice. This is the cognitive account (3.7.104's buddhy-avastha; 3.9.78's cognition's own gathering). Ekatvabhyam samutpannam evam va tat pratiyate: *or* it is understood as *arisen from two onenesses* — two real ones make a real two, no mind required. The realist account. So: is duality a fact about the world or an act of counting? The samuddesa canvasses both and 3.11.17 will add a third — and the reason for the care is that number is where the constructivism of the whole kanda is most exposed. If anything were objectively so, it would be that there are two pots.

Verse 3.11.17.translation: Or it is a collection of onenesses; or, in oneness taken severally, with mutual dependence, there is duality — thus their dual-ending form would arise.
Verse 3.11.17.commentary: The third and fourth accounts. Ekatva-samudayo va: or it is a *collection of onenesses* — two is a heap of ones (3.8.4's samuha, the collection whose non-difference the intellect constructs). Sapekse va prthak prthak ekatve dvitvam iti evam: or, in *oneness taken severally*, with *mutual dependence*, there is *duality* — two ones, each depending on the other for its twoness: neither is two, and their mutual reference is. Tayor dvi-vacanam bhavet: thus their *dual-ending* form would arise — and the morphology follows whichever account is taken. Four accounts now: one-plus-mind, two-ones, a collection, mutual dependence. Bhartrhari does not choose, and the not-choosing is 3.11.2's move (distinct or their very self; the ontology undecided, the office definite). Sanskrit's dual has one form and four possible metaphysics behind it.

Verse 3.11.18.translation: Even one aggregate, by difference of qualities, would construct a difference; for the difference of substratum and what rests on it is grounded in that substratum.
Verse 3.11.18.commentary: How one thing can be counted as many. Eko 'pi guna-bhedena sangho bhedam prakalpayet: even *one* aggregate, by *difference of qualities*, would *construct* a *difference* — one herd is many by its cattle's colours; one man is several by his offices (3.9.32's carpenter and cook; 3.8.63's teacher and uncle). Asraya-asrayi-bhedo hi tad-asraya-nibandhanah: for the difference of *substratum* and *what rests on it* is *grounded in that substratum*. So the counting follows the qualities, and the qualities rest on the thing, and the thing is one. Number therefore does not track things: it tracks the aspects under which things are taken (3.7.104's buddhy-avastha carving one thing into agent, instrument and object). One aggregate, many counts, and the counts are all correct.

Verse 3.11.19.translation: The aggregate of the numbering of an aggregate of countables is called 'number'; in 'twenty' and the rest, it is the differentiator of another substance-aggregate.
Verse 3.11.19.commentary: The higher numbers analysed. Samkhyeya-sangha-samkhyana-sanghah samkhya iti kathyate: the *aggregate of the numbering* of an *aggregate of countables* is called *'number'* — twenty is not twenty things; it is the gathering of the countings of a gathering. Two levels of aggregation: the things are gathered, the countings are gathered, and the number is the second gathering. Vimsaty-adisu sa anyasya dravya-sangha-sya bhedika: in *'twenty'* and the rest, it is the *differentiator* of *another* substance-aggregate — and this second-order number then differentiates a further group. So 'twenty cows' has: the cows, gathered; their countings, gathered into twenty; and the twenty differentiating the herd. This is 3.11.3's number-numbering-itself given its structure, and it explains why the higher numerals behave grammatically as nouns rather than as adjectives.

Verse 3.11.20.translation: In the number 'twenty-one', of the two which resemble another number, there is a construction as though of parts, in one number, by a non-repetition of the intellect.
Verse 3.11.20.commentary: The compound numerals. Eka-vimsati-samkhyayam: in the number *'twenty-one'*. Samkhyantara-sarupayoh: of the *two* which *resemble another number* — 'eka' and 'vimsati' look like the numbers one and twenty, and are not those numbers here (2.169's kupa-supa-yupa: phonetic containment is semantically inert; 2.177's rule: different meaning-behaviour, different base). Ekasyam buddhy-anavrttya bhagayor iva kalpana: in *one* number, by a *non-repetition of the intellect*, there is a construction *as though of parts*. Buddhy-anavrtti: the mind does not go twice — one grasps twenty-one at a stroke, not twenty and then one. So the numeral's parts are apparent (iva) and its grasp is single. This is 2.36's asvakarna-principle (the compound understood whole, its members idle) applied to arithmetic: twenty-one is one number, and its look of composition is the analyst's.

Verse 3.11.21.translation: From its not being an aggregate of numbers, the number-operation is prescribed; but on its being a collection, that would not hold — as with an aggregate of one's own limbs and the like.
Verse 3.11.21.commentary: The argument for 3.11.20. Asamkhya-samudayatvat samkhya-karyam vidhiyate: from its *not being an aggregate of numbers*, the *number-operation* is *prescribed* — twenty-one takes number-morphology precisely because it is not a heap of numbers but one number. Samuhatve tu tan na syat: on its being a *collection*, that would *not* hold. And the illustration: sva-anga-adi-samudayavat — as with an *aggregate of one's own limbs* and the like: a body is not a number of limbs; it is a body, and one does not conjugate it as a plural. So collections do not take number-operations, and twenty-one does; therefore twenty-one is not a collection. The argument is from the grammar's own behaviour (2.443's Panini does not legislate vacuously), and it confirms 3.11.20's single grasp: the morphology treats the compound numeral as a unit because it is one.

Verse 3.11.22.translation: That number which operates among numbers dependent on other countables, and whose countable is a repetition-class — such a number they know as of that kind.
Verse 3.11.22.commentary: Multiplication analysed. Samkhyeyantara-tantrasu ya samkhyasu pravartate: that *number* which operates among *numbers* *dependent on other countables* — a number whose objects are themselves numbers. Avrtti-varga-samkhyeya: and whose *countable* is a *repetition-class* — avrtti-varga: a class of repetitions; 'three fours' counts not four things but the repeating of four. Tam samkhyam tadrsim viduh: *such* a number they know as *of that kind*. So multiplication is the counting of repetitions, and its countables are recurrences rather than things. The analysis is exact and it fits the samuddesa's frame: number ordains difference (3.11.12), and multiplying ordains difference among ordainings — number applying to itself (3.11.3, 3.11.11's reflexivity).

Verse 3.11.23-26.translation: (23) 'Two tens' is not possible either in the number or in the countable — not in the number, from the absence of difference; not in its substratum, from contradiction. (24) The two ten-classes are counted by a number, as 'two tens'; or, in a countable of that form, the repetition is enumerated. (25) 'Number' is not a number; it is a designation, as is said; likewise 'form' is not a form — for that is a designation, in 'white' and the rest. (26) But from connection with the universal 'numbering', a number is called a number; and from connection with the universal 'form-hood', a form is remembered as a form.
Verse 3.11.23-26.commentary: A four-verse knot about self-application, and it is the samuddesa's finest technical passage. (23) states the difficulty: 'two tens' can be neither in the *number* — bheda-abhavat, from the *absence of difference*: ten is ten, and two tens are not two different tens; nor in the *countable* — virodhat, from *contradiction*: the twenty things are not two tens, they are twenty. So 'two tens' has no home. (24) offers two resolutions: the two *ten-classes* are counted (the classes, not their members), or the *repetition* is enumerated (3.11.22's avrtti-varga). (25) then makes the deep move: samkhya nama na samkhya asti — '*number*' is *not a number*: the word 'number' does not name a number; samjna esa iti yatha ucyate, it is a *designation*. And the parallel: rupam na rupam api evam — '*form*' is not a *form*; samjna sa hi sitadisu, it is a designation, in 'white' and the rest. So the category-words are names, not instances (3.11.11's reflexivity now qualified: the self-application is nominal). (26) supplies what saves it: samkhyana-jati-yogat tu samkhya samkhya iti kathyate — from *connection with the universal 'numbering'*, a *number* is called a *number*. So 'number' names a number by way of the universal, not by being one — which dissolves the regress of 3.11.11 exactly as 3.3.28 dissolved all regresses: the self-application runs through a jati and does not iterate.
Verse 3.11.23-26.contested: the four verses are compressed and their argumentative joints are hard; we read 23 as the aporia, 24 as two resolutions, and 25-26 as the deeper diagnosis (category-words as designations, saved by jati-connection). Readers who take 25 as a further horn of the aporia will find 26 the sole answer, which the text permits

Verse 3.11.27.translation: In 'one', the occasioning ground is not expressed by the case-ending; but the oneness of what possesses it — there the case-ending operates.
Verse 3.11.27.commentary: What the singular ending actually says. Nimittam ekam iti atra vibhaktya na abhidhiyate: in *'one'*, the *occasioning ground* is *not expressed* by the *case-ending* — the ending does not name oneness-the-property. Tadvatas tu yad ekatvam vibhaktis tatra vartate: but the *oneness of what possesses it* — *there* the case-ending *operates*. So the singular says: this thing is one — not: here is oneness. The ending predicates number of a bearer and does not name number. This is 3.7.13's doctrine (the karaka-property is not expressed by the noun; the assistance is understood by the case-endings alone) applied to number: the endings mark, and marking is not naming (3.10.9's upalaksana: indication, pointing without describing).

Verse 3.11.28.translation: An accumulation of one is seen, and likewise a collection of two; therefore, apart from the occasioning ground, a different number is the differentiator.
Verse 3.11.28.commentary: The argument for 3.11.27. Ekasya pracayo drstah: an *accumulation of one* is *seen* — one thing, accumulated (many ones); samuhas ca dvayos tatha: and likewise a *collection of two*. So the same oneness appears in accumulations and the same duality in collections — the numbers recur across their instances. Nimitta-vyatirekena samkhya anya bhedika tatah: *therefore*, *apart from the occasioning ground*, a *different number* is the *differentiator*. The number that differentiates is other than the property that occasions it. So there are two things: oneness-the-property (the nimitta) and the number that the ending marks — and the ending marks the second. This confirms 3.11.27: the singular does not name the property; it marks a differentiation the property occasioned.

Verse 3.11.29.translation: And that oneness, though one, apart from the hearing of a case-ending, is not expressed by that word; but together with the case-ending it is expressed.
Verse 3.11.29.commentary: The stem's silence about number. Tad ekam api ca ekatvam vibhakti-sravanad rte na ucyate tena sabdena: that *oneness*, though *one*, *apart from the hearing of a case-ending*, is *not expressed* by that *word* — the bare stem says nothing about number; 'go-' is neither singular nor plural. Vibhaktya tu saha ucyate: but *together with* the case-ending it *is* expressed. So number enters at inflection and not before — which is 3.2.11's finding for the whole word (prior to the case-ending, the meaning of what ends in the suffix does not exist) applied to number specifically. The stem has no number to be transplanted (3.9.97's unmixed stem having no tense), and the ending supplies it. Stem and ending, meaning and number: the division of labour the whole Padakanda has been mapping.

Verse 3.11.30.translation: And agreement and difference would obtain, if there were another number-form; and in the absence of that, the base-meaning is not constructed.
Verse 3.11.30.commentary: The distributional test applied to number. Anvaya-vyatirekau ca yadi syad vacanantaram: *agreement and difference* would obtain — the method of 2.12 and 2.166 (vary the form, watch the meaning) — *if* there were *another number-form*: one can test the singular's contribution by substituting a plural. Syatam asati tasmims ca prakrty-artho na kalpyate: and *in the absence of that*, the *base-meaning* is *not constructed*. Where no alternative form exists, the method cannot isolate the base's contribution — one cannot subtract what has no contrast. So number's semantic contribution is established by contrast and only by contrast, which is why 3.1.51-52 could find the singular and plural unintended (they are obligatory, hence contrastless in most contexts) and the dual intended (it contrasts). The samuddesa's method and the Jati-samuddesa's result agree.

Verse 3.11.31.translation: In 'oneness', 'one' — it is the qualifier of the pure substance; but the base-meaning with its quality is differentiated by the case-ending's meaning.
Verse 3.11.31.commentary: Two levels distinguished. Ekatvam eka iti atra suddha-dravya-visesanam: in *'oneness'*, *'one'* — it is the *qualifier of the pure substance*: the numeral qualifies the bare thing (3.4.3's dravya defined by pronominal referability, with nothing else in it). Sa-gunas tu prakrty-artho vibhakty-arthena bhidyate: but the *base-meaning with its quality* is *differentiated* by the *case-ending's meaning*. So there are two operations: the numeral qualifies the pure substance, and the ending differentiates the qualified base. Number enters twice — once lexically, once inflectionally — and the two are not the same. This is the stratification 3.11.19 found in the higher numerals (the countables gathered, the countings gathered) reappearing between the numeral-word and the ending.

Verse 3.11.32.translation: From the designation 'of two and one', there is a possibility even in mere number; but the well-known state of 'eka' and the rest is said to be having a countable for its meaning.
Verse 3.11.32.commentary: The samuddesa's close, and it lands on the distinction it has been building. Dvy-ekayor iti nirdesat samkhya-matre 'pi sambhavah: from the designation *'of two and one'* — Panini's own phrase (1.4.22: dvy-ekayor dvi-vacana-eka-vacane, prescribing the dual and singular for two and one) — there is a *possibility even in mere number*: the rule's words could mean the numbers themselves, abstractly. Ekadinam prasiddha tu samkhyeya-arthatvam ucyate: but the *well-known* state of 'eka' and the rest is said to be *having a countable for its meaning* — in established usage, 'one' means one *thing*, not the number one. So the numerals mean countables and could mean numbers, and prasiddhi decides (2.253's currency-doctrine; 3.9.62's schools sorted). The Sankhya-samuddesa ends where the Padakanda always ends: two construals available, usage settling it, and the settling neither arbitrary nor found in the things.

Verse 3.12.1.translation: That difference of meaning which is somewhere understood from the atmanepada — and from another substitute of 'la' as well — they consider that to be 'upagraha'.
Verse 3.12.1.commentary: The samuddesa's subject defined ostensively rather than by essence. Ya atmanepadad bhedah kvacid arthasya gamyate: that *difference of meaning* which is *somewhere* understood from the *atmanepada* — the middle-voice endings, which Sanskrit opposes to the parasmaipada (the 'word for another'). Anyatas ca api la-adesat: and from *another substitute of 'la'* as well — the other personal endings. Manyante tam upagraham: they *consider that* to be *upagraha*. So upagraha is not defined; it is *pointed at*: whatever semantic difference the voice-endings carry, that is it. And kvacit — *somewhere*: not always. The definition's evasiveness is honest, because the Sanskrit middle's meaning is notoriously elusive, and the samuddesa will spend twenty-seven pages showing that it has no single one.

Verse 3.12.2.translation: Somewhere it is the means itself; somewhere it is the qualifier of that. There the means is the object and the rest; the qualifier is of clear speech.
Verse 3.12.2.commentary: Upagraha's two roles. Kvacit sadhanam eva asau: *somewhere* it is the *means itself* — the voice-ending marks a karaka: the middle says the agent is also the beneficiary. Kvacit tasya visesanam: *somewhere* it is the *qualifier of that* — it does not mark a karaka but modifies one. Sadhanam tatra karmadi: there the *means* is the *object* and the rest; vyakta-vaco visesanam: the *qualifier* is *of clear speech*. So the voice-ending sometimes *is* a karaka-marker and sometimes merely colours one — two quite different offices in one morphology (3.7.13's assistance understood by the case-endings alone, now with the endings doing two jobs). The samuddesa's problem is set: one ending, two functions, and no rule saying which.

Verse 3.12.3.translation: The action is differentiated by difference of domain, in 'livelihood' and the rest; that difference of action is restricted, by the 'la'-substitutes, even in sentences.
Verse 3.12.3.commentary: Where the voice does its work. Kriya visaya-bhedena jivikadisu bhidyate: the *action* is *differentiated by difference of domain*, in *'livelihood'* and the rest — the same root means differently in different spheres: 'jiv' in the domain of livelihood is not 'jiv' elsewhere. Ladesaih sa kriya-bhedo vakyesv api niyamyate: that *difference of action* is *restricted*, by the *'la'-substitutes*, *even in sentences*. So the voice-endings restrict which domain-sense of the root is in play — they are disambiguators. This is the domain-relativity that 3.9.32 found for the seasons (one time named by what it is doing) and 2.315's fourteen contextual determinants, now carried by morphology: the ending selects the root's sphere.

Verse 3.12.4.translation: The root's meaning, and also its particular, is somewhere called 'upagraha'; the root's meaning would be 'gandhana' and the rest; reciprocity is the qualifier.
Verse 3.12.4.commentary: The two things upagraha names. Dhatv-arthah tad-visesas ca api uktah kvacid upagrahah: the *root's meaning*, and also *its particular*, is *somewhere called* 'upagraha' — the term covers both the sense and its specification. Dhatv-artho gandhanadih syat: the root's meaning would be *'gandhana'* and the rest — gandhana: divulging, informing; the traditional first item in Panini's list of senses that condition the atmanepada (1.3.24ff.: the middle is used when the root means gandhana, avaksepana, sevana...). Vyatiharo visesanam: *reciprocity* is the *qualifier* — vyatihara: mutual action, another of Panini's conditions (1.3.14: karta-sthe ca). So upagraha names both the root's own sense-condition and the modification laid on it, and the samuddesa is tracking a term that its own tradition uses two ways.

Verse 3.12.5.translation: In the proceeding of an action, by some, being-for-oneself and being-for-another is declared — whether unreal or real, grounded in what is intended.
Verse 3.12.5.commentary: The middle's classical meaning, and its status. Kriya-pravrttau akhyata kaiscit sva-artha-para-arthata: in the *proceeding of an action*, by *some*, *being-for-oneself* and *being-for-another* is declared — the standard account: atmanepada means the fruit accrues to the agent, parasmaipada that it goes elsewhere. Asati va sati vapi vivaksita-nibandhana: *whether unreal or real*, *grounded in what is intended*. And there is the samuddesa's verdict in six syllables: vivaksita-nibandhana — grounded in *vivaksa*. Whether the fruit really comes to the agent is beside the point; what the voice marks is whether the speaker *means* it so. Asati va sati: real or not. So the middle does not report a fact about where benefits land; it records an intention (1.155's criterion; 3.7.2's kvacit kimcit vivaksyate).

Verse 3.12.6.translation: For some, in the agent's intention, it is optional together with the causative affix; for others, the atmanepada is as the base for that purpose.
Verse 3.12.6.commentary: The rules' disagreement. Kesamcit kartr-abhipraye nica saha vikalpate: for *some*, in the *agent's intention*, it is *optional* together with the *causative affix* — where the agent intends the fruit for himself and a causative is present, the middle is optional. Atmanepadam anyesam tad-artha prakrtir yatha: for *others*, the *atmanepada* is *as the base* for that purpose — the middle is the base-form for that sense, not an option. So two schools: one makes the middle optional in causatives, the other makes it basic. Vikalpate — optional: and 2.85 already recorded that option is the science's last resort. The disagreement is evidence that no fact settles the middle's application — which 3.12.5 predicted, since what it marks is an intention and intentions are not read off situations.

Verse 3.12.7.translation: 'Krinisva', 'vapate', 'dhatte', 'cinoti' and also 'cinute' — usages of the authoritative are seen, in which the causative's meaning is expressed.
Verse 3.12.7.commentary: Attested usage against the rules. Apta-prayogah drsyante: *usages of the authoritative* are *seen* — apta: the trustworthy, the sistas whose usage is the criterion (1.27's sadhutva established from the cultivated). And the list: krinisva (buy!), vapate (he sows), dhatte (he places), cinoti and cinute (he gathers, active and middle both). Yesu ny-arthah abhidhiyate: in which the *causative's meaning* is *expressed* — these forms carry a causative sense without causative morphology (3.7.73's antarbhuta-nij-artha: roots with the causative included within). And cinoti / cinute: the same root in both voices, both attested, both authoritative. So the sistas use both, and no rule distinguishes them. The verse is a small dossier of counter-examples, and its force is that usage outruns the rules (2.483's the world's practice, followed by the science).

Verse 3.12.8.translation: The arranging of 'paci' and the rest is somewhere understood as the meaning; occasioned by that, another action too, such as setting-on-the-fire and the like, is understood.
Verse 3.12.8.commentary: The middle marking the whole enterprise. Samvidhanam pacadinam kvacid arthah pratiyate: the *arranging* of *'paci'* and the rest is somewhere understood as the *meaning* — 'pacate' can mean not the cooking but the *managing* of a cooking: the householder who has the meal made. Tan-nimitta yatha anya api kriya adhisrayanadika: *occasioned by that*, *another* action too, such as *setting-on-the-fire* and the like, is understood. So the middle can shift the verb from the act to its supervision, and the subordinate acts come along. This is a real and delicate function — Sanskrit's middle does mark a kind of managerial involvement — and it is a third office beside 3.12.2's two. The samuddesa keeps finding new things the ending does.

Verse 3.12.9.translation: In the sutra, the agent's-intention is an indication of a difference of action; for that action which is of such a kind — its agent is the one who partakes of the fruit.
Verse 3.12.9.commentary: The rule's key term reinterpreted. Kartr-abhiprayata sutre kriya-bheda-upalaksanam: in the *sutra*, the *agent's-intention* is an *indication of a difference of action* — Panini's condition (1.3.72: svaritanitah kartr-abhiprayekriya-phale, the middle where the fruit is intended for the agent) does not name a psychological state; it *indicates* a kind of action. Upalaksana: indication, marking-out without describing (3.10.9's man on the horse; 3.3.51's saptaparna). Taya-bhuta kriya ya hi tat-karta phala-bhag yatah: for *that* action which is *of such a kind* — its *agent* is the one who *partakes of the fruit*. So 'agent's intention' names an action-type, and the type is one whose agent gets the fruit. The reinterpretation matters: it moves the middle's condition from the speaker's mind into the action's structure — and 3.12.10 will explain how such a structure is detected.

Verse 3.12.10.translation: As time is indicated by the appearance of stars and the like — so a difference of action is exhibited by a particular fruit.
Verse 3.12.10.commentary: How 3.12.9's action-type is known, and the simile is the Kala-samuddesa's. Yatha upalaksyate kalah taraka-darsanadibhih: as *time* is *indicated* by the *appearance of stars* and the like — 3.9.44's very doctrine (the naksatras following time's operation, cihna-matram tu tarakah: the stars are a *mere mark*). Tatha phala-visesena kriya-bhedo nidarsyate: so a *difference of action* is *exhibited* by a *particular fruit*. So one reads the action's type from where its fruit lands, as one reads the hour from the stars — and in both cases the mark is not the thing. The fruit indicates the action-type; it does not constitute it. This is why 3.12.9 could relocate the middle's condition from intention to action-structure without making it a physical fact: the structure is read off a mark, and marks are marks (3.9.45's birdsong and shadow).

Verse 3.12.11.translation: For some, in the expressing of a particular action, the capacity is obstructed; but others are made — those with svarita-marker, and likewise those with n-marker.
Verse 3.12.11.commentary: The technical apparatus. Kriya-visesa-vacane samarthyam uparudhyate kesamcid: for *some*, in the *expressing of a particular action*, the *capacity* is *obstructed* — where the root already expresses the special action, the voice-ending's capacity is blocked (it has nothing left to do). Anye tu krtah svarita-itah nitas tatha: but *others are made* — those with the *svarita*-marker and those with the *n*-marker: Panini's anubandhas, the diacritic markers on roots in the Dhatupatha that determine which voice a root takes (1.3.72's svaritanitah). So the roots are pre-sorted by invisible markers, and the sorting decides the voice. The verse introduces the samuddesa's last topic: whether these markers encode a meaning or merely a class.

Verse 3.12.12.translation: And a marker is attached, for the sake of remembrance, in a meaning already established; and even among things of like meaning, there is not necessarily one property for all.
Verse 3.12.12.commentary: The markers' status, and it is deflationary. Anubandhas ca siddhe 'rthe smrty-artham anusajyate: a *marker* is *attached*, *for the sake of remembrance*, in a *meaning already established* — the anubandha does not create the root's behaviour; it is a mnemonic for behaviour the usage already has (2.13's divisions for the unlearned; 3.11.6's world explaining by another's property for comprehension's sake). Tulya-arthesv api ca avasyam na sarvesv eka-dharmata: and even among things of *like meaning*, there is *not necessarily* *one property* for *all*. So roots that mean alike need not behave alike, and the markers record the behaviour rather than deriving it from the meaning. This is the samuddesa's central negative result: the voice-assignment is not a function of the sense.

Verse 3.12.13.translation: In 'drs' and 'iks', though the meaning is similar, there is no non-difference when preceded by 'prati'; therefore those which take up the causative's meaning are not of like meaning with 'paci' and the rest.
Verse 3.12.13.commentary: The evidence for 3.12.12. Drsi-iksyoh sadrse 'py arthe: in *'drs'* (to see) and *'iks'* (to look), *though the meaning is similar* — two roots, near-synonyms. Na abhedah prati-purvayoh: there is *no non-difference* when *preceded by 'prati'* — pratidrs and pratiks behave differently, though drs and iks are alike. Ny-artha-upadayinah tasman na tulya-arthah pacadibhih: *therefore* those which *take up the causative's meaning* are *not of like meaning* with 'paci' and the rest. So near-synonymous roots diverge under the same preverb, and roots with built-in causative sense are not equivalent to plain roots. The verse is fine distributional evidence (2.12's anvaya-vyatireka) that meaning does not predict behaviour — which is why the markers of 3.12.11 are needed.

Verse 3.12.14.translation: Of 'kr', standing in the meaning of 'umbh', whose properties are different — the taking-up of the causative's meaning; therefore the capacities of words are fixed.
Verse 3.12.14.commentary: The samuddesa's methodological conclusion, and it is the Padakanda's. Umbhy-arthe vartamanasya karoteh bhinna-dharmanah: of *'kr'* (to do), *standing in the meaning of 'umbh'* (to fill), *whose properties are different* — the same root in a borrowed sense behaves by other rules. Ny-artha-upadayita: the *taking-up of the causative's meaning*. Tasman niyatah sabda-saktayah: *therefore* the *capacities of words* are *fixed*. Niyatah — fixed: not derivable, not predictable from meaning, simply so (3.3.29's yogyata: the word-meaning fitness, beginningless, as the eye's for colour; 2.97's siddha niyata, established and fixed). So the samuddesa's answer to what the middle means is: nothing derivable. The voice-capacities are fixed as sense-capacities are fixed, and one learns them rather than deducing them. Which is why 3.12.1 could only point at upagraha and not define it.

Verse 3.12.15.translation: For thus, in the atmanepada of 'kr' which is a subsequent employment — the taking-up 'as before' being obtained, the svarita presented itself.
Verse 3.12.15.commentary: A technical case where a marker is invoked to save a rule. Anuprayogasya karoteh atmanepade: in the *atmanepada* of *'kr'* which is a *subsequent employment* — the periphrastic perfect (edham cakre), where 'kr' is added after the reduplicated stem and must take the same voice as the main root. Purvavad-grahanam prapte: the taking-up *'as before'* being *obtained* — Panini's rule that the auxiliary follows the main root's voice (1.3.63: aam pratyayavat krno 'nuprayogasya). Svaritam samupasthitam: the *svarita* *presented itself* — the marker (3.12.11's svarita-it) is what makes the rule applicable. So the anubandha does real work in the derivation, and 3.12.12's account of it as a mere mnemonic is under pressure: a mnemonic does not condition a rule. The verse is honest about the tension it creates.

Verse 3.12.16.translation: Even where the action stated is one, it is differentiated by the number of the means' substratum; but the difference called 'gender' is not there dependent on that.
Verse 3.12.16.commentary: Number and gender contrasted in their reach into the verb. Ekatve 'pi kriya-akhyate sadhana-asraya-samkhyaya bhidyate: even where the *action stated is one*, it is *differentiated* by the *number* of the *means' substratum* — the verb agrees in number with its agent: one cooking, and 'pacati' or 'pacanti' according to how many cook. Na tu linga-akhyo bhedas tatra tad-asritah: but the difference called *'gender'* is *not there dependent on that* — Sanskrit verbs do not agree in gender. So number reaches the verb and gender does not, though both are properties of the same nouns. The asymmetry is a fact about the language and it prepares the Linga-samuddesa: gender is a nominal affair, sealed off from the verb, which is why it can be (as 3.13 will argue) a property of words rather than of things.

Verse 3.12.17.translation: Therefore, even where the meaning stands settled, the capacity of some word is obstructed; and that very thing is what is expounded in the science as a rule.
Verse 3.12.17.commentary: The samuddesa's central finding, stated as a general law. Tasmad avasthite 'py arthe kasyacit pratibadhyate sabdasya saktih: *therefore*, even where the *meaning stands settled*, the *capacity* of *some word* is *obstructed* — the meaning is there, the word could express it, and its capacity is blocked (3.9.24's jara obstructing a capacity while its contraries are released). Sa tv esa sastre 'nvakhyayate vidhih: and *that very thing* is what is *expounded in the science* as a *rule*. So the grammar's rules *are* these obstructions: a rule is a place where a word's capacity is blocked though the meaning would license it. This is a striking account of what a grammatical rule is — not a description of how meaning maps to form, but a record of where the mapping is prevented. And it follows from 3.12.13-14's finding: capacities are fixed, not derivable, so the rules record blockages rather than deriving expressions.

Verse 3.12.18.translation: That meaning for the accomplishing of which 'paci' and the rest are undertaken — that is their principal fruit; profit and the like is not their purpose.
Verse 3.12.18.commentary: The verb's fruit distinguished from the agent's motive. Yasya arthasya prasiddhy-artham arabhyante pacadayah: that *meaning* for the *accomplishing* of which *'paci'* and the rest are *undertaken*. Tat pradhanam phalam tesam: *that* is their *principal fruit* — the cooking's fruit is the cooked, and nothing else. Na labhadi prayojanam: *profit* and the like is *not* their *purpose* — the cook may cook for money, and the money is not the cooking's fruit. So the verb's phala is internal to the action and the agent's motives are external — which matters for the middle, since 3.12.9 made its condition 'the agent partakes of the fruit'. If the fruit is the cooked rice and not the wages, then the cook who cooks for hire still partakes of the fruit, and the middle should apply. The distinction is being drawn to control the next verses' hard case.

Verse 3.12.19.translation: But where both master and servant undertake a joint action — the root does not there operate simultaneously, by a difference of properties.
Verse 3.12.19.commentary: The hard case. Yatra ubhau svami-dasau tu prarabhete saha-kriyam: where *both* *master and servant* *undertake* a *joint action* — both are cooking, in different senses: the master by arranging (3.12.8's samvidhana), the servant by stirring. Yugapad dharma-bhedena dhatus tatra na vartate: the *root* does *not there operate* *simultaneously*, by a *difference of properties*. One verb cannot carry both relations at once (3.7.144's cloud, where contradictory properties cannot be intended simultaneously). So 'they cook' cannot mean master-arranging and servant-stirring in one breath. The case is well chosen: it is exactly where the middle's fruit-condition (3.12.9) becomes undecidable, since both partake and differently. And 3.12.20 gives the resolution.

Verse 3.12.20.translation: Where 'paci' has the meaning of arranging, there the atmanepada; the parasmaipada elsewhere, when it expresses the refinement and the rest.
Verse 3.12.20.commentary: The resolution of 3.12.19, and it splits the verb. Yatra prati-vidhana-arthah pacih: where *'paci'* has the meaning of *arranging* — the master's sense (3.12.8's samvidhana). Tatra atmanepadam: *there* the *atmanepada*. Parasmaipadam anyatra samskaradyabhidhayini: the *parasmaipada* *elsewhere*, when it expresses the *refinement* and the rest — the servant's sense, the actual working of the food. So the voice sorts the two senses: the manager takes the middle, the labourer the active. And this is a real fact about Sanskrit — the middle does carry a shade of self-involved supervision — given here its precise statement. The verse resolves 3.12.19 by denying that one verb must serve both: two senses, two voices, and the joint action described twice.

Verse 3.12.21.translation: And from the proximity of the arranger, the property is attached to the servant — as, from the proximity of the word 'plaksa', plaksa-ness attaches to the nyagrodha.
Verse 3.12.21.commentary: How the master's property leaks onto the servant. Samvidhatus ca sannidhyat dase dharmo 'nusajyate: from the *proximity of the arranger*, the *property* is *attached* to the *servant* — the servant, working beside the master, takes on the master's arranging-property, and so the middle can be used of him too. And the simile: plaksa-sabdasya sannidhyan nyagrodhe plaksata yatha — as, from the *proximity of the word 'plaksa'*, *plaksa-ness* attaches to the *nyagrodha*: two fig-trees named together, and the first's name colours the second (the grammarians' standing example of ekasesa-adjacent contamination). So properties leak by proximity, in trees and in households alike. This is 2.299's lamp lighting what it was not brought for, and 3.11.13's likeness to the occasioning ground: what stands near takes on what it does not have.

Verse 3.12.22.translation: And the designation 'purodasa' is as it stands in respect of the grains and the rest; and from connection with an umbrella-bearer, there is the state of being expressible by the word 'umbrella-bearer'.
Verse 3.12.22.commentary: Two more cases of leaking designation. Purodasa-abhidhanam ca dhanadisu yatha sthitam: the designation *'purodasa'* (the sacrificial cake) is as it *stands* in respect of the *grains* and the rest — the name of the finished cake is applied to the grains it will be made from (3.7.7's janyamana karman: the object that does not exist until the action completes, held in place by buddhi). Chatrina ca abhisambandhat chatri-sabda-abhidheyata: and from *connection with an umbrella-bearer*, there is the state of being expressible by the word *'umbrella-bearer'* — the classic chatrino gacchanti: 'the umbrella-bearers go', said of a group in which one has an umbrella. So the name leaks from the future thing to the present stuff, and from one man to his company. Both are 3.12.21's mechanism, and both are ordinary speech.

Verse 3.12.23.translation: Mutual being-for-another, understood from the meaning, is unintended; thus this is described by some as the domain of the 'remainder'.
Verse 3.12.23.commentary: The relation of master and servant placed grammatically. Arthat pratitam anyonyam para-arthyam avivaksitam: *mutual being-for-another*, *understood from the meaning*, is *unintended* — each works for the other (the servant for the master's meal, the master for the servant's wage), and the mutuality is not what anyone means to say. Avivaksita: unintended (1.136's canon; 3.1.51's forced categories). Ity ayam sesa-visayah kaiscid atra anuvarnyate: thus *this* is described by *some* as the *domain of the 'remainder'* — the sesa, the genitive's territory, where relations stand that no karaka covers (3.7.44's sasthi sese). So the master-servant relation is a sesa-relation: real, understood, unintended, and expressed by the genitive if at all. It is not a karaka and it does not condition the voice.

Verse 3.12.24.translation: Now, that arranger who ploughs with five ploughs — why has it not been cited in the Bhasya? The atmanepada is obtained there.
Verse 3.12.24.commentary: An objection from the Bhasya's silence. Atha prati-vidhata yo halaih krsati pancabhih: the *arranger* who *ploughs* with *five ploughs* — a man who manages five ploughmen: an arranger by 3.12.8's criterion, and 'krsati' is active. Bhasye na udahrtam kasmat: *why has it not been cited* in the *Bhasya*? Praptam tatra atmanepadam: the *atmanepada* is *obtained* there — by 3.12.20's rule, the arranging sense should take the middle, and Patanjali does not list this case. So either the rule is wrong or the Bhasya is incomplete. The objection is from authority's silence, and it is a real move in this tradition: what the Bhasya does not say, it means not to say (2.475's bhasye eva upavarnitah, where the citation was positive). Bhartrhari takes the silence seriously enough to answer it.

Verse 3.12.25.translation: If, from the meaning's being understood, remainder-hood is constructed — that option, being already obtained, would not be the excluder of those having a svarita-marker.
Verse 3.12.25.commentary: The first answer to 3.12.24, and it is rejected. Pratitatvat tad-arthasya sesatvam yadi kalpyate: if, from the *meaning's being understood*, *remainder-hood* is *constructed* — if one says the ploughing-arrangement is a sesa-relation (3.12.23) and so does not condition the voice. Na syat prapta-vibhasa asau svarita-itam nivartika: that *option*, being *already obtained*, would *not be* the *excluder* of those having a *svarita-marker*. The reasoning: if the sense were sesa, no option would arise at all, and the rule providing an option for svarita-marked roots would have nothing to exclude. Prapta-vibhasa: an option on what is already obtained — the technical category. So the sesa-answer proves too much: it removes the option the grammar provides, and grammars do not provide vacuous options (2.443's Panini does not legislate vacuously).

Verse 3.12.26.translation: But in the pure meaning of arranging, 'krs' is accepted here by some; the atmanepada would not be there, as with 'yaji' whose property is that.
Verse 3.12.26.commentary: The second answer. Suddhe tu samvidhana-arthe kaiscid atra isyate krsih: in the *pure* meaning of *arranging*, *'krs'* is accepted here by *some* — the ploughing taken as pure management, no working. Tad-dharma yajir iti evam na syat tatra atmanepadam: the *atmanepada* would *not be* there, as with *'yaji'* whose *property is that* — 'yaj' (to sacrifice) is a root whose sense is inherently managerial (the sacrificer arranges; the priests act) and which nonetheless takes the middle by its marker, not by its sense. So if 'krs' in the pure arranging sense behaved like 'yaj', it would take the middle — and it does not. Therefore the arranging sense alone does not confer the middle, and 3.12.20's rule needs a further condition. The answer is offered and the verse's structure leaves it standing as a report.

Verse 3.12.27.translation: But here this difference of meaning is understood by the upapada; the option is made on what is already obtained — therefore there is no atmanepada.
Verse 3.12.27.commentary: The samuddesa's resolution of 3.12.24, and it is grammatical rather than semantic. Atra tu upapadena ayam artha-bhedah pratiyate: *here* this *difference of meaning* is understood by the *upapada* — the accompanying word ('with five ploughs') carries the arranging-sense, not the root. Prapte vibhasa kriyate: the *option* is made on what is *already obtained*. Tasman na asti atmanepadam: *therefore* there is *no atmanepada*. So the ploughman's case takes the active because the arranging-sense comes from the upapada and not from 'krs' itself — and the voice follows the root's own capacity (3.12.14's niyatah sabda-saktayah: the capacities of words are fixed), not the sentence's sense. This is the samuddesa's thesis holding under pressure: the voice is a property of the word, fixed, not derivable from what the sentence means. The Bhasya's silence is thus explained: there was no case to cite.

Verse 3.13.1-2.translation: (1) The connection with breasts, hair and the rest; or the breasts and the rest as qualified; the universal manifested by those; the state of the qualities; and likewise the qualities themselves; (2) the meaning-self generated by the word; and 'refinement of the word' as well — seven alternatives concerning gender have been shown by those who know gender's reality.
Verse 3.13.1-2.commentary: The samuddesa opens with a doxographic list, and its seven entries map the whole dispute. (i) Stana-kesadi-sambandhah: the *connection with breasts, hair* and the rest — gender as the possession of secondary sexual characters: the crudest account, and the tradition's starting-point. (ii) Visista va stanadayah: or the breasts and the rest *as qualified* — not the connection but the characters themselves, specified. (iii) Tad-upavyanjana jatih: the *universal manifested* by those — femininity as a jati, of which the characters are the manifestors (3.1.29's face manifested by water). (iv) Guna-avastha: the *state of the qualities*. (v) Gunas tatha: and *likewise the qualities* themselves — gender as a quality, not a universal. (vi) Sabda-upajanito 'rtha-atma: the *meaning-self generated by the word* — gender as something the word *makes*: the boldest entry, and the one Bhartrhari's system favours (3.1.6's every word expressing its own universal first). (vii) Sabda-samskara iti api: and *'refinement of the word'* — gender as a purely morphological fact, no meaning at all. Seven, and note the drift: from bodies, through universals and qualities, to the word's own doing. The list's order is an argument.

Verse 3.13.3.translation: And seven alternatives of assumption concerning genders have been described, which stand established among words by option and by conjunction.
Verse 3.13.3.commentary: A second seven, and it is about the words rather than the things. Upadana-vikalpas ca lingaanam sapta varnitah: seven alternatives of *assumption* concerning genders have been *described* — how a word *takes up* its gender, as against what gender is. Vikalpa-samniyogabhyam ye sabdesu vyavasthitah: which stand established among *words* by *option* and by *conjunction* — some words take gender optionally (the tradition's long lists of dvi-linga and tri-linga stems), some by fixed conjunction. So there are two sevens: seven theories of what gender is, and seven ways words get it. And the second seven's existence is already an argument for the second half of the first: if words take gender by option and conjunction, gender is at least partly a fact about words (entries vi and vii).

Verse 3.13.4.translation: For some, these are just three universals, standing established — not in conflict with the mutually conflicting universals of cow, buffalo and the rest.
Verse 3.13.4.commentary: The jati-account (entry iii) stated. Tisro jataya eva etah kesamcit samavasthitah: for *some*, these are just *three universals* — femininity, masculinity, neutrality, each a jati. Aviruddha viruddhabhih go-mahisyadi-jatibhih: *not in conflict* with the *mutually conflicting* universals of *cow*, *buffalo* and the rest. The point is careful: cowhood and buffalohood are mutually exclusive — nothing is both — but femininity is not exclusive of either; a cow is female *and* a cow. So the gender-universals cross-cut the species-universals, which is unusual: most jatis nest or exclude. Hence gender-universals are a special class, pervading the others (3.1.44's brahminhood standing present in all living beings). The account is coherent and its cost is a second, orthogonal system of universals.

Verse 3.13.5.translation: In a cow-elephant and in a mare, there is a concomitance of the cognition 'female'; therefore they desire that universal, inhering in substance and the rest.
Verse 3.13.5.commentary: The argument for 3.13.4. Hastinyam vadavayam ca stri iti buddheh samanvayah: in a *cow-elephant* and in a *mare*, there is a *concomitance* of the *cognition 'female'* — the same cognition arises across species: one recognizes femaleness in an elephant and in a horse, and the recognition is one. Samanvaya: concomitance, the running-together that is a universal's mark (3.1.14's anuvrtti, recurrence). Atas tam jatim icchanti dravyadi-samavayinim: *therefore* they desire that *universal*, *inhering in substance* and the rest. So the recurrent cognition evidences a recurrent universal, by the standard inference. The argument is the tradition's best for gender-realism, and its evidence is genuine: femaleness *is* recognized across kinds, and that is what universals explain.

Verse 3.13.6.translation: That gender which belongs to what is dependent, when abstraction is intended — there that 'refinement of the word' rests upon words alone.
Verse 3.13.6.commentary: The seventh entry (sabda-samskara) placed. Paratantrasya yal lingam apoddhare vivaksite: that *gender* which belongs to what is *dependent* — the adjective's gender, which is not its own but taken from its noun — *when abstraction is intended*: when the analyst extracts the word (3.1.1's apoddhara). Tatra asau sabda-samskarah sabdaih eva vyapasritah: *there* that *'refinement of the word'* *rests upon words alone*. So the dependent word's gender is purely morphological — 'sukla' is feminine in 'sukla gauh' because 'gauh' is, and nothing about whiteness is female. The seventh account is thus certainly right for *some* cases: adjectival gender is agreement, and agreement is a fact about words. The question is how far the concession runs — and 3.13.7 extends it.

Verse 3.13.7.translation: And there is a possibility even in genders whose form is constructed by the intellect; for that settled arrangement of femininity and the rest is designated by the genders.
Verse 3.13.7.commentary: The concession extended. Buddhya kalpita-rupesu lingesv api ca sambhavah: there is a *possibility* even in *genders whose form is constructed by the intellect* — genders assigned to what has no sex: 'tata' (the shore, masculine), 'nadi' (the river, feminine), 'kula' (the bank, neuter) — the same water's edge in three genders. Stritvadinam vyavastha hi sa lingair vyapadisyate: for that *settled arrangement* of femininity and the rest *is designated by the genders*. So where there is no sex, the gender is buddhi-constructed and the endings designate the construction. And since most Sanskrit nouns name sexless things, the constructed case is the *normal* one. This is the samuddesa's decisive turn: 3.13.5's cross-species femaleness is real and rare; gender's ordinary employment has nothing to do with it.

Verse 3.13.8-9.translation: (8) As an appearance of water arises in mirages — a cognition arising, where there is no water, from a seed conducive to the apprehension of water; (9) just so, from indescribable causes, differences among stars and the like stand established in the world, as though from primary genders.
Verse 3.13.8-9.commentary: The mirage, and it is the samuddesa's central image. (8): salila-nirbhasa mrga-trsnasu jayate — an *appearance of water* arises in *mirages*; jala-upalabdhy-anugunad bijad buddhir jale asati: a *cognition* arising, *where there is no water*, from a *seed* conducive to the apprehension of water. Bija: the seed, the trace (1.84's ahita-bija; 3.9.39's deposits). So the mirage's water comes from a disposition in the seer, not from the desert. (9) applies it: tatha eva avyapadesyebhyo hetubhyah taraka-adisu — just so, from *indescribable causes*, among *stars* and the like — bheda loke vyavasthitah: *differences* stand established *in the world* — mukhyebhya iva lingebhyah: *as though* from *primary* genders. So 'taraka' is feminine and 'naksatra' neuter, and the stars have no sex: the gender-differences arise from indescribable causes as the mirage's water does, and present *as though* primary. The iva is the doctrine. Gender, in its ordinary employment, is a mirage — real as appearance, seeded in the speaker, absent from the thing.

Verse 3.13.10.translation: But from the seeing of breasts and the rest, whose forms are manifest, among manifest things — from the non-manifestation of the unmanifest manifestor, a universal is not constructed.
Verse 3.13.10.commentary: An argument against the jati-account. Vyaktesu vyakta-rupanam stanadinam tu darsanat: from the *seeing* of *breasts* and the rest, *whose forms are manifest*, among *manifest* things — where there are sexual characters, they are plainly visible. Avyakta-vyanjana-avyakteh jatir na parikalpyate: from the *non-manifestation* of the *unmanifest manifestor*, a *universal* is *not constructed*. The argument: a jati needs manifestors (3.1.29's face in water); where the characters are absent — in stars, rivers, shores — nothing manifests femininity, so no universal can be posited there. And most nouns are of that kind. So the jati-account works exactly where the characters are and fails everywhere else — which makes it an account of sex, not of gender. The samuddesa is separating the two, and 3.13.5's cross-species recognition is being confined to its proper domain.

Verse 3.13.11.translation: And for one who, having asserted existence, desires that it is always unseen — in utter non-seeing there would be no ascertainment as to non-existence.
Verse 3.13.11.commentary: The realist's escape blocked. The jati-realist might say: femininity is present in the river too, merely unmanifest. Astitvam ca pratijnaya sada adarsanam icchatah: for one who, having *asserted existence*, *desires* that it is *always unseen* — the universal is there and never shows. Atyanta-adarsane na syad asattvam prati niscayah: in *utter non-seeing* there would be *no ascertainment* as to *non-existence*. The reply is double-edged and Bhartrhari lets both edges show: if the universal is never seen, one cannot establish that it is absent — and equally, one cannot establish that it is present. An always-unseen posit is unfalsifiable and unconfirmable, which is not a defence but a diagnosis. The escape saves the thesis by making it idle.

Verse 3.13.12.translation: And a word preceded by non-seeing is not sufficient for an inference; for when the seeing is established, what would be the purpose of an inference?
Verse 3.13.12.commentary: The realist's second escape blocked. He might infer the unseen universal from the word: 'nadi' is feminine, therefore femininity is there. Na ca alam anumanaya sabdo 'darsana-purvakah: a *word* preceded by *non-seeing* is *not sufficient* for an *inference* — a word cannot evidence what nobody has ever seen; inference runs from a mark to what has been observed with it (the vyapti requires prior co-observation). Siddhe hi darsane kim syad anumana-prayojanam: for when the *seeing is established*, what would be the *purpose* of an inference? Where the characters show, one sees and does not infer; where they do not, no inference is available. So the word cannot bridge to the universal, and the realist has neither perception nor inference for gender in the sexless case. This closes the argument begun at 3.13.10: gender-as-jati has no evidence outside the bodies.

Verse 3.13.13.translation: Manifestation, disappearance, and standing — these unfailing properties in all corporeal things have been exhibited as gender.
Verse 3.13.13.commentary: The samuddesa's own account begins, and it is startling. Avirbhavas tirobhavah sthitis ca iti anapayinah: *manifestation*, *disappearance*, and *standing* — the three that 3.8.25-26 gave as the vikaras' reduction, and 3.9.11's moments of a thing's career. Dharma murtisu sarvasu linga-tvena anudarsitah: these *unfailing properties* in *all corporeal things* have been *exhibited as gender*. So the three genders are these three: feminine is manifestation (the coming-forth), masculine is standing, neuter is disappearance — or some such mapping; the verse does not assign them, and the tradition's assignment varies. What matters is the move: gender is not sex but *phase* — the universal three-beat of arising, abiding, ceasing, which every corporeal thing exhibits. This is why every noun has a gender: everything has the three, and the ending marks which is salient.

Verse 3.13.14.translation: Of sound and the rest, which are the very selves of all corporeal things — in each quality, those three properties beginning with 'sattva' stand established everywhere.
Verse 3.13.14.commentary: The three grounded in the Samkhya gunas. Sarva-murty-atma-bhutanam sabdadinam gune gune: of *sound* and the rest — the five sense-qualities, which are the *very selves* of all corporeal things (nothing is but its qualities: 3.11.7's para-upakara-tattvanam) — *in each quality*.
