Trayah sattvadi-dharmas te sarvatra samavasthitah: those *three* properties beginning with *'sattva'* stand established *everywhere* — sattva, rajas, tamas: the three gunas of Samkhya, present in every quality of everything. So 3.13.13's three phases are the three gunas: manifestation is sattva's, standing is rajas', disappearance is tamas'. And since the gunas are in everything, the three genders are in everything — which explains why every noun has one, and why the assignment can seem arbitrary: all three are always present, and the ending marks whichever the word fixes on.
Verse 3.13.14.contested: the mapping of the three genders onto the three gunas is implied rather than stated, and the tradition's assignments differ; we set out the structure the verses build and decline to fix the correspondence the text leaves open

Verse 3.13.15.translation: And of the mere selves of form — white and the rest — at every moment, some one dissolves away and some one somehow increases.
Verse 3.13.15.commentary: The three phases at their finest grain. Rupasya ca atma-matranam suklaadinam prati-ksanam: of the *mere selves of form* — *white* and the rest — *at every moment*. Ka cit praliyate ka cit katham cid abhivardhate: *some one* *dissolves away* and *some one* *somehow increases*. So even a colour is, at every instant, partly waning and partly waxing — the three phases running continuously in the smallest quality. Katham cit — *somehow*: the shrug (2.171, 3.7.34, 3.8.62's kathamcit), marking that the mechanism gives out. And prati-ksanam, at every moment: this is momentariness in a grammarian's treatise, and it is offered not as Buddhist doctrine but as what the guna-analysis requires. If the three gunas are in every quality and they are phases, then every quality is always arising and ceasing — and gender marks which phase a word fixes on.

Verse 3.13.16.translation: And their unsettled operation, like boiling water — ceaselessly, of all entities — has been described in the Bhasya itself.
Verse 3.13.16.commentary: The momentariness of 3.13.15 given its image and its authority. Kvathita-udakavat ca esam anavasthita-vrttita: their *unsettled operation*, *like boiling water* — the pot at a rolling boil, where nothing holds still and the whole is a churn of arising and subsiding. Ajasram sarva-bhavanam: *ceaselessly*, of *all entities* — no thing is exempt, and no moment. Bhasya eva upavarnita: has been described *in the Bhasya itself*. So Patanjali said it, and Bhartrhari is drawing it out rather than importing it (2.475's bhasye eva upavarnitah; 3.9.103's Bhasya-citation). The image is chosen well: boiling water is unmistakably one body of water and unmistakably never the same twice — which is exactly the standing-and-not-standing that gender's three phases (3.13.13) mark.

Verse 3.13.17.translation: Uniformity of operation, or evenness, is called 'standing'; or it stands established by an operation of manifestation-and-disappearance.
Verse 3.13.17.commentary: What sthiti — standing — amounts to on the boiling-water picture. Pravrtter eka-rupatvam samyam va sthitir ucyate: *uniformity of operation*, or *evenness*, is called *'standing'* — a thing 'stands' when its arising and ceasing are in balance: not because nothing happens but because the churn is steady. Avirbhava-tirobhava-pravrttya va avatisthate: *or* it stands established by an *operation of manifestation-and-disappearance*. So standing is not a third thing beside manifesting and disappearing; it is those two in equilibrium (3.8.26's janma eva asrita-sarupyam sthitih iti abhidhiyate: birth alone, having resorted to a likeness of form, is spoken of as standing). The middle of the three phases dissolves into the other two, and what looked like a triad is a pair with a balance-point.

Verse 3.13.18.translation: Or 'standing' is held to be the state of being the occasioning ground for the cognition 'qualities' merely; and from standing, all genders are said to have the state of being pronouns.
Verse 3.13.18.commentary: A second account of standing, and a startling consequence. Guna iti eva buddher va nimittatvam sthitir mata: 'standing' is held to be the state of being the *occasioning ground* for the cognition '*qualities*' merely — a thing stands just insofar as it occasions the thought 'here are qualities'. And then: sthiteh ca sarva-lingaanam sarva-namatvam ucyate — from *standing*, *all genders* are said to have the state of being *pronouns*. Sarvanaman: the pronoun, Panini's own category — and the pronouns are precisely the words that take all three genders (sa, sa, tat). So gender's ground in standing explains why the pronouns are gender-indifferent: what merely stands, occasioning the thought of qualities, is not yet any gender, and the pronoun names exactly that (3.4.3's dravya defined by pronominal referability: whatever you can point at with 'this').

Verse 3.13.19.translation: All genders standing established, the operation of some one — resting on the restriction of intention — is somewhere accepted in the refinement of the word.
Verse 3.13.19.commentary: The samuddesa's central mechanism. Sthitesu sarva-lingesu: *all* genders *standing established* — every thing has all three, since all three gunas are in everything (3.13.14). Vivaksa-niyamasrayah kasya cid vyaparah: the *operation* of *some one* — resting on the *restriction of intention*. Kva cid isyate sabda-samskare: is *somewhere accepted* in the *refinement of the word*. So: all three genders are present, vivaksa restricts to one, and the restriction shows as the word's morphological form. Sabda-samskara — the seventh entry of 3.13.1-2's list, now adopted as the account. Gender is where the speaker's selection among ever-present phases gets into the word — which is 2.400's directed eye (the word, when intended, becomes the expressor of a meaning) applied to what the endings mark.

Verse 3.13.20.translation: When the occasioning grounds are in proximity, some one alone is the impeller — as with the words 'taksan' and the rest, so is the restriction in respect of genders.
Verse 3.13.20.commentary: The selection of 3.13.19 exemplified. Sannidhane nimittanam kim cid eva pravartakam: when the *occasioning grounds* are in *proximity*, *some one alone* is the *impeller* — many conditions are present and one operates (3.7.119's sa-vyaparatara: one property understood as more operative; 3.8.39's kascid pradhanyena pratiyate). Yatha taksadi-sabdanam lingesu niyamas tatha: as with the words *'taksan'* (carpenter) and the rest, so is the *restriction* in respect of *genders*. The taksadi class is Panini's list of stems with fixed gender-behaviour — the same word, one gender, no option, and no reason in the thing. So gender-selection works as every selection in this kanda works: a crowd of available conditions, one operative, and the operating not derivable from the crowd (3.12.14's niyatah sabda-saktayah: the capacities of words are fixed).

Verse 3.13.21.translation: The cultivated, who see the reality of entities, standing in the meanings of words — whatever gender comes to be a limb in respect of whatever property, that they declare.
Verse 3.13.21.commentary: The authority for the assignments. Bhava-tattva-drsah sistah: the *cultivated*, who *see the reality of entities* — the sistas, whose usage is the criterion of correctness (1.27's sadhutva established from the cultivated; 1.141's aviccheda). Sabdarthesu vyavasthitah: *standing in the meanings of words*. Yad yad dharme 'ngatam eti lingam tat tat pracaksate: *whatever* gender comes to be a *limb* in respect of *whatever* property, *that* they *declare*. Angata: the Mimamsa relation of subsidiary to principal (2.81-82). So the sistas assign the gender that serves the property in view — and their assignment is the fact. There is no appeal past it, because gender is a sabda-samskara (3.13.19) and the cultivated are who refine words. The verse locates gender's authority exactly where Kanda I located all linguistic authority.

Verse 3.13.22.translation: As words are correct in another domain by a difference of accent — so, from an established difference of gender, correctness is followed along.
Verse 3.13.22.commentary: The parallel that settles gender's status. Svara-bhedad yatha sabdah sadhavo visayantare: as *words* are *correct* in *another domain* by a *difference of accent* — the same syllables, differently accented, are correct in different senses (and the Indra-satru of 3.10.5 is the tradition's monument to what accent decides). Linga-bhedat tatha siddhat sadhutvam anugamyate: so, from an *established difference of gender*, *correctness* is *followed along*. So gender is like accent: a formal feature that fixes correctness domain by domain, learned rather than derived, and carrying no meaning of its own beyond the fixing. This is the samuddesa's cleanest placement of its subject — and the accent-parallel is exact, since nobody supposes an accent means anything.

Verse 3.13.23.translation: Where employment and non-employment are found in the world, there the science is undertaken — not where there is no inversion of employment.
Verse 3.13.23.commentary: Why the grammar legislates gender at all. Prayogo viprayogas ca loke yatra upalabhyate: where *employment* and *non-employment* are *found in the world* — where usage varies, some saying one thing and some another. Sastram arabhyate tatra: *there* the *science is undertaken*. Na prayoga-aviparyaye: *not* where there is *no inversion* of employment — where usage is uniform, no rule is needed. So the grammar's gender-rules exist precisely where the sistas' usage wavers, and are absent where it does not. This is a fine account of what a rule-system is for: it is a record of the contested cases (2.343's vyavahara constructed by division for the sake of the operation), and its silence marks agreement rather than oversight — which is why 3.12.24's argument from the Bhasya's silence had to be answered.

Verse 3.13.24.translation: From a difference of adjunct among meanings, the state of being the occasioning ground, in correctness, belongs to some quality-property; and intention stands established.
Verse 3.13.24.commentary: The two variables that fix gender. Upadhi-bhedad arthesu: from a *difference of adjunct* among *meanings* — upadhi: the limiting condition (3.2.2's asatya-upadhi; 3.9.36's kriya-upadhi). Guna-dharmasya kasya cin nimitta-bhavah sadhutve: the state of being the *occasioning ground*, in *correctness*, belongs to *some quality-property*. Vivaksa ca vyavasthita: and *intention* stands established. So gender-correctness has two conditions: which quality-property is in view (fixed by the adjunct) and what the speaker intends. Neither is a fact about the thing's sex. And 3.13.25 will show both working on a single pair of words.

Verse 3.13.25.translation: In 'hima' and 'aranya', femininity stands established when joined with greatness; and of a hut, distinguished by the adjunct 'small', there is the fitness for the productive suffix.
Verse 3.13.25.commentary: The two variables of 3.13.24 exhibited. Himaranye mahattvena yukte stritvam avasthitam: in *'hima'* (snow) and *'aranya'* (forest), *femininity* stands established when *joined with greatness* — himani, aranyani: the feminine forms mean the *great* snow, the *great* forest (Panini 4.1.49's himaranyayor mahattve). So femininity here marks magnitude, and has nothing to do with sex. Hrasva-upadhi-visistayah kutyah prasava-yogita: and of a *hut*, distinguished by the *adjunct 'small'*, there is the *fitness for the productive suffix* — kuti, the little hut, taking the feminine for smallness. So the *same* feminine marks greatness in one word and smallness in another, and both are correct. Nothing could show more plainly that gender is a sabda-samskara: one ending, opposite significations, and usage settling each.

Verse 3.13.26.translation: The means, for comprehension, of other words in a different meaning — having determined as though a oneness — have been shown for the sake of brevity.
Verse 3.13.26.commentary: A methodological note on the grammar's gender-classes. Sabdantaranam bhinne 'rtha upayah pratipattaye: the *means*, for *comprehension*, of *other words* in a *different meaning*. Ekatam iva niscitya: having *determined as though a oneness* — the grammar groups words of different senses into one gender-class as though they were one kind. Laghv-artham upadarsitah: have been shown *for the sake of brevity* — laghava, the Paninian virtue (2.343's laghu; 2.176's instruction by the general for brevity's sake). So the gender-classes are economy-devices: a list of stems treated as one because listing them severally would be endless, and the iva marks that their oneness is the list's and not theirs. This is 3.7.78's method (one karaka exhibited by way of difference) inverted: many things exhibited as one, for the same reason.

Verse 3.13.27.translation: For others, 'origination' is production, 'destruction' is congealing; but the own-form of entities is designated as 'standing'.
Verse 3.13.27.commentary: A rival naming of the three phases. Utpattih prasavo 'nyesam: for *others*, *'origination'* is *production* — prasava: bringing-forth, the word 3.6.10 used for what direction-words lack (no engendering). Nasah samstyanam iti api: *'destruction'* is *congealing* — samstyana: thickening, coagulating; destruction as a hardening rather than a vanishing (3.7.118's samurcchita iva: the meaning's self as though congealed). Atma-rupam tu bhavanam sthitir ity apadisyate: but the *own-form* of entities is designated as *'standing'*. So the three are renamed: production, congealing, and the thing's own form. And the middle term is now the thing itself — standing is not a phase alongside the others but what the entity *is*, with arising and ceasing as its edges. The renaming shifts the doctrine's weight: gender marks the thing's own form and its two boundaries.

Verse 3.13.28.translation: For some, the occasioning ground is seen as standing established, like a universal and the rest; but a mere refinement of the word, as though seen, is constructed.
Verse 3.13.28.commentary: The two camps set side by side, and the samuddesa's verdict is in the second clause. Drstam nimittam kesam cij jaty-adivad avasthitam: for *some*, the *occasioning ground* is *seen* as *standing established*, *like a universal* and the rest — the realists (3.13.4-5's jati-account): femininity is there and shows. Drstavat sabda-samskara-matram tu parikalpitam: but a *mere refinement of the word*, *as though seen*, is *constructed*. Drstavat: *as though* seen — the sabda-samskara is a construction that wears the look of an observation. So the two accounts are not two readings of one datum: on the second, the datum itself is manufactured, and the realists are reading a construction as a finding. Parikalpitam: constructed (3.13.9's mirage). And matram — *mere*: nothing but a refinement of the word.

Verse 3.13.29.translation: As, even where oneness is well established, words — for those addicted to manifoldness — as it were generate manifoldness; that same procedure is in gender too.
Verse 3.13.29.commentary: The mechanism named. Yatha prasiddhe 'py ekatve nanatva-abhinivesinah: as, even where *oneness* is *well established*, for those *addicted to manifoldness* — abhinivesin: clinging, obsessed (2.266's gauna-artha-abhinivesinam, settling upon a secondary meaning). Nanatvam janayanti iva sabdah: *words* — *as it were* — *generate manifoldness*. Linge 'pi sa kramah: *that same procedure* is in *gender* too. So words manufacture plurality where there is one thing, for minds disposed to find it — and gender is that manufacture. The iva keeps it honest: words do not really generate; they present. And nanatva-abhinivesinah is a diagnosis of the hearer, not of the language: the manifoldness is generated *for those addicted to it*, which is everyone in vyavahara (3.6.18's dik-kala-parikalpana standing like consciousness itself).

Verse 3.13.30.translation: 'This' or 'she' or 'he' — a mere refinement of the word: from the seeing of an occasioning ground in the meaning, it is described by some as everywhere so.
Verse 3.13.30.commentary: The doctrine at its most general. Idam va iyam ayam va iti: '*this*' or '*she*' or '*he*' — the three pronominal genders, and note that the neuter comes first. Sabda-samskara-matrakam: a *mere refinement of the word* — matraka: nothing but. Nimitta-darsanad arthe kaiscit sarvatra varnyate: *from the seeing of an occasioning ground in the meaning*, it is described by *some* as *everywhere* so. So: some hold that gender is a sabda-samskara *everywhere* — not merely in the sexless cases (3.13.7's rivers and shores) but in all cases, including the bodies. Even 'she' said of a woman is a refinement of the word, occasioned by something seen in the meaning and not naming it. This is the seventh entry of 3.13.1-2 taken to its limit, and it is reported (kaiscit) with evident sympathy: the samuddesa's whole argument has been driving here.

Verse 3.13.31.translation: The occasioning ground does not necessarily stand established as an object; as the sense-faculty and the rest, being unseen, is accepted as the cause of difference.
Verse 3.13.31.commentary: The samuddesa's last defence of 3.13.30. The objection: if gender is a refinement of the word occasioned by something in the meaning, that something must be an object one could point to — and in rivers there is none. The reply: na avasyam visayatvena nimittam vyavatisthate — the *occasioning ground* does *not necessarily* stand established *as an object*. And the precedent: indriyadi yatha adrstam bheda-hetus tad isyate — as the *sense-faculty* and the rest, being *unseen*, is accepted as the *cause of difference*. This is 3.1.30-31's argument exactly: the eye's condition differs and is never itself perceived, and it makes my seeing differ from yours. Ungrasped, it conditions. So gender's occasioning ground may be real and never an object — like the eye, working by not appearing. The realist demanded a visible femininity in the river; the reply is that causes need not be objects, and the finest ones never are.

Verse 3.14.1.translation: But a word of completed meaning is joined with censure, praise and excess; since all, beginning with its own meaning, are the causes of censure and the rest.
Verse 3.14.1.commentary: The samuddesa opens on the affixes of attitude. Kutsa-prasamsa-atisayaih: with *censure*, *praise* and *excess* — the taddhitas that add an evaluation: the ka of contempt, the affixes of praise, the tarap and tamap of comparison. Samapta-artham tu yujyate padam: a word of *completed meaning* is *joined* with them — samapta-artha: the word is already whole before the attitude arrives (3.8.17's krtarthatva; 2.4's ekartham). Svarthadayah sarve yasmat kutsadi-hetavah: since *all*, beginning with its *own meaning*, are the *causes* of censure and the rest. So what occasions the contempt is everything about the word's meaning — and the affix does not add a content but registers a stance toward one. The Vrtti-samuddesa's whole subject is what happens when words are joined; it begins with the simplest case, where a completed word takes on an attitude, and even here the question is what the addition adds.

Verse 3.14.2.translation: The hearing 'censured' operates in the censure of Devadatta and the rest; but that censure which resides in the censured — for that meaning, 'ka' is prescribed.
Verse 3.14.2.commentary: Two ways of censuring, and they are not the same. Devadattadi-kutsayam vartate kutsita-srutih: the *hearing 'censured'* operates in the *censure of Devadatta* and the rest — one says 'kutsito devadattah', 'Devadatta is contemptible', and the word 'kutsita' names him as censured. Kutsita-stha tu ya kutsa tad-arthah kah vidhiyate: but that *censure* which *resides in the censured* — for *that* meaning, *'ka'* is *prescribed*: 'devadattakah', with the contemptuous suffix, expresses not that he is censured but the censure itself, lodged in him. So: the adjective predicates contempt, the affix carries it. And the difference is the samuddesa's opening lesson about vrtti — what a compressed form does is not what its analysis says.

Verse 3.14.3.translation: 'Prakrsta' is the expressor of the excellence of white and the rest; but in the excellence of the excellent, 'tarap' and the rest is prescribed.
Verse 3.14.3.commentary: The comparative's structure, and it is doubled. Prakrsta iti sukladi-prakarsasya abhidhayakah: *'prakrsta'* (excellent) is the *expressor* of the *excellence* of white and the rest — the word names the eminence a quality has (3.5.2's prakarsa; 1.64's guna as prakarsa-hetu). Prakrstasya prakarse tu tarab-adir vidhiyate: but in the *excellence of the excellent*, *tarap* and the rest is *prescribed* — 'suklatara', whiter: the comparative marks not excellence but excellence *of what is already excellent*. So the comparative affix operates one level up, on an eminence already predicated. This is the stratification 3.11.19 found in the higher numerals (the countings gathered) and 3.11.3's number numbering itself — and it explains why 'suklatara' is not merely a stronger 'sukla': the affix takes the excellence as its object.

Verse 3.14.4.translation: Either what is to be censured by way of being censured, or what is not properly censured — when it is expressed by its own word, by what is a qualified meaning understood?
Verse 3.14.4.commentary: A dilemma for the contempt-affix. Kutsitatvena kutsyo va: either what is *to be censured* *by way of being censured* — the affix censures what is already censurable, and adds nothing; na samyag vapi kutsitah: or what is *not properly censured* — the affix censures what does not deserve it, and misfires. Sva-sabda-abhihite kena visisto 'rthah pratiyate: when it is expressed by its *own word*, *by what* is a *qualified* meaning understood? If 'kutsita' already says he is contemptible, what does '-ka' qualify? The dilemma is 3.14.2's distinction pressed: if the adjective and the affix both censure, one is idle (2.34's add-or-subtract test: against the sentence's one meaning, addition yields not more meaning but meaninglessness).

Verse 3.14.5.translation: And a present censure is not understood, from the absence of difference; one is honoured by way of being censured, and censured by way of being praised.
Verse 3.14.5.commentary: The dilemma of 3.14.4 answered by an observation about irony, and it is one of the finest things in the samuddesa. Na ca samprataki kutsa bheda-abhavat pratiyate: a *present* censure is *not understood*, from the *absence of difference* — the affix does not deliver a censure occurring now, because nothing in the form marks it off. And then the evidence: pujyate kutsitatvena — one is *honoured* *by way of being censured*: the affectionate insult, the friend called a scoundrel; prasastatvena kutsyate — and *censured* *by way of being praised*: the sneer that says 'how admirable'. So the contempt-affix can honour and the praise-affix can wound, and neither carries its attitude reliably. Which answers 3.14.4: the affix does not censure at all; it marks a stance whose direction the occasion supplies (2.315's fourteen determinants; 3.12.5's vivaksita-nibandhana). Irony is not a deviant use — it is what shows that the attitude was never in the form.

Verse 3.14.6.translation: The state of being qualifier and qualified arises for two words; and the nominal stem's meaning is there thus set apart.
Verse 3.14.6.commentary: The samuddesa turns to its real subject: what happens when two words are joined. Visesana-visesyatvam padayor upajayate: the state of being *qualifier and qualified* *arises* for *two words* — upajayate: arises, comes to be. Neither word is a qualifier by itself; the relation is generated by the joining. Va pratipadika-arthas ca tatra evam vyatiricyate: and the *nominal stem's meaning* is *there* thus *set apart* — the stem's own meaning is separated off from the relational role it acquires. So joining two words produces two things: a qualifier-qualified structure that neither had, and a distinction between what each stem means and what it is doing. This is 3.7.13's doctrine (the karaka-property is not expressed by the noun) at the level of nominal composition, and it opens the whole vrtti-analysis.

Verse 3.14.7.translation: The qualified would be what is not ascertained; the qualifier is what has an ascertained meaning. From being-for-another, remainder-hood belongs to all that assist.
Verse 3.14.7.commentary: The two roles defined by epistemic status. Visesyam syad anirjnatam: the *qualified* would be what is *not ascertained* — the head is what one does not yet know: 'gauh' leaves open which cow. Nirjnata-artho visesanam: the *qualifier* is what has an *ascertained* meaning — the modifier is what one knows: 'sukla' brings a settled content to an unsettled one. So the qualifier-qualified relation is not a matter of importance or of grammatical rank but of *what is known*: the known narrows the unknown. And the consequence: para-arthatvena sesatvam sarvesam upakarinam — from *being-for-another*, *remainder-hood* belongs to *all that assist* (2.113's parartha; 3.7.44's sesa). Everything that helps is a remainder — which means the qualifier, for all its determinacy, is the subordinate.

Verse 3.14.8.translation: A difference of case-ending is by rule for the two expressing quality and quality-possessor; the establishing of co-referentiality is for two substance-words.
Verse 3.14.8.commentary: The morphology of qualification. Vibhakti-bhedo niyamad guna-guny-abhidhayinoh: a *difference of case-ending* is *by rule* for the two expressing *quality* and *quality-possessor* — where one word names a quality as such and the other its bearer, the cases differ: 'suklasya gauh' would be the genitive construction, quality and possessor at different case-levels. Samanadhikaranyasya prasiddhir dravya-sabdayoh: the *establishing of co-referentiality* is for two *substance*-words — samanadhikaranya: same-locus-hood, the grammar's term for two words in the same case referring to one thing: 'suklo gauh', both nominative. So co-reference requires both words to be substance-words, and where one is a pure quality-word the cases split. The rule matters because it means 'sukla' in 'suklo gauh' is not naming a quality at all — it is naming a substance, whitely (3.14.9's example makes this vivid).

Verse 3.14.9.translation: The word 'krsna' is employed with respect to a substance whose universal is unascertained; and the word 'tila' operates with respect to one whose quality is unascertained.
Verse 3.14.9.commentary: The mechanism of co-reference, and the example is exact. Take 'krsnas tilah', the black sesame. Dravye anirjnata-jatiye krsna-sabdah prayujyate: the word *'krsna'* (black) is employed with respect to a substance *whose universal is unascertained* — 'black' gives you a black *thing* and does not say what kind: the jati is open. Anirjnata-gune ca eva tila-sabdah pravartate: and the word *'tila'* operates with respect to one *whose quality is unascertained* — 'sesame' gives you the kind and leaves the colour open. So each word presents a substance with a gap, and the gaps are complementary: black-something and sesame-of-some-colour. Neither is a qualifier by nature (3.14.6's upajayate: the roles arise in the joining); each fills what the other left open. This is the finest analysis of adjectival co-reference in the ancient world, and it depends on 3.14.7's insight that qualification is the known narrowing the unknown.

Verse 3.14.10.translation: From the non-connection of the generals, those two stand established in the particular; from non-difference of form, they cannot manifest that particular.
Verse 3.14.10.commentary: Why neither word alone suffices. Samanyanam asambandhat: from the *non-connection of the generals* — blackness-in-general and sesame-hood-in-general are not connected; nothing links the colour-universal to the species-universal. Tau visese vyavasthitau: those two stand established *in the particular* — they meet only in this black sesame seed, not in their generals. Rupa-abhedad visesam tam abhivyanktum na saknutah: from *non-difference of form*, they *cannot manifest* that *particular* — each word's form is the same whether it means the general or this instance, so neither can single out the particular by itself. So the two words meet in a particular that neither can reach alone — and the meeting is what the joining accomplishes. The verse explains why qualification is necessary: generals do not connect, particulars are not namable, and only the crossing of two open generals lands on one thing.

Verse 3.14.11.translation: Those two, thus fallen together, in conveying by way of difference — having as it were deposited a delimitation, draw away the doubt.
Verse 3.14.11.commentary: The resolution, and its verbs are precise. Tau evam sannipatitau: those two, thus *fallen together* — sannipata: the falling-together, collision, conjunction (3.7.109's sannipata). The two words simply land in one utterance. Bhedena pratipadane: in *conveying by way of difference* — each conveying its own content, differently. Avacchedam iva adhaya samsayam vyapakarsatah: having *as it were deposited* a *delimitation*, they *draw away the doubt*. Two operations: each deposits a limit on the other's openness (avaccheda: delimitation, 2.45's avacchinatti, the relation delimiting the hovering general), and together they *draw away the doubt* — samsayam vyapakarsatah: the ambiguity is dragged off. So qualification is doubt-removal by mutual delimitation, and neither word does anything to the other but stand beside it. The iva is careful: they deposit a delimitation *as it were* — nothing is really added; two openings simply overlap.

Verse 3.14.12-14.translation: (12) The substance-self is resorted to severally from a difference of quality-commingling; and from a difference of universal-connection, it is grasped as though a second. (13) That likeness to the occasioning ground which comes from connection with the occasioning grounds — by it, even of one thing, manifoldness is constructed, from non-difference of form. (14) But there is a third substance-state, in which the two are commingled; from the difference of those two states, it is applied to the state of being substratum.
Verse 3.14.12-14.commentary: The metaphysics beneath 3.14.9-11's analysis, and it makes one thing into three. (12): dravya-atma guna-samsarga-bhedad asriyate prthak — the *substance-self* is resorted to *severally* from a *difference of quality-commingling*: the sesame taken as black is one resort; jati-sambandha-bhedac ca dvitiya iva grhyate — and from a *difference of universal-connection*, it is grasped *as though a second*: the same seed taken as sesame is another. One thing, two resorts, and the second only *as though*. (13) names the mechanism: nimittaih abhisambandhad ya nimitta-sarupata — the *likeness to the occasioning ground* that comes from connection with the grounds (3.11.13 verbatim) — taya ekasya api nanatvam rupa-abhedat prakalpyate: by it, even of *one* thing, *manifoldness* is *constructed*, from *non-difference of form*. (14) supplies the third: dravya-avastha trtiya tu yasyam samsrjyate dvayam — a *third* substance-state, in which *the two are commingled* — tayor avasthayor bhedad asrayatve niyujyate: from the *difference* of those two states, it is *applied* to the state of being *substratum*. So: the thing as black, the thing as sesame, and the thing as the substratum in which both meet — three states of one seed, and the third is what the co-referential phrase names. The whole apparatus of qualification rests on a threefold construction of one thing by buddhi, which 3.14.15 is about to say outright.

Verse 3.14.15.translation: By the intellect one is differentiated, and the differentiated attains oneness; by the intellect the states are divided out — for it is the ordainer of the meaning.
Verse 3.14.15.commentary: The samuddesa's thesis, and it is the Padakanda's. Buddhya ekam bhidyate: by the *intellect*, *one* is *differentiated* — the sesame becomes black-thing and sesame-thing. Bhinnam ekatvam ca upagacchati: and the *differentiated* attains *oneness* — and the two become one seed again, in the phrase. So buddhi both divides the one and unifies the divided, and the whole machinery of 3.14.9-14 is its work. Buddhy-avastha vibhajyante: by the intellect the *states are divided out* — 3.14.14's three states are buddhi's (3.7.104's ekasya buddhy-avasthabhih bhede parikalpite: objecthood, instrument-hood and agency arising when the intellect's states carve one thing). And the last clause is the verse's weight: sa hi arthasya vidhayika — for *it* is the *ordainer of the meaning*. Vidhayika: the ordainer, the one who prescribes (3.11.12's vidhayinim: number ordaining difference among things that have transcended it). The intellect does not find the meaning's structure; it legislates it. Everything the Vrtti-samuddesa will say about compounds and derivatives rests here.

Verse 3.14.16.translation: As in the 'vyapadesivat' maxim, there is a construction of many meanings by the intellect in one thing; by that, the meaning-self, its difference constructed, is designated.
Verse 3.14.16.commentary: The doctrine of 3.14.15 given its Paninian precedent. Vyapadesivat: Panini's own metarule (vyapadesivad ekasmin — 'as though there were a designation, in respect of one'), which allows a single item to be treated as though it were two so that a rule requiring two can apply. The grammar has an explicit licence to double one thing. Ekasmin buddhya nanartha-kalpana: in *one* thing, a *construction of many meanings* by the *intellect*. Taya kalpita-bhedah sann artha-atma vyapadisyate: by that, the *meaning-self*, its *difference constructed*, is *designated*. So the sastra's own maxim is the samuddesa's thesis in miniature: buddhi doubles what is single, the doubling is kalpita, and the designation proceeds on the doubled. Bhartrhari is not importing a philosophy into the grammar; he is reading the philosophy the grammar's metarules already presuppose.

Verse 3.14.17.translation: Of stones and the like, seen again and again through a difference of action — some one seeing is designated by another seeing.
Verse 3.14.17.commentary: The doubling of 3.14.16 in perception. Kriya-bhedena drstanam asmadinam punah punah: of *stones* and the like, *seen again and again* through a *difference of action* — the same stone met in different doings: lifted, thrown, stumbled over. Kimcid darsanam anyena darsanena apadisyate: *some one seeing* is *designated by another seeing*. So one identifies a present sighting by reference to a previous one — 'the stone I threw' — and the identification treats two seeings as one object's. The verse supplies the perceptual case of vyapadesivat: buddhi doubles the stone into seen-then and seen-now, and then re-identifies them. And this is what makes qualification possible (3.14.9-11): the two open generals that meet in one particular are two takings of one thing.

Verse 3.14.18.translation: Having constructed the many-formedness of roots from a difference of employment — taking up difference and non-difference, mono-vocalic-ness is somewhere stated.
Verse 3.14.18.commentary: The doubling in the grammar's own root-list. Prayoga-bhedad dhatunam prakalpya bahu-rupatam: having *constructed* the *many-formedness* of *roots* from a *difference of employment* — one root, employed variously, is treated as several (the Dhatupatha's homonymous entries). Bheda-abhedav upadaya kvacid ekac-tvam ucyate: *taking up difference and non-difference*, *mono-vocalic-ness* is *somewhere stated* — ekac: having one vowel, a Paninian condition for certain rules. So a root is counted as one-vowelled or not according to which of its constructed forms is in view, and the counting takes up both difference and non-difference at once (3.7.179's bhedabheda-abhyupagame na virodho 'sti kascana: in accepting both difference and non-difference there is no contradiction). The grammar doubles its roots and then counts their vowels — and which count applies depends on the doubling.

Verse 3.14.19.translation: One thing, constructed as meaningful by agreement and difference, from the departure of the root's meaning is figuratively treated as a mere phoneme.
Verse 3.14.19.commentary: The limit of the doubling. Anvaya-vyatirekabhyam arthavan parikalpitah: *constructed as meaningful* by *agreement and difference* — the distributional method posits a meaningful element (2.12's anvaya-vyatirekau vyavahara-nibandhanam). Ekah dhatv-artha-vigamad varnatvena upacaryate: *one*, from the *departure of the root's meaning*, is *figuratively treated* as a *mere phoneme*. So an element posited as meaningful, once its root-sense withdraws, is demoted to a bare sound — upacaryate: figuratively (2.253's upacara). This is 2.167's nut and sap (formatives real as units and empty as meanings) given their genesis: they are meaningful posits whose meaning has departed, and the grammar keeps the unit and drops the sense. The doubling of 3.14.18 thus has a terminus: past a point, what analysis isolates is a phoneme.

Verse 3.14.20.translation: Therefore three substance-selves stand established as many in the intellect, by the property of substratum and what rests on it — this is the tradition from the earlier ones.
Verse 3.14.20.commentary: The three states of 3.14.12-14 confirmed on authority. Dravya-atmanas trayas tasmad buddhau nana vyavasthitah: *therefore* *three* substance-selves stand established *as many* *in the intellect* — the thing as qualified, the thing as kind, and the thing as their substratum (3.14.14's third state). Asraya-asrayi-dharmena: by the *property of substratum and what rests on it* — the three are generated by the bearer/borne relation applied to one thing. Ity ayam purvebhya agamah: *this* is the *tradition from the earlier ones* — purvebhya agamah: 3.3.11's very phrase, and 2.485's counsel about waiting on the elders. So the threefold construction is inherited, not invented, and Bhartrhari cites rather than claims it. The Padakanda's boldest analytical apparatus is presented as received.

Verse 3.14.21.translation: And by some, the co-referentiality of two words is accepted; or the state of qualifier and qualified; or else the state of name and named.
Verse 3.14.21.commentary: Three accounts of what joins two words. Samanadhikaranyam ca sabdayoh kaiscid isyate: by *some*, the *co-referentiality* of two words — samanadhikaranya: same-locus-hood (3.14.8), the two words pointing at one thing. Visesana-visesyatvam: or the state of *qualifier and qualified* (3.14.6-7's relation, arising in the joining). Samjna-samjnitvam eva va: or else the state of *name and named* — the two words as designation and designatum. Three relations for one juxtaposition, and they are not equivalent: co-reference is symmetric, qualification is not, and naming is a third thing again. The verse's catalogue is doing what the samuddesa's catalogues always do — showing that the joining admits several descriptions and that none is privileged (2.179's licensed double description).

Verse 3.14.22.translation: For some, the derivative formation of a universal and a quality inhering in one object is accepted, in 'black sesames', where the word expresses a substance.
Verse 3.14.22.commentary: The compound's condition, on one account. Kesamcij jati-gunayor eka-artha-samavetayoh: for *some*, of a *universal and a quality* *inhering in one object* — sesame-hood and blackness, both in the seed (eka-artha-samavaya: the Vaisesika's device for relating two qualities through their common bearer, 3.3.13). Vrttih krsna-tilesu ista: the *derivative formation* is accepted in *'black sesames'*. Sabde dravya-abhidhayini: where the *word expresses a substance*. So compounding is licensed when a universal and a quality share a bearer and the word names the bearer — and the condition is precise: both members must reach the same substance, or there is nothing for the compound to be about. This is 3.14.9-10's analysis (the two open generals meeting in a particular) stated as a rule of composition.

Verse 3.14.23.translation: But the substratum of form, taste and the rest, being existent, is not expressed; without the expressing of a substance, they are therefore constituents of a dvandva.
Verse 3.14.23.commentary: The contrasting case. San tu rupa-rasadinam asrayo na abhidhiyate: the *substratum* of *form*, *taste* and the rest, *being existent*, is *not expressed* — the mango's colour and flavour have a bearer, and 'rupa-rasau' does not name it. Dravya-abhidhanena vina tatas te dvandva-bhavinah: *without* the *expressing of a substance*, they are *therefore* *constituents of a dvandva*. So where no substance is named, the two quality-words cannot form a qualifier-qualified compound (3.14.22's condition fails) and fall into a dvandva instead — a mere coordination, 'colour-and-taste'. The rule is elegant: what determines the compound-type is whether a substance is expressed, and the qualities, left without a bearer, can only be listed. This explains why quality-words coordinate and do not qualify each other.

Verse 3.14.24.translation: 'Krsna' and the rest, expressing a substance, operates as possessing expectancy; from its conforming to the occasioning ground, that does not exist in 'tila' and the rest.
Verse 3.14.24.commentary: The asymmetry between the two members of 'krsnas tilah'. Dravya-abhidhayi krsnadir akanksavan pravartate: *'krsna'* and the rest, *expressing a substance*, operates as *possessing expectancy* — 'black' names a substance and *wants* to know which: it is incomplete (3.14.7's visesyam anirjnatam, inverted — here the qualifier is the needy one). Nimitta-anuvidhayitvat: from its *conforming to the occasioning ground* — the colour-word follows whatever bears the colour. Tat tiladau na vidyate: *that* does *not exist* in *'tila'* and the rest — 'sesame' has no such expectancy; it names its kind and is content. So the two words are not symmetric after all: 'krsna' reaches for a bearer and 'tila' does not, and the asymmetry is what makes one the qualifier. This refines 3.14.9-11: the mutual delimitation is real, and the reaching is one-way.

Verse 3.14.25.translation: Thus, in a substance possessing a universal, which is near to the action — a substance pervaded by a quality, whose property is the quality, serves for differentiation.
Verse 3.14.25.commentary: The qualification's office in the sentence. Evam jatimati dravye pratyasanne kriyam prati: in a substance *possessing a universal*, which is *near to the action* — the sesame, ready to figure as a karaka (3.7.94's proximity to the accomplishment). Guna-dharma gunavista dravyam bhedaya kalpate: a *substance pervaded by a quality*, whose *property is the quality*, serves for *differentiation*. So 'krsna' contributes a quality-pervaded substance, and its office is to differentiate the sesame from other sesames — which is what an action needs, since actions take determinate participants (3.7.87's ipsitama: the existing thing not yet attained, taken under the aspect it will have). The verse locates qualification's purpose: it is for the verb, and it hands the verb a narrowed participant.

Verse 3.14.26.translation: Some desire the state of expressing a mere quality in the derivative formations; therefore, in 'asva' and the rest, from connection, it is as of conventional terms by conventional terms.
Verse 3.14.26.commentary: A rival account of the compound's members. Guna-matra-abhidhayitvam kecid icchanti vrttisu: *some* desire the state of expressing a *mere quality* in the *derivative formations* — in the compound, they say, 'krsna' names blackness itself, not a black substance (against 3.14.8's rule that co-reference requires substance-words). Atah asvadisu sambandhad rudhinam iva rudhibhih: *therefore*, in *'asva'* and the rest, from *connection*, it is as of *conventional terms* by *conventional terms* — the compound's members relate as two rudhi-words do: by convention, not by qualification. So on this view compounds are not qualifications at all; they are conventional pairings (2.37's rudhi-sabdatva acknowledged in the science itself). The account is reported and its cost is visible: it makes every compound an idiom.

Verse 3.14.27.translation: Or, 'tila' having been taken up first, the 'matup' affix is desired there itself; and that property, in compounds, is the quality — therefore it is the qualifier.
Verse 3.14.27.commentary: A third account, and it makes the compound a possessive. Tile purvam upatte va: or, *'tila'* having been *taken up first* — the substance-word leads. Tatra eva matub isyate: the *'matup'* affix is desired *there itself* — matup: the possessive suffix ('having'), so that 'krsna-tila' is analysed as 'sesame having blackness'. Sa ca dharmah samasesu gunas tasmad visesanam: and *that property*, in *compounds*, is the *quality* — *therefore* it is the *qualifier*. So the compound conceals a possessive: the substance is the head, the quality is possessed, and the possession makes the quality a qualifier. The account is neat and it explains the asymmetry 3.14.24 found — but it requires an unuttered matup, and 2.219's bahuvrihi already showed the samuddesa's suspicion of members that are not there.

Verse 3.14.28.translation: One presentation arises, as though interwoven by the two differences; when that co-intention is meant, they call it 'dvandva' and 'ekasesa'.
Verse 3.14.28.commentary: The coordinative compounds, and their mechanism is a weaving. Anusyuta iva bhedabhyam eka prakhya upajayate: *one presentation* arises, *as though interwoven* by the two *differences* — anusyuta: 1.123's very word (all knowledge appears as if interwoven, anuviddham iva, with word), the weaver's term. So two differences are threaded into one showing, and the iva marks it. Yada saha-vivaksam tam ahur dvandva-ekasesayoh: when that *co-intention* is meant, they call it *'dvandva'* and *'ekasesa'*. Saha-vivaksa — co-intention: meaning two things *together*. So the coordinative compounds are what happens when two things are meant at once rather than one through the other — and the difference between qualification and coordination is a difference of vivaksa (3.7.2's kvacit kimcit vivaksyate), not of the things.

Verse 3.14.29.translation: But mutual conjunction belongs to those expressing distinct groups; and that collection is completed severally, in the members of the collection.
Verse 3.14.29.commentary: The dvandva's structure. Itaretara-yogas tu bhinna-sangha-abhidhayinam: *mutual conjunction* belongs to those expressing *distinct groups* — itaretara-yoga: the grammar's name for the dvandva whose members remain distinct ('rama-krsnau', two men, both there). Pratyekam ca samuho 'sau samuhisu samapyate: and that *collection* is *completed severally*, in the *members* of the collection — pratyekam samapyate: the whole-at-each formula (2.18, 3.8.5). So the dvandva's collection is complete in each member: the pairhood is wholly in Rama and wholly in Krsna, not divided between them. This is why the dvandva takes a dual and not two singulars — and why 2.221-225's analysis of distribution applied to it: the collection is one and its completing is severally.

Verse 3.14.30.translation: As the derivative formation of an aggregate of operations is severally, like a universal, in the setting-on-the-fire and the rest — so also in the words of a dvandva.
Verse 3.14.30.commentary: The dvandva's structure paralleled with the action's. Vyapara-samudayasya yatha adhisrayanadisu: as the *aggregate of operations* — in *setting-on-the-fire* and the rest: the cooking's sub-actions (3.8.9's parts of 'paci'). Pratyekam jativad vrttih: the *derivative formation* is *severally*, *like a universal* — the cooking is wholly in the fetching and wholly in the stirring, as cowhood is wholly in each cow (3.1.25's jati manifested in each instance). Tatha dvandva-padesv api: *so also* in the *words of a dvandva*. So the dvandva is to its members what an action is to its sub-actions and a universal to its instances: wholly present in each, not divided among them. The parallel unifies three of the kanda's analyses — and it is the reason 3.14.29's collection completes severally.

Verse 3.14.31.translation: 'Saunda', 'ardharca', 'purodasa', 'chatrin' are the illustrations here; and 'they are the Visnumitras' — among distinct accompanying things.
Verse 3.14.31.commentary: A class of cases where a name spreads to its neighbours. Four specimens: saunda (the drunkard's class); ardharca (the gana of 3.1.103, words whose analysis wavers); purodasa (3.12.22's sacrificial cake, whose name is applied to the grains it will be made from); chatrin (3.12.22's umbrella-bearer, whose designation covers his umbrella-less companions). And a fifth: te visnumitra iti — '*they are the Visnumitras*', a family called by one member's name. Bhinnesu sahacarisu: among *distinct accompanying things* — in every case the things are genuinely distinct and one name covers them. So the samuddesa opens its analysis of composition by assembling the cases where designation already leaks across differences (3.12.21's plaksa-ness attaching to the nyagrodha). Compounding will turn out to be this leak, regularized.

Verse 3.14.32.translation: The state of expressing another meaning belongs likewise to those operating in another meaning; and by which two a single one has many meanings, by those very two another word has them.
Verse 3.14.32.commentary: The leak of 3.14.31 generalized. Arthantara-abhidayitvam tatha arthantara-vartinam: the state of *expressing another meaning* belongs likewise to those *operating in another meaning* — a word that has strayed into a neighbour's sense goes on expressing that stray sense. Yabhyam ca ekam anekartham tabhyam eva aparam padam: and by *which two* a *single one* has *many meanings*, by *those very two* *another word* has them. So whatever mechanism multiplies one word's senses multiplies another's — the leak is systematic, not idiosyncratic. This is 2.250-251's omni-significance (one word, many meanings, carved by nimittas) applied to composition: the conditions that carve one word carve its neighbour, and the compound is where two such carvings meet.

Verse 3.14.33.translation: And from its being another aggregate, such a meaning is not worldly; nor is a scientific meaning seen by agreement and difference.
Verse 3.14.33.commentary: The compound's meaning placed nowhere. Samudayantaratvat ca tadrso 'rtha na laukikah: from its being *another aggregate*, such a meaning is *not worldly* — the compound is a new aggregate, and its meaning is not one the world already had (2.208's disputed emergence: from meaningful elements a complex arises with a distinct meaning). Anvaya-vyatirekabhyam sastrartho 'pi na drsyate: nor is a *scientific meaning* seen by *agreement and difference* — the distributional method (2.12's tool) does not find it either: vary the members and the compound's sense does not track. So the compound's meaning is neither in the world's inventory nor recoverable by the analyst's method. It is a genuine novelty, and the samuddesa is honest that its own instrument cannot locate it.

Verse 3.14.34.translation: Therefore it is cited in the Bhasya as difficult and hard to establish; but that simultaneous-expressing-ness is resorted to for the sake of practice.
Verse 3.14.34.commentary: The difficulty acknowledged on authority. Duhkha durupapada ca tasmad bhasye 'py udahrta: *therefore* it is cited in the *Bhasya* as *difficult* and *hard to establish* — Patanjali himself found it so, and Bhartrhari cites the admission rather than papering over it (2.475's bhasye eva upavarnitah; 3.13.16's Bhasya-citation). Yugapad-vacita sa tu vyavahara-artham asrita: but that *simultaneous-expressing-ness* is *resorted to* *for the sake of practice*. Yugapad-vacita: the compound's expressing its members' senses *at once* — the doctrine that is hard to establish and indispensable. Vyavahara-artham asrita: resorted to for practice's sake. So the account is adopted because the transactions need it, not because it can be defended (3.3.82's beings raised up by construction, as though by a primary word-meaning). The samuddesa's honesty here is exact: this is a working posit.

Verse 3.14.35.translation: Having commenced with the aggregate, the word is employed in that; in the designation by division, it is therefore said to have two meanings.
Verse 3.14.35.commentary: How the two-meaning account arises. Samudayam upakramya padam tasyam prayujyate: having *commenced with the aggregate*, the word is *employed in that* — one starts from the compound as a whole and uses it whole. Vibhagena samakhyane tatah tad dvy-artham ucyate: in the *designation by division*, *therefore*, it is said to have *two meanings*. So the two meanings appear only when one divides — the analysis produces them, and the usage never had them. This is 2.10's apoddhara exactly (words extracted from sentences as suffixes from words) at the compound's grain: the members are what division yields, and 3.14.34's yugapad-vacita is what one must say once one has divided. The samuddesa is tracking its own instrument's artifacts.

Verse 3.14.36.translation: Even in a sentence certain properties are fixed, and likewise in a derivative formation, for the two; but they have been described, without difference, as mere capacity.
Verse 3.14.36.commentary: Sentence and compound compared. Vakye 'pi niyata dharmah kecid: even in a *sentence* certain *properties are fixed* — the analytic phrase has its own settled behaviour. Vrttau dvayos tatha: and likewise in a *derivative formation*, for the *two* — the compound has its own. So both have fixed properties, and they differ. Te tv abhedena samarthya-matra eva upavarnitah: but they have been described, *without difference*, as *mere capacity* — samarthya-matra: capacity alone, the condition Panini states for compounding (samarthah padavidhih, 2.1.1). So the grammar's single condition — capacity — covers two things with different properties, and does not distinguish them. The verse identifies the gap the samuddesa must fill: what makes capacity issue in a compound rather than a phrase?

Verse 3.14.37.translation: In a derivative formation, from its operating in the particular; in separation, the state of expressing the general — this has been illustrated for 'syama' and the rest, in the comparison-compound and so on.
Verse 3.14.37.commentary: The samuddesa's first substantive difference between compound and phrase. Vrttau visesa-vrttitvat: in a *derivative formation*, from its *operating in the particular* — inside a compound, a member means a *particular*. Bhede samanya-vacita: in *separation*, the state of expressing the *general* — the same word, standing alone, means the general. And the illustration: upamana-samasadau syamadinam — for *'syama'* (dark) and the rest, in the *comparison-compound*: 'syama' alone means darkness-in-general; in 'megha-syama' (dark as a cloud) it means this particular shade. So composition narrows: the member drops from general to particular by being compounded. This is a real and delicate observation, and it explains 3.14.33's finding — the compound's meaning is not in the world's inventory because the world's inventory has the generals, and the compound has particulars.

Verse 3.14.38.translation: That derivative formation which is in another's word-meaning — of that there is no possibility in sentences; and of dvandva-words in the sense of 'ca', there is no formation in separation.
Verse 3.14.38.commentary: Two compounds that no sentence can render. Vrttir anya-padarthe ya tasya vakyesv asambhavah: the formation which is in *another's word-meaning* — the bahuvrihi, whose meaning is a possessor named by no member (2.219's aprayukta-padas ca artho bahuvrihau: the meaning whose word is not employed) — of *that* there is *no possibility in sentences*: no analytic phrase says what 'citragu' says, because the phrase must name the possessor and the compound does not. Ca-arthe dvandva-padanam ca bhede vrttir na vidyate: and of *dvandva*-words in the sense of *'ca'*, there is no formation *in separation* — the dvandva's togetherness (3.14.28's saha-vivaksa) is lost the moment one says 'and'. So two compound-types have no sentential equivalent at all, and the paraphrase-method (3.7.77's bheda-vakya, disowned) fails for them outright. Composition can say what syntax cannot.

Verse 3.14.39.translation: When there is separation, there is no possibility of 'nir' and the rest in the senses of 'having gone beyond' and so on; and before the formation, there is no expressing of the universal in 'gaura-khara' and the rest.
Verse 3.14.39.commentary: Two more cases where separation destroys the sense. Bhede sati niradinam krantady-arthesv asambhavah: when there is *separation*, there is *no possibility* of *'nir'* and the rest in the senses of *'having gone beyond'* — the preverb's transcending-sense exists only in composition; 'nir' standing alone means nothing of the kind (3.7.190's obligatory preverbs, whose roots are meaningless apart from them). Prag vrtteh jati-vacitvam na ca gaura-kharadisu: and *before the formation*, there is no *expressing of the universal* in *'gaura-khara'* and the rest — the compound names a kind that its members, uncompounded, do not reach. So the sense arrives *with* the composition and is not in the pieces beforehand. This is 2.36's asvakarna-principle (the compound understood whole, its members idle) given its temporal form: there was no meaning to compound.

Verse 3.14.40.translation: And in 'play' and 'livelihood', from non-expression by a sentence — the taking-up of 'nitya' is not proper, as in the yan-rule for 'crookedness'.
Verse 3.14.40.commentary: A rule-technical consequence. Kridayam jivikayam ca vakyena avacanat: in *'play'* and *'livelihood'* — Panini's compound-classes for names of games and occupations — from *non-expression by a sentence*: no phrase renders them. Na nitya-grahanam yuktam: the taking-up of *'nitya'* is *not proper* — the grammar need not call these compounds 'obligatory' (nitya-samasa: the class where no analysis exists), because their obligatoriness follows from there being nothing to analyse into. Kautilye yan-vidhau yatha: as in the *yan*-rule for *'crookedness'* — the intensive affix, likewise needing no obligatoriness-marker. So the category 'obligatory compound' is redundant: where no sentence expresses the sense, the compound is obligatory by that fact alone, and stipulating it is otiose (2.443's Panini does not legislate vacuously).

Verse 3.14.41.translation: Since in the domain of determination and the rest, mutual expectancy alone stands — therefore the prohibitions of compounding have no purpose.
Verse 3.14.41.commentary: The converse redundancy. Nirdharanadi-visaye vyapeksa eva yatah sthita: since in the domain of *determination* (3.7.147's nirdharana: singling out from a class) and the rest, *mutual expectancy alone* stands — vyapeksa: the mutual reaching of separate words, which is what a *sentence* has (2.4's sakanksa-avayavam). Samasa-pratisedhanam tato na asti prayojanam: *therefore* the *prohibitions of compounding* have *no purpose*. Where only vyapeksa is available, no compound could form, and forbidding it is idle. So the pair 3.14.40-41 finds redundancy at both ends: obligatory-compound rules where nothing else is possible, prohibition-rules where compounding is impossible anyway. And both redundancies point at the same distinction — vyapeksa versus something else — which 3.14.44 will name.

Verse 3.14.42.translation: By injunctions and prohibitions, an exhibition of difference and non-difference has been made — in the dvandva's singular-treatment, as in the teaching of the group-formation.
Verse 3.14.42.commentary: What the redundant rules of 3.14.40-41 are actually doing. Vidhibhih pratisedhais ca bheda-abheda-nidarsanam krtam: by *injunctions and prohibitions*, an *exhibition of difference and non-difference* has been *made* — the rules that seemed pointless are *exhibiting* something: where compounding is enjoined, non-difference; where prohibited, difference. Dvandva-ekavad-bhave: in the *dvandva's singular-treatment* — the rule making certain dvandvas take a singular ('pani-padam', hand-and-foot as one neuter): non-difference exhibited. Sangha-vrtty-upadesavat: as in the *teaching of the group-formation*. So the rules are nidarsana — exhibitions, teaching-devices (2.13's divisions for the avidvan; 3.7.147's separations for the unlearned). They do not legislate the facts; they display the difference-and-non-difference that composition involves.

Verse 3.14.43.translation: Capacity, though stated without specification, has been divided — by the world's settled arrangement — into formation and non-formation, by comprehenders who know usage.
Verse 3.14.43.commentary: The gap of 3.14.36 filled, and the filler is usage. Samarthyam avisesa-uktam api: *capacity*, though stated *without specification* — Panini's samarthah padavidhih says only 'capacity', and does not say when capacity yields a compound. Loka-vyavasthaya: by the *world's settled arrangement*. Vrtty-avrttyoh prayoga-jnaih vibhaktam pratipattrbhih: divided into *formation and non-formation*, by *comprehenders* who *know usage* — prayoga-jna: those who know how it is said. So the grammar leaves the distinction unstated and the sistas supply it: what compounds and what does not is settled by usage, and the rule's silence is not a gap but a deference (2.483's the world's practice, followed by the science; 3.13.21's cultivated declaring the gender).

Verse 3.14.44.translation: From the meaning's having withdrawn, elision and the rest is not contradicted; therefore the designation 'compound' is prescribed in becoming-one-meaning alone.
Verse 3.14.44.commentary: The samuddesa's central definition, and it is one of the great sentences of Indian grammar. Arthasya vinivrttatvat lug-adi na virudhyate: from the *meaning's having withdrawn*, *elision* and the rest is *not contradicted* — inside a compound, the members' meanings have *withdrawn* (vinivrtta: 2.240's withdrawing meanings; 3.12.27's jahat-svartha, abandoning one's own meaning), and so the grammar may elide their case-endings without violating anything: there is no meaning left for the endings to express. Ekarthibhava eva atah samasa-akhya vidhiyate: *therefore* the designation *'compound'* is prescribed in *ekarthibhava* alone. Ekarthibhava: *becoming-one-meaning* — the compound is not two meanings joined but a becoming-one, and that is what distinguishes it from the sentence's vyapeksa (3.14.41's mutual expectancy). So: sentence = two meanings reaching for each other; compound = two meanings become one. And the morphology follows: endings elide because their meanings are gone.

Verse 3.14.45.translation: And a settled option in the general is accepted by some; thus a sentence in mutual expectancy, a compound elsewhere, is taught.
Verse 3.14.45.commentary: The distinction of 3.14.44 given its rule-form. Vyavasthita-vibhasa ca samanye kaiscid isyate: a *settled option* in the *general* is accepted by *some* — vyavasthita-vibhasa: the grammar's category for an option that is not free but fixed by domain (some cases take one, some the other, and no case takes either). Tatha vakyam vyapeksayam samaso 'nyatra sisyate: thus a *sentence* in *mutual expectancy*, a *compound* *elsewhere*, is *taught*. So the option is settled: where vyapeksa obtains, a phrase; where ekarthibhava, a compound; and the speaker does not choose. This is why 3.14.40-41's rules were redundant — the domains do not overlap, and the option is settled by which relation obtains. The verse turns 3.14.44's definition into the grammar's operative rule.

Verse 3.14.46.translation: From likeness of hearing, even in the reality, when 'rajan' and the rest is resorted to — in a derivative formation, the expectancy for a qualifier withdraws, from its not being a conveyor.
Verse 3.14.46.commentary: The consequence of ekarthibhava for the members' behaviour. Tulya-srutitvat tattve 'pi rajadinam upasrite: from *likeness of hearing*, even in the *reality*, when *'rajan'* and the rest is *resorted to* — the word 'rajan' sounds the same inside 'rajapurusa' as outside it, and the same thing is meant. Vrttau visesana-akanksa agamakatvat nivartate: in a *derivative formation*, the *expectancy for a qualifier* *withdraws*, from its *not being a conveyor*. So inside the compound, 'rajan' stops wanting a qualifier — it is agamaka, not a conveyor: it no longer conveys anything on its own, so nothing can qualify it. This is 3.14.44's ekarthibhava in its effects: the members' meanings having withdrawn, their expectancies withdraw with them, and the compound admits no internal modification. The rule is real — one cannot insert an adjective into a compound — and here it is derived rather than stipulated.

Verse 3.14.47.translation: A relatum-word, being dependent, is always employed as such, all of it; that mutual expectancy of it, like its own meaning, is not lost even in a derivative formation.
Verse 3.14.47.commentary: The great exception to 3.14.46, and it is a real one. Sambandhi-sabdah sapekso nityam sarvah prayujyate: a *relatum-word* — one whose very sense is relational: 'father', 'master', 'enemy' — is *always* employed as *dependent*, *all of it*: there is no such thing as a father who is nobody's. Sva-arthavat sa vyapeksa asya vrttav api na hiyate: that *mutual expectancy* of it, *like its own meaning*, is *not lost* even in a *derivative formation*. So where 3.14.46 said the members' expectancies withdraw inside the compound, the relatum-words keep theirs — because their expectancy *is* their meaning: strip 'father' of its reaching-for-a-child and nothing is left to compound. The exception is principled and it marks the limit of ekarthibhava: meanings can withdraw, and a meaning that is nothing but a reaching cannot withdraw without vanishing.

Verse 3.14.48.translation: Those whose connection is with an aggregate — with 'guru-kula' and the like — having touched the members, are joined with the possessor of that.
Verse 3.14.48.commentary: How relatum-words reach through a compound. Samudayena sambandho yesam guru-kuladina: those whose connection is with an *aggregate* — with *'guru-kula'* (the teacher's house, the school) and the like: the compound names a whole. Samsprsya avayavan te ca yujyante tadvata saha: *having touched the members*, they are *joined with the possessor of that*. So a relatum-word connecting to 'guru-kula' touches the members — the teacher, the house — and then joins with what possesses the aggregate. The reaching passes *through* the compound's members without stopping at them. This is why 3.14.47's exception does not break the compound: the relatum-word's expectancy is satisfied by the whole, having grazed the parts. And samsprsya — having touched — is exact: it does not rest there.

Verse 3.14.49.translation: And the means, for the unlearned, are various, for comprehension's sake; from being another word, the difference of sentence and compound is utter.
Verse 3.14.49.commentary: The samuddesa's methodological reminder, placed where it is needed. Abudhan praty upayas ca vicitrah pratipattaye: the *means*, for the *unlearned*, are *various*, *for comprehension's sake* — the analyses, the paraphrases, the exhibitions of 3.14.42: all upaya, all discardable (2.38's things taken up to be let go; 2.13's divisions for the avidvan). And then the flat statement: sabdantaratvat atyanta-bhedo vakya-samasayoh — from *being another word*, the difference of *sentence and compound* is *utter*. Atyanta-bheda: utterly different. 'Rajnah purusah' and 'rajapurusah' are two words, not one word twice (2.177's rule: different meaning-behaviour, different base; 3.7.77's paraphrase having no contact with the root). So the paraphrase is a ladder and the compound is not the phrase — and everything said by analysing the compound into a phrase is said about the ladder.

Verse 3.14.50.translation: From the non-appearance of 'yukta' and the rest in 'go-ratha' and the like, both in the non-compound and in the compound — no following of withdrawal has been made by the science.
Verse 3.14.50.commentary: A rule-technical confirmation. Asamase samase ca go-rathadisv adarsanat: from the *non-appearance* of *'yukta'* and the rest in *'go-ratha'* (ox-cart) and the like, *both in the non-compound and in the compound* — the word 'yukta' (yoked) appears in neither: not in 'gobhir yuktah rathah' as a required element, nor in 'go-rathah'. Yuktadinam na sastrena nivrtty-anugamah krtah: *no following of withdrawal* has been *made* by the *science* — the grammar does not track 'yukta' as something that withdrew in compounding, because it was never there. So 'go-ratha' does not mean 'cart yoked with oxen' by suppressing 'yoked'; it means what it means, and the yoking is the hearer's supplement (2.163's penumbra supplied by association). The verse blocks the temptation to read compounds as ellipses.

Verse 3.14.51.translation: From being another word, 'yukta' and the rest is somewhere employed in a sentence; and the word 'gata' in the derivative formations, in 'praparna', 'prapalasa' and so on.
Verse 3.14.51.commentary: The complement of 3.14.50. Sabdantaratvad yuktadih kvacid vakye prayujyate: from being *another word*, 'yukta' and the rest is *somewhere employed in a sentence* — where one wants the yoking said, one says it, and the sentence is a different word from the compound (3.14.49's atyanta-bheda). Praparna-prapalasadau gata-sabdas ca vrttisu: and the word *'gata'* (gone) in the *derivative formations*, in *'praparna'* (leaf-fallen), *'prapalasa'* and so on — here the compound *does* carry a 'gone' sense that no member names. So: sometimes the sentence has a word the compound lacks (3.14.50), and sometimes the compound has a sense no member supplies (here). Neither is the other's abbreviation. The pair is the samuddesa's evidence for 3.14.49's utter difference — the two forms do not stand in any regular relation of expansion and contraction.

Verse 3.14.52.translation: For some, the state of qualifier and qualified, and likewise one substratum — from their seeing the reality in the means — is accepted for both derivative formation and sentence.
Verse 3.14.52.commentary: A rival that assimilates the two. Visesana-visesyatvam kaiscid ekas tatha asrayah: for *some*, the state of *qualifier and qualified*, and likewise *one substratum* — the two relations 3.14.21 catalogued. Upaye tattva-darsitvad isyate vrtti-vakyayoh: from their *seeing the reality in the means*, is accepted for *both* derivative formation *and* sentence. Upaye tattva-darsitvat: seeing the reality *in the means* — these theorists take the ladder for the thing (3.14.49's upaya, mistaken for what it reaches). So they hold that compound and phrase have the same structure, differing only in compression. Bhartrhari reports it and 3.14.53 answers: the structure is not shared, because the compound has no parts to bear it.

Verse 3.14.53.translation: Just as a word such as 'vrksa' stands established in a qualified meaning — so 'nilotpala' and the rest operates without the two portions.
Verse 3.14.53.commentary: The answer to 3.14.52, and the parallel is decisive. Padam yatha eva vrksadi visiste 'rthe vyavasthitam: just as a word such as *'vrksa'* (tree) stands established in a *qualified* meaning — 'tree' means a determinate thing, and nobody looks for qualifier and qualified inside it. Nilotpaladi api tatha bhagabhyam vartate vina: so *'nilotpala'* (blue lotus) and the rest operates *without the two portions* — the compound means its thing as simply as 'vrksa' does, and has no more internal structure. So the compound is a *word*, not a compressed phrase: it stands to its meaning as any stem does. This is 2.36's asvakarna (the compound understood whole, its members idle) and 3.14.44's ekarthibhava (becoming-one-meaning) shown in the plainest case — and it refutes 3.14.52 by pointing out that nobody demands parts of 'vrksa'.

Verse 3.14.54.translation: And of 'srotriya', 'ksetriya' and the rest, the cognition in the world is not by way of difference, as with 'vasistha' and 'gargya' — from the absence of concomitance with a like form.
Verse 3.14.54.commentary: Evidence from how derivatives are actually understood. Srotriya-ksetriyadinam: of *'srotriya'* (a learned brahmin), *'ksetriya'* (an incurable disease) and the rest — irregular derivatives whose bases are obscure. Na ca vasistha-gargyavat bhedena pratyayo loke: the *cognition* in the *world* is *not by way of difference*, as with *'vasistha'* and *'gargya'* — the patronymics, where everyone hears the base (Vasistha's son) and the derivative. Tulya-rupa-asamanvayat: from the *absence of concomitance* with a *like form* — 'srotriya' has no transparent base to concomit with. So some derivatives are heard as derived and some are not, and the difference is whether a like form recurs elsewhere (2.12's anvaya-vyatireka). Where it does not, the word is simply a word — which is 3.14.53's finding, and here it is confirmed by what hearers actually do.

Verse 3.14.55.translation: Like 'saptaparna' and the rest, no difference exists anywhere in a derivative formation; the division into conventional and non-conventional is also made for comprehension's sake.
Verse 3.14.55.commentary: The samuddesa's flat statement, with the work's favourite example. Sapta-parnadivat: like *'saptaparna'* — the seven-leaved tree that has not seven leaves (2.309's simile: the name indicates without describing, and everyone finds the tree). Bhedo na vrttau vidyate kvacit: *no difference* exists *anywhere* in a *derivative formation* — kvacit: anywhere at all. The compound has no internal division, as saptaparna has no seven. Rudhy-arudhi-vibhago 'pi kriyate pratipattaye: the division into *conventional* and *non-conventional* is *also* made *for comprehension's sake*. So even the grammar's distinction between opaque compounds (rudhi) and transparent ones is a teaching-device: *all* of them are opaque, and the transparent ones merely invite an analysis they do not require (2.37's rudhi-sabdatva acknowledged in the science; 2.36's sarvesu pratyayas tatha: the cognition is like that in all cases).

Verse 3.14.56-57.translation: (56) That designation which rests on the general, and that whose domain is the particular — from the taking-up of 'bahulam', there is no operation of both of those. (57) If, in 'su-suksma-jata-kesa' and the like, there were a compound in a member — then, from the internality of the single-performance, the member-accent would be the annuller.
Verse 3.14.56-57.commentary: Two technical cases. (56): ya samanya-asraya samjna visesa-visaya ca ya — the designation resting on the *general* and that whose domain is the *particular*: two classes of technical term. Bahula-grahanat na asti pravrttir ubhayos tayoh: from the taking-up of *'bahulam'* — Panini's word for 'variously', the marker of a rule that applies irregularly — there is *no operation of both*. Where the grammar says bahulam, it declines to specify, and neither designation governs. (57) turns to accent: su-suksma-jata-kesadau samaso 'vayave yadi syat — if, in *'su-suksma-jata-kesa'* (having fine matted hair) and the like, there were a *compound in a member*: an inner compound inside an outer one. Syat tantra-antarangatvat badhako 'vayava-svarah: then, from the *internality* of the *single-performance*, the *member-accent* would be the *annuller* — the inner compound's accent, being antaranga (settled first: 3.7.182), would override the outer's. So nesting compounds generates accent-conflicts, and antarangatva decides them.

Verse 3.14.58.translation: And in the derivative formation of an aggregate, a single portion is not optioned; since, in the option, difference alone is the fixed domain.
Verse 3.14.58.commentary: Why inner compounds cannot be optional. Samudayasya vrttau ca na ekadeso vibhasyate: in the *derivative formation of an aggregate*, a *single portion* is *not optioned* — one cannot make part of a compound optional while the rest stands. Bheda eva vibhasayam niyato visayo yatah: since, in the *option*, *difference alone* is the *fixed domain* — an option requires two separable alternatives, and separability is difference (3.14.45's vyavasthita-vibhasa: the settled option, its domains not overlapping). But a compound is ekarthibhava, becoming-one-meaning (3.14.44), and what has become one has no portions to option. So the impossibility follows from the definition: options need parts, and compounds have none. This is 3.14.55's finding (no difference anywhere in a vrtti) doing rule-technical work.

Verse 3.14.59.translation: And since that is not its domain, therefore there is no unfulfilled purpose — from the utter removal of the differences, in the commencement of non-difference.
Verse 3.14.59.commentary: The redundancy-worry answered. One might object: if inner portions cannot be optioned (3.14.58), the rules providing such options are purposeless. Yatas ca avisayah so 'syat tasmat na asti akrtarthata: since *that* is *not its domain*, *therefore* there is *no unfulfilled purpose* — a rule is not idle for failing to apply where it was never meant to. Abheda-prakrame 'tyantam bhedanam apasaranat: from the *utter removal* of the *differences*, in the *commencement of non-difference*. So once ekarthibhava commences, the differences are utterly removed (apasarana: driven off), and the option-rules simply have no territory there — which is not a defect in them. This is 3.14.41's argument (prohibitions of compounding have no purpose where only vyapeksa stands) run in the other direction: rules are not vacuous for having bounded domains.

Verse 3.14.60.translation: In 'maha-kastha-srita' and the like, there would be no difference among the three words — since the 'a'-substitution of a derivative-formation's member is not prohibited.
Verse 3.14.60.commentary: A three-word compound and its accent. Maha-kastha-srita ity evam na syad bhedah pada-traye: in *'maha-kastha-srita'* (resorting to great difficulty) and the like, there would be *no difference* among the *three words* — the three would compound into one without internal boundaries. Vrtty-avayavasya attvam yasman na pratisidhyate: since the *'a'-substitution* of a derivative-formation's *member* is *not prohibited* — 'maha' becomes 'maha-' by the rule that substitutes a for the final of 'mahat' in compounds, and nothing blocks it inside a member. So the substitution runs, the boundaries dissolve, and the three-word compound is one word with one accent. The verse is testing 3.14.44's ekarthibhava on a hard case: three members, and still no difference.

Verse 3.14.61.translation: But in 'maharanyam atita', the accent differs from that of a three-word form; since there, from internality, the member-accent is the annuller.
Verse 3.14.61.commentary: The contrast with 3.14.60. Maharanyam atite tu tri-padad bhidyate svarah: in *'maharanyam atita'* (having passed the great forest), the *accent differs* from that of a *three-word* form. Yasmat tatra antarangatvad badhako 'vayava-svarah: since *there*, from *internality*, the *member-accent* is the *annuller* — 'maharanya' is itself a compound, formed first (antaranga), and its accent is fixed before the outer compounding; so it overrides.
