So two superficially similar cases behave differently: 3.14.60's three words form one flat compound, and here an inner compound's accent survives into the outer. And the difference is antarangatva — what was formed first. The pair shows that ekarthibhava does not erase everything: what became one *earlier* leaves a mark on what becomes one later.

Verse 3.14.62.translation: But from the greater strength of what is taught while the other is present, it is the 'thathadi'-accent alone; therefore in a two-word form the three are not compounded simultaneously.
Verse 3.14.62.commentary: The accent-conflict resolved by a metarule. Sati-sista-baliyastvat: from the *greater strength* of *what is taught while the other is present* — the sati-sista principle: a rule taught in the presence of another, and therefore later, prevails (the Paribhasa tradition's own maxim). Thathadi-svara eva tu: it is the *'thathadi'*-accent alone — Panini's accent-rule for the affix class beginning with tha. Dvi-pade tena yugapat tritayam na samasyate: *therefore* in a *two-word* form, the *three* are *not compounded simultaneously*. So the accent-facts prove a derivational fact: because the thathadi-accent wins, the three members cannot have compounded at once; they compounded two at a time. The argument runs from accent back to derivation-order — and it is exactly the sort of thing 3.14.61's antarangatva made possible. The samuddesa is reading composition's history off its phonology.

Verse 3.14.63.translation: Those which have the state of not being honoured, in what has for its purpose the following of another's aim — for those, even the state of qualifier and qualified is not conceivable.
Verse 3.14.63.commentary: The bahuvrihi's members reduced to nothing. Yesam apujyamanatvam parartha-anugamarthake: those which have the state of *not being honoured* — apujyamana: unhonoured, i.e. not principal — in what has for its purpose the *following of another's aim*: the bahuvrihi, whose whole business is to reach a possessor outside itself (3.14.38's anya-padartha). Visesana-visesyatvam api tesam na kalpate: for those, *even* the state of *qualifier and qualified* is *not conceivable*. So in 'citragu' neither 'citra' nor 'go' is principal — both are unhonoured, both point away — and where nothing is principal, nothing can be qualified. This finishes off 3.14.52's rival: the qualifier-qualified structure it wanted to find in every compound is unavailable in the one compound-type that most obviously has two members.

Verse 3.14.64.translation: A particular, though heard, among principals or among subordinates — in a sentence, from its being another word; but in a derivative formation it never exists.
Verse 3.14.64.commentary: Visesah sruyamano 'pi pradhanesu gunesu va: a *particular*, though *heard*, among *principals* or among *subordinates*. Sabdantaratvad vakye: in a *sentence*, from its being *another word* — the phrase has particulars because the phrase is a different word (3.14.49's atyanta-bheda) and its members really do stand apart. Vrttau nityam na vidyate: but in a *derivative formation* it *never* exists — nityam: ever, at all. So the particulars one seems to hear inside a compound belong to the phrase one is silently substituting, not to the compound. This is 3.14.37 sharpened: there the compound's member meant a particular; here the compound has no members to mean anything, and what one hears is one's own paraphrase.

Verse 3.14.65.translation: Even when the connection with a particular object has been consumed by 'krta' and the rest, another word 'krta', independent of the particular, operates.
Verse 3.14.65.commentary: Visesa-karma-sambandhe nirbhukte 'pi krtadibhih: even when the connection with a *particular object* has been *consumed* — nirbhukta: used up, eaten through — by *'krta'* (done) and the rest. Visesa-nirapekso 'nyah krta-sabdah pravartate: *another* word *'krta'*, *independent* of the particular, *operates*. So in 'krta-purvin' (one who has done it before), the 'krta' that ate its object is not the 'krta' standing in the compound: a second, object-indifferent 'krta' is doing the work. Bhartrhari's move is characteristic — where the analysis produces a contradiction (the object is both consumed and still available), he does not repair the analysis but posits that the analysis was tracking two different words. Which is 3.14.49's principle again, applied inside a single form.

Verse 3.14.66.translation: When there is intransitivity, that kta-ending which expresses the bare state — from that arises the objects' connection with an agent possessed of the action.
Verse 3.14.66.commentary: The mechanism of 3.14.65. Akarmakatve saty evam ktantam bhava-abhidhayi yat: when there is *intransitivity*, that *kta*-ending which expresses the *bhava* — the bare state, the action with no object in view (3.7.115's bhava-vacana; 3.8.44's action as its own object). Tatah kriyavata kartra yogo bhavati karmanam: *from that* arises the *objects'* connection with an *agent* possessed of the *action*. So the sequence is: the kta expresses the state alone; the state implies an agent who has the action; and only then do the objects attach — to the agent, not to the kta. The object is thus reachable without the kta ever taking it, which is exactly what 3.14.65 needed. And the derivation runs through bhava — the actionless action — which is this work's standard solvent for transitivity puzzles.

Verse 3.14.67.translation: As the action residing in 'gata' and the rest, without analysis, is connected with objects such as 'village' — likewise that residing in 'krta-purvin' and the rest.
Verse 3.14.67.commentary: The precedent. Avigraha gatadi-stha yatha gramadi-karmabhih sambadhyate kriya: as the action residing in *'gata'* (gone) and the rest, *without analysis* — avigraha: unanalysed, the compound taken whole — is *connected* with objects such as *'village'*: 'grama-gata' means gone-to-the-village though nothing in it governs the village. Tadvat krta-purvyadisu sthita: *likewise* that residing in *'krta-purvin'* and the rest. So the connection is made by the whole compound, unanalysed, and no member takes the object. Avigraha is the load-bearing word: the compound relates to the world directly, without passing through its own supposed structure. This is 3.14.53's finding (the compound stands to its meaning as 'vrksa' does) doing the work that the analysis could not.

Verse 3.14.68.translation: 'Mundi', 'sutri' and the rest are as though pervaded by parts that are not there; the roots, divided, have imagined selves — like 'kutti' and 'carci'.
Verse 3.14.68.commentary: One of the work's great verses, and the beginning of its most radical stretch. Mundi-sutryadayo 'sadbhir bhagair anugata iva: *'mundi'* (to shave), *'sutri'* (to thread) and the rest are *as though* pervaded by parts that are *not there* — asadbhir bhagaih: by *unreal* parts; iva: as though. The grammarian analyses 'mundi' into a nominal base plus a denominative affix, and the analysis is a fiction: nothing in the root is that base. Vibhaktah kalpitatmano dhatavah kutti-carcivat: the *roots*, *divided*, have *imagined selves* — kalpita-atman: their very selfhood is a construct — like *'kutti'*, *'carci'*, curadi roots whose supposed derivation is invisible. So division does not find the root's parts; it *invents* them, and the root's identity is an artifact of the inventing. This is 2.10's apoddhara pushed below the word: the extraction goes all the way down and finds constructs at every level.

Verse 3.14.69.translation: In 'putriya' there is no son; rather there is a particular desire of such a kind that, without any following of 'son', it stands settled upon a son.
Verse 3.14.69.commentary: The fiction demonstrated. Putriyatau na putro 'sti: in *'putriya'* — the denominative 'to want a son' — there is *no son*: no putra-element is doing anything in the verb. Visesecchha tu tadrsi: rather there is a *particular desire* of *such a kind*. Vinaiva putra-anugamat ya putre vyavatisthate: which, *without any following of 'son'*, *stands settled upon a son* — the desire is son-directed without containing 'son'. So 'putriya' names a single, unanalysable wanting whose specificity is intrinsic, not composed. This is 3.14.44's ekarthibhava at the root's own level: the meaning has become one, and 'putra + iccha' is the grammarian's reconstruction of something that never was two. The verse is also a small phenomenology — the desire is son-shaped all the way through, and does not consist of a general wanting plus a son.

Verse 3.14.70.translation: As 'dhr', having the breaths as object, without the breaths, in 'jiv'; and here there is neither 'dhr' nor breaths — 'jiv' is a different action.
Verse 3.14.70.commentary: The decisive parallel, and the tradition's most famous instance. Pranair vina yatha dharir jivatau prana-karmakah: as *'dhr'* (to hold), having the *breaths* as object, *without* the breaths, in *'jiv'* (to live) — the standard gloss makes 'jivati' = 'pranan dharayati', holds the breaths. Na catra dharir na pranah: and *here* there is *neither* 'dhr' *nor* breaths — the verb contains no holding and no breaths. Jivatis tu kriyantaram: 'jiv' is a *different action*. Living is not breath-holding; it is its own single act, and the gloss is a gloss. So the whole apparatus of derivation-by-paraphrase is put in question by one example everyone accepts: nobody thinks 'jivati' is secretly about pranas, and nobody should think 'putriya' is secretly about a putra. The argument is short and it is fatal.

Verse 3.14.71.translation: Likewise, without 'is' and 'putra', in 'putriya' there is a different action; but the divisions, for the sake of exposition, are similar conveyors.
Verse 3.14.71.commentary: The parallel closed, and the concession made. Tatha vina isi-putrabhyam putriyayam kriyantaram: likewise, without *'is'* (to desire) and *'putra'*, in *'putriya'* there is a *different action* — as 'jiv' is not dhr-plus-pranas, 'putriya' is not is-plus-putra. Anvakhyanaya bhedas tu sadrsah pratipadakah: *but* the *divisions*, for the sake of *exposition*, are *similar conveyors*. Anvakhyana: the grammarian's proper business — explaining forms already given. Sadrsah pratipadakah: *similar* conveyors — they resemble what they convey, and they do convey it. So Bhartrhari does not abolish the analysis; he relocates it. It is not a discovery of the word's composition but an exposition by resemblance — and it works, which is why it survives its own falsity (3.14.49's upaya; 2.38's what is taken up in order to be let go).

Verse 3.14.72-73.translation: (72) And from implication, by a usage operating in another domain, the object of the desiderative kyac, though existing, is employed only in a sentence. (73) The word made by the well-known one expressing censure of the state — in the repetition, from likeness of form, the yan-ending is not employed.
Verse 3.14.72-73.commentary: Two rule-technical confirmations. (72): aksepac ca prayogena visayantara-vartina — from *implication*, by a *usage* operating in *another domain*. Sad api iccha-kyacah karma vakya eva prayujyate: the *object* of the desiderative *kyac*, *though existing*, is employed *only in a sentence*. The object exists — 'putriya' is about a son — but it can only be *said* in a phrase, never in the derivative: which is 3.14.69's point in Panini's own currency. (73): prasiddhena krtah sabdo bhava-garha-abhidhayina — the word made by the *well-known* one *expressing censure of the state*. Abhyase tulya-rupatvan na yan-antah prayujyate: in the *repetition*, from *likeness of form*, the *yan*-ending is *not employed* — where the reduplicated form would look identical, the affix is withheld. Both verses show the grammar itself conceding that the analysis and the form come apart.

Verse 3.14.74.translation: As words are divided by parts as though imagined, so are they to be expounded; the science stands very far off.
Verse 3.14.74.commentary: The samuddesa's most famous line, and one of the boldest sentences a grammarian has written about grammar. Sabda yatha vibhajyante bhagair iva vikalpitaih: as *words are divided* by *parts as though imagined* — vikalpita: constructed, optional, made up; iva: as though. Anvakhyeyas tatha: *so* are they *to be expounded* — the exposition follows the imagined division, faithfully. Sastram atidure vyavasthitam: the *science* stands *very far off*. Atidure: at a *great distance*. So Panini's whole apparatus — bases, affixes, substitutions, the entire machinery — is erected on parts that are not there, and the science that erects it is nowhere near the thing (1.1's indivisible sphota; 2.10's apoddhara). This is not scepticism about grammar; it is grammar's self-knowledge. The science works and it does not touch. Bhartrhari says so without flinching and without giving anything up.

Verse 3.14.75.translation: Some following of the meaning, imagined by the view alone: the word in the sentence, the root in the word, and the part in the root — like 'mundi'.
Verse 3.14.75.commentary: The distance of 3.14.74 measured at every level. Arthasya anugamam kancid drstya eva parikalpitam: *some* following of the *meaning*, imagined by the *view alone* — drstya eva: by the analytic look and nothing else; parikalpita: fully constructed. And then the three storeys of the fiction: padam vakye — the *word* in the *sentence* (1.73's the sentence alone is real; 2.1's akhandata); pade dhatuh — the *root* in the *word*; dhatau bhagas ca mundivat — the *part* in the *root*, like *'mundi'* (3.14.68's roots with imagined selves). So the same construction repeats at every scale, and at every scale there is only a resemblance of meaning-following, seen by the one who looks for it. The regress is complete and the verse states it flatly: sentence, word, root, sub-root — all the way down, and nothing found.

Verse 3.14.76.translation: Non-separation is in correctness; derivation is unsettled. One should not regard the means of the comprehenders as true.
Verse 3.14.76.commentary: The moral, stated in the imperative. Aviprayogah sadhutve: *non-separation* is in *correctness* — what is correct is the undivided form itself; sadhutva attaches to the whole word, not to its parts (1.27's correctness not compositional). Vyutpattir anavasthita: *derivation* is *unsettled* — anavasthita: without a fixed footing, the etymologist's constructions shifting with the etymologist. Upayan pratipattrnam na abhimanyeta satyatah: one should *not* regard the *means* of the *comprehenders* as *true*. Na abhimanyeta satyatah: do not take them for the truth. This is the samuddesa's — perhaps the work's — governing instruction, and it applies retroactively to everything Bhartrhari has himself constructed: the upayas are for comprehension, they are not descriptions, and taking them for descriptions is the one error the whole book is written against.

Verse 3.14.77.translation: Just as 'day' in 'dittha', so 'pac' in 'pacaka'; and both 'day' and 'pac' alike are non-worldly.
Verse 3.14.77.commentary: The argument that gives 3.14.76 its teeth, and it is merciless. Yathaiva ditthe dayatih: just as *'day'* in *'dittha'* — 'dittha' is the grammarians' standard meaningless proper name, the very example of a word with no derivation; and yet one *could* extract a root 'day' from it, and the extraction would be pure fabrication. Pacake pacatis tatha: *so* 'pac' in *'pacaka'* (cook). Dayatis ca pacis caiva dvav apy etav alaukikau: and *both*, 'day' *and* 'pac', are *alaukika* — *not of the world*. So the root everyone accepts and the root everyone would laugh at stand on exactly the same footing: both are products of the same extraction, and neither is out there. The only difference is that one extraction has a tradition behind it. Alaukika is the grammarians' own word for their technical fictions, and Bhartrhari turns it on their most cherished object.

Verse 3.14.78.translation: Base and affix are to be conjectured from the word, and the word likewise from those two; the science is by the learned, from indicatory letters, accents and the rest — not directed at those.
Verse 3.14.78.commentary: The circle exhibited, and the science placed. Prakrti-pratyayav uhyau padat: *base* and *affix* are to be *conjectured* from the *word* — uhya: to be surmised, guessed at, reasoned out; they are not given, they are inferred backwards from the form. Tabhyam padam tatha: and the *word* from *those two*, *likewise*. So the circle is closed and admitted: the parts are conjectured from the whole, and the whole is then derived from the parts. Anubandha-svaradibhyah sistaih sastram na tan prati: the *science* is by the *learned*, from *indicatory letters*, *accents* and the rest — the it-markers, the tone-marks, the whole notational apparatus — na tan prati: *not directed at those*. The science is built out of its own devices and is not about them. So the Vrtti-samuddesa's method-stretch ends where it must: grammar is a circle of conjectures made of notation, it stands atidure (3.14.74), it is true of nothing, and it is exactly right.
Verse 3.14.78.contested: The final pada admits more than one construal. We take 'na tan prati' as denying that the sastra is *about* its own devices (anubandhas, accents) — the notation is instrumental, not the subject-matter. It can also be read as denying that the sastra is directed at the prakrti and pratyaya of pada a, i.e. that the science does not concern the conjectured parts as realities; on this reading the circle is being disowned rather than the notation. Both readings serve the samuddesa's conclusion; they differ on what 'tan' resumes.

Verse 3.14.79.translation: But the science's way of seeing, even where the mere obtaining of a rule is undetermined, is joined with harm; the science is an eye for those who do not see.
Verse 3.14.79.commentary: The counterweight to 3.14.76, and it arrives immediately. Sastra-drstis tu sastrasya praptimatre 'py aniscite: the *science's way of seeing* — sastra-drsti: construing the world by the grammar — even where the *mere obtaining* of a rule is *undetermined*. Yujyate pratyavayena: is *joined with harm* — pratyavaya: fault, damage. So one may not run the sastra's vision past the point where the sastra itself has settled anything. And then the concession, which is also the vindication: sastram caksur apasyatam — the *science* is an *eye* for those who *do not see*. Caksuh: an eye. So 3.14.76's warning does not demote grammar; it locates it. The science is not true, and it is *sight* — for those who lack it there is nothing to see with. The two verses together are the samuddesa's settled position: an eye is not the world, and one does not throw away one's eyes.

Verse 3.14.80.translation: And from the expressing of another meaning, priority and posteriority are not differentiated, in 'rajadanta', 'ahitagni' and the rest, and in 'rajasva' and the rest, in every way.
Verse 3.14.80.commentary: Word-order dissolved along with the parts. Arthantara-abhidhanac ca paurvaparyam na bhidyate: from the *expressing of another meaning*, *priority and posteriority* are *not differentiated* — the compound expresses a meaning other than its members', so the order of members carries nothing. Rajadanta-ahitagny-adi-rajasvadisu sarvatha: in *'rajadanta'* (the class where the expected first member goes second), *'ahitagni'* (the bahuvrihi with its participle first), *'rajasva'* and the rest — *sarvatha*: in every way. So the irregular orders are not irregular: order was never semantic to begin with, and Panini's rajadanta-gana simply registers usage. This follows from 3.14.68-78: if the members are not there, their sequence is not there either, and the rules governing sequence are notation (3.14.78's anubandha-svaradi).

Verse 3.14.81.translation: Those which, without affixes, express a different meaning in the derivative formation — 'garga' and the rest — their correctness is followed out by means of elision.
Verse 3.14.81.commentary: How the grammar copes with what it cannot derive. Vinaiva pratyayair vrttau ye bhinnarthabhidhayinah: those which, *without affixes*, express a *different meaning* in the derivative formation — 'gargah' meaning Garga's descendants, with no visible patronymic affix. Gargadayo luka tesam sadhutvam anugamyate: *'garga'* and the rest — their *correctness* is *followed out* by means of *luk*, elision. So the grammar posits an affix and then deletes it, and the form is thereby certified sadhu. Anugamyate is the exact word: the correctness is *followed*, tracked, come after — not conferred. The form was already correct (3.14.76's aviprayogah sadhutve); the luk is how the science catches up with it. This is 3.14.79's eye at work: seeing an affix that is not there is what lets the science see the word at all.

Verse 3.14.82.translation: If, from the connection 'this is that', 'babhru' and the rest were employed without an affix, there would be a restriction to offspring.
Verse 3.14.82.commentary: The cost of taking the elision seriously. So 'yam ity abhisambandhat: from the *connection* 'this is that' — the identification by which a descendant is called by the ancestor's name. Pratyayena vina yadi babhrvadayah prayujyeran: *if* *'babhru'* and the rest were *employed* *without an affix* — i.e. if the naked stem simply meant the descendant, with no elided taddhita behind it. Apatye niyamo bhavet: there would be a *restriction to offspring* — the identification would have to be restricted to offspring, which nothing in 'this is that' warrants: an heir, a pupil, a namesake all satisfy it. So the elided affix earns its keep after all: it is what narrows the identification. The verse concedes the point against itself, which is the samuddesa's habit — the fictions of 3.14.74 are not idle fictions, and where one removes them, real work goes undone.

Verse 3.14.83.translation: In the connection 'this is that', apart from an indicatory mark, in 'prastha' and the rest the wife alone is not understood by restriction.
Verse 3.14.83.commentary: The same argument on a second case. So 'yam ity abhisambandhe linga-upavyanjanad rte: in the connection 'this is that', *apart from* an *indicatory mark* — linga: here the gender-sign, and upavyanjana: the accompanying indication. Prasthadisu na jaya eva niyamena pratiyate: in *'prastha'* and the rest, the *wife alone* is not *understood by restriction* — 'prastha' in the feminine is supposed to yield the headman's wife, but the identification 'this is that' does not by itself pick out a wife rather than a daughter or a servant. So again the bare relation underdetermines, and something else — the gender-mark — must do the restricting. Both 3.14.82 and this verse show the same shape: identification is broad, usage is narrow, and the grammar's machinery is what covers the distance.

Verse 3.14.84-85.translation: (84) When a particular connection of measure and measured is accepted, the incorrectness of 'prastha' and the rest would follow, without the taddhita. (85) The taddhita, by difference of rule, or else the sentence, would be optional; there, in the case of excess over the measure, the first case-ending is again taught.
Verse 3.14.84-85.commentary: Measure-words, and the same lesson a third time. (84): mana-meya-abhisambandha-visese 'ngikrte — when a *particular connection* of *measure and measured* is *accepted*: 'prastha' as a unit, and what is measured by it. Prasthadinam asadhutvam taddhitena vina bhavet: the *incorrectness* of 'prastha' and the rest would follow, *without the taddhita* — without the affix, the measure-word cannot be certified. (85): taddhito yoga-bhedena vakyam va syad vibhasitam — the *taddhita*, by *difference of rule*, or else the *sentence*, would be *optional*. Parimana-adhike tatra prathama sisyate punah: there, in the case of *excess over the measure*, the *first* case-ending is *again* taught — where the quantity exceeds the unit, the nominative returns. So the grammar handles measure by an affix, an option, and a case-rule, and every one of them is machinery (3.14.78).

Verse 3.14.86.translation: In the correctness of the excess, that very thing is the illustration; that, with its excess accepted, is joined with all the case-endings.
Verse 3.14.86.commentary: Atiriktasya sadhutve tad eva ca nidarsanam: in the *correctness* of the *excess*, *that very thing* is the *illustration* — the excess-case of 3.14.85 is itself the demonstration. Yujyate 'ngikrtadhikyam tat sarvabhir vibhaktibhih: that, with its *excess accepted*, is *joined with all the case-endings* — once the excess is granted, the form takes every case freely, and no restriction survives. So the measure-word, having been certified by the taddhita, behaves like any nominal: the machinery does its work and then withdraws, leaving an ordinary word. Which is the pattern of the whole stretch — the grammar's constructs are scaffolding, they hold the form up while it is being certified, and the finished word stands without them (3.14.71's upayas, similar and discardable).

Verse 3.14.87.translation: In 'sukla' and the rest, the elision of 'matup', from the seeing of the difference; for the sake of removing incorrectness they are correct, like 'bida' and the rest.
Verse 3.14.87.commentary: The construct named for what it is. Sukladisu matub-lopo vyatirekasya darsanat: in *'sukla'* (white) and the rest, the *elision* of *'matup'* — the possessive affix — from the *seeing of the difference*: one sees that 'sukla' behaves as though it meant 'possessing whiteness', so one posits matup and deletes it. Asadhutva-nivrtty-artham sadhavas te bidadivat: *for the sake of removing incorrectness* they are *correct*, like *'bida'* and the rest. Asadhutva-nivrtty-artham is the confession: the elided affix exists in order to *stop the form being ungrammatical* — that is its entire purpose, and it has no other. So the science posits what it needs to certify what usage already gives it (3.14.43's the world's settled arrangement; 3.14.81's anugamyate). Bhartrhari does not object. He only insists one know what one is doing.

Verse 3.14.88.translation: From the qualifier, in the qualified meaning, when there is an accumulation of that state, and again in the withdrawal — some declare there to be a derivative formation.
Verse 3.14.88.commentary: A rival account of what composition is, and it is psychological. Visesanad visesye 'rthe tad-bhava-abhyuccaye sati: *from the qualifier*, in the *qualified meaning*, when there is an *accumulation* of *that state* — abhyuccaya: heaping up, the qualifier's character piling into the qualified. Punas ca pratisamhare: and *again* in the *withdrawal* — pratisamhara: the drawing-back, the qualifier retracting once it has deposited its contribution. Vrttim eke pracaksate: *some* declare there to be a *derivative formation*. So on this view a compound is a two-beat mental event: the qualifier pours itself into the qualified and then withdraws, and what is left — qualified, and standing alone — is the compound. This is 3.14.9-11's deposited delimitation given a temporal structure, and 3.14.92-93 will endorse its phenomenology while denying its divisions.

Verse 3.14.89-90.translation: (89) The cognition of the cause, being prior, is not reached by what is caused; and of the cognition in the possessor of the cause, there is no likeness of form with the cause. (90) From cognition accompanied by an impression, there is no contact even from memory; in the operation of those causes, the graspable would not be of that kind.
Verse 3.14.89-90.commentary: An epistemological objection to 3.14.88's two-beat account. (89): nimitte pratyayah purvo na anuprapto nimittina — the *cognition of the cause* (the qualifier), being *prior*, is *not reached* by what is *caused*: the earlier cognition is over before the later one exists, so the later cannot get at it to receive a deposit. Nimittavati buddhes ca na nimitta-sarupata: and of the *cognition* in the *cause-possessor* there is *no likeness of form* with the cause — the qualified's cognition does not resemble the qualifier's, so nothing was transferred. (90): samskara-sahitaj jnanan nopaslesah smrter api — even bringing in *impressions* and *memory* gives no *contact* (upaslesa: adhesion). Vyapare tan-nimittanam na grahyam syat tathavidham: in their *operation*, the *graspable* would not be *of that kind*. So the deposit-and-withdraw story has no mechanism: two cognitions cannot touch.

Verse 3.14.91.translation: And in the operation of the inner organ, the imagining of an external object is purposeless. Therefore either there is no assistance, or the graspable is not so situated.
Verse 3.14.91.commentary: The objection driven home, and then the dilemma. Antahkarana-vrttau ca vyartha bahyartha-kalpana: in the *operation of the inner organ*, the *imagining of an external object* is *purposeless* — vyartha: pointless. If the qualifier-qualified business happens in the mind, positing an outer object to anchor it adds nothing (2.129's the external is a support one does not need; 3.3.53's meanings that are the intellect's own). Tasmad anupakaro va grahyam va na tatha sthitam: *therefore* either there is *no assistance* — the object contributes nothing — *or* the *graspable* is *not so situated*: it is not out there as supposed. Both horns are unwelcome to 3.14.88's theorists, and Bhartrhari leaves them there. The dilemma clears the ground for 3.14.92's own account, which will keep the phenomenon and drop the transaction.

Verse 3.14.92-93.translation: (92) The intellect operates in the meaning as though threaded through, from the conjunction; the expounders, having divided, then declare it by way of difference. (93) And in that self, undivided, having resorted to a second cognition, they think there is as it were a division of qualifier and qualified.
Verse 3.14.92-93.commentary: The samuddesa's own account, and it is the work's whole thesis in two verses. (92): anusyuta iva samsrster arthe buddhih pravartate — the *intellect* operates in the *meaning* *as though threaded through*, from the *conjunction*. Anusyuta: sewn through, continuous, one thread running the length of it — the single most beautiful word in the samuddesa, and the exact opposite of composition. The cognition of 'nilotpala' is not blue plus lotus; it is one thread. Vyakhyatharo vibhajya atha tam bhedena pracaksate: the *expounders*, *having divided*, *then* declare it *by way of difference* — the division comes first, from the expositor, and the difference is announced afterwards. (93): tad-atmany avibhakte ca buddhy-antaram upasritah — in that *self*, *undivided*, having *resorted to a second cognition*. Vibhagam iva manyante visesana-visesyayoh: they *think* there is *as it were* a *division* of qualifier and qualified. Iva: as it were. So the division is not in the meaning; it is in the second cognition one brings to it (1.89's the knower's own construction returned to the known). Qualifier and qualified are what a mind that has already divided finds when it looks. This is 1.1's indivisible sphota and 3.14.74's imagined parts and 2.10's apoddhara, all resolved into a single psychological fact: the thread is one, and the expositor cuts it.

Verse 3.14.94.translation: And carrying on the constructed derivation for the unlearned, they speak of the property of abandonment and of accumulation in the expression of another's meaning.
Verse 3.14.94.commentary: The tradition's two doctrines placed as upayas. Abudhan prati vrttim ca vartayantah prakalpitam: *carrying on* the *constructed* derivation *for the unlearned* — prakalpita: fabricated (3.14.75's parikalpita); abudhan prati: aimed at those who do not know (3.14.49). Ahuh pararthavacane tyaga-abhyuccaya-dharmatam: they speak of the *property* of *abandonment* and of *accumulation* in the *expression of another's meaning*. Tyaga: the jahat-svartha doctrine — in a bahuvrihi the members abandon their own meanings (3.12.27). Abhyuccaya: the ajahat-svartha doctrine — they retain and heap them up (3.14.88). The tradition's long quarrel between these two, and Bhartrhari's verdict is that both are teaching-devices for the unlearned, spoken over a meaning that was never divided (3.14.92's anusyuta). Neither party is wrong; both are describing their own cut.

Verse 3.14.95.translation: That meaning is understood from agreement, or the contradictory one withdraws; in the two-meaninged, or else in another meaning — there they call it 'subordinate'.
Verse 3.14.95.commentary: Upasarjana defined by what happens to the loser. Anvayad gamyate so 'rtho: that meaning is understood *from agreement* — the surviving sense, carried by concomitance. Virodhi va nivartate: *or* the *contradictory* one *withdraws* — where two senses conflict, one leaves. Dvyartham arthantare vapi tatrahur upasarjanam: in the *two-meaninged*, or else in *another meaning* — *there* they call it *'upasarjana'*, subordinate. So Panini's technical term for the compound's non-principal member is being derived from three quite different situations: agreement carrying one sense, contradiction expelling one, and the whole thing decamping into a third meaning. The term is one and the phenomena are three — which is what 3.14.96 is about to say in general.

Verse 3.14.96.translation: Multiplicity is a mere means; that aggregate is one alone. Or, by alternation and accumulation, the construction of difference and of conjunction.
Verse 3.14.96.commentary: The samuddesa's summary sentence, and it is eight words long. Upaya-matram nanatvam: *multiplicity* is a *mere means* — nanatva: manyness, plurality, the whole apparatus of parts; upaya-matra: a means and *nothing else* (3.14.76's do not take the upayas for true). Samudayas tv eka eva sah: the *aggregate* is *one alone* — eka eva: one, simply. Vikalpa-abhyuccayabhyam va bheda-samsarga-kalpana: *or*, by *alternation* and *accumulation*, the construction of *difference* and of *conjunction* — the two doctrines of 3.14.94 (tyaga and abhyuccaya) recast as two ways of *constructing* what is not there. Kalpana: construction. So the tradition's rival theories of the compound are alternative fabrications of a multiplicity that is a device, laid over an aggregate that is one. This is 3.14.92's anusyuta stated as doctrine.

Verse 3.14.97.translation: Of those thus carrying on the derivation for the unlearned's comprehension, the means of instruction are diverse, unsettled among persons.
Verse 3.14.97.commentary: Why the tradition has rival theories at all. Vrttim vartayatam evam abudha-pratipattaye: of those *carrying on* the derivation for the *unlearned's comprehension* (3.14.94's abudhan prati). Bhinnah sambodhana-upayah purusesv anavasthitah: the *means of instruction* are *diverse*, *unsettled among persons* — anavasthita: without fixed footing (3.14.76's vyutpattir anavasthita), and purusesu: varying from teacher to teacher. So the quarrel between jahat-svartha and ajahat-svartha is not a disagreement about the facts: it is two teachers' pedagogy, and pedagogy varies because pupils vary. Nothing in the object adjudicates, because the object is one (3.14.96) and neither doctrine is about it. This is the most irenic thing in the samuddesa and also the most deflationary.

Verse 3.14.98.translation: Those case-endings which are expressive or indicative of numbers — when the member has that form, in a derivative formation the difference of number withdraws.
Verse 3.14.98.commentary: Number inside compounds. Vacika dyotika vapi sankhyanam ya vibhaktayah: the case-endings which are *expressive* or *indicative* of *numbers* — the samuddesa's standing distinction (2.190's vacaka against dyotaka: does the ending *say* the number or merely *reveal* it?), and here left open, because it does not matter for the point. Tad-rupe 'vayave vrttau sankhya-bhedo nivartate: when the *member* has *that form*, in a *derivative formation* the *difference of number* *withdraws*. So 'brahmana-kula' does not say how many brahmanas: the number-difference goes out with the case-ending. This is 3.14.44's ekarthibhava applied to number — the meanings having withdrawn, their number withdraws with them (3.14.46's expectancy withdrawing). And it raises the question 3.14.99 must answer: what number, then, does the compound have?

Verse 3.14.99.translation: Or another number altogether arises there — the number of non-different unity; that is called the unanalysed form of conjunction of the numbers.
Verse 3.14.99.commentary: The answer, and it is not zero. Abhedaikatva-sankhya va tatra anya eva upajayate: *another* number *altogether* *arises* there — the *number of non-different unity*: abheda-ekatva, a oneness that is not the numeral one but the absence of internal difference. Samsarga-rupam sankhyanam avibhaktam tad ucyate: that is called the *unanalysed form* of the *conjunction* of the *numbers* — avibhakta: undivided (3.14.93's tad-atmany avibhakte). So the compound does not lack number; it has a number of its own, which is the numbers of its members fused past recovery. The members' numbers are in there the way the herbs are in the honey — which is exactly the image 3.14.100 reaches for.
Verse 3.14.99.contested: The source records a variant at pada c: samsarga-rupam ('the form which is conjunction') against samsargi-rupam ('the form of what is conjoined'). We translate the former. The variant shifts the emphasis slightly — whether the unanalysed thing is the conjoining or the conjuncts — but on either reading the point stands: what results is undivided.

Verse 3.14.100.translation: As all the herb-juices, their potencies deposited in honey, exist without division — such they know that number to be.
Verse 3.14.100.commentary: One of the loveliest images in the work, and it does real analytic work. Yatha ausadhi-rasah sarve madhuny ahita-saktayah: as *all* the *herb-juices*, their *potencies deposited* in *honey* — ahita-sakti: their powers *placed into* it, the bees having gathered from a hundred plants. Avibhagena vartante: they exist *without division* — avibhaga: nothing in the honey is this flower's and nothing that flower's, and yet every flower is in it and acting. Tam sankhyam tadrsim viduh: *such* they know that *number* to be. So the compound's abheda-ekatva (3.14.99) is honey: the members' numbers are wholly present, wholly potent, and wholly unrecoverable. The image is better than any argument could be, because it exhibits a case where fusion does not mean loss — the honey has all the herbs' powers and none of their boundaries. Which is what a compound is.

Verse 3.14.101.translation: Or, from the abandonment of the differences, that number-self is of such a kind; from the operation of the universal-portion, it operates by the exclusion of differences.
Verse 3.14.101.commentary: The alternative account of the same fact. Bhedanam va parityagat sankhyatma sa tathavidhah: *or*, from the *abandonment* of the *differences*, that *number-self* is of *such a kind* — not fusion (3.14.100's honey) but renunciation: the differences are simply dropped. Vyaparaj jati-bhagasya bhedapohena vartate: from the *operation* of the *universal-portion*, it operates by the *exclusion of differences* — the jati-element in the meaning does the work, and its way of working is apoha, exclusion. So two accounts stand side by side: the honey, where the differences are all present and indistinguishable, and the universal, where they are excluded. Bhartrhari gives both without choosing — which is 3.14.97's point about upayas being unsettled among persons, and 3.14.96's about multiplicity being a device either way.

Verse 3.14.102-103.translation: (102) As one possessing form is made known by form whose particular is not grasped, and the difference-form 'white' and the rest is not grasped — (103) so, when the entry of the difference-form is present but not intended for expression, some illumined portion is taken in the science as a subsidiary.
Verse 3.14.102-103.commentary: How a general can be present without its particulars. (102): agrhita-visesena yatha rupena rupavan prakhyayate — as one *possessing form* is made known by *form* whose *particular is not grasped*: one sees that a thing is coloured before, and without, seeing which colour. Na sukladi-bheda-rupas tu grhyate: the *difference-form* — 'white' and the rest — is *not grasped*. The perception is real, general, and particular-free. (103): bheda-rupa-samavese tatha saty avivaksite — *so*, when the *entry* of the *difference-form* is *present* but *not intended for expression* (avivaksita: the samuddesa's standing device — 3.11's numbers not meant to be said). Bhagah prakasitah kascic chastre 'ngatvena grhyate: some *illumined portion* is taken in the *science* as a *subsidiary*. So the compound's number is like coloured-ness: the differences are in there and unmeant, and what the grammar picks up is the lit portion.

Verse 3.14.104.translation: Then that portion is cognized in the form of number-in-general — the meaning having many powers, by words having fixed powers.
Verse 3.14.104.commentary: The conclusion of the number-analysis. Sankhya-samanya-rupena tada so 'msah pratiyate: *then* that *portion* is cognized in the form of *number-in-general* — sankhya-samanya: numberhood, not a number. So what a compound carries is the bare fact of quantity with no quantity specified — which is what 3.14.102's coloured-without-a-colour was the model for. And the reason: arthasyaneka-saktitve sabdair niyata-saktibhih — the *meaning* having *many powers*, by *words* having *fixed powers*. The meaning is inexhaustibly potent (3.14.100's honey with all the herbs in it), and words are each fixed to one power. So no word ever takes more than a slice, and the slice a compound takes at the number-place is number-in-general. This is 2.250's omni-significance seen from the other side: the object overflows every word, and each word is narrow by constitution.

Verse 3.14.105.translation: The property of the indeclinables, and the sequence of those possessing difference — the intermediate between these two deserves the designation 'non-different'.
Verse 3.14.105.commentary: The compound placed on a scale. Avyayanam ca yo dharmo: the *property* of the *indeclinables* — the avyaya, which has no number at all, admits no differentiation, and is one end of the scale. Yas ca bhedavatam kramah: and the *sequence* of those *possessing difference* — inflected words with their numbers laid out in order: the other end. Abhinna-vyapadesarham antaralam tad etayoh: the *intermediate* between these two *deserves the designation 'non-different'*. Antarala: the space between. So the compound is neither numberless like an indeclinable nor numbered like a noun; it occupies the middle, and abheda (3.14.99's abheda-ekatva) is the name that middle earns. Vyapadesarha — *deserving the designation* — is careful: the name is apt, and it is still a name.

Verse 3.14.106.translation: And the singular-treatment of the non-elided is not taught when that exists; and that is the property of 'gosucara' and the rest in another number.
Verse 3.14.106.commentary: A rule made redundant by 3.14.99's doctrine. Alukas ca ekavad-bhavas tasmin sati na sisyate: the *singular-treatment* of the *aluk* — compounds where the case-ending is *not* elided — is *not taught* when *that* (the abheda-ekatva) *exists*: if the compound already has non-different unity, no rule need impose the singular on it. Sa ca gosucaradinam dharmo 'sti vacanantare: and that is the *property* of *'gosucara'* (grazing among cows) and the rest, in *another number*. So the aluk-compounds, which visibly keep their endings, still get their singularity from the doctrine rather than from a rule — and the rule that supplies it is idle (3.14.41's prohibitions with no purpose; 3.14.59's rules with bounded domains). The doctrine of 3.14.99 is paying for itself in rule-economy.

Verse 3.14.107.translation: From the absence of the dual in the universal, that does not exist in derivative formations; but when the rule is rejected, 'gosucara' and the rest are in the substance.
Verse 3.14.107.commentary: Jatau dvivacana-abhavat: from the *absence of the dual* in the *universal* — a jati has no two: universals do not come in pairs (3.1's the universal is one, everywhere, always). Tad vrttisu na vidyate: *that* — the dual — *does not exist* in *derivative formations*, since the compound's meaning has gone over to the general (3.14.101's jati-bhaga; 3.14.104's number-in-general). Pratyakhyane tu yogasya dravye gosucaradayah: but in the *rejection* of the rule, *'gosucara'* and the rest are in the *substance* — if one throws out the rule, these compounds must denote substances instead, and substances do come in twos. So the choice is exact: keep the rule and the compound means a universal with no dual, or drop it and the compound means a substance that has one. The samuddesa lays out the fork and lets the reader see what the rule is buying.

Verse 3.14.108-109.translation: (108) From the substratum's possession of difference, there is concomitance with all differences; the view that substance is expressed also does not obtain in the naming of a universal. (109) And thus the reaching of all substances, and ekasesa, is not stated; if the sutra were rejected otherwise, there would be a reaching of different substances.
Verse 3.14.108-109.commentary: The fork of 3.14.107 worked out. (108): asrayad bhedavattayah sarva-bheda-samanvayah — from the *substratum's* possession of *difference*, there is *concomitance with all differences*: if the compound rests on a substance, every difference the substance has comes along. Dravyabhidhana-pakso 'pi jaty-akhyayam na vidyate: the view that *substance is expressed* does *not obtain* in the *naming of a universal* — one cannot have both. (109): sarva-dravya-gatis caivam ekasesas ca nocyate — *thus* the *reaching of all substances*, and *ekasesa* (3.14.30's residue-of-one), is *not stated*: the doctrine makes both rules unnecessary. Pratyakhyate 'nyatha sutre bhinna-dravya-gatir bhavet: if the *sutra* were *rejected otherwise*, there would be a *reaching of different substances* — the wrong result. So the accounting is complete: take the universal, and two rules become idle; take the substance, and the forms come out wrong.

Verse 3.14.110.translation: The 'treatment as though joined' which is taught in derivative formation, in 'varana' and the rest — in the number of non-different unity, in 'goda' it does not succeed there.
Verse 3.14.110.commentary: A difficulty for the doctrine, honestly filed. Vrttau yo yuktavad-bhavo varanadisu sisyate: the *yuktavad-bhava* — Panini's provision that a derivative be treated *as though* it were the analysed phrase, for gender and number — taught in *'varana'* and the rest. Abhedaikatva-sankhyayam godau tatra na sidhyati: in the *number of non-different unity* (3.14.99), in *'goda'* it *does not succeed*. So the rule requires the compound to behave as its phrase does, and the doctrine has just said the compound has a number its phrase never had. In 'goda' the two prescriptions collide and the rule fails. Bhartrhari raises the objection against his own account and states it flatly — which is the samuddesa's manner throughout (3.14.34's the Bhasya found it hard). The following verses will have to answer it.

Verse 3.14.111.translation: Before the derivative formation, under 'treatment as though joined', the genitive would rest on difference; in the formation, from the abandonment of the number-particulars, difference withdraws.
Verse 3.14.111.commentary: The answer to 3.14.110. Prag vrtter yuktavad-bhave sasthi bhedasraya bhavet: *before* the derivative formation, under *yuktavad-bhava*, the *genitive* would *rest on difference* — the rule looks back to the analysed phrase, where the genitive really did relate two differentiated things. Vrttau sankhya-visesanam tyagad bhedo nivartate: *in* the formation, from the *abandonment* of the *number-particulars*, *difference* *withdraws*. So the two prescriptions do not collide after all: they apply at different moments. Yuktavad-bhava reaches back to a stage where difference obtained; abheda-ekatva describes the stage after. The objection assumed a single time, and there are two — which is 3.14.61's antarangatva argument (what was formed first leaves its mark) in a semantic key.

Verse 3.14.112.translation: While the numbers exist, some know another number — called 'non-difference', comprehending; and that arises in the derivative formation.
Verse 3.14.112.commentary: The doctrine restated with its authorities. Vidyamanasu sankhyasu: *while the numbers exist* — the members' numbers are not annihilated; they are there. Kecit sankhyantaram viduh: *some* know *another number* — a further one, over and above them. Abhedakhyam upagrahi: called *'non-difference'*, *comprehending* — upagrahin: taking-in, including; the new number does not replace the old ones but *contains* them. Vrttau tac ca upajayate: and *that* *arises* in the *derivative formation*. So the honey-image of 3.14.100 is now precise: the herb-juices are still there (vidyamanasu), and the honey is a further thing that has taken them in (upagrahi). Nothing is destroyed by composition; something is added that comprehends what it was made of.

Verse 3.14.113.translation: That goes into operation somewhere through its own members, called 'difference'; somewhere its self, independent of the differences, attains causality.
Verse 3.14.113.commentary: Why the compound behaves two ways. Vyaparam yati bhedakhyais tat svair avayavaih kvacit: *that* goes into *operation* *somewhere* through its *own members*, called *'difference'* — in some rules the compound acts through its parts, and the parts are live. Atma bhedanapekso 'sya kvacid eti nimittatam: *somewhere* its *self*, *independent of the differences*, attains *causality* — nimittata: the state of being the occasioning cause. So the compound has two modes: through-the-parts and as-a-whole, and which one operates depends on the rule. This is the formal statement of what 3.14.111 discovered — the same compound answers to yuktavad-bhava through its members and to the number-rules through its self — and it saves the doctrine without softening it. The whole is one (3.14.96); it is also, when a rule asks, addressable through parts it does not have.

Verse 3.14.114.translation: 'The slave-woman's husband' is manifest, and 'godau' is seen; the difference in the number's operation is therefore settled in this way.
Verse 3.14.114.commentary: The two modes of 3.14.113 exhibited. Dasyah patir iti vyaktah: *'dasyah patih'* — the slave-woman's husband, the tradition's standing case of a phrase that resists compounding — is *manifest*: here the parts are live and the genitive stands. Godav iti ca drsyate: and *'godau'* — the dual, the very form 3.14.110 said would not come out — *is seen*: it is there in usage, whatever the theory says. Vyapara-bhedah sankhyayah tasmad evam vyavasthitah: the *difference in the number's operation* is *therefore* *settled* in this way. So the doctrine is not being defended a priori: two forms are pointed at, one behaving through parts and one as a whole, and the two-mode account is read off them. Drsyate — *is seen* — is the argument. Usage settles it (3.14.43's loka-vyavastha).

Verse 3.14.115.translation: And of 'dvi' and the rest, in 'dviputra' and the like, the external difference, expressible by the case-ending, withdraws, since it is the word's own meaning; but the cause remains.
Verse 3.14.115.commentary: Numerals inside compounds. Dvyadinam ca dviputradau bahyo bhedo nivartate: of *'dvi'* and the rest, in *'dviputra'* (two-sonned) and the like, the *external* difference *withdraws* — bahya: outer, the duality that would have been carried by an ending. Vibhakti-vacyah svarthatvat: *expressible by the case-ending*, since it is the word's *own meaning* — 'dvi' *means* two, so it does not also need an ending to say two: what the stem says, the ending need not (3.11's the numeral and the number-ending saying the same thing twice). Nimittam tv avatisthate: but the *cause* *remains* — the duality is still the occasion of the form, even with its outer expression gone. So the number is not lost; it is relocated from the ending into the stem's own sense, and it goes on causing (3.14.113's nimittata).

Verse 3.14.116.translation: In an aggregate where duality is subordinate, the word 'dvi' operates there, from the connection 'this is that'; in the word 'ubha' it is not so.
Verse 3.14.116.commentary: Dvitva-upasarjane sanghe dvi-sabdas tatra vartate: in an *aggregate* where *duality* is *subordinate*, the word *'dvi'* *operates* there — where twoness is the subordinate element (3.14.95's upasarjana), 'dvi' can stand. So 'yam ity abhisambandhat: from the *connection* 'this is that' — the identification that lets a numeral name a group. Ubha-sabde na tat tatha: in the word *'ubha'* (both) it is *not so*. So 'ubha' will not do what 'dvi' does: it cannot be subordinated, cannot enter the identification. The difference is that 'dvi' counts and 'ubha' *totalizes* — it says not two but *the* two, both of them, and a totality has nothing above it to be subordinate to. The following verses work out what follows from this.

Verse 3.14.117.translation: 'Ubhaya', equal in meaning to it, is always employed in the derivative formation; in the sutra too, the taking-up of 'nitya' is stated for that purpose.
Verse 3.14.117.commentary: Ubhayas tasya tulyartho vrttau nityam prayujyate: *'ubhaya'* — *equal in meaning* to 'ubha' — is *always* employed in the *derivative formation*: where a compound is wanted, 'ubhaya' goes in and 'ubha' does not. Sutre 'pi nitya-grahanam tad-artham abhidhiyate: in the *sutra* too, the taking-up of *'nitya'* is stated *for that purpose*. So Panini's word 'nitya' in the relevant rule exists precisely to force this substitution — and here, unlike 3.14.40's redundant nitya, the word earns its place. The pair is instructive: the same technical device is idle in one rule and load-bearing in another, and only inspection tells which. Bhartrhari has no general verdict on the grammar's economy; he checks case by case.

Verse 3.14.118.translation: And in 'ap' and 'ka', from their serving another's purpose, non-difference does not arise; therefore 'ubhe' — the difference-usage is employed, in its own meaning.
Verse 3.14.118.commentary: Api ke ca aparartatvat na abheda upajayate: in *'ap'* and *'ka'* — the affixes — from their *serving another's purpose*, *non-difference* does *not arise*: these affixes exist for someone else's sake and so cannot bring about the fusion of 3.14.99. Ubhe iti tatah svarthe bheda-vrttih prayujyate: *therefore* *'ubhe'* — the *difference-usage* is employed, *in its own meaning*. So where the affix cannot fuse, the form keeps its difference and stands in its own sense: 'ubhe', the dual, with its twoness intact. This confirms 3.14.116 — 'ubha' resists subordination and therefore resists fusion, and its dual survives everywhere.
Verse 3.14.118.contested: Pada a is compressed and its reading is not certain. We take 'api ke ca' as naming the affixes ap (tap) and ka, whose 'serving another's purpose' (apararthatva) then explains why abheda fails. The line can also be construed with 'api' as the particle and 'ke' otherwise resolved; the verse's conclusion — that 'ubhe' keeps its difference — is unaffected.

Verse 3.14.119.translation: Even on the view that femininity is expressed, there is a reversal of subordination; from its own nature, from its serving another's purpose, difference is not lost there.
Verse 3.14.119.commentary: The same result on the rival account. Stritva-abhidhana-pakse 'pi: even on the view that *femininity is expressed* — that the feminine affix says femininity rather than merely revealing it (3.13's vacaka/dyotaka quarrel about gender). Guna-bhava-viparyayah: there is a *reversal of subordination* — what was subordinate becomes principal and vice versa. Svabhavad apararthatvat tatra bhedo na hiyate: from its *own nature*, from its *serving another's purpose*, *difference* is *not lost* there. So whichever way one decides the gender-question, 'ubhe' keeps its difference — the affix serves another and cannot fuse. Bhartrhari's habit again: show that the result survives the disputed premise, so the dispute need not be settled to get on (3.14.98's vacika va dyotika vapi, left open for the same reason).

Verse 3.14.120.translation: Therefore, from the dual and from 'tap', 'ubhaya' is seen elsewhere; leaving aside the affix 'tayap', it does not exist again in the second member.
Verse 3.14.120.commentary: Tasmad dvivacanat tapas ca ubhayo 'nyatra drsyate: *therefore*, from the *dual* and from *'tap'*, *'ubhaya'* is *seen elsewhere* — the form turns up outside the compound-context, where the dual and the feminine affix operate normally. Pratyayam tayapam hitva na asty uttarapade punah: *leaving aside* the affix *'tayap'*, it does *not exist* *again* in the *second member*. So 'ubhaya' is derived by tayap and, once derived, cannot recur as the latter member of a compound. The restriction is exactly what 3.14.116-119 predicted: a totalizer resists every subordinate position, and the second member of a compound is a subordinate position. The rule falls out of the semantics rather than being stipulated — which is the pattern the whole samuddesa has been demonstrating.

Verse 3.14.121.translation: The obtaining of the term 'pragrhya' would not arise from the affix-indication; for in 'kumaryagara' there is no compound in another number.
Verse 3.14.121.commentary: Praptih pragrhya-samjnaya na syat pratyaya-laksanat: the *obtaining* of the term *'pragrhya'* — the designation for a final vowel exempt from sandhi, which duals characteristically get — would *not arise* from the *affix-indication*: pratyaya-laksana, the principle that a rule conditioned by an affix still applies once the affix is elided. Kumaryagare na hy asti samaso vacanantare: for in *'kumaryagara'* (the girl's chamber) there is *no compound* in *another number*. So the pragrhya-designation cannot be got at through an elided dual, because the compound in question is not in that number at all: there is no dual there to be exempted, elided or otherwise. This is 3.14.98's number-difference withdrawing, cashed out in a sandhi-rule — the compound's number is not the members' number, and the rules that reach for the members' number find nothing.

Verse 3.14.122.translation: For one and two, the optional elision of 'yañ' and the rest is not conceivable; 'yausmaka' and 'tavaka' do not succeed, from the absence of difference.
Verse 3.14.122.commentary: Eka-dvayor yanadinam vibhasa lun na kalpate: for *one* and *two*, the *optional elision* of *'yan'* and the rest is *not conceivable* — the patronymic affix's optional luk requires a plurality to operate on, and one and two do not supply it. Yausmakas tavakas ceti bhedabhavan na sidhyati: *'yausmaka'* and *'tavaka'* — the derivatives from 'yusmad' and 'tvad' — do *not succeed*, from the *absence of difference*. So two rule-failures from the same cause: where the number has fused (3.14.99's abheda-ekatva), the rules that need a difference to work on have nothing. Bhartrhari is cataloguing the doctrine's costs as carefully as its benefits (3.14.106's benefit, this verse's cost), and he does not net them out.

Verse 3.14.123.translation: Difference is seen in 'gargyatara', and likewise in 'gargatarah'; 'yusmat-pita' and 'tvat-pita' — thus the two substitutes are settled.
Verse 3.14.123.commentary: The counter-evidence, admitted. Drsto gargyatare bhedah: *difference* is *seen* in *'gargyatara'* — the comparative, which requires the difference 3.14.122 said was absent. Tatha gargatara iti: and *likewise* in *'gargatarah'*. Yusmat-pita tvat-piteti tatha adesau vyavasthitau: *'yusmat-pita'* and *'tvat-pita'* — thus the *two substitutes* are *settled*: the plural-based and singular-based substitutes both occur, each in its own domain. Drstah is again the operative word (3.14.114's drsyate): the forms exist and the theory must accommodate them. So difference does survive in some derivatives, and 3.14.124 must say how — which it does, by relocating the number into the base.

Verse 3.14.124.translation: That number which, being an adjunct, is settled in the base, is manifested by substitutes, or by a technical term, even without a case-ending.
Verse 3.14.124.commentary: The resolution of 3.14.122-123. Upadhi-bhuta ya sankhya prakrtau samavasthita: that *number* which, being an *upadhi* — an adjunct, a qualifying condition rather than a constituent — is *settled in the base*. Adesaih samjnaya vapi vibhaktya vyajyate vina: is *manifested* by *substitutes*, or by a *technical term*, *even without a case-ending*. Vyajyate: is *manifested*, revealed — not said (3.14.98's dyotika, and the word is chosen). So the number that 3.14.98 said withdraws from the compound has not gone anywhere: it has become an adjunct of the base, where no ending expresses it and the substitutes betray it. This is why 3.14.123's forms come out: 'tvat-pita' shows its singular through its substitute, having no ending to show it with. Number in a compound is not spoken; it is given away.

Verse 3.14.125.translation: In 'saurpika' and in 'masa-jata', the measure, by its own nature, resting on the number which is an adjunct, operates by way of difference.
Verse 3.14.125.commentary: The upadhi-number of 3.14.124 at work. Saurpike masa-jate ca parimanam svabhavatah: in *'saurpika'* (measured by a surpa) and *'masa-jata'* (a month old), the *measure*, by its *own nature*. Upadhi-bhutam asritya sankhyam bhedena vartate: *resting on* the *number* which is an *adjunct*, operates *by way of difference*. So measure-words carry their difference through the adjunct-number in the base, not through any ending: 'masa-jata' says one month and no ending says it. Svabhavatah is doing work — the measure is *by its own nature* quantitative, so it needs no help to reach the number that is sitting in its base. This finishes the account 3.14.84-86 began, and the two now agree: measure is certified by machinery and carried by the base.

Verse 3.14.126.translation: In 'vayasvin' and in 'krita', the delimitation is not understood; the sense intended, apart from difference — there the measure is purposeless.
Verse 3.14.126.commentary: The contrast case. Vayasvini paricchedah krite capi na gamyate: in *'vayasvin'* (aged, possessing age) and in *'krita'* (bought), the *delimitation* is *not understood* — pariccheda: the cutting-off, the definite quantity. 'Vayasvin' says one has age, not how much; 'krita' says bought, not for how much. Isto bhedad rte tatra parimanam anarthakam: the sense *intended*, *apart from difference* — there the *measure* is *purposeless*. Anarthaka: without purpose. So these words carry no adjunct-number at all (3.14.124), and reaching for one gets nothing. The pair with 3.14.125 is the point: some derivatives have quantity in their base and some have only quality, and no rule can tell which — one has to know the word. Which is 3.14.43 once more: the world's settled arrangement, and the science following after.

Verse 3.14.127.translation: From the expression of non-difference of what is different, the rule of 'sas' after 'prastha' and the rest; but from non-difference, because that is its property, it is not seen after 'ghata' and the rest.
Verse 3.14.127.commentary: Bhinnasya abheda-vacanat prasthadibhyah saso vidhih: from the *expression of non-difference* of what is *different*, the rule of *'sas'* after *'prastha'* and the rest — the distributive affix, giving 'prastha-sah', prastha by prastha: many prasthas said as one distributive sweep. Tad-dharmatvad abhedat tu ghatadibhyo na drsyate: but from *non-difference*, *because that is its property*, it is *not seen* after *'ghata'* and the rest. So the distributive attaches to measure-words and not to pots: a measure is intrinsically a repeatable unit — abheda is *its property* — and a pot is not. This is 3.14.125-126's finding again (quantity is in some bases and not others), now settling which stems take an affix. The grammar's gana is not arbitrary; it tracks which words have a unit built into them.

Verse 3.14.128.translation: Where the number-ending is heard, there the state is specified; that ending which withdraws — of that, there is no being.
Verse 3.14.128.commentary: A methodological principle, stated with force. Sruyate vacanam yatra bhavas tatra visisyate: *where* the *number-ending* is *heard*, *there* the *state* is *specified* — an audible ending does specifying work. Nivartate yad vacanam tasya bhavo na vidyate: that ending which *withdraws* — of *that*, there is *no being*. Bhavo na vidyate: it has no existence at all. So Bhartrhari refuses the halfway house: an elided ending is not a quiet ending, it is *not there*. This is stronger than 3.14.87's asadhutva-nivrtty-artham (where the elided matup was allowed to exist in order to certify) and stands in tension with it — the samuddesa is exploring both, and the tension is the subject-matter (3.14.113's two modes). What is not heard does not specify.

Verse 3.14.129.translation: The operation rests on existence; from the science, non-application is non-appearance. What is seen utterly in the sentence — of that there is absence in derivative formations.
Verse 3.14.129.commentary: The consequence of 3.14.128. Karyam satta-asrayam: the *operation* rests on *existence* — a rule cannot act on what is not there. Sastrad apravrttir adarsanam: from the *science*, *non-application* is *non-appearance* — the rule's failing to apply *is* the thing's not showing up; there is no further fact. Vakye drstam yad atyantam abhavas tasya vrttisu: what is *seen utterly* in the *sentence* — of that there is *absence* in *derivative formations*. So the sentence's visible articulation is *absent* — not hidden, not suppressed — in the compound. This is the samuddesa's core claim (3.14.49's atyanta-bheda) stated in the vocabulary of rule-application: nothing withdrew into the compound, because nothing was there to withdraw.

Verse 3.14.130.translation: For the sake of differentiating the domain of the technical term, the non-appearance of what was applicable is taught; but the ending which is heard is apprehended as specified.
Verse 3.14.130.commentary: Samjna-visaya-bhedartham prasakta-adarsanam smrtam: for the sake of *differentiating the domain* of the *technical term*, the *non-appearance* of what was *applicable* is *taught* — prasakta: what would have applied; adarsana: its not showing. The grammar deliberately teaches that an applicable thing fails to appear, and the teaching's purpose is to carve the term's domain. So the elision is a demarcation-device, not a report (3.14.42's rules as nidarsana). Sruyamanam tu vacanam visistam upalabhyate: but the ending which *is heard* is apprehended as *specified*. The audible one does real work; the elided one draws a boundary. Both are the grammar's; only one is the word's.
Verse 3.14.130.contested: Pada c is transmitted in our source as 'ksuyamanam', which yields no sense here; we read 'sruyamanam' ('being heard'), which the contrast with 3.14.128's 'sruyate' and with pada d's 'upalabhyate' requires. We note the source reading rather than silently normalising it.

Verse 3.14.131.translation: Where absence, or elision, or a substitute possessing form is prescribed — there, from the deviation of the cause, incorrectness follows.
Verse 3.14.131.commentary: The cost of the whole apparatus. Abhavo va luko yatra rupavan va vidhiyate: where *absence*, or *luk*, or a *substitute possessing form* is *prescribed* — the grammar's three ways of handling what is not audibly there. Vyabhicaran nimittasya tatra asadhuh prasajyate: *there*, from the *deviation* of the *cause*, *incorrectness* *follows*. Vyabhicara: straying, failure of invariable concomitance — the occasioning cause does not hold steady across the cases. So wherever the grammar posits an absence, the conditioning breaks down somewhere and a bad form is generated. This is 3.14.76's vyutpattir anavasthita made concrete: the derivational machinery leaks, and it leaks precisely at the points where it posits what is not there (3.14.74's imagined parts).

Verse 3.14.132.translation: Difference, number, or particular has been expounded for the derivative formation and the sentence; but the particular need not everywhere be of that kind.
Verse 3.14.132.commentary: A caution against over-generalizing what has been established. Bhedah samkhya viseso va vyakhyato vrtti-vakyayoh: *difference*, *number*, or *particular* has been *expounded* for the *derivative formation* and the *sentence* — the samuddesa's results so far. Sarvatra eva visesas tu na avasyam tadrso bhavet: but the *particular* need *not everywhere* be *of that kind*. Na avasyam: not necessarily. So the analysis was of certain cases and does not automatically extend. Bhartrhari is guarding his own conclusions against the very error he charged others with (3.14.76's taking the upayas for true): a result reached on some forms is an upaya for those forms, and carrying it everywhere would make it a doctrine. The samuddesa polices itself by the standard it set.

Verse 3.14.133.translation: And from the universal's being the cause of difference, the principal is not qualified by gender in 'mrga-dugdha' and the rest; in 'gargi-putra' that sequence does not hold.
Verse 3.14.133.commentary: Jates ca bheda-hetutvat: from the *universal's* being the *cause of difference* — the jati is what differentiates, so it does the qualifying. Na lingena visesyate pradhanam mrga-dugdhadau: the *principal* is *not* qualified by *gender* in *'mrga-dugdha'* (deer's milk) and the rest — the milk is not made feminine by the doe: gender does not reach across into the principal. Gargi-putre na sa kramah: in *'gargi-putra'* (Gargi's son) that *sequence* does *not hold* — here the feminine of the first member is doing something, and the sequence reverses. So two compounds, alike in form, differ in whether gender penetrates. This is 3.14.132's caution being exercised at once: the particular is not everywhere of the same kind, and here is a pair to prove it.

Verse 3.14.134.translation: In non-difference, from connection with gender and number, 'suklam patah' being applicable — the science is undertaken for the establishing of gender and number.
Verse 3.14.134.commentary: Why the grammar has gender-and-number rules at all. Abhede linga-samkhyabhyam yogat: in *non-difference*, from *connection* with *gender and number*. Suklam pata iti prasakte: *'suklam patah'* — the ungrammatical mixture, a neuter singular adjective with a masculine plural noun — being *applicable*: without a rule, that is what would come out, since abheda leaves the qualifier with no reason to agree. Sastram arabdham siddhaye linga-samkhyayoh: the *science* is *undertaken* for the *establishing* of *gender and number*. So agreement is not a fact of the meaning — the meaning is undivided and has no agreement in it (3.14.92's anusyuta) — and the grammar must impose it. This is a striking admission: concord is the sastra's contribution, not the world's.

Verse 3.14.135.translation: That quality which, for another's sake, attains the state of remainder in derivative formations — that is designated in the sutra by way of being a qualifier.
Verse 3.14.135.commentary: Pararthaam sesa-bhavam yo vrttisu pratipadyate: that *quality* which, *for another's sake*, attains the *state of remainder* in *derivative formations* — sesa: what is left over, the residue after the principal has taken what it needs. Guno visesanatvena sa sutre vyapadisyate: that is *designated* in the *sutra* by way of being a *qualifier*. Vyapadisyate: is *designated* — the samuddesa's careful verb for a name that is apt without being a description (3.14.105's vyapadesarha). So Panini's 'visesana' names whatever is left over serving another's purpose, and the naming is a convenience. The qualifier is not a thing in the meaning; it is the grammar's label for a residue — which is why 3.14.134's concord had to be imposed on it.

Verse 3.14.136.translation: Although particulars are heard in sentences, from their being other words — from the derivative formation's non-different form, in them there is no derivative formation.
Verse 3.14.136.commentary: Sabdantaratvad vakyesu visesa yady api srutah: although *particulars* are *heard* in *sentences*, from their being *other words* — the phrase has audible particulars, because the phrase is a different word (3.14.49's atyanta-bheda; 3.14.64). Vrtter abhinna-rupatvat tesu vrttir na vidyate: from the *derivative formation's* *non-different form*, in *them* there is *no* derivative formation. So one cannot find the compound inside the phrase: the phrase's particulars are not the compound's members, and there is no compounding going on in a phrase at all. This closes the door 3.14.52's rival wanted open. The compound is not a phrase that has undergone anything; the two are different words, and the particulars one hears belong to one of them only.

Verse 3.14.137-138.translation: (137) And since the word's formation, from its form, has the general for its domain — therefore gender and number resting on that follow. (138) Then the word, with gender and with number, is connected with what expresses the substance; there, the hearing of those two would be separate.
Verse 3.14.137-138.commentary: How gender and number get into a word at all. (137): rupac ca sabda-samskarah samanya-visayo yatah — the *word's formation*, from its *form*, has the *general* for its *domain*: the grammar operates on shapes, and shapes are general (3.14.78's science made of notation). Tasmat tad-asrayam lingam vacanam ca prasajyate: *therefore* *gender* and *number* resting on *that* *follow* — they attach to the general form. (138): salingam ca sasamkhyam ca tato dravyabhidhayina sambadhyate padam — *then* the *word*, with gender and number already on it, is *connected* with what *expresses the substance*. Tatra tayor bhinna srutir bhavet: *there*, the *hearing* of those two would be *separate*. So gender and number arrive with the form, before the substance is in view, and one would then hear them twice — which is the difficulty 3.14.139 answers.

Verse 3.14.139.translation: The gender and number of the qualities, which come from the statement of the future, external substratum — those are taught by the sutra.
Verse 3.14.139.commentary: The answer. Bhavino bahirangasya vacanad asrayasya ye lingasankhye gunanam: the *gender and number* of the *qualities*, which come from the *statement* of the *substratum* — and the substratum is *bhavin*, *future* (not yet in view when the form was made), and *bahiranga*, *external* (settled later: 3.7.182's antaranga/bahiranga). Te sutrena pratipadite: *those* are *taught* by the *sutra*. So the double hearing of 3.14.138 does not arise: the qualifier's gender and number are not its own at all — they are borrowed from a substratum that comes afterwards and from outside, and the borrowing is what the rule prescribes. This is 3.14.134's admission completed: concord is imposed, and it is imposed by making the qualifier answer to something not yet there.

Verse 3.14.140.translation: And even though it operates in the particular, since the particular is unmarked, from the non-difference of form — therefore here the difference-operation is not conceivable.
Verse 3.14.140.commentary: Visesa-vrtter api ca rupabhedad alaksitah yasmad visesah: even though it *operates in the particular* (3.14.37's visesa-vrttitva), the *particular* is *unmarked* — alaksita: uncharacterized, bearing no sign — from the *non-difference of form*: the form does not show which particular it has got. Tena atra bheda-karyam na kalpate: *therefore* here the *difference-operation* is *not conceivable*. So a rule that needs a difference to operate on finds none, because the difference — though present — has left no mark on the shape, and the grammar reads shapes (3.14.137's samanya-visaya). This is the deepest version of the samuddesa's recurring finding: the science's blindness to particulars is not an oversight but a consequence of its being a science of forms.

Verse 3.14.141.translation: The general is the particular itself, since it is differentiated from the particular; for the non-difference of the particulars, when resorted to, is exclusive.
Verse 3.14.141.commentary: One of the boldest lines in the kanda. Visesa eva samanyam: the *general* *is* the *particular itself* — eva: nothing else. Visesad bhidyate yatah: *since* it is *differentiated from* the particular — and here is the paradox stated on purpose: the general is the particular, and it is what the particular is marked off from. The general is not a further thing; it is the particular seen as excluding others. Abhedo hi visesanam asrito vinivartakah: for the *non-difference* of the *particulars*, when *resorted to*, is *exclusive* — vinivartaka: what turns others away. So sameness *is* exclusion: to take several particulars as non-different just is to turn away everything else. There is no positive general over and above the turning-away. This is very close to what the Buddhists would build apoha out of, arrived at from the other side — Bhartrhari's samanya is real, and its reality consists in excluding.

Verse 3.14.142.translation: Whatever is resorted to, that excludes something else; but the division into difference and non-difference is not determined in the general.
Verse 3.14.142.commentary: The principle generalized, and then withheld. Yad yad asriyate tat tad anyasya vinivartakam: *whatever* is *resorted to*, *that* *excludes* something *else* — every resort is an exclusion, without exception. To take up anything at all is to turn away what it is not (3.14.101's bhedapohena vartate; 2.129's the mind's fixing on one thing being its exclusion of the rest). Bhedabheda-vibhagas tu samanye na nirupyate: *but* the *division* into *difference and non-difference* is *not determined* in the *general*. Na nirupyate: is not ascertained, cannot be settled. So the question 'is the general the same as its particulars or different?' has no answer — not because we lack information, but because the general is constituted by exclusion and exclusion is not one of the two. Bhartrhari states his own doctrine and then declines to metaphysicalize it: the general excludes, and asking whether it is one with what it excludes is asking the wrong thing.

Verse 3.14.143.translation: And the general is an extraction — so it stands only in the condition of a cause; therefore it is grasped by its own property.
Verse 3.14.143.commentary: The general's status settled by naming its origin. Apoddharas ca samanyam: the *general* is an *apoddhara* — an *extraction*, the work's technical term for what the analyst lifts out of an undivided whole (2.10's words extracted from sentences as suffixes from words; 3.14.75's three storeys of extraction). So the samanya is not found; it is pulled out. Nimittavastham eva atah: *therefore* it stands *only in the condition of a cause* — nimitta-avastha: it exists in the state of being an occasioning condition, and in no other state. Tat sva-dharmena grhyate: it is *grasped by its own property*. So the general is real exactly as far as it causes, and no further — which is 3.14.142's refusal to say whether it is one with its particulars, now given its ground. An extraction that occasions has nothing left over to be same or different.
