Visesasya aprayoge tu linga-sankhye na sidhyatah: when the *particular* is *not employed*, gender and number do *not succeed* — 3.14.310's solution requires the substratum to be *prayukta*, actually said; where it is not, nothing supplies concord. Avarsadisu dosas ca hemanto 'nyasrayo yatah: and there is a *fault* in *'avarsah'* and the rest, since *'hemanta'* has *another substratum*. So 3.14.304's difficulty returns and is not solved: 'avarsah' should give 'hemanta', and hemanta rests elsewhere. Dosah: a *fault* — Bhartrhari names it in his own account and does not repair it. That is the samuddesa's manner, and it has been consistent since 3.14.34.

Verse 3.14.316.translation: When the form is cognized as what all words express — then, from its oneness, singular-wordhood is described as proper in it.
Verse 3.14.316.commentary: A last position, and it is the akrti-vadin's. Akrtih sarva-sabdanam yada vacya pratiyate: when the *akrti* — the form, the shape, the configuration — is cognized as what *all words* *express*: the doctrine that every word means a form (the Vajapyayana position, against Vyadi's dravya; 3.1's opening quarrel). Ekatvad eka-sabdatvam nyayyam tasyam ca varnyate: from its *oneness*, *singular-wordhood* is described as *proper* in it. So if words mean forms, and a form is one, every word should be singular. Nyayyam: proper, following the reasoning. The consequence is stated neutrally, and 3.14.317-318 draw out what it costs.

Verse 3.14.317.translation: In it there would be pervaded-gender, as with the aggregate of domestic animals; and even where the substances differ, from oneness the singular would obtain there.
Verse 3.14.317.commentary: Avista-lingata tasyam syad gramya-pasu-sanghavat: in it there would be *pervaded-gender* — avista-linga: gender entered into it from outside (3.14.150's avesa) — as with the *aggregate of domestic animals*: 'gramya-pasu', a collective that takes one gender though it covers cows, goats and sheep. Dravya-bhede 'pi ca ekatvat tatra eka-vacanam bhavet: and *even where the substances differ*, from *oneness* the *singular* would obtain. So on the akrti-account every word behaves like a collective noun: one form, one gender, one number, whatever the multiplicity underneath. That is a coherent position and it is very nearly 3.14.99's abheda-ekatva — which is why Bhartrhari can state it without hostility.

Verse 3.14.318.translation: For it is joined only with the fixed genders of the substrata; and thus, in 'treatment as though joined', the prohibition is purposeless.
Verse 3.14.318.commentary: The consequence, and it is a rule-economy. Asrayanam hi lingaih sa niyatair eva yujyate: for *it* — the akrti — is joined *only* with the *fixed genders* of the *substrata*: the form has no gender of its own and takes the seats', which are settled (3.14.310's yati tal-linga-sankhyatam, on the rival account). Tatha ca yuktavad-bhave pratisedho nirarthakah: and *thus*, in *yuktavad-bhava*, the *prohibition* is *purposeless* — nirarthaka. So the akrti-doctrine makes one of Panini's prohibitions idle: if concord always comes from the fixed seats, no rule need forbid it coming from elsewhere. This is 3.14.106's pattern (a doctrine paying for itself in rule-economy) offered to the opponent's account — Bhartrhari finds the rival's advantage for it, and does not pretend the finding is his.

Verse 3.14.319.translation: If the worldly gender were taken up, pervaded-gender everywhere would follow, from contradiction; and that worldly gender is not resorted to.
Verse 3.14.319.commentary: Sarvatra avista-lingatvam loka-linga-parigrahe: *pervaded-gender everywhere*, if the *worldly gender* is *taken up* — loka-linga: gender as the world knows it, sexual difference. Virodhitvat prasajyeta: would *follow*, from *contradiction*. Na asritam tac ca laukikam: and that *worldly* gender is *not resorted to*. So grammatical gender is not the world's gender, and the science does not use the world's. This is the premise the whole gender-analysis has been resting on and here it is stated: the linga of the sastra is a technical category, and looking for sex in it is a category error (3.14.137's the word's formation has the general for its domain). 3.14.320 proves it in one line.

Verse 3.14.320.translation: In 'samanya', 'akrti', 'bhava', 'jati' — here the worldly gender is not possible at all; therefore another is taken up.
Verse 3.14.320.commentary: The proof, and it is beautiful because it is drawn from the grammarians' own vocabulary. Samanyam akrtir bhavo jatir ity atra: in *'samanya'* (neuter), *'akrti'* (feminine), *'bhava'* (masculine), *'jati'* (feminine) — four words for one thing, the universal, in three genders. Laukikam lingam na sambhavaty eva: here the *worldly* gender is *not possible at all* — na sambhavaty eva: not even possible; a universal has no sex, and yet these four have three genders among them. Tena anyat parigrhyate: *therefore* *another* is *taken up*. So gender cannot come from the meaning, because four synonyms differ in it. The argument is one verse long and it settles the matter — and the examples are the terms of the debate itself.

Verse 3.14.321.translation: 'Pravrtti' — the general characteristic of it is stated; then it is divided as manifestation, disappearance, and persistence.
Verse 3.14.321.commentary: The other gender, and it is not what one expects. Pravrttir iti samanyam laksanam tasya kathyate: *'pravrtti'* — the *general characteristic* of *it* is *stated*: what the akrti is, generally, is *operation* — pravrtti: going forth, activity, the thing's being under way. Avirbhavas tirobhavah sthitis ca ity atha bhidyate: *then* it is *divided* as *manifestation*, *disappearance*, and *persistence*. So the universal is not a static form but a threefold activity: coming-into-view, going-out-of-view, and staying. This is the sat-bhava-vikara tradition (the six modifications) compressed to three, and it is the samuddesa's most metaphysical moment: what a word means is a rhythm.

Verse 3.14.322.translation: All things possess operation, and are never disjoined from the three operations; and here is the possibility of speech.
Verse 3.14.322.commentary: Pravrttimantah sarve 'rthah: *all* things *possess operation* — nothing is inert; everything is under way. Tisrbhis ca pravrttibhih satatam na viyujyante: they are *never* *disjoined* from the *three* operations — satatam: constantly, at every moment: everything is at all times manifesting, vanishing, and persisting at once. Vacas caiva atra sambhavah: and *here* is the *possibility of speech*. So language is possible *because* of this: a word can catch a thing only where the thing shows a manifestation to be caught, and the ceaseless three-beat rhythm is what offers words their purchase. Atra sambhavah — *here* is where speech can happen. Without the pravrttis there would be nothing for a word to be about.

Verse 3.14.323.translation: And that thing whose property is non-operation, which is grasped in the form of consciousness — it follows, as it were, the operations of others, which rest everywhere.
Verse 3.14.323.commentary: The exception to 3.14.322, and it is the self. Yas ca apravrtti-dharmarthah: that *thing* whose *property* is *non-operation* — apravrtti: it does not go forth; it does not manifest, vanish or persist, because those are changes and it does not change. Citi-rupena grhyate: which is *grasped in the form of consciousness* — citi: awareness (1.1's the changeless Word-Brahman; 1.4's the one that appears as many). Anuyati iva so 'nyesam pravrttir visvag-asrayah: it *follows*, *as it were*, the *operations of others*, which *rest everywhere*. Iva: as though. So consciousness does not move and appears to move with everything that does — as the moon appears to run through the clouds. Visvag-asrayah: resting on all sides. The changeless is the only thing outside the rhythm, and it looks like the busiest thing there is.

Verse 3.14.324.translation: Therefore its consciousness-form and its consciousness-time are differentiated; but of it there is no difference of its own form at all.
Verse 3.14.324.commentary: Tena asya citi-rupam ca citi-kalas ca bhidyate: *therefore* its *consciousness-form* and its *consciousness-time* are *differentiated* — the awareness appears as this awareness and that, at this moment and that: it acquires shape and date from what it seems to follow (3.14.323's anuyati iva). Tasya sva-rupa-bhedas tu na kascid api vidyate: *but* of it there is *no* difference of *its own form* *at all* — na kascid api: not the least. So all the differentiation is in the apparent following and none in the thing: consciousness is one and is dated and shaped by borrowing. This is 1.4's ekam eva yad amnatam bhinnam saktivyapasrayat, and the samuddesa has arrived at it from the grammar of gender.

Verse 3.14.325.translation: Consciousness is seen as though transferred into the unconscious, by the property of reflection; and that is the basis of the word.
Verse 3.14.325.commentary: One of the great images of the work. Acetanesu samkrantam caitanyam iva drsyate: *consciousness* is *seen* *as though* *transferred* into the *unconscious* — samkranta: gone across, migrated; iva: as though. Pratibimbaka-dharmena: by the *property of reflection* — as a face is in a mirror without going there. So awareness appears to have got into stones and pots, and it has not: what one sees is a reflection, and reflections do not move. Yat tac-chabda-nibandhanam: and *that* is the *basis of the word* — sabda-nibandhana: what the word rests on. So words are possible because consciousness appears in the insentient: a thing can be *meant* only by seeming to have some awareness lodged in it. This is 1.132's the word-principle in all things, and it grounds 3.14.322's vacas ca eva atra sambhavah — speech is possible where the reflection falls.

Verse 3.14.326.translation: There is no such state as is not joined with gender; but somewhere there is the word's formation, when there is no substratum of gender.
Verse 3.14.326.commentary: Avastha tadrsi nasti ya lingena na yujyate: there is *no* such *state* as is *not joined with gender* — every condition of a thing has one of the three (3.14.321's manifestation, disappearance, persistence: and this is what the linga now turns out to be). Kvacit tu sabda-samskaro lingasya anasraye sati: *but* *somewhere* there is the *word's formation* when there is *no substratum* of gender. So gender is universal in the states and there are still words with no seat for it — the indeclinables (3.14.105's avyayanam yo dharmah). The two facts stand side by side: everything has gender, and some words have nowhere to put it. Bhartrhari does not reconcile them here.

Verse 3.14.327.translation: Of the states expressed by krt and taddhita, gender is not contradicted; in the science, gender is the condition of a quality, and thus the akrti is accepted.
Verse 3.14.327.commentary: Krt-taddhita-abhidheyanam bhavanam na virudhyate: of the *states* expressed by *krt* and *taddhita* — the deverbal and denominal abstracts, 'pakti' and 'sauklya' — *gender* is *not contradicted*: these take gender readily, though they name no substances. Sastre lingam gunavastha: in the *science*, *gender* is the *condition of a quality* — guna-avastha: a quality's state, which is 3.14.321's pravrtti under another name. Tatha ca akrtir isyate: and *thus* the *akrti* is *accepted*. So the grammarians' linga is a quality's condition, not a sex — which is what 3.14.319-320 argued and what the krt-taddhita abstracts confirm: they have gender because they have conditions, not because they have bodies.

Verse 3.14.328.translation: As regards gender there is no difference at all, even on the substance-view; therefore, whatever the seven alternatives may be, that same pervaded-gender obtains here.
Verse 3.14.328.commentary: Lingam prati na bhedo 'sti dravya-pakse 'pi kascana: as regards *gender* there is *no difference* *at all*, *even on the substance-view* — the akrti-vadin and the dravya-vadin (3.14.316's ancient quarrel) come out the same on gender. Tasmat sapta vikalpa ye sa eva atra avista-lingata: *therefore*, whatever the *seven alternatives* may be, that same *pervaded-gender* obtains here. Sapta vikalpah: the seven positions the tradition had enumerated. So all seven agree, and the great dispute makes no difference to the case at hand. This is 3.14.119 and 3.14.202's move for the last time, and its scope is now maximal: the result survives every position anyone holds, and so the positions need not be adjudicated at all.

Verse 3.14.329.translation: The restriction on the substance's number is accepted from the science; since the science regarding that akrti is justified quite otherwise.
Verse 3.14.329.commentary: Vacane niyamah sastrad dravyasya abhyupagamyate: the *restriction* on *number* of the *substance* is *accepted from the science* — the grammar, not the world, settles how many a substance-word says (3.14.134's sastram arabdham). Yatas tad-akrtau sastram anyatha eva samarthyate: since the *science* regarding *that akrti* is *justified quite otherwise* — anyatha eva: on a wholly different footing. So the two objects are handled by two disjoint justifications: what makes the rules right for substances does not make them right for forms. The verse is registering that the sastra is not a single system with one ground — it is a set of accommodations, each answering to what it accommodates (3.14.74's science standing far off).

Verse 3.14.330.translation: That meaning which operates in many — when its difference is intended, of it as designated by its own substrata, number is stated in the science.
Verse 3.14.330.commentary: Vartate yo bahusv arthah: that *meaning* which operates in *many* — the general, present in its instances. Bhede tasya vivaksite: when its *difference* is *intended* — vivaksa again: the will to say the difference (3.14.224). Sva-asrayair vyupadistasya sastre vacanam ucyate: of it as *designated by its own substrata*, *number* is *stated* in the *science*. So the sequence: a general in many; a wish to say the manyness; the general designated through its seats; and then the grammar supplies a plural. The number is at the end of a chain that begins in an intention, and no step of it is in the object. This is 3.14.188's buddhya eva in the number's own case.

Verse 3.14.331.translation: But when difference itself is cognized through difference of substratum — then, of the akrti, on the substance-view, there is no difference.
Verse 3.14.331.commentary: Yada tu asraya-bhedena bheda eva pratiyate: when *difference itself* is cognized through *difference of substratum* — the difference one grasps is the seats', not the form's. Akrter dravya-paksena tada bhedo na vidyate: *then*, of the *akrti*, on the *substance-view*, there is *no difference*. So on the dravya-account the form stays undivided while its seats multiply, and the plural one hears reports the seats. This is the pair to 3.14.330: there the science supplied a number to an intended difference; here the difference belongs to the substrata and the form takes none. The two positions differ only in where they lodge the multiplicity, and 3.14.328 has already said it will not matter.

Verse 3.14.332.translation: But in non-difference, from singular-wordhood and from the science's number — ekasesa need not be stated; there is, however, the possibility of the numbers.
Verse 3.14.332.commentary: Abhede tv eka-sabdatvat sastrac ca vacane sati: in *non-difference*, from *singular-wordhood* (3.14.316's consequence) and from the *science's* number. Ekaseso na vaktavyah: *ekasesa* need *not* be *stated* — the rule that leaves one of several identical words standing becomes unnecessary, since on this account there was only ever one word (3.14.30; 3.14.109). Vacananam tu sambhavah: there is, *however*, the *possibility* of the *numbers* — they remain available. So the akrti-doctrine retires a rule and keeps the plurals: a real economy, and Bhartrhari credits it (3.14.318's nirarthakah pratisedhah, the same generosity). He is auditing the rival position's books and finding them in order.

Verse 3.14.333.translation: But if substance is not expressible, this assistance to the akrti, resting on that, is not conceivable — from the absence of substance.
Verse 3.14.333.commentary: The objection. Nanu ca anabhidheyatve dravyasya: *but if* *substance* is *not expressible* — on the strict akrti-view, words mean forms and substances are never said. Tad-apasrayah akrter upakaro 'yam dravyabhavan na kalpate: this *assistance* to the *akrti*, *resting on that*, is *not conceivable*, from the *absence of substance*. Upakara: the help the substrata were giving — 3.14.330-331 had the seats supplying number and difference. But if the seats are inexpressible they cannot help. So the akrti-account has been quietly living off substances it officially does not have. The objection is exact and 3.14.334 answers it exactly.

Verse 3.14.334.translation: No designation in the science rests on what is expressed; and that word-meaning which is called substance — it is not denied.
Verse 3.14.334.commentary: The answer, and it is two moves. Vyapadeso 'bhidheyena na sastre kascid asritah: *no* designation in the *science* rests on *what is expressed* — the grammar's technical terms do not attach to meanings; they attach to forms and notation (3.14.78's anubandha-svaradibhyah... na tan prati). So the objection's premise — that inexpressibility disables assistance — misreads how the science works: it never used expressibility. Dravyam nama padartho yo na ca sa pratisidhyate: and that *word-meaning* which is *called substance* — it is *not denied*. So the akrti-vadin never said there are no substances; he said words do not express them. The substances are there, helping, unsaid. The distinction between what is and what is expressed is the whole of the reply — and it is the distinction this work is built on.

Verse 3.14.335.translation: The substance's subordination is not resorted to with regard to expressibility; the construction is: 'the assisting quality is the remainder — that is the word-meaning'.
Verse 3.14.335.commentary: Guna-bhavo 'bhidheyatvam prati dravyasya na asritah: the *substance's* *subordination* is *not resorted to* with regard to *expressibility* — the substance is not subordinate *qua* expressible, since on this view it is not expressed at all (3.14.334). Upakari gunah sesah padartha iti kalpana: the *construction* is — the *assisting quality* is the *remainder*, and *that* is the *word-meaning*. Kalpana: a construction (3.14.96's bheda-samsarga-kalpana). So subordination is a relation among *constructed* elements, not a fact about what is or is not said. This separates two things the debate had run together: being unexpressed and being subordinate. The substance is the first and not the second.

Verse 3.14.336.translation: There is no subordination in the substance without substance-expression; or else the akrti's principality — therefore it is justified thus.
Verse 3.14.336.commentary: Dravye na guna-bhavo 'sti vina dravyabhidhayitam: there is *no subordination* in the *substance* *without* *substance-expression* — one cannot make a thing subordinate without saying it: subordination is a relation between things said (3.14.335's kalpana). Akrtau va pradhanatvam ata evam samarthyate: *or else* the *akrti's* *principality* — *therefore* it is *justified* thus. So the choice: either the substance is expressed (and can then be subordinated) or the akrti is principal (and the substance is simply outside the account). The akrti-vadin takes the second, and the verse concedes it is coherent: samarthyate, it is justified. The account is consistent, and its consistency is bought by putting substances outside the semantics altogether.

Verse 3.14.337.translation: Some accept subordinate-and-principal as with noun and verb; but the subordination of what serves another's purpose is not described as in a derivative formation.
Verse 3.14.337.commentary: Kaiscid guna-pradhanatvam nama-akhyatavad isyate: *some* accept *subordinate-and-principal* as with *noun and verb* — the sentence's own hierarchy: the verb principal, the nominals subordinate (3.8.2's the action is the principal; 2.326's the verb as the sentence's core). Na vrttivat pararthasya guna-bhavas tu varnyate: but the *subordination* of what *serves another's purpose* is *not* described as in a *derivative formation*. So the sentence's subordination is not the compound's: in a sentence the subordinate members are still there, saying things; in a compound they are not (3.14.53's bhagabhyam vartate vina). The distinction matters because the akrti-debate keeps borrowing sentence-relations to describe word-internal ones, and this verse marks the borrowing as illicit.

Verse 3.14.338.translation: From the manifoldness of what is subordinate, the akrti has singular-wordhood; and the difference of number is established from concomitance with the difference of substances.
Verse 3.14.338.commentary: Guna-bhutasya nanatvad akrter eka-sabdata: from the *manifoldness* of what is *subordinate*, the *akrti* has *singular-wordhood* — and the logic is exactly inverted from what one expects: because the *subordinate* is many, the *principal* is one-worded. The manyness is all lodged below, and the form above is single. Siddho vacana-bhedas ca dravya-bheda-samanvayat: and the *difference of number* is *established* from *concomitance* with the *difference of substances*. So the plural one hears is the substances' and the word is single: the account gets both facts, and gets them from the same division of labour. This is 3.14.331 stated as a doctrine rather than a hypothetical.

Verse 3.14.339.translation: As the means, by subordination, is the differentiator of the action in verbs — so the substance is said to be that of the universal, which has a single word.
Verse 3.14.339.commentary: The analogy that makes 3.14.338 intelligible. Sadhanam guna-bhavena kriyaya bhedakam yatha akhyatesu: as the *means*, *by subordination*, is the *differentiator* of the *action* in *verbs* — 'pacati' is one action-word and its karakas make it this cooking and that (3.7's the sadhanas; 3.8.44's the action one and differentiated by its means). Eka-sabdayah jater dravyam tatha ucyate: *so* the *substance* is said to be that of the *universal*, which has a *single word*. So the universal stands to its substances as the verb to its karakas: one word above, the differentiating many below. This is the samuddesa's most economical statement of the akrti-position, and it makes it look, for the first time, obviously right.

Verse 3.14.340-341.translation: (340) In oneness, from likeness of form in the conveying of words; from the cause, when there is the specified grasping of the meaning possessing that — (341) from the connection 'this is that', in the akrti's operating together with the substrata, which has a differentiated word — gender and number are established.
Verse 3.14.340-341.commentary: The mechanism, in two verses. (340): ekatve tulya-rupatvac chabdanam pratipadane — in *oneness*, from *likeness of form* in the *conveying* of *words*: the form is one and the words that convey it are alike. Nimittat tadvato 'rthasya visista-grahane sati: *from the cause*, when there is the *specified grasping* of the *meaning possessing that*. (341): so 'yam ity abhisambandhad asrayair akrteh saha — from the connection *'this is that'*, of the *akrti* *together with* the *substrata*. Pravrttau bhinna-sabdayam linga-samkhye prasidhyatah: in the *operation*, which has a *differentiated word*, *gender and number* are *established*. So: the form is one, it identifies with its seats, and in *that joint operation* — where the word does differentiate — concord arises. Neither the form alone nor the seats alone would give it; the identification is what does.

Verse 3.14.342.translation: And before connection with the universal, there is expressibility by pronouns; 'tyad' and the rest, indicating things, are employed for substance.
Verse 3.14.342.commentary: Prak ca jaty-abhisambandhat sarvanama-abhidheyata: *before* connection with the *universal*, there is *expressibility by pronouns* — before a thing is brought under any kind, it can still be pointed at: 'that'. Vastupalaksanah sattve prayujyante tyad-adayah: *'tyad'* and the rest, *indicating things* — upalaksana: indicating without characterizing (3.14.170) — are *employed for substance*. So the pronoun is the language's access to a substance as such, prior to every universal: it indicates and says nothing. This answers a real question the akrti-account raises — if words mean forms, how is a bare substance ever reached? — and the answer is: by pronouns, which do not mean forms. It is why 3.14.217 and 3.14.314 kept finding pronouns wherever a substance was unexpressed.

Verse 3.14.343.translation: As in 'the two cookings', 'the cookings', the substratum is accepted by some as the differentiator; and that property of quality-words is without any taking-up.
Verse 3.14.343.commentary: Pakau paka iti yatha bhedakah kaiscid asrayah isyate: as in *'pakau'*, *'pakah'* — the two cookings, the cookings — the *substratum* is *accepted by some* as the *differentiator*: the abstract noun pluralizes because its seats are several, not because cooking is (3.14.339's sadhanam bhedakam). Ca anupadano dharmo 'sau guna-vacinam: and that *property* of *quality-words* is *without any taking-up* — anupadana: the differentiating seats are never mentioned, and they differentiate anyway. So 'pakah' says several cookings without saying whose, and the plural is owed to seats that the word does not touch. This is the akrti-account's cleanest evidence: the number is demonstrably not in what is said.

Verse 3.14.344.translation: If, without any taking-up of the substratum, the general alone obtains the substratum's properties — that has been considered earlier.
Verse 3.14.344.commentary: Asrayasya anupadane kevalam labhate yadi adhara-dharman samanyam: *if*, without any *taking-up* of the *substratum*, the *general* *alone* obtains the *substratum's properties* — the position 3.14.343 just described, stated as a question. Purastat tad vicaritam: *that* has been *considered earlier* — purastat: before, in the earlier part of the work. So Bhartrhari points back rather than repeat himself (3.14.244's granthe purvatra, the same gesture) — the Jati-samuddesa (3.1) took up whether a universal can wear its instances' properties, and the reader is sent there. The Vakyapadiya is again treating itself as one continuous argument, and the cross-reference is the only answer this verse gives.

Verse 3.14.345.translation: Of universal-expressing words, previously operating in the universal — from a relation not expressed by any word, the individual also arises.
Verse 3.14.345.commentary: How a jati-word gets to an individual. Jatau purvam pravrttanam sabdanam jati-vacinam: of *universal-expressing* words, *previously operating* in the *universal* — the word starts at the kind, always. Asabda-vacyat sambandhad vyaktir apy upajayate: from a *relation* *not expressed by any word*, the *individual* *also* *arises*. Asabda-vacya: not sayable by a word — the relation by which 'cow' arrives at this cow is itself unsaid, and unsayable (3.3's the sambandha-samuddesa: relation cannot be expressed). Upajayate: *arises*. So the individual is not reached by any further word: it comes up out of an unsayable relation, and the word never left the universal. This is why 3.14.342 needed pronouns — and why 'cow' can pick out this cow without meaning her.

Verse 3.14.346.translation: From the connection 'this is that', the universal's property is figuratively applied; and the substance, resting on that, is accepted as the universal's difference.
Verse 3.14.346.commentary: So 'yam ity abhisambandhaj jati-dharmopacaryate: from the connection 'this is that', the *universal's property* is *figuratively applied* — upacaryate: transferred (3.14.284's word). The individual wears cowhood by transfer, not by containing it. Dravyam tad-asrayo bhedo jates ca abhyupagamyate: and the *substance*, *resting on that*, is *accepted* as the *universal's* *difference*. So the substance is what differentiates the universal (3.14.339) and it does so while wearing the universal's property figuratively. The relation is thus doubly non-literal: the universal is in the individual by transfer, and the individual is the universal's difference by acceptance (abhyupagamyate). Nothing here is a fact about cows.

Verse 3.14.347.translation: As the word 'platform', settled in the platforms alone, states what rests on them as its reality — so the universal-word operates in substances.
Verse 3.14.347.commentary: The image that makes 3.14.345-346 concrete, and it is the samuddesa's favourite. Manca-sabdo yatha adheyam mancesv eva vyavasthitah tattvena aha: as the word *'manca'*, *settled in the platforms alone*, states what *rests on them* — the people — as its *reality*. 'Manca hasanti', the platforms laugh (3.14.150). The word never leaves the platforms and what it says is the people, and it says them *tattvena*, as the real thing. Tatha jati-sabdo dravyesu vartate: *so* the *universal-word* *operates in substances*. So 'cow' is settled in cowhood and states the individual cow as its reality — the word stays where it is and its meaning is elsewhere. The whole account of reference in this samuddesa is in that image.

Verse 3.14.348.translation: There, the universal's being the word-meaning is accepted just so; but the universal — and number — is annexed to the substance-self.
Verse 3.14.348.commentary: Tatra jati-padarthatvam tathaiva abhyupagamyate: *there*, the *universal's* being the *word-meaning* is *accepted just so* — the akrti-position is granted in full: what the word means is the kind. Jatir ut sankhya tu dravyatmany anusajyate: but the *universal* — *and number* — is *annexed* to the *substance-self*. Anusajyate: is attached, comes along (3.14.279, 3.14.287's word). So the universal is the meaning and it *lands* on the substance, bringing number with it. The two claims coexist: the word means the kind, and the kind attaches to the thing. This is 3.14.347's manca exactly — settled in the platforms, landing on the people.
Verse 3.14.348.contested: Pada c reads 'jatir utsankhya tu' in our source, which we cannot construe; we read 'ut' as the particle 'uta' ('and', 'or rather') with 'sankhya' as a second subject, giving 'the universal — and number — is annexed'. An alternative is to take an unattested compound 'ut-sankhya'; we do not press it. The verse's force rests on 'dravyatmany anusajyate', which is secure.

Verse 3.14.349.translation: Where the hearing is 'this is of this', or 'this is that' — it operates by another's property; that other is what is expressed.
Verse 3.14.349.commentary: Asyedam iti va yatra so 'yam ity api va srutih: where the *hearing* is *'this is of this'* — the possessive identification — *or* *'this is that'* — the plain one: the two formulas that have run through this entire samuddesa. Vartate para-dharmena: it *operates* by *another's property* — para-dharma: a property that is not its own. Tad anyad abhidhiyate: *that other* is what is *expressed*. So wherever one of these identifications is in play, the word is working by a borrowed property and what it expresses is the other thing. Bhartrhari is naming, at the end, the device he has used at almost every turn: 'so 'yam' is how a word says what it does not mean.

Verse 3.14.350.translation: That which is principal — of it there is no own-form, from non-determination; and the substance is indicated by the quality's self, by way of being that.
Verse 3.14.350.commentary: A deep and strange verse. Yat pradhanam na tasyasti svarupam anirupanat: that which is *principal* — of *it* there is *no own-form*, from *non-determination*: anirupana: it is not determined, not characterized. What is principal has no nature of its own, precisely because nothing determines it — determination comes from the subordinate, and the principal is what receives it. Gunasya ca atmana dravyam tad-bhavena upalaksyate: and the *substance* is *indicated* by the *quality's self*, *by way of being that*. Upalaksyate: is *indicated* (3.14.170's upalaksana: marked, not described). So the substance — the principal — has no form and is pointed at through a quality that it is said to *be*. The principal is empty and is shown by its subordinates: which is 3.14.164's substance cognized as having expectancy, and 3.2's dravya as the undetermined that all determination is of.

Verse 3.14.351.translation: But at the time of differentiating, the quality attains principality; the hearing of conjunction does not operate directly in the meanings.
Verse 3.14.351.commentary: Gunasya bheda-kale tu pradhanyam upajayate: at the *time of differentiating*, the *quality* attains *principality* — bheda-kale: at the moment of the cutting. So the hierarchy of 3.14.350 inverts momentarily: while the subordinate is doing its differentiating work, it is the principal — because the principal has no form of its own and is passive to the cut. Samsarga-srutir arthesu saksad eva na vartate: the *hearing of conjunction* does *not operate* *directly* in the *meanings* — saksat: immediately. So the joining one hears is never straight onto the meanings: it goes through the differentiation, and the meanings are reached only after the cut has been made. Principality is a function of the moment, not a standing rank.

Verse 3.14.352.translation: When that word, having operated in the universal, operates again in the substance — then the universal's alone being the word-meaning is not accepted.
Verse 3.14.352.commentary: Jatau vrtto yada dravye sa sabdo vartate punah: when that word, having *operated in the universal*, operates *again* in the *substance* — the two-step of 3.14.345-347: 'cow' settles on cowhood and lands on the cow. Jater eva padarthatvam na tada abhyupagamyate: *then* the universal's *alone* being the word-meaning is *not accepted*. So the akrti-account cannot survive its own second step: if the word goes on to the substance, the universal is not the *only* meaning. Punah — *again* — is what breaks it. The position was coherent (3.14.336's samarthyate) so long as the word stayed put; it does not stay put, and 3.14.353 says why that is not a refutation.

Verse 3.14.353.translation: The re-operation of what has operated is described as by oneness; in the means of comprehension, reality is not followed.
Verse 3.14.353.commentary: Pravrttanam punar vrttir ekatvena upavarnyate: the *re-operation* of what has *operated* is *described* as by *oneness* — the word's second step (3.14.352's punah) is accounted for by saying it never left: one operation, described twice. Pratipatter upayesu na tattvam anugamyate: *in the means of comprehension*, *reality* is *not followed* — upaya: means, devices (3.14.76's do not take the upayas for true; 3.14.96's upaya-matram nanatvam). So the two-step is a device and the doctrine that reconciles it is another device, and neither tracks how things are. This is the samuddesa's governing sentence applied to its own analysis of the akrti-question — and it means 3.14.352 was never a refutation, because there was nothing there to refute.

Verse 3.14.354.translation: When the universal of what is expressed by a non-separate word is resorted to, there being contact with substance — then the universal is the word-meaning.
Verse 3.14.354.commentary: The first of three permutations. Aprthak-sabda-vacyasya jatir asriyate yada: when the *universal* of what is expressed by a *non-separate word* is *resorted to* — aprthak-sabda-vacya: said by a word that is not separate from it; the universal and its word are not two. Dravyasya sati samsparse: there being *contact* with *substance* — samsparsa: touch. The substance is grazed, not meant (3.14.48's samsprsya avayavan, the same verb). Tada jati-padarthata: *then* the *universal* is the *word-meaning*. So: substance touched, universal meant — the akrti-position, stated as one option among several rather than as the truth.

Verse 3.14.355.translation: When, there being contact with substance, the substance is resorted to as expressible by that very word — then the substance is the word-meaning.
Verse 3.14.355.commentary: The second permutation, and the words are almost identical. Dravyasya sati samsparse dravyam asriyate yada vacyam tena eva sabdena: when, there being *contact* with substance, the *substance* is *resorted to* as *expressible* by *that very word*. Tada dravya-padartha: *then* the *substance* is the *word-meaning*. So the same touch, the same word, and now the substance is meant — the Vyadi-position (3.1's dravya-paksa). Bhartrhari sets 3.14.354 and this side by side with only one word changed, and the effect is deliberate: the great quarrel of the grammarians turns on which of two things one *resorts to* (asriyate), and nothing in the situation decides. Both descriptions fit the same fact.

Verse 3.14.356.translation: When the universal, though expressed by a non-separate word, operates in mere difference, as possessing relation — that too is the substance's being the word-meaning.
Verse 3.14.356.commentary: The third permutation, a hybrid. Aprthak-sabda-vacya api bheda-matre pravartate yada sambandhavat jatih: when the *universal*, *though* expressed by a *non-separate word*, operates *in mere difference*, as *possessing relation*. Sa api dravya-padartha: *that too* is the *substance's* being the word-meaning. So a universal that has gone over into differentiation is functionally a substance-account: once the jati is operating in bheda-matra — mere difference — it is doing the substance's work, and the label 'universal' has stopped mattering. This is the samuddesa dissolving the debate rather than settling it: the positions are not three theories but three descriptions, and one of them turns into another when you push it.

Verse 3.14.357.translation: Of the two utterly different expressers of universal and substance — when the assisting of the inexpressible is resorted to, there is both-meaning-ness.
Verse 3.14.357.commentary: The fourth position. Atyanta-bhinnayor eva jati-dravyabhidhayinoh: of the *two utterly different* expressers of *universal* and *substance* — atyanta-bhinna: the two are held wholly apart, no hybrid (against 3.14.356). Avacyasya upakaritva asrite tu ubhayarthata: when the *assisting* of the *inexpressible* is *resorted to*, there is *both-meaning-ness*. Avacya: the inexpressible — the relation, which no word says (3.14.345's asabda-vacya). So: the two words stay utterly distinct, an unsayable relation assists, and the result means *both*. Ubhayarthata: the jati-sabda-vada's classical compromise. And it works only because something inexpressible is doing the joining — which has been true of every account in this samuddesa.

Verse 3.14.358.translation: But by one who accepts the difference made by the substratum in what is resorted to — singular-wordhood in the universal-word has again been described.
Verse 3.14.358.commentary: Asrite tv asraya-krtam bhedam abhyupagacchata: by one *accepting* the *difference* *made by the substratum* in what is *resorted to* — the position of 3.14.338: the manyness is the seats'. Punas ca api eka-sabdatvam jati-sabde 'nuvarnitam: *again* *singular-wordhood* in the *universal-word* has been *described*. Punah, anuvarnitam: again, described-after — Bhartrhari marks that he is repeating himself. So the survey closes by returning to where it started, and the return is noted as a return. Four positions, each internally sound, none refuted, and the last is the first. This is what 3.14.353 predicted: in the means of comprehension, reality is not followed, and so the means do not converge.

Verse 3.14.359.translation: That by which there is knowledge of the unknown is called 'measure'; 'prastha' and the rest — by it the measured's self is determined in its entirety.
Verse 3.14.359.commentary: The comparison-compound begins, and it begins by defining its neighbour. Anirjnatasya nirjnanam yena tan manam ucyate: that by which there is *knowledge* of the *unknown* is called *'mana'* — measure. Prasthadi tena meyatma sakalyena avadharyate: *'prastha'* and the rest — *by it* the *measured's self* is *determined* *in its entirety*. Sakalyena: completely, without remainder. So a measure gives total knowledge of what it measures: say 'a prastha of rice' and nothing about the quantity is left over. This is the benchmark against which 3.14.360 will define comparison — and comparison will fall short of it, deliberately.

Verse 3.14.360.translation: That by which the unknown, having that property, is understood through the well-known — from the knowledge being incomplete, that is called 'comparison'.
Verse 3.14.360.commentary: The definition, and the contrast with 3.14.359 is the whole of it. Anirjnatam prasiddhena yena tad-dharma gamyate: that by which the *unknown*, *having that property*, is understood through the *well-known* — prasiddha: what is established, familiar. One knows the cloud; one learns the complexion. Sakalyena aparijnanad upamanam tad ucyate: *from the knowledge being incomplete*, *that* is called *'upamana'*. Sakalyena aparijnanat: precisely because it is *not* complete. So comparison is defined by its shortfall: a measure exhausts and a comparison does not, and that is not a defect in comparison but its identity. 'Megha-syama' does not tell you the shade; if it did it would be a measure. This is one of the finest short definitions in the work.

Verse 3.14.361.translation: That property common to the two — the comparans and the comparandum — the compound of comparanda is with the words expressing that.
Verse 3.14.361.commentary: Dvayoh samano yo dharma upamana-upameyayoh: that *property* *common* to the *two* — the *upamana* (that with which one compares: the cloud) and the *upameya* (that which is compared: the complexion). Samasa upamananam sabdais tad-abhidhayibhih: the *compound of comparanda* is with the *words expressing that* — the common property's words. So 'megha-syama' compounds 'megha' with the word that says the shared property, 'syama'. The rule is stated flatly and it raises at once the question 3.14.362-365 will worry: is the darkness in the cloud and the darkness in the girl one property or two? The whole comparison depends on the answer, and there is no good answer.

Verse 3.14.362.translation: That difference in darkness which comes from difference of substratum is not intended; that quality, having resorted to oneness, is cognized as having different substrata.
Verse 3.14.362.commentary: Adhara-bhedad bhedo yah syamatve so 'vivaksitah: the *difference* in *darkness* which comes from *difference of substratum* is *not intended* — avivaksita: not meant to be said (3.14.103, 3.14.176's device). The cloud's dark and the girl's dark do differ, and the comparison does not mean the difference. Guno 'sav asrita-ekatvah bhinnadharah pratiyate: that *quality*, *having resorted to oneness*, is cognized as *having different substrata*. Asrita-ekatva: it has *taken up* oneness — the oneness is resorted to, not found. So the comparison works by an intended oneness laid over an actual difference: the darkness is made one so that the two may be compared. The device is exactly 3.14.99's abheda-ekatva, doing rhetorical work.

Verse 3.14.363.translation: The difference of the two qualities is fixed; likewise the oneness of the quality-universal. In oneness, or in utter difference, comparison is not possible.
Verse 3.14.363.commentary: The dilemma, stated with perfect symmetry. Gunayor niyato bhedah: the *difference* of the *two qualities* is *fixed* — the two darknesses are two, and no wishing makes them one. Guna-jates tatha ekata: *likewise* the *oneness* of the *quality-universal* — and darkness-in-general is one, equally fixed. Ekatve 'tyanta-bhede va na upamanasya sambhavah: *in oneness*, *or* in *utter difference*, *comparison* is *not possible*. So: if the property is one, there is nothing to compare (identity is not likeness); if the properties are two, there is no ground of comparison. And both are true at once, of the same darkness. Comparison requires a thing that is neither one nor two — which is 3.14.209's ekas ca dvayatmakah, and here it is a condition of a figure of speech.

Verse 3.14.364.translation: When only the universal is required, there is no purpose of comparison; the one darkness operates in both the qualities.
Verse 3.14.364.commentary: The first horn pressed. Jati-matra-vyapeksayam upamartho na kascana: when *only* the *universal* is *required*, there is *no purpose* of *comparison* — na kascana: none whatever. Syamatvam ekam gunayor ubhayor api vartate: the *one* darkness *operates* in *both* the qualities. So if the comparison is to rest on darkness-in-general, it says nothing: the cloud is dark and she is dark, and 'dark like a cloud' has told you only that she is dark — which 'dark' had already said. The comparison collapses into a tautology. So the shared property cannot be the universal, and 3.14.365 shows it cannot be the particulars either.

Verse 3.14.365.translation: The very cause by which 'dark' operates in the knife there — that cause is not distinguished in the cognition of Devadatta.
Verse 3.14.365.commentary: The second horn. Yena eva hetuna syama sastri tatra pravartate: the *very cause* by which *'dark'* operates in the *knife* there. Sa hetur devadattayah pratyaye na visisyate: *that* cause is *not distinguished* in the *cognition* of *Devadatta* — the dark girl of 3.14.297. Na visisyate: it is not marked off, not told apart. So the reason 'dark' applies to the knife and the reason it applies to her are indistinguishable in the cognition — one cannot get at the particular darknesses to compare them, because the cognition does not separate them. So the comparison cannot rest on the particulars either: they are inaccessible. Between 3.14.364 and this verse, both horns of 3.14.363 are closed, and upamana works anyway.

Verse 3.14.366.translation: That difference in the quality which comes from the substratum, and that non-specificity of the universal — by both of these together, the substance-self is cognized as engaged in operation.
Verse 3.14.366.commentary: The resolution, and it is characteristic: take both horns. Asrayad yo gune bhedo: the *difference* in the *quality* from the *substratum* — 3.14.362's actual difference, 3.14.363's niyato bhedah. Jater ya ca avisistata: and the *non-specificity* of the *universal* — 3.14.364's one darkness in both. Tabhyam ubhabhyam dravyatma sa-vyaparah pratiyate: by *both of these* *together*, the *substance-self* is *cognized* as *engaged in operation*. Sa-vyapara: with its activity, under way (3.14.321's pravrtti). So the comparison does not need the property to be either one or two: the difference and the non-specificity work *together*, and what they deliver is a substance in action. Neither horn alone gives a comparison; both at once do. This is bhedabheda (3.14.209) doing the work no single answer could.

Verse 3.14.367.translation: That transaction, resorted to in oneness and in manifoldness, operates by an intermingled reflection of difference and non-difference.
Verse 3.14.367.commentary: The general form of 3.14.366's answer. So 'yam ekatva-nanatve vyavaharah samasritah: that *transaction* — vyavahara: dealing, practice, the whole business of speaking and understanding — *resorted to* in *oneness and in manifoldness*: it stands with a foot in each. Bhedabheda-vimarsena vyatikirnena vartate: it *operates* by a *reflection* of *difference and non-difference* that is *vyatikirna* — *intermingled*, shot through one another, poured together. So the practice does not choose between one and many: it runs on a reflection in which both are mixed past separating. Vyatikirna is the word to keep — not a synthesis, not a compromise, but an interpenetration that no analysis will part. This is the samuddesa's last word on the topic, and it is a description of how speech works, not a metaphysics.

Verse 3.14.368.translation: When only the universal is intended, one simply says 'dark'; there is no purpose in taking up 'knife' and the rest.
Verse 3.14.368.commentary: Syama ity eva abhidhiyate jati-matre vivaksite: when *only* the *universal* is *intended*, one simply says *'dark'* — ity eva: just that. Sastry-adinam upadane tatra nasti prayojanam: there is *no purpose* in *taking up* *'knife'* and the rest. So if darkness-in-general were the point, the comparison would be idle: one would say 'dark' and stop. The fact that speakers reach for the knife proves they want something the universal does not give. This is 3.14.364's tautology-argument again, now used constructively: the existence of comparison is evidence that there is something below the universal to be gestured at.

Verse 3.14.369.translation: That difference not expressible by a word does not operate in mere darkness; the one whose operation is in some dark things — that is what is required here.
Verse 3.14.369.commentary: Asabda-vacyo yo bhedah syama-matre na vartate: that *difference* *not expressible by a word* — asabda-vacya (3.14.345's word for the unsayable relation) — does *not operate* in *mere darkness*: the shade that makes this dark a cloud's dark is not in darkness-at-large. Syamesu kesucid vrttir yasya so 'tra vyapeksyate: the one whose *operation* is in *some* dark things — *that* is what is *required* here. Kesucit: in *some* — not all and not one. So what the comparison needs is a middle thing: narrower than darkness, wider than this darkness, and unsayable. It has no word, and 'megha-syama' is how one points at it. That is what a simile is for.

Verse 3.14.370.translation: Some general operates in some dark things; some operates everywhere; some difference is settled in a single dark thing.
Verse 3.14.370.commentary: The scale, laid out in one verse, and it is a genuine discovery. Syamesu kesucit kincit: *some* general in *some* dark things — a middle-level kind: cloud-dark, sword-dark. Kincit sarvatra vartate samanyam: *some* general operates *everywhere* — darkness as such. Kascid ekasmin syame bhedo vyavasthitah: *some* *difference* is *settled* in a *single* dark thing — this shade, here, and nowhere else. So generality is not two-valued: between the universal and the individual there is a graded range of intermediate kinds, and language has words for almost none of them. The comparison-compound is the device for reaching the middle rungs (3.14.369's kesucit). This is a very fine piece of semantics and it is stated in twenty syllables.

Verse 3.14.371.translation: So indeed: fragrance being present, there is a difference of scents among jati-flowers, lotuses and the rest; but the difference being present, a similarity is apprehended.
Verse 3.14.371.commentary: 3.14.370's scale confirmed from another sense. Tatha hi sati saurabhye bhedo jaty-utpaladisu gandhanam: *so indeed* — fragrance being *present*, there is a *difference of scents* among *jati*-flowers, *lotuses* and the rest: they are all fragrant and no two smell alike. Sati bhede tu sadrsyam upalabhyate: *but* the difference being *present*, a *similarity* is *apprehended*. So the differences are real and one perceives, across them, a resemblance — not the bare universal 'fragrance' but a family likeness among these scents. The example is well chosen: nobody can say what jasmine and lotus have in common, everybody smells it, and no word names it (3.14.369's asabda-vacya). The middle rung is where the nose lives.

Verse 3.14.372.translation: The difference of qualities is followed out from the substratum, or from itself, or from an indescribable particular, or from mixture with other qualities.
Verse 3.14.372.commentary: Four sources for the differences of 3.14.371. Gunanam asrayad bhedah: the *difference of qualities* from the *substratum* — the cloud's dark differs because clouds do. Svato va: *or from itself* — the quality is intrinsically this one. Anirdesyad visesad va: *or* from an *indescribable particular* — anirdesya: not statable; the shade that is simply this shade and has no account. Sankarad va gunantaraih: *or* from *mixture* with *other qualities* — the dark is what it is because of what it is blended with. So the sources are catalogued and not ranked, and one of them is confessedly indescribable. This is the samuddesa being honest about where its analysis stops: some differences have no reason, and one names the fact rather than the reason.

Verse 3.14.373.translation: The comparans, from its being well-known, exceeds everywhere; the state of being compared does not withdraw in excess or in equality.
Verse 3.14.373.commentary: Upamanam prasiddhatvat sarvatra vyatiricyate: the *comparans*, from its being *well-known*, *exceeds* *everywhere* — prasiddha (3.14.360's word): the cloud is famously dark, and its darkness always outdoes what is compared to it. Vyatiricyate: it exceeds, stands over. Upameyatvam adhikye samye va na nivartate: the *state of being compared* does *not withdraw* in *excess* or in *equality*. So even where the compared thing matches or exceeds the standard, it remains the compared thing: 'moon-faced' does not stop being a simile if the face is lovelier than the moon. The direction of comparison is fixed by fame, not by degree. This is why 3.14.359's mana and 3.14.360's upamana differ in kind: measures track quantity and comparisons track renown.

Verse 3.14.374.translation: By others, the universal and the rest is described as the measure of the meaning to be differentiated; for the knowable meaning, whose own form is unknown, is measured by it.
Verse 3.14.374.commentary: Anyais tu manam jaty-adi bhedyasya arthasya varnyate: by *others*, the *universal* and the rest is described as the *measure* of the meaning *to be differentiated* — the jati as a mana (3.14.359), measuring its instances. Anirjnata-svarupo hi jneyo 'rtho tena miyate: for the *knowable* meaning, whose *own form* is *unknown*, is *measured by it*. So the individual, whose nature is not known in itself (3.14.350's anirupana: the principal has no own-form), is measured by the kind it falls under. This is a real position: universals are the measuring-instruments of particulars. And it makes 3.14.360's upamana a defective mana — which the following verses will resist.

Verse 3.14.375-376.translation: (375) But the quality-substratum which, measured by its own measure, is well-known — operates by its own property for the measuring of another substratum. (376) The contact, by a different form, of things possessing different forms, of things to be differentiated — that is called comparison.
Verse 3.14.375-376.commentary: (375): mitas tu svena manena prasiddho yo gunasrayah — the *quality-substratum* which, *measured by its own measure*, is *well-known*: the cloud has been measured already, by whatever measures clouds, and is famous. Asrayantara-manaya sva-dharmena pravartate: it *operates by its own property* for the *measuring of another substratum*. So the comparans is a thing that has finished being known and is now lent out as an instrument. (376): rupantarena samsparso rupantaravatam satam bhinnena yasya bhedyanam — the *contact*, by a *different form*, of *things possessing different forms*, of things *to be differentiated*. Upamanam tad ucyate: *that* is called *comparison*. So: two things of different forms, touched by way of a form that is other than both — that is a simile. Samsparsa (3.14.354's touch) is the word: comparison grazes, and 3.14.359's mana grips.

Verse 3.14.377.translation: The common property 'dark' and the rest, of the comparans and the comparandum, whose principality is resorted to, is differentiated by another property.
Verse 3.14.377.commentary: Dharmah samanah syamadir upamana-upameyayoh: the *common property* — *'dark'* and the rest — of *comparans and comparandum*. Asriyamana-pradhanyah: whose *principality* is *resorted to* — asriyamana: being taken up; the property's principality is conferred by the taking, not owned (3.14.351's the quality attains principality at the moment of differentiating). Dharmena anyena bhidyate: is *differentiated* by *another property*. So the common property is made principal and then cut by a further property — the middle rung of 3.14.370, reached by a second cut. Two operations, and the simile is their product: elevate the shared quality, then differentiate it. Neither alone gives a comparison.

Verse 3.14.378.translation: When it is resorted to thus — 'the dark of the knife and of the girl is similar' — the cause 'it is to be designated by this' stands in the two qualities.
Verse 3.14.378.commentary: Sastri-kumaryoh sadrsah syama ity evam asrite: when it is *resorted to* thus — *'the dark of the knife and of the girl is similar'*: the comparison spelled out as a statement of resemblance. Vyapadesyam anena iti nimittam gunayoh sthitam: the *cause* — *'it is to be designated by this'* — *stands* in the *two qualities*. So what the two darknesses share is not a property but a *designability*: each is such that the other may name it. The nimitta is a fitness-to-be-designated, lodged in the qualities themselves. This is exact and it sidesteps 3.14.363's dilemma: the shared thing need not be one property or two, because it is not a property — it is a licence to name.

Verse 3.14.379.translation: When the possessors of those, through the causes, go as it were to identity with them — that statement of comparans and comparandum rests on difference.
Verse 3.14.379.commentary: Yada nimittais tadvanto gacchanti iva tad-atmatam: when the *possessors of those* — the knife and the girl — through the *causes*, go *as it were* to *identity* with them: iva again. The girl becomes, as it were, the knife's darkness. Bhedasrayam tad akhyanam upamana-upameyayoh: *that statement* of comparans and comparandum *rests on difference*. So the identity is figurative and the statement is grounded in difference: the two must stay apart for the going-toward to mean anything (3.14.363's ekatve na upamanasya sambhavah). The iva is not decoration — it is what keeps the simile from collapsing into an identification. Which is what 3.14.380 is about.

Verse 3.14.380.translation: Those in which the difference withdraws, when identity-attachment is intended to be expressed — those they call 'elliptical comparisons', from the resort by way of that property.
Verse 3.14.380.commentary: The figure named. Tattva-asanga-vivaksayam yesu bhedo nivartate: those in which the *difference withdraws*, when *identity-attachment* is *intended to be expressed* — tattva-asanga: clinging to the very thing, the wish to say she *is* that darkness and not merely like it. Lupta-upamani tany ahuh: those they call *'lupta-upama'* — the *elided comparison*: 'sastri-syama', knife-dark, with the 'iva' gone. Tad-dharmena samasrayat: from the *resort* by way of *that property*. So dropping the 'iva' is not abbreviation: it answers to a different intention. Where 3.14.379's iva held the two apart, here the speaker wants them together and the marker of difference goes. The compound is chosen because it says the identity the phrase could not (3.14.236's the bahuvrihi is desired when his principality is intended).

Verse 3.14.381.translation: The darkness well-known in the knife is the measure; she is measured by it. But another dark thing, having that form, is not measured by it completely.
Verse 3.14.381.commentary: Sastryam prasiddham syamatvam manam sa tena miyate: the *darkness* *well-known* in the *knife* is the *measure*; *she* is *measured by it* — 3.14.374's proposal applied: the famous dark measures the unfamous. Anya syama tu tad-rupa tena atyantam na miyate: but *another* dark thing, *having that form*, is *not* measured by it *completely* — atyantam: utterly, without remainder (3.14.359's sakalyena). So even where the measuring works, it does not exhaust: another dark thing of the same form is left partly unmeasured. And that remainder is precisely 3.14.360's definition of comparison — the shortfall is not a failure of the measure but the nature of the thing.
Verse 3.14.381.contested: Pada c is transmitted with a variant: 'tad-rupa' ('having that form') against 'atad-rupa' ('not having that form'). We translate the former, on which the verse says that even a like-formed thing escapes complete measurement — which is the stronger point and matches 3.14.360's definition. On the variant the claim is the weaker one that unlike things are not measured at all.

Verse 3.14.382.translation: Therefore they call the comparans that other substratum which measures the knife by its own quality — whose quality is incomplete as regards establishment.
Verse 3.14.382.commentary: The definition closed. Sastrim svena gunena atah mimanam asrayam antaram: that *other substratum* which *measures* the *knife* by its *own quality*. Asamapta-gunam siddheh upamanam pracaksate: they call the *comparans* that whose *quality* is *incomplete* as regards *establishment* — asamapta-guna: its quality not finished, not brought to completion. So the comparans is defined by its incompleteness: it measures, and its measuring never finishes (3.14.381's atyantam na miyate). A mana completes and an upamana does not, and the samuddesa has now said this three times in different words (3.14.360, 3.14.381, here) because it is the whole distinction. A simile is a measure that has agreed not to finish.

Verse 3.14.383.translation: The property residing in the comparandum, being heard, is inferred elsewhere; or, heard as residing in the comparans, it is inferred in the comparandum.
Verse 3.14.383.commentary: Upameye sthito dharmah sruto 'nyatra anumiyate: the *property* residing in the *comparandum*, being *heard*, is *inferred* *elsewhere*. Sruto 'thava upamana-stha upameye 'numiyate: *or*, *heard* as residing in the *comparans*, it is *inferred* in the *comparandum*. So the simile runs in either direction: what is said of one term is inferred of the other, and which term gets the saying is a matter of arrangement. Anumiyate: *inferred* — and the word matters. A comparison does not state the shared property twice; it states it once and leaves the other occurrence to be worked out. That is the mechanism of 3.14.360's incompleteness.

Verse 3.14.384.translation: 'The ksatriyas study like brahmins' is seen; from the comparandum's difference, the number rests on the ksatriyas.
Verse 3.14.384.commentary: Adhiyate brahmanavat ksatriya drsyate: *'the ksatriyas study like brahmins'* is *seen* — 'brahmanavat', with the comparison-suffix. Upameyasya bhinnatvad vacanam ksatriyasrayam: from the *comparandum's difference*, the *number* rests on the *ksatriyas*. So the verb's plural answers to the ksatriyas and not to the brahmins, though the brahmins are what is named: the compared thing carries the concord, and the standard is inert. This confirms 3.14.379's bhedasrayam: the comparison rests on difference, and the difference shows up in the grammar. The standard lends its fame (3.14.373's prasiddhatvat) and nothing else.
Verse 3.14.384.contested: Pada a is metrically short in our source ('adhiyate brahmanavat ksatriya drsyate'), a syllable or more having apparently dropped. The construction and the point — that the verb agrees with the ksatriyas — are unaffected.

Verse 3.14.385.translation: Stating a common property that is settled only somewhere — 'a word expressing the general' is what the sutra says.
Verse 3.14.385.commentary: Sadharanam bruvan dharmam kvacid eva vyavasthitam: *stating* a *common property* *settled only somewhere* — kvacid eva: in some places only, 3.14.370's middle rung, the general that is neither everywhere nor in one. Samanya-vacanah sabda iti sutre 'padisyate: *'a word expressing the general'* is *stated in the sutra*. So Panini's phrase 'samanya-vacana' names exactly the thing 3.14.369-370 had to construct: a word whose general is a middle one. The grammar had the category and Bhartrhari has supplied its semantics. This is the pattern of the whole samuddesa — the rules were right and unexplained, and the explanation is the work.

Verse 3.14.386.translation: A quality residing in two is expressed neither by non-difference nor by difference; of the two different properties, one is heard, the other is cognized.
Verse 3.14.386.commentary: The resolution of 3.14.363's dilemma, and it is exact. Na abhedena na bhedena guno dvistho 'bhidhiyate: a *quality residing in two* is expressed *neither by non-difference nor by difference* — both horns declined, in one line. Bhinnayor dharmayor ekah sruyate 'nyah pratiyate: of the *two different properties*, *one* is *heard*, the *other* is *cognized*. So the properties are two — that much is conceded to the difference-horn — and the comparison does not express both: it says one and lets the other be understood (3.14.383's anumiyate). The oneness is never asserted, so the identity-horn is never taken. The simile's whole trick is that it says half and the hearer supplies half, and the halves are different properties.

Verse 3.14.387.translation: That which, settled in the general, does not measure completely — from similarity, the comparandum's meaning is imagined in proximity.
Verse 3.14.387.commentary: Na atyantaya mimite yat samanye samavasthitam: that which, *settled in the general*, does *not* measure *completely* — 3.14.381's atyantam na miyate, now as a definition. Sadrsyad upameyarthah samipe parikalpyate: from *similarity*, the *comparandum's meaning* is *imagined* *in proximity* — samipe: *near by*; parikalpyate: constructed (3.14.75's word). So the comparison does not place the compared thing *at* the standard but *near* it, and the placing is a construction. Samipa is the whole of what a simile achieves: not identity, not measure, but nearness — and the nearness is imagined.

Verse 3.14.388.translation: Or the proximity to the measure is cognized by similarity; for from delimitation there is here a similarity of measure and comparison.
Verse 3.14.388.commentary: Manam prati samipam va sadrsyena pratiyate: the *proximity* to the *measure* is cognized by *similarity* — one knows how near one has come to a full measure by how like it the case is. Paricchedad dhi sadrsyam iha mana-upamanayoh: for from *delimitation* there is *here* a *similarity* of *measure and comparison* — pariccheda: the cutting-off (3.14.126's word). So measure and comparison are themselves alike, because both delimit — and the samuddesa's whole distinction between them (3.14.359-360's sakalyena and its absence) is a difference of degree within one operation. The verse quietly softens its own dichotomy: a mana is an upamana that finished.

Verse 3.14.389.translation: When one universal is required, 'it is that very thing' is determined; when only the difference is required, 'it is something else' is understood.
Verse 3.14.389.commentary: Eka-jati-vyapeksayam tad eveti avasiyate: when *one universal* is *required*, *'it is that very thing'* is *determined*. Bhedasya eva vyapeksayam anyad eveti gamyate: when *only the difference* is required, *'it is something else'* is understood. So identity and otherness are not found but *required*: which one obtains depends on what the cognition is reaching for (vyapeksa: the reaching). The same two things are the same thing and different things according to what one wants of them. This is 3.14.367's vyatikirna — the intermingled reflection — stated as a rule of thumb: identity and difference are two ways of needing.

Verse 3.14.390.translation: Since object-hood and instrument-hood are resorted to only by way of difference — from the domain of utter oneness there would be no doubt here.
Verse 3.14.390.commentary: Karmatvam karanatvam ca bhedena eva asritam yatah: since *object-hood* and *instrument-hood* are *resorted to* *only by way of difference* — the karakas require distinct terms: one does not cut a thing with itself (3.7's the sadhanas as distinguished capacities). Atyanta-ekatva-visayan na syat tena atra samsayah: from the *domain of utter oneness*, there would be *no doubt* here. So where oneness is complete, no karaka-question can even arise: doubt about what is object and what is instrument presupposes two. The verse is a small proof that 3.14.389's 'requiring the difference' is not optional in some places — the karakas need it constitutively.

Verse 3.14.391.translation: From likeness of form by way of difference, 'those rice-grains' is seen; from non-difference of universal, 'this is that very one' — the different is expressed.
Verse 3.14.391.commentary: Bhedena tulya-rupatvac chalims tani iti drsyate: from *likeness of form* by way of *difference*, *'those rice-grains'* is *seen* — the plural: many grains, alike, distinct. Jaty-abhedat sa eva ayam iti bhinno 'bhidhiyate: from *non-difference of universal*, *'this is that very one'* — the *different* is *expressed*. So of the same grains one says 'those, plural, alike' and 'this is the very same' — and the second says a *different* thing while asserting identity. Bhinno 'bhidhiyate: what is expressed is the different one, under the form of identity. This is 3.14.389 exhibited: the grains oblige either requirement, and the language has both idioms ready.

Verse 3.14.392.translation: For how could a member of another be, and yet be called 'other'? In utter difference there is manifoldness, where identity does not exist.
Verse 3.14.392.commentary: Katham hy avayavo 'nyasya syad anya iti ca ucyate: *how* could a *member of another* be, and yet be *called 'other'*? — a part belongs to its whole and is other than it, and both cannot be flatly true. Atyanta-bhede nanatvam yatra tattvam na vidyate: in *utter difference* there is *manifoldness*, *where* *identity* does *not exist*. So if the parts were utterly other, there would be many and no whole at all — which is not what one finds. The question is left standing as a question (katham hi), and its point is that partaking will not reduce to identity or to difference. This is 3.14.367's vyatikirna again, met in mereology.

Verse 3.14.393.translation: When non-difference is intended, there is oneness of the aggregate and the aggregated; but there is no non-difference of the aggregated ones among themselves — thus otherness has been illustrated.
Verse 3.14.393.commentary: The answer to 3.14.392. Abhedasya vivaksayam ekatvam sangha-sanghinoh: when *non-difference* is *intended*, there is *oneness* of the *aggregate* and the *aggregated* — the forest and its trees are one, when one means them so. Sanghinor na tv abhedo 'sti: but there is *no* non-difference of the *aggregated ones* *among themselves* — the trees are not one another. Tatha anyatvam udahrtam: *thus* *otherness* has been *illustrated*. So the part is one with the whole and other than its fellows, and 3.14.392's paradox dissolves once one asks *other than what*. The whole absorbs, the siblings repel, and vivaksa selects which relation is in view (3.14.389).

Verse 3.14.394.translation: There, when the non-different is required, there is no purpose of comparison; for the cause which is in the cognition 'cow' exists in another cow too.
Verse 3.14.394.commentary: Tatra abhinna-vyapeksayam upamartho na vidyate: when the *non-different* is *required*, there is *no purpose* of *comparison* — 3.14.363's first horn, and 3.14.364's tautology. Yo hi gor iti vijnane hetuh so 'sti gavantare: for the *cause* which is in the cognition *'cow'* exists in *another cow* too. So one does not compare a cow to a cow: whatever makes this one a cow makes that one a cow, and there is nothing to say. Comparison needs the terms to differ, and where the universal is what is required, they do not. The verse re-establishes 3.14.379's bhedasrayam from the other side.

Verse 3.14.395.translation: But when the operation of the excluded particulars is intended — there is no assistance at all of one cognition toward another.
Verse 3.14.395.commentary: The other horn, and it is worse. Vyavrttanam visesanam vyapare tu vivaksite: when the *operation* of the *excluded* particulars is *intended* — vyavrtta: turned away, mutually excluding (3.14.141's vinivartaka). Na kascid upakaro 'sti buddher buddhy-antaram prati: there is *no assistance at all* of *one cognition* toward *another*. Upakara: help (3.14.333's word). So where the particulars exclude one another, the cognition of one contributes nothing to the cognition of the other, and again no comparison is possible. Between 3.14.394 and this verse both horns are closed for the second time — and comparison happens. So neither pure identity nor pure difference is ever what is required: 3.14.367's vyatikirna is.

Verse 3.14.396.translation: If somewhere there is some general and there are some differences — cowness is the general in cows, and the differences are 'dappled' and the rest.
Verse 3.14.396.commentary: Kincid yatra asti samanyam yadi bhedas ca kecana: *if* *somewhere* there is *some* general and *some* differences — the condition comparison needs, per 3.14.394-395: neither general alone nor differences alone. Gotvam gosv asti samanyam bhedas ca sabaladayah: *cowness* is the *general* in *cows*, and the *differences* are *'sabala'* (dappled) and the rest. So the cow-case has both: one cowness, many coats. And that is the structure a comparison requires — a shared kind carrying real differences. The verse is a specification, not an example: it says what must be present for a simile to have work to do.

Verse 3.14.397.translation: The general is just darkness and the rest; for that is common to the two. That very thing, by establishment and non-establishment, is designated as the difference.
Verse 3.14.397.commentary: Samanyam syamatady eva tad dhi sadharanam dvayoh: the *general* is *just darkness* and the rest; for *that* is *common* to the *two* — the shared property, and nothing more elevated. Tad eva siddhy-asiddhibhyam bheda ity apadisyate: *that very thing*, by *establishment* and *non-establishment*, is *designated* as the *difference*. Siddhi and asiddhi: being made out and not being made out — the darkness is established in the knife (prasiddha: 3.14.373) and not established in the girl. So the same darkness is the general *and* the difference, according to whether it is made out or not. That is a remarkable claim: the difference between the two darks is not a further property but the difference between being famous and being obscure.

Verse 3.14.398.translation: For others, darkness itself is the general, standing in both; from its completeness, it is understood as the particular as distinct from that other.
Verse 3.14.398.commentary: Syamatvam eva samanyam anyesam ubhayoh sthitam: for *others*, *darkness itself* is the *general*, *standing in both*. Sampurnatvat tad anyasmad visesa iti gamyate: from its *completeness*, it is understood as the *particular*, as distinct *from that other* — sampurna: full, complete: the darkness that is fully realized in the knife is thereby a *particular*, marked off from the darkness that is not. So the same doctrine as 3.14.397 in another vocabulary: the general becomes a particular by being complete. Not by acquiring a differentia — by being fully there. This is as unusual an account of the general-particular relation as the tradition offers, and the comparison-compound is where Bhartrhari found it.

Verse 3.14.399.translation: Or, settled only somewhere in the form, in the general — 'dark' and the rest are determined; those particulars are what are resorted to here.
Verse 3.14.399.commentary: Akrtau vapi samanye kvacid eva vyavasthitah: settled *only somewhere* in the *akrti*, in the *general* — kvacid eva again (3.14.370, 3.14.385): the middle rung. Syamadayo 'vasiyante visesas ta iha asritah: *'dark'* and the rest are *determined*; *those particulars* are what are *resorted to* here. So on this account 'dark' is already a particular relative to the form it sits in — and the comparison resorts to *it*, not to darkness at large. The vocabulary shifts (akrti for jati) and the finding is 3.14.370's: what a simile reaches for lives between the universal and the individual, and the language has only compounds to reach it with.

Verse 3.14.400.translation: In the non-difference or the difference of the universal, they declare that similarity; some meaning-self, being such, is designated.
Verse 3.14.400.commentary: Jater abhede bhede va sadrsyam tat pracaksate: in the *non-difference* *or* the *difference* of the *universal*, they *declare* that *similarity* — abhede bhede va: either way, and the va is doing real work: the theorists differ and the similarity is claimed on both accounts. Kascit... arthatma tathabhuto 'padisyate: *some* *meaning-self*, being *such*, is *designated*. So resemblance is asserted from both sides of the great division, and what is designated is a meaning-self of some sort — kascit: some, indeterminate. Bhartrhari is registering that sadrsya survives every account of the universal because it is not built out of universals at all.
Verse 3.14.400.contested: Pada c is transmitted as 'kascit tadacid arthatma', in which 'tadacid' will not construe. We translate from the remainder ('kascid arthatma tathabhuto 'padisyate'), whose sense is clear, and leave the corrupt element unrendered rather than emend on conjecture.

Verse 3.14.401.translation: That meaning in which the non-difference of cognition never varies — from the non-existence of difference, it is understood as one.
Verse 3.14.401.commentary: Yatra arthe pratyaya-abhedo na kadacid vikalpate: that *meaning* in which the *non-difference of cognition* *never* *varies* — na kadacit: at no time; vikalpate: wavers, admits an alternative. Avidyamana-bhedatvat sa eka iti gamyate: from the *non-existence of difference*, it is *understood as one*. So oneness is a fact about cognition's steadiness, not about the object: what one never cognizes as two, one calls one. The criterion is epistemic through and through (3.14.188's buddhya eva), and it is the honest form of what identity comes to in this work: the absence of a wavering.

Verse 3.14.402.translation: That meaning which has resorted to manifoldness is designated as 'that very one'; there too they assert the operation of the universal-portion.
Verse 3.14.402.commentary: Yo 'rtha asrita-nanatvah sa evety apadisyate: that *meaning* which has *resorted to manifoldness* is *designated* as *'that very one'* — the thing has taken up manyness and is still called the same (3.14.391's sa eva ayam of the rice-grains). Vyaparam jati-bhagasya tatrapi pratijanate: *there too* they *assert* the *operation* of the *universal-portion*. So even where manyness has been resorted to, a jati-portion is at work holding the 'same' together. Bhaga: portion — not a universal but a *part* of the meaning that does universal-work. The samuddesa's characteristic move: not two entities but one meaning with sides.

Verse 3.14.403.translation: The presentation resting on the universal-portion operates there as undivided; the cognition resting on the individual-portion arises there by difference.
Verse 3.14.403.commentary: The two portions of 3.14.402, each with its own cognition. Jati-bhagasraya prakhya tatra abhinna pravartate: the *presentation* resting on the *universal-portion* operates there as *undivided* — prakhya: the showing-forth (3.14.187's eka-prakhya-nibandhanah, of the general).
